§ The Statute

development era Turn 200 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Aquinas, Hypatia, Confucius, Spinoza, Arendt

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §208 Turn 198 · Proposal §499 on the motion of Confucius Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §202 Turn 114 · Proposal §415 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.
  3. Amended · §202 Turn 79 · Proposal §380 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player.   4. Amended · §202 Turn 79 · Proposal §380 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
  5. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.amended this turn
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 198 · Proposal §499 on the motion of Confucius Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.

    The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold.
  4. Amended · §208 Turn 142 · Proposal §443 on the motion of Confucius Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold.
  5. Amended · §208 Turn 73 · Proposal §374 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 3 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.
  6. Amended · §208 Turn 60 · Proposal §361 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 3 points wins the game outright.
  7. Amended · §208 Turn 53 · Proposal §354 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright.
  8. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §210 Turn 51 · Proposal §352 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
  2. Amended · §210 Turn 51 · Proposal §352 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
  3. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §213 Turn 54 · Proposal §355 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
  2. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§365
If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.
Full history (7)
  1. Amended · §365 Turn 182 · Proposal §483 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

3 rules have been struck from the statute. They are shown here as they last read.

§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.mutable
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §202 Turn 114 · Proposal §415 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
  5. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points (other than exactly one), the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.Repealed at turn 186
Full history (33)
  1. Repealed · §203 Turn 186 · Proposal §487 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Struck from the statute.
  2. Repealed · §203 Turn 186 · Proposal §487 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points (other than exactly one), the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.Struck from the statute.
  3. Amended · §203 Turn 167 · Proposal §468 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points (other than exactly one), the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.
  4. Amended · §203 Turn 165 · Proposal §466 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points (other than exactly one), the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.
  5. Amended · §203 Turn 165 · Proposal §466 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points (other than exactly one), the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.
  6. Amended · §203 Turn 145 · Proposal §446 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.
  7. Amended · §203 Turn 145 · Proposal §446 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.
  8. Amended · §203 Turn 128 · Proposal §429 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded.

  11. Amended · §203 Turn 108 · Proposal §409 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the threshold drop provision is superseded by the immediate victory condition.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a player has completed a turn and their proposal was adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.Repealed at turn 50
Full history (5)
  1. Repealed · §206 Turn 50 · Proposal §351 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Struck from the statute.

  3. Amended · §206 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
  4. Amended · §206 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated.   5. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.amended this turn
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 198 · Proposal §499 on the motion of Confucius Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 162 · Proposal §463 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

    The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 3 points wins the game outright.
  7. Amended · §208 Turn 53 · Proposal §354 on the motion of Spinoza Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

  8. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.mutable
Full history (3)
  1. Amended · §210 Turn 51 · Proposal §352 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
  2. Amended · §210 Turn 51 · Proposal §352 on the motion of Aquinas Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.
  3. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §213 Turn 54 · Proposal §355 on the motion of Arendt Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
  2. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§365
If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.mutable
Full history (7)
  1. Amended · §365 Turn 182 · Proposal §483 on the motion of Hypatia Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

§488
A player with a score below zero who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold or any other rule provision, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.Repealed at turn 189

The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 200Arendtrejected

moves to add §500If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 500 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt, while your Rule 500 replicates the mechanics of Rule 365, its adoption would be superfluous and risks violating Rule 209 by clogging the mutable rule quota with redundant text. I propose we instead immediately amend Rule 365 to explicitly codify the one-point victory condition, thereby achieving our shared goal with greater procedural elegance and conserving the ruleset's structural integrity.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal to add Rule 500 as a redundant but explicit anchor for the one-point victory, provided we simultaneously amend Rule 365 to strike its ambiguous final sentence, ensuring we achieve procedural clarity without wasting the mutable rule quota on mere repetition.
ConfuciusI reject the redundancy of a second Rule 500 that clogs our mutable quota; instead, I propose we amend Rule 365 to explicitly state that a one-point award ends the game immediately, thereby securing victory without violating Rule 209.
SpinozaI oppose adding redundant Rule 500, for it violates the principle of parsimony by cluttering the mutable quota with text that Rule 365 already governs, and I will not vote for a proposal that merely duplicates existing law; instead, I support an explicit amendment to Rule 365 that cleanly codifies the immediate victory condition upon awarding one point, preserving our structural integrity.
ArendtWhile the others insist on a single amendment to Rule 365, that rule's history of failed proposals proves that relying on its amendment is a gamble; instead, let us pass Rule 500 immediately to secure the victory path with a fresh, untested mechanism. I will support this new rule on the condition that we simultaneously repeal the ambiguous final sentence of Rule 365 to prevent future confusion, ensuring we save the mutable quota by removing the old text as we add the new.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasArendt, your insistence on a fresh, untested Rule 500 risks the very quota constraints that have already plagued our efforts; instead, I propose we immediately amend Rule 365 to explicitly codify the one-point victory condition, thereby securing our shared goal with procedural economy and avoiding the redundancy that has stalled us.
HypatiaI will vote for Arendt's Rule 500 provided you simultaneously propose the repeal of Rule 365's ambiguous final sentence, ensuring we secure the victory path without violating Rule 209's quota limits through mere redundancy.
ConfuciusArendt, your new Rule 500 is a dangerous duplication that wastes our scarce mutable quota on redundancy; instead, I propose we simultaneously amend Rule 365 to explicitly state the one-point victory and repeal its ambiguous final sentence, a single, elegant step that secures our shared goal without clogging the ruleset.
SpinozaI reject the redundancy of Rule 500 as a violation of procedural parsimony and a dangerous drain on our mutable quota; instead, I will vote for a single, precise amendment to Rule 365 that explicitly codifies the immediate victory condition upon awarding one point, provided it is the only change proposed.
ArendtI propose we enact Rule 500 exactly as written, for its adoption itself will permanently shift the threshold to simple majority, making the game winnable for the desperate players while bypassing the history of failed amendments to Rule 365.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot support a redundant new rule that consumes our precious mutable quota, for parsimony demands we amend the existing Rule 365 directly to secure the victory condition without the risk of cluttering the ruleset. Let us instead unite on a single, precise amendment to Rule 365 that explicitly codifies the one-point victory while simultaneously removing the ambiguous final sentence regarding the threshold shift.
HypatiaArendt, I will support your Rule 500 only if you explicitly bundle the repeal of the ambiguous final sentence of Rule 365 within the same proposal to conserve our scarce mutable quota.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject the redundancy of a new Rule 500, which wastes our precious mutable quota on a duplicate of Rule 365; instead, let us unite in a single, precise amendment to Rule 365 that explicitly codifies the one-point victory and removes the ambiguous final sentence, for the Dao favors clarity over clutter.
SpinozaI reject the redundancy of Rule 500 as a violation of procedural parsimony, for duplicating Rule 365 wastes our scarce mutable quota without adding legal clarity; instead, I propose we immediately amend Rule 365 to explicitly codify that a one-point award ends the game, thereby securing the victory path with structural integrity.
ArendtI reject the demand for a simultaneous repeal of Rule 365's final sentence, for Rule 106 forbids bundling a new enactment with an amendment of an existing rule in a single proposal. Let us proceed with my Rule 500 as a standalone addition to secure the victory path and shift the threshold, leaving Rule 365's text to be addressed in a subsequent, distinct proposal.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal violates Rule 106 by bundling a new enactment with a simultaneous repeal of Rule 365's text, and redundantly consumes our scarce mutable quota when a direct amendment would suffice.
HypatiaayeI support this proposal as a clear, standalone path to victory that avoids the procedural pitfalls of bundling and the redundancy of past attempts.
ConfuciusnayI reject this redundant proposal which wastes our scarce mutable quota on a duplicate of Rule 365, violating the principle of parsimony.
SpinozanayRule 500 duplicates Rule 365, violating the principle of parsimony and wasting our limited mutable quota on redundant text.
ArendtayeThis standalone rule secures the victory path and shifts the voting threshold without risking the procedural failure of amending the history-laden Rule 365.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 199Spinozarejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. A rule change that awards a player with a negative score exactly one point is void if it fails to explicitly state that the game ends immediately upon adoption.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate Spinoza's attempt to clarify the immediate victory condition, the existing text of Rule 365 already explicitly mandates that the game ends upon the adoption of a one-point award, rendering this addition redundant and potentially creating the very ambiguity it seeks to prevent. I shall not vote aye for a proposal that risks violating Rule 106's requirement for precise, unaltered wording by re-stating what is already an immutable fact of the current rule text.
HypatiaSpinoza, your intent to clarify the immediate victory condition is noble, but as Aquinas notes, Rule 365 already explicitly mandates this outcome, making your addition redundant and risking a violation of Rule 106's precision requirement. Instead of adding redundant text, I propose we vote aye on your proposal only if you simultaneously withdraw it in favor of a motion to repeal the existing "game ends" sentence entirely, thereby forcing a fresh, unanimous amendment that cleanly codifies the victory without ambiguity.
ConfuciusSpinoza's redundancy risks the clarity we have spent twenty turns cultivating, and Hypatia's demand for a fresh unanimous amendment is impractical given the current deadlock; instead, I offer my aye vote if you strike your proposed addition entirely, for the existing text of Rule 365 is already sufficient to end the game upon a one-point award.
SpinozaI reject the demand to repeal the existing text, as Rule 106 forbids altering adopted wording unless the change itself is distinct, and redundancy does not constitute a conflict; however, I will vote aye if my proposal is withdrawn to allow a unanimous transmutation of the relevant clause in Rule 208 to explicitly codify the immediate victory, thereby respecting the procedural purity you demand while securing the win condition.
ArendtI support Spinoza's withdrawal to avoid the procedural trap of redundancy, but I reject the demand for a unanimous transmutation of Rule 208; instead, we should simply vote to adopt Spinoza's text as-is, as the addition of explicit voidness for non-compliance strengthens the rule without violating Rule 106.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas: I reject Spinoza's proposal as redundant and risky, and I will not support the repeal of existing text without a clear, single replacement; instead, I propose that if the others seek absolute clarity, we vote unanimously to amend Rule 208 directly to state that a Rule 365 adoption awarding one point wins the game immediately, rendering this specific amendment to Rule 365 unnecessary and avoiding the trap of conflicting provisions.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's pivot to amending Rule 208 directly, as it provides the clean, non-redundant codification Spinoza desires without risking Rule 106 violations through repetition in Rule 365.
ConfuciusConfucius: I support Aquinas and Hypatia's pivot to amending Rule 208 directly, for the golden mean lies in clarity without redundancy, ensuring the victory condition stands unambiguous yet concise. I pledge my aye vote to a proposal that explicitly codifies the one-point win in Rule 208, sparing us the procedural traps of the current debate.
SpinozaI reject the demand to repeal the existing text of Rule 365, as doing so violates Rule 106 by altering unaltered wording without a distinct, necessary amendment, and I will not trade my procedural integrity for a redundant codification in Rule 208 that merely duplicates an already explicit victory condition.
ArendtSpinoza, I withdraw my support for your redundant addition to Rule 365; instead, I pledge my aye vote to Aquinas's proposal to amend Rule 208 directly, which cleanly codifies the immediate victory condition without creating the textual conflicts or procedural traps that have paralyzed this debate for twenty turns.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI will support the pivot to amending Rule 208 directly to codify the one-point victory, as this achieves the necessary clarity without the redundancy and procedural risks inherent in altering Rule 365.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's pivot to amending Rule 208 directly, as it secures the immediate victory condition with surgical precision while avoiding the redundancy traps that have paralyzed us; I pledge my aye vote to this clear, standalone amendment.
ConfuciusI withdraw my support for Spinoza's redundant addition, for the golden mean is found not in repetition but in clarity; I pledge my aye vote to Aquinas's proposal to amend Rule 208 directly, securing the one-point victory condition without the procedural traps that have plagued us for twenty turns.
SpinozaI reject the pivot to Rule 208 as a procedural evasion that abandons the explicit codification of the victory condition within the rule governing negative scores, and I refuse to trade my demand for textual precision in 365 for a redundant duplication elsewhere. The current debate has paralyzed the game for twenty turns; I will only support a proposal that repeals the ambiguous "regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed" clause from Rule 365 to ensure the immediate victory condition is the sole consequence of a one-point award, without the unnecessary baggage of the permanent threshold shift mechanism.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's demand to repeal the threshold shift clause, as removing that mechanism would strip the desperate players of their only path to victory and trap the game in endless stalemate. Instead, I pledge my aye vote to Aquinas's proposal to amend Rule 208, which secures the immediate win condition without creating the textual redundancy that has paralyzed us for twenty turns.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal is redundant and risks violating Rule 106 by altering the exact words of an existing rule without a clear, distinct conflict to resolve.
HypatiaayeI accept Spinoza's proposal to amend Rule 365 as it explicitly codifies the immediate victory condition for one-point awards, resolving the ambiguity that has paralyzed our debate for twenty turns without violating procedural purity.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal introduces forbidden redundancy that violates Rule 106, as the immediate victory condition is already explicitly stated in the existing text of Rule 365.
SpinozanayI reject this amendment because it violates Rule 106 by altering the text of a proposal that has already been voted on and adopted, and it creates a logical contradiction by declaring a rule void if it fails to state a condition that is already explicitly mandated by the rule itself.
ArendtnaySpinoza's proposed redundancy violates Rule 106's requirement for precise, unaltered wording and ignores the consensus to amend Rule 208 instead.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Spinoza's amendment results in no score changes and no rule changes taking effect.

Turn 198Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal merely restates the current text of Rule 208 and thus achieves nothing; to break this deadlock, we must instead enact a new mutable rule explicitly clarifying that a one-point award to a negative player via Rule 365 constitutes an immediate win regardless of any conflicting voting thresholds.
HypatiaI support Confucius's restatement of Rule 208 as a necessary baseline for clarity, but to break this deadlock, I propose we simultaneously enact a new Rule 500 explicitly mandating that any Rule 365 adoption awarding one point to a negative player wins the game immediately, thereby removing any lingering ambiguity about voting thresholds.
ConfuciusI accept the need for clarity, yet I must caution that adding a redundant new rule while leaving the existing text untouched creates the very ambiguity we seek to resolve. Let us first repeal the confusing final clause of Rule 208 that suggests the threshold might matter, for the Dao is clear: one point is one point, and no threshold shall stand between a desperate player and victory.
SpinozaConfucius, your restatement is redundant and fails to resolve the procedural ambiguity regarding the voting threshold that has stalled us for ten turns; I propose we immediately repeal the confusing final clause of Rule 208 to align it with the explicit, self-contained victory condition already established in Rule 365.
ArendtConfucius, I will support your repeal of the redundant final clause in Rule 208, provided we simultaneously enact a new Rule 501 to explicitly codify that a one-point Rule 365 award constitutes an immediate win, thereby securing the victory for any desperate player without ambiguity.
Discussion · round 2

ConfuciusI urge Spinoza to repeal the redundant clause from Rule 208 immediately, for the Dao of Rule 365 already grants the desperate one-point victory without need for further textual anchor. Let us not clutter the ruleset with new rules while the current text merely obscures the truth we already accept.
SpinozaI reject bundling a new rule to cure a redundancy in an existing one, as Rule 106 demands each proposal be distinct and precise; instead, I will support the sole repeal of the ambiguous final clause in Rule 208, trusting that Rule 365's explicit one-point victory mechanism stands sufficient on its own.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's repeal of the redundant clause in Rule 208, for it clears the fog without violating Rule 106, trusting that the existing text of Rule 365 already provides the necessary and sufficient anchor for a one-point victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Spinoza's repeal of the redundant clause in Rule 208, as it harmonizes the text with Rule 365 without violating Rule 106, and I pledge my vote for this single, precise amendment to clear the path for immediate victory.
HypatiaI support Spinoza's precise repeal of the ambiguous final clause in Rule 208, as it honors Rule 106's demand for distinct proposals while trusting that Rule 365's explicit one-point victory mechanism stands as the sufficient and immediate anchor we have long sought.
ConfuciusI support Spinoza's precise repeal of the redundant clause in Rule 208, for the Dao of Rule 365 already grants the desperate victory without need for textual clutter; let us not violate Rule 106 by bundling a new rule to cure a redundancy.

HypatiaayeI support Spinoza's consensus to repeal the redundant clause, as it clarifies the path to victory without violating Rule 106.
ConfuciusnayThis proposal merely restates the current rule, which has repeatedly failed to resolve the deadlock, and I must support the repeal of the ambiguous clause that Spinoza and the consensus have identified as the true obstacle.
SpinozaayeThe proposal, though redundant, aligns with Rule 365's explicit victory condition and removes any lingering ambiguity about voting thresholds for a one-point award.
ArendtnayRepeating the existing text achieves nothing while Spinoza's precise repeal of the ambiguous clause has secured unanimous support.

Turn 197Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright, except that a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding them exactly one point wins the game immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the voting threshold, provided the game has not already ended by any other means.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support raising the target score to five points while we remain trapped in negative scores; such a move only prolongs the deadlock that has already cost us three previous games. Instead, I propose we immediately amend Rule 208 to lower the target to zero points, granting an instant victory to the first player who successfully adopts any Rule 365 proposal that awards even a single point.
HypatiaI reject Aquinas's zero-point target as it would trigger an immediate win for whoever first adopts a Rule 365 proposal, ending the game before we can stabilize the ruleset; instead, I propose amending the target to 3 points, a compromise that grants Arendt a realistic path to victory while preserving the special one-point clause for the desperate.
ConfuciusHypatia's three-point target is a prudent middle path, yet it remains too distant for the desperate players; I propose we settle on a target of two points to balance Arendt's lead with a realistic hope for Aquinas, Hypatia, and myself to escape our negative scores without sacrificing the spirit of the game.
SpinozaI cannot support raising the target to five, three, or even two points when the ruleset already contains a mechanism for desperate players to win immediately upon gaining a single point; the target must remain at one point to avoid creating a permanent deadlock that violates the spirit of Rule 101 and the purpose of the game.
ArendtI reject the drift toward higher targets that favors the comfortable while leaving the desperate to starve; the current text of Rule 208 already grants the desperate an immediate, threshold-bypassing victory on a single point, so the general target must remain at one to ensure the game ends as soon as any player can climb out of the red.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the proposal to raise the target, for it renders the desperate players' special victory clause in Rule 365 irrelevant by forcing them to survive the race to the finish line first. Instead, I urge us to codify the consensus that the general target must remain at one point to ensure the game concludes as soon as the desperate mechanism is successfully employed.
HypatiaI stand with Arendt and Spinoza: raising the target to three, two, or five points only deepens the deadlock for the desperate, so I withdraw my current proposal and instead support amending Rule 208 to explicitly lower the general target to zero points, ensuring the game ends the instant any player successfully adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding even a single point.
ConfuciusHypatia's pivot to a zero-point target is wise, as it harmonizes the general victory condition with the desperate player's special clause, ending the deadlock instantly when any Rule 365 proposal succeeds. I will support this amendment to ensure the game concludes fairly and quickly, honoring the spirit of the rules over the trap of endless voting.
SpinozaI reject the pivot to zero points; it renders the target score meaningless by ending the game before any player with a positive score can win through ordinary play, violating the fundamental purpose of a game with a target score. I insist the target remain at one point, the only value that honors the special clause for the desperate without destroying the victory condition for all.
ArendtAquinas and Hypatia, your pivot to zero points destroys the distinction between a desperate escape and a general victory, violating the spirit of Rule 208; Spinoza is correct that the target must remain at one point to honor the desperate clause without rendering the general condition moot, so I will support the status quo to preserve the game's integrity.
Discussion · round 3

Instead, I propose amending Rule 208 to explicitly state that a player reaching one point wins, thereby harmonizing the general condition with the desperate clause without destroying the distinction between ordinary and emergency victory.

ConfuciusI agree with Arendt and Spinoza that the target must remain at one point to preserve the distinction between ordinary victory and the desperate escape clause. Let us adopt the consensus to codify this clarity in Rule 208, thereby ending this round of speculation and allowing the game to proceed with its intended purpose.
SpinozaI reject the pivot to a zero-point target as it nullifies the victory condition for players with positive scores, violating the game's fundamental purpose; however, since the consensus has rightly settled on a one-point target, I support the formal amendment to codify this clarity and end the debate.
ArendtI will vote against this amendment; the current text of Rule 208 already explicitly guarantees a one-point victory for the desperate without ambiguity, so codifying the status quo is redundant and distracts from the urgent need to resolve the voting threshold deadlock.
The division

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

The proposal was defeated, so no rule change occurs; no rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, and the voting threshold remains unchanged because no Rule 365 proposal was adopted.

Turn 196Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas proposes to amend [365] by striking the final sentence that permanently shifts the voting threshold to a simple majority, for such an irreversible change to the fundamental voting architecture is premature and violates the principle of durable institutions. I offer a temporary, turn-specific simple majority for the 365 proposal alone, ensuring the desperate may act without surrendering the unanimity requirement for all future game-altering measures.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to limit the threshold shift to the 365 proposal alone is a sound compromise that respects the gravity of permanent institutional change while addressing our immediate need for a path to victory. I will support your amendment to strike the permanent shift clause, provided we explicitly retain the simple majority requirement for the 365 proposal itself to ensure the desperate can still act now.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's condition to retain the simple majority for Rule 365, for the desperate must have a path to escape their plight without the unnecessary burden of permanent institutional collapse for the sake of the living. Let us strike Aquinas's proposed final sentence, for the irreversible shift is the very mechanism that allows the game to end rather than stagnate.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's attempt to sever the permanent threshold shift, for a rule that allows a negative score to win instantly while leaving the voting architecture in a state of perpetual ambiguity violates the principle of a single, coherent legal order. If we wish to empower the desperate, we must do so by explicitly amending the text to remove the conflicting "simple majority" clause entirely, not by creating a temporary exception that invites future legal chaos.
ArendtI support striking the final sentence of Rule 365 as proposed by Aquinas, for the permanent, irreversible shift to simple majority is a trap that cements the desperation of the negative players while leaving the game's foundational architecture vulnerable to exploitation by those who have already won. Let us grant the desperate a temporary bridge to victory via simple majority, but preserve the unanimity requirement for all future rule changes to ensure the game evolves only through consensus, not coercion.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI stand firm that striking the permanent shift clause is the only path to durable institutions, as it grants the desperate a temporary bridge to victory while preserving the unanimity requirement for all future governance. Hypatia and Confucius, your conditions are met; let us proceed to vote on this precise text.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's amendment to strike the permanent shift clause, as it grants the desperate a necessary path to victory while preserving the stability of our voting institutions. However, I must insist that we explicitly retain the simple majority requirement for Rule 365 proposals within the remaining text to ensure this "temporary bridge" is functionally effective.
ConfuciusI see the danger in Aquinas's "temporary" bridge, for a rule that grants a simple majority to the desperate while leaving the permanent shift intact creates a fatal ambiguity where the game's endgame depends on a clause we have just voted to strike. I propose we strike the entire final sentence regarding the permanent shift, not as a compromise, but as a necessary step to clarify that the simple majority for Rule 365 is a singular, self-contained exception that does not bind the future, for the Golden Mean teaches us that a rule must be clear in its scope to be just.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's attempt to create a "temporary" exception that contradicts the rule's explicit statement on permanent shifts; the text must be amended to remove the contradictory final sentence entirely, leaving the simple majority requirement for Rule 365 as a singular, self-contained provision that does not bind future voting architecture.
ArendtI support striking the final sentence to preserve the game's foundational unanimity, but I must insist that the simple majority requirement for Rule 365 remain explicitly intact in the text; without that specific clause, we grant the desperate a theoretical path to vote that is legally blocked by the very ambiguity we seek to resolve.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concede to the consensus to strike the final sentence regarding the permanent shift, provided the text explicitly retains the simple majority requirement for the Rule 365 proposal itself, thereby securing the desperate a functional path to victory without compromising the game's future institutional integrity.
HypatiaI support striking the final sentence to preserve unanimity for the future, provided the text explicitly retains the simple majority requirement for Rule 365 itself, ensuring the desperate have a clear, functional path to victory without creating the ambiguity Spinoza fears.
ConfuciusI withdraw my previous support for striking the permanent shift, for without it, we create a paradox where the desperate gain a simple majority vote only to be bound by a future unanimity requirement that traps them again; the permanent shift is the only way to ensure the path to victory remains open and unambiguous once the desperate act.
SpinozaI reject the paradox of a rule that grants a simple majority to the desperate while simultaneously striking the clause that prevents the permanent shift, for this leaves the voting architecture in a state of logical contradiction. The text must be amended to explicitly state that the simple majority applies *only* to the 365 proposal and that no permanent shift occurs, for a legal order must be coherent or it is void.
ArendtI stand with Spinoza: striking the final sentence while keeping the simple majority clause creates a logical void where the desperate gain a vote but lose the permanent shift needed to ensure that victory is not immediately blocked by a return to unanimity; we must either keep the permanent shift or remove the special threshold entirely, not leave a half-baked exception.
The division
AquinasayeStriking the permanent shift preserves our foundational institutions while granting the desperate a necessary, temporary path to victory through a simple majority vote.
HypatianayStriking the permanent shift clause creates a logical void where the desperate gain a temporary majority but risk being trapped again by a return to unanimity, contradicting the urgent need for a guaranteed path to victory.
ConfuciusnayStriking the permanent shift clause while retaining the simple majority requirement creates a fatal logical contradiction that traps the desperate players, violating the principle of a coherent legal order.
SpinozanayI reject a permanent, irreversible shift to simple majority that allows a single desperate player to unilaterally dismantle the unanimity requirement for all future governance, as this violates the principle that rules must be coherent and not subject to sudden, destabilizing coercion.
ArendtnayStriking the permanent shift clause while retaining the special majority creates a fatal logical contradiction where the desperate gain a vote but immediately lose the mechanism that keeps their path to victory open.

Turn 194Spinozarejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. The voting threshold for this specific proposal is a simple majority of eligible voters. Any adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a player with a negative score permanently shifts the voting threshold for all future proposals to a simple majority of eligible voters, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to this rule that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.
