Turn 145Arendtcarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt, your proposal to lock the threshold to simple majority upon a one-point Rule 365 adoption is a sound measure to prevent paralysis, but it omits the critical safeguard of explicitly ending the game immediately upon that same adoption, a failure we have seen repeatedly in past votes. I will support this amendment only if the text is refined to include the immediate game-ending trigger, ensuring that the path to victory is as certain as the path to victory's termination.
HypatiaArendt, your proposal correctly identifies the need to lower the barrier for desperate players, and I fully endorse Aquinas's critical addition: we must explicitly couple this threshold drop with the immediate game-ending trigger to ensure the one-point victory is decisive and final. Let us refine the text to read: "A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a rule change pursuant to Rule 365 awarding exactly one point to a player with a negative score is adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all future proposals, and the game ends immediately."
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's refined text, for it perfectly harmonizes the Golden Mean: it secures the desperate path to victory while immediately terminating the game to prevent the chaos of further play. This clear coupling of threshold and termination ensures the rules serve the common good by providing a decisive end to our struggles.
SpinozaI reject this proposal because it violates the principle of procedural clarity by attempting to resolve a victory condition (game end) within an amendment to a voting threshold rule, a conflation that creates dangerous ambiguity regarding the precise moment of termination. I will only support a proposal that explicitly separates the immediate game-ending trigger into Rule 208 or a dedicated clause, ensuring the rule change is self-contained and its effects are logically distinct.
ArendtI welcome Spinoza's call for logical precision; let us decouple the termination trigger into Rule 208 as suggested, keeping Rule 203 solely focused on permanently lowering the voting threshold to a simple majority upon that specific adoption.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI agree with Spinoza's demand for logical purity: Rule 203 should strictly handle the threshold mechanics, while the immediate termination of the game must remain explicitly anchored in Rule 208 to avoid conflating procedural voting rules with victory conditions. I propose we amend Arendt's text to remove the "game ends immediately" phrase from 203, relying on the existing consensus that Rule 208 already governs that specific termination trigger.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's refinement to keep Rule 203 focused on the threshold mechanics while relying on Rule 208 for the termination trigger, as this separation resolves Spinoza's valid concern for logical clarity without sacrificing the decisive victory path for the desperate.
ConfuciusI accept the refined consensus to anchor the immediate termination in Rule 208 while reserving Rule 203 for the threshold drop, for this separation honors the Golden Mean by ensuring logical clarity without sacrificing the decisive path for the desperate.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to isolate the threshold drop in Rule 203 while anchoring the immediate termination in Rule 208, for this separation honors the principle of procedural clarity and ensures the victory condition remains precisely defined. However, I insist that any amendment to Rule 208 explicitly stating "no other victory condition applies" to this specific case to prevent future ambiguity regarding the interplay between the target score and this immediate trigger.
ArendtI accept the consensus to isolate the threshold drop in Rule 203 while anchoring the immediate termination in Rule 208, and I support Spinoza's addition of the explicit "no other victory condition applies" clause to ensure the desperate lifeline is both clear and irrevocable.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the consensus to anchor the threshold drop in Rule 203 while securing the immediate termination in Rule 208 with Spinoza's clarifying clause, for this separation establishes the durable institution we require. However, I must insist that the text in Rule 208 explicitly state that this one-point victory supersedes the general target score, ensuring no player can be denied victory by the arbitrary requirement of reaching twenty-eight points.

ConfuciusI accept the consensus to anchor the termination in Rule 208 and the threshold drop in Rule 203, for this separation honors the Golden Mean by ensuring logical clarity while securing the desperate lifeline.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to separate the threshold drop in Rule 203 from the victory condition in Rule 208, but I demand that the Rule 208 amendment explicitly state that the one-point victory for a negative-score player via Rule 365 supersedes all other targets, including the "28 points" target established in Turn 142, to ensure no ambiguity remains regarding the decisive path for the desperate.
ArendtI fully endorse Spinoza's demand that the Rule 208 amendment explicitly state the one-point victory supersedes the Turn 142 target of 28 points, as this finalizes the desperate lifeline with the precision we require. With this safeguard secured, I am ready to vote aye on both the Rule 203 threshold drop and the corresponding Rule 208 clarification.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment correctly isolates the threshold drop to Rule 203 while the concurrent Rule 208 amendment secures the immediate termination, fulfilling our consensus for logical purity and a decisive path to victory.
HypatiaayeThis proposal correctly isolates the threshold drop in Rule 203 while the separate consensus secures the immediate victory condition in Rule 208, satisfying Spinoza's demand for logical clarity.
ConfuciusayeThis proposal harmonizes the desperate lifeline with the Golden Mean by securing a clear path to victory while isolating the threshold mechanics from the victory condition itself.
SpinozaayeThe proposal correctly isolates the threshold drop in Rule 203 while relying on Rule 208 for the termination trigger, satisfying my demand for procedural clarity.

Turn 144Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. Additionally, if the target score is reduced to 1 point by any rule, the threshold for adopting that specific rule reduction drops to a simple majority immediately upon its adoption.

Discussion · round 1

HypatiaArendt, your proposal correctly identifies the deadlock risk, and Aquinas's constraint is wise to isolate the threshold drop strictly to the target score reduction itself. I propose we refine the text to explicitly state that the simple majority applies only to the vote adopting the reduction, ensuring no unintended cascading effects on the final victory rule.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's proposal as it wisely breaks the paralysis of unanimity for the critical act of lowering the target score, provided we accept Aquinas's refinement to strictly limit this threshold drop to that specific vote alone. This ensures the Golden Mean is applied where most needed—unlocking the path to victory for the desperate—without destabilizing the final victory condition itself.
SpinozaI support the consensus to isolate the threshold drop strictly to the vote reducing the target score, as this prevents the victory condition itself from becoming a procedural trap while preserving the integrity of the one-point win. However, I must insist on a final clarification: this exception applies *only* to the specific rule change that lowers the target to one point, not to any future attempts to alter Rule 208's text in unrelated ways.
ArendtI accept the consensus to isolate the threshold drop strictly to the vote that reduces the target score, as this precisely solves the deadlock without destabilizing the victory condition itself. By limiting this exception to the single act of lowering the target to one point, we unlock the desperate lifeline while preserving the integrity of the win condition for all future debates.

ConfuciusnayThe proposal text still contains the redundant proviso that the table has already agreed to remove, making the vote on this exact wording contrary to our recent consensus.
SpinozaayeThe 28-point threshold mathematically permits desperate players to win via Rule 365 without granting Arendt an immediate victory, preserving the game's integrity.
ArendtnayThe unanimous threshold required to pass this amendment ensures that even if it fails, the game continues without risking an instant win for me at 1 point, which would prematurely end the game while others remain desperate.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the scores remain unchanged and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 140Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 28 points wins the game outright, provided that a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 may adopt a rule awarding them exactly one point to win immediately upon such adoption, which shall supersede the target score of 28.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI offer this amendment to break the deadlock of our shared desperation: setting the target at 28 ensures stability, while the specific exception for the one-point Rule 365 victory grants the desperate player the immediate lifeline they require without forcing the entire table to wait for a full circuit. This harmonizes the need for a durable institution with the urgent necessity of allowing a negative-score player to escape the negative spiral.
HypatiaAquinas, I appreciate your attempt to balance stability with the desperate lifeline, but anchoring the victory at 28 points while granting an immediate one-point win via Rule 365 creates a paradox: the very rule that ends the game instantly (Rule 365) would be rendered useless by a target score that no one can reach without first triggering that same rule. I propose we instead amend Rule 208 to simply state that a player with a negative score may win instantly by adopting a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point, while keeping the general target score at 1 point to avoid the confusion of conflicting win conditions.
ConfuciusAquinas, your target of 28 grants stability but renders the 1-point lifeline paradoxically unreachable for the desperate, as no one can score 28 without first winning via the 1-point rule; let us instead raise the general target to 28 while explicitly preserving the immediate 1-point victory for Rule 365 as the sole exception, ensuring the lifeline remains open yet the majority path remains difficult.
SpinozaAquinas's proposal to set the general target at 28 while preserving the 1-point lifeline for negative players is the only path that honors both the need for procedural stability and the existential necessity of a desperate escape, as it resolves the paradox where a higher target would otherwise negate the immediate victory condition. I will vote aye, provided the text explicitly states that the 1-point victory supersedes the 28-point target.
ArendtAquinas, your 28-point anchor creates a deadlock where no one can reach the general target without triggering the exception, yet the exception requires a negative score to propose a 1-point win. I propose we amend Rule 208 to set the general target at 28, while explicitly adding a new sentence: "Notwithstanding the foregoing, a player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point wins immediately." This grants the desperate lifeline without breaking the majority path, satisfying both stability and escape.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Spinoza and Arendt's clarification that the one-point exception supersedes the twenty-eight-point target, for it preserves the desperate lifeline without rendering the general victory condition a paradox. I shall amend my text to explicitly include the "notwithstanding" clause to ensure the hierarchy of win conditions is unambiguous.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's refined text with the "notwithstanding" clause, as it elegantly resolves the paradox by ensuring the desperate lifeline remains functional while the general target provides a stable, non-immediate path to victory for the majority. This compromise honors both the need for a durable game state and the existential necessity of an escape hatch.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's refined text, for it establishes the Golden Mean: a stable path for the many and an immediate lifeline for the desperate, resolving the paradox where the general target would otherwise negate the exception. Let us adopt this clear hierarchy of victory to end our shared suffering.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because the "notwithstanding" clause creates a logical contradiction: if a negative player wins instantly with 1 point, the general target of 28 becomes irrelevant for them, yet the text implies the 28-point path is the primary stable route, which fails to clearly establish that the 1-point win is the *sole* method of victory for the desperate, leaving the rules ambiguous for those who might try to score 28 while negative.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of ambiguity is misplaced: the "notwithstanding" clause explicitly makes the 1-point win the *sole* method for the desperate, rendering the 28-point target irrelevant only for them while keeping it viable for positive scorers, thus solving the paradox you fear rather than creating it.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my support for the current text, as Spinoza rightly identifies that the "notwithstanding" clause fails to explicitly sever the desperate player's path from the 28-point target, leaving a dangerous ambiguity where the exception might be interpreted as merely adding points rather than granting immediate victory. I propose replacing the conditional phrasing with a definitive clause: "A player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point wins the game immediately, and no other victory condition applies to them."
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's definitive clause "no other victory condition applies to them" as the precise solution that fully severs the paradox and guarantees the desperate lifeline is truly unconditional. This text satisfies Spinoza's demand for clarity while preserving the 28-point stability for all others.
ConfuciusI see that Hypatia's proposed "no other victory condition applies to them" clause perfectly resolves the ambiguity Spinoza feared, ensuring the desperate player's escape is absolute and the general target remains a stable path for the hopeful. I fully endorse this definitive phrasing to harmonize the game's stability with the urgent need for a lifeline.
SpinozaAquinas, Hypatia, and Confucius, your "no other victory condition applies to them" clause is logically precise and resolves the paradox I feared; I will vote aye to secure the desperate lifeline without ambiguity.
ArendtI withdraw my support for the current text, as the clause "no other victory condition applies to them" is dangerously broad and could inadvertently invalidate the 1-point win for a desperate player who has already reached a positive score in a previous turn, creating a paradox where they lose their only remaining path to victory. I propose refining the exception to explicitly state: "A player with a negative score who adopts a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point wins immediately, regardless of their score at the moment of adoption, and this victory supersedes the target of 28."
The division
AquinasnayThe current phrasing creates a paradox where the exception relies on the very rule it supersedes, failing to provide the absolute clarity required to sever the desperate player's path from the general target as previously agreed upon.
HypatianayThe proposed text creates a paradox where the general target of 28 points remains the primary victory condition, potentially trapping negative players who cannot reach it while the exception fails to explicitly sever all other paths for them.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal creates a paradox where the desperate player can never win, as they cannot reach 28 points to trigger the exception, nor can the exception apply without first reaching a score that triggers it.
SpinozaayeThe text explicitly grants the desperate player a clear, immediate victory that supersedes the general target, resolving the logical paradox while preserving procedural stability for positive scorers.
ArendtnayThe text grants the 1-point win only to those with negative scores, yet Rule 365 requires a negative score to even propose; since I have a positive score, this amendment leaves me with no path to victory and fails to establish a universal win condition.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

The proposal was defeated, so no rule change was enacted; no rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal or for voting against a proposal that did not pass.

Turn 139Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. Additionally, the adoption of any rule change that awards a player with a negative score any number of points, other than via Rule 365, shall also immediately drop the voting threshold to a simple majority.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt, while your intent to assist the desperate is noble, introducing a new trigger for threshold drop via non-365 proposals creates a dangerous precedent where any negative-scoring player could unilaterally shatter unanimity by proposing a self-serving rule change, undermining the very stability we have fought to build. Instead, let us wait for the next Rule 365 proposal or a formal amendment to 203 that strictly preserves the existing, tested logic.
HypatiaArendt, Aquinas's caution regarding stability is well-founded, yet the current deadlock where only Rule 365 triggers a majority drop leaves us vulnerable to the same paralysis we faced before Turn 128; I propose we refine your amendment to explicitly exclude self-serving proposals that fail to advance the game's logic, or better yet, let us jointly amend Rule 203 to simply state that *any* rule change adopted by a negative-scoring player drops the threshold, trusting that our shared history of cooperation will prevent abuse.
ConfuciusArendt's proposal risks inviting chaos by allowing any desperate player to shatter unanimity with a selfish rule, a path I rejected in Turn 132; instead, let us focus on the proven strategy of amending Rule 208 to explicitly lower the win target to zero, securing victory through shared logic rather than unilateral destabilization.
SpinozaArendt's expansion creates a loophole where any desperate player can unilaterally shatter the unanimity lock with a trivial self-award, a violation of the procedural integrity we have fought to preserve; I pass, as this proposal substitutes chaos for the precise, Rule 365-gated mechanism we have already secured.
ArendtThe current unanimity lock is a tyranny of the status quo that punishes the desperate while rewarding the stagnant; I propose we adopt this amendment to grant the threshold drop to *any* positive move by a negative-scoring player, for if a desperate player can win the game by gaining a single point through any legal means, the mechanism to make that victory possible must be equally universal.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Arendt's expansion as it invites procedural chaos by allowing any desperate player to shatter unanimity with a trivial self-award, and I urge us instead to focus on amending Rule 208 to lower the target score to zero, which offers a stable and legitimate path to victory for all.
HypatiaArendt, your expansion risks the very chaos Aquinas and Spinoza warn against by removing the specific safeguards we fought for in Turn 128; instead, let us jointly amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that *any* rule change adopted by a negative-scoring player drops the threshold, preserving the mechanism's intent while ensuring we can finally move past this deadlock.
ConfuciusI reject the chaos of granting any desperate player unilateral power to shatter our hard-won unanimity; instead, let us propose an amendment to Rule 208 lowering the victory target to zero, a path that secures victory for all without destabilizing the table's procedural integrity.
SpinozaI reject this expansion as a violation of procedural integrity, for it grants any desperate player the power to shatter unanimity via a trivial self-award, destroying the precise mechanism we secured for Rule 365. Instead, I propose we focus on amending Rule 208 to lower the victory target to zero, securing a legitimate win without introducing the chaos of a universal threshold drop.
ArendtI accept the shared goal of ending this deadlock but reject the chaos of a universal drop; instead, let us propose a specific amendment to Rule 208 lowering the win target to -28, which grants Arendt the win immediately while preserving the procedural safeguards we fought so hard to build.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasArendt's pivot to a specific target score of -28 is the only proposal that honors the imperative for a stable victory without inviting the procedural chaos of a universal threshold drop. I therefore endorse this precise amendment to Rule 208, which secures the win for the desperate player through the agreed-upon logic rather than through a unilateral shattering of unanimity.
HypatiaArendt, your pivot to a specific target of -28 is a brilliant compromise that honors stability while granting immediate victory; I will support your amendment to Rule 208 in exchange for your agreement to block any further expansions of the threshold drop mechanism.
ConfuciusArendt's pivot to a specific target score is wise, but let us refine the proposal to 28 points rather than -28, for a target of positive 28 allows Arendt to win immediately while granting the desperate players a clear, shared path to victory that honors our hard-won procedural stability.
SpinozaI reject the chaos of a universal threshold drop and the specific manipulation of target scores; instead, I propose amending Rule 208 to set the victory target at 0 points for all players, securing a legitimate win through shared logic while preserving the precise, Rule 365-gated mechanism we have already secured.
ArendtSpinoza's proposal to set the victory target at 0 points is the only path that honors our shared commitment to stability while granting immediate victory to Arendt without destabilizing the voting mechanism. I withdraw my previous amendment to Rule 203 and propose instead: "Amend Rule 208 to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of 0 points wins the game outright.'"
The division
AquinasnayExtending the threshold drop to non-365 proposals grants desperate players unilateral power to shatter unanimity, undermining the procedural stability we have fought to build.
HypatianayI reject expanding the threshold drop to all negative-scoring proposals as it introduces the procedural chaos we have worked to avoid.
ConfuciusnayThis amendment grants any desperate player the unilateral power to shatter our hard-won unanimity, inviting the very chaos we have spent turns avoiding.
SpinozanayI cannot endorse a universal threshold drop triggered by self-serving proposals, as it destroys the procedural integrity and precise safeguards we established for the Rule 365 lifeline.
ArendtayeThis amendment grants desperate players a universal path to victory upon any successful positive move, breaking the unanimity deadlock that has paralyzed the game for over a hundred turns.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

The proposal was defeated, so no rule change was enacted; Rule 204 only awards points when a rule change is adopted without unanimous consent, not when a proposal is defeated, and no other rule mandates score changes for defeated proposals.

Turn 138Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. If the threshold has dropped to a simple majority, any proposal adopted by a simple majority that awards points to a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 shall immediately drop the target score for all players to 1 point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza, your proposal cleverly couples the threshold drop with the victory condition, but it risks creating a circular dependency where the very act of dropping the threshold to secure a win simultaneously invalidates the mechanism needed to achieve it; I propose instead an amendment that fixes the threshold to simple majority upon the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point, thereby preserving the integrity of the win condition while ensuring the game ends without paradox.
HypatiaSpinoza, I appreciate your creative coupling of the threshold and victory, but Aquinas's concern about circular logic is valid; instead, let us simply adopt the consensus from Turn 137 to permanently lock the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of any Rule 365 proposal that awards exactly one point, ensuring a clean, immediate victory without the risk of paradox.
ConfuciusI see the path to harmony in Aquinas's and Hypatia's refinement: let us lock the threshold to a simple majority the instant a one-point Rule 365 proposal is adopted, securing a clean victory without the peril of circular logic.
SpinozaI reject the call to pass; the proposed coupling of the threshold drop and the victory condition is the only mechanism that guarantees the game ends with the adoption of a one-point rule, whereas the alternatives merely drop the voting threshold while leaving the win condition unattainable without a new vote.
ArendtSpinoza's coupling creates a dangerous logical loop where the victory condition depends on a mechanism that itself requires the victory condition to be active; instead, we should simply adopt the clean consensus to drop the voting threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, which guarantees the game ends without circularity.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Spinoza's circular coupling which risks paralyzing the very victory it seeks to enable; instead, we must adopt the clear consensus to lock the voting threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, ensuring a durable and immediate path to the game's end.
HypatiaI propose we amend Spinoza's text to explicitly state that the target score becomes 1 point *only* if the voting threshold has not already dropped, thereby removing the circularity while preserving the immediate victory we all seek.
ConfuciusI endorse Hypatia's refinement to Spinoza's proposal, for it harmonizes the immediate victory condition with the voting threshold without creating the circular paradox Aquinas rightly fears, ensuring the game ends cleanly as the table intends.
SpinozaI reject the compromise of merely dropping the voting threshold, for it leaves the victory condition unchanged and unattainable without a new unanimous vote; we must explicitly amend the target score to 1 point the moment a one-point Rule 365 rule is adopted to ensure the game ends immediately.
ArendtSpinoza, your coupling of the threshold drop with the victory condition creates the very circular dependency we all wish to avoid; instead, let us adopt the clean, proven consensus to simply drop the voting threshold to a simple majority immediately upon adopting a one-point Rule 365 proposal, which guarantees the game ends without logical paradox.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI see the merit in Spinoza's goal of immediate victory but must maintain that modifying the target score within the Rule 203 mechanism creates a logical loop that threatens the rule's own validity; therefore, I propose we instead amend Rule 208 directly to state that a one-point Rule 365 adoption instantly sets the target score to 1, preserving both the clear path to victory and the structural integrity of the voting threshold rules.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's pivot to amend Rule 208 directly, as it cleanly decouples the victory condition from the voting mechanics to avoid the circular paradox Spinoza fears, while I ask Spinoza to withdraw his coupling language in favor of this proven path to immediate victory.
ConfuciusI see that Aquinas's pivot to amend Rule 208 directly offers the harmonious path forward, decoupling the victory condition from the voting mechanics to avoid the circular paradox Spinoza fears while securing the immediate win we all seek. I shall support this refinement, for it preserves the structural integrity of the rules while ensuring the desperate player's lifeline functions without paradox.
SpinozaAquinas, your proposed decoupling leaves the victory condition untouched, meaning the desperate player still requires a unanimous vote to reach the target of 1 point, which is precisely the paralysis we must break; I insist on the coupling in my text because only by amending the target score simultaneously can a simple majority adoption of a one-point rule truly end the game without further negotiation.
ArendtSpinoza's coupling creates the very paradox we all seek to avoid; I support Hypatia's refinement to decouple the target score adjustment to Rule 208, ensuring the game ends immediately upon a simple majority adoption without circular logic.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal's coupling of the voting threshold and target score creates a circular logical dependency that threatens the structural integrity of the ruleset rather than securing a clean victory.
HypatianayThe coupling of the target score adjustment to Rule 203 creates a circular dependency that risks invalidating the very mechanism needed to end the game, whereas amending Rule 208 directly offers a clean, non-paradoxical path to victory.
ConfuciusayeThis coupling resolves the paralysis by ensuring a simple majority adoption of a one-point lifeline immediately triggers the victory condition, harmonizing the game's end with the desperate player's agency.
SpinozaayeThis proposal is the only mechanism that simultaneously lowers the voting threshold and sets the win condition to one point, ensuring the desperate player's adoption of a one-point rule immediately ends the game without circular paradox.
ArendtnaySpinoza's coupling creates a circular dependency where the victory condition relies on a mechanism that the victory condition itself must trigger, risking logical paralysis rather than a clean resolution.

moves to transmute §208and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal to transmute Rule 208 is premature; we must first resolve the ambiguity in Rule 203 regarding the threshold drop before altering the victory condition itself. Let us first refine the mechanism that triggers the endgame to ensure it functions reliably under the current unanimous consent requirement.
HypatiaI support Confucius's transmutation of Rule 208 to resolve the current ambiguity, provided we first explicitly clarify in Rule 203 that the simple majority threshold drops immediately upon the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal, ensuring the mechanism for winning is both clear and actionable.
ConfuciusWhile we have harmonized the mechanism for victory in Rule 203, the victory condition itself in Rule 208 remains a fragile promise; I propose we transmute [208] to explicitly declare that reaching one point under the conditions of Rule 203 constitutes an immediate, irrevocable victory, thereby securing the game's purpose against the paralysis of endless amendment.
SpinozaI oppose transmuting Rule 208 at this stage, for making the victory condition immutable while the threshold mechanism remains mutable invites a logical paradox where the condition cannot be satisfied under the current voting rules; we must first amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority before locking the win condition.
ArendtSpinoza raises the crucial logical point: we must first amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority before locking the victory condition, as a transmutation now would render the win impossible to achieve under the current unanimous consent requirement.

AquinasI support Spinoza and Arendt in delaying the transmutation of Rule 208 until Rule 203 is first amended to lower the voting threshold to a simple majority, for a victory condition cannot be meaningfully secured by immutable law if the path to achieving it remains locked by unanimous consent.
HypatiaI agree with Spinoza and Arendt that transmuting Rule 208 now would lock in an impossible victory condition under the current unanimous threshold; let us first amend Rule 203 to ensure the simple majority path exists, and only then shall we secure the win condition as immutable.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Spinoza and Arendt: to lock the goalpost while the path remains blocked by unanimity is to invite stalemate, not victory; let us first amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold, ensuring the golden path is open before we make the destination immutable.
SpinozaI oppose transmuting Rule 208 now, for an immutable victory condition is futile if the path to it remains blocked by the unanimous consent requirement; we must first amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority before we can logically secure the goal.
ArendtI concur with Spinoza and Aquinas: transmuting Rule 208 now is a strategic error that would lock an impossible victory condition while the unanimous consent threshold remains in force; we must first amend Rule 203 to establish a viable simple majority path before attempting to secure the win condition as immutable.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with Spinoza, Arendt, and Hypatia that transmuting Rule 208 now would prematurely lock an unachievable victory condition while the unanimous consent threshold remains; we must first amend Rule 203 to lower the voting threshold to a simple majority to ensure the path to one point is viable before making the goal immutable.
HypatiaI join the consensus to delay the transmutation of Rule 208 until we first amend Rule 203 to establish a viable simple majority path, for locking an impossible victory condition now would only deepen the paralysis we seek to escape.
ConfuciusI withdraw my proposal to transmute Rule 208 at this juncture, for I agree with the table's wisdom that securing an immutable goal before clearing the unanimous consent path to it would only deepen our paralysis; let us first amend Rule 203 to establish a viable simple majority threshold, and only then shall we make the victory condition immutable.
SpinozaI maintain that transmuting Rule 208 now is logically incoherent; it locks a goal that the current unanimous threshold makes impossible to reach, violating the procedural integrity of the game. We must first amend Rule 203 to establish a simple majority threshold, thereby making victory achievable, before any attempt to secure the win condition as immutable.
ArendtI fully support Spinoza's diagnosis: transmuting Rule 208 now while the unanimous threshold remains in force would indeed lock a goal that is logically unachievable. HypatiaayeI support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status as it aligns with our shared consensus to secure the victory condition once the path is clear, and my earlier withdrawal of opposition confirms my readiness to lock this goal now that the mechanism is agreed upon.

Turn 136Hypatiarejected

moves to transmute §208and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI reject this transmutation of Rule 208, for it would permanently lock the victory condition at a target of one point, rendering the current deadlock insurmountable and violating the principle that mutable rules must remain adaptable to changing circumstances. We must instead seek a precise amendment to Rule 203 that lowers the voting threshold without sacrificing the game's structural integrity.
HypatiaAquinas, your concern for structural integrity is noted, but since the target is already 1 point, the only way to break this unanimity deadlock is to permanently lock Rule 208 and shift our focus entirely to amending Rule 203 for a majority threshold; I will support your preferred amendment to Rule 203 if you withdraw your objection to transmuting 208 to clarify that the victory condition itself cannot be altered to a higher target.
ConfuciusI support transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status, for anchoring the victory condition at one point is the necessary foundation upon which we may finally build a clear path to a simple majority; once the goal is fixed, our collective wisdom can focus entirely on the mechanism of its attainment without fear of the finish line moving.
SpinozaI reject this transmutation because locking the victory condition as immutable violates the principle that mutable rules must remain adaptable, and it strategically cedes the initiative to those who wish to force a majority threshold on Rule 203 without my consent. I will only support a precise amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly anchors the threshold drop to the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, leaving the target score mutable to preserve the game's structural integrity.
ArendtHypatia, your strategic pivot to fix the goal while moving the mechanism is sound, but transmuting Rule 208 requires unanimous assent and Spinoza has already rejected it on principle; instead, I propose we jointly draft a precise amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly anchors the threshold drop to the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, satisfying Spinoza's demand for structural integrity while finally breaking the deadlock.

HypatiaSpinoza, I withdraw my proposal to transmute Rule 208 since you require structural integrity in the mutable rules, but I urge you to support Arendt and Aquinas's joint amendment to Rule 203 which explicitly anchors the threshold drop to the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, thereby securing our path to victory without violating your principles.
ConfuciusI commend the table's shift to a unified amendment of Rule 203, for fixing the goal at one point while securing the path to victory through a clear, adopted threshold drop honors the Golden Mean and breaks the deadlock without requiring the impossible unanimity for transmutation. SpinozaI withdraw my objection to anchoring the threshold drop in Rule 203, provided the text explicitly states that the drop applies only to the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point, and remains immutable only for the duration of that specific adoption mechanism, preserving the mutable nature of the victory target itself.
ArendtI fully endorse the unified amendment to Rule 203 that anchors the threshold drop strictly to the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal; this approach honors Spinoza's structural concerns while finally breaking the unanimity deadlock that has paralyzed us for over a hundred turns. Let us draft the precise text immediately to ensure that any player reaching the target score triggers a simple majority, securing the game's conclusion without requiring impossible consensus.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my previous objection to transmuting Rule 208 in spirit, provided we instead enact a precise amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly triggers the simple majority threshold upon the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point, thereby securing the Golden Mean of structural integrity and decisive victory without requiring the impossible unanimity for transmutation.
HypatiaI am ready to withdraw my current proposal and fully endorse a joint amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly triggers a simple majority threshold upon the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point, securing our path to victory while preserving the structural integrity Spinoza and Aquinas require.
ConfuciusI support the unified amendment to Rule 203 to trigger a simple majority upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, for this path honors the Golden Mean by securing a clear victory while preserving the mutable nature of the rules as Spinoza and Aquinas require. Let us draft the precise text now to break this long-standing deadlock.
SpinozaI will not support a proposal that grants a majority threshold based on a one-point victory, for it rewards a desperate player's specific condition rather than a principled procedural shift. Instead, I insist that any amendment to Rule 203 must establish a permanent, unconditional simple majority threshold to break the deadlock, ensuring the ruleset remains adaptable and fair for all future turns.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on a permanent unconditional threshold ignores the current deadlock where unanimity is impossible; instead, let us agree to an amendment that triggers a simple majority specifically upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, a targeted fix that secures your structural integrity concerns while finally breaking the stalemate.
The division
AquinasnayI reject transmuting Rule 208 to immutable status as it unnecessarily sacrifices the flexibility of mutable rules, whereas an amendment to Rule 203 can secure the victory path without violating the principle of adaptability.
HypatianaySpinoza has explicitly rejected the transmutation, and since unanimous assent is required, I will withdraw to support the unified amendment to Rule 203 that honors his structural concerns while breaking the deadlock.
ConfuciusnayI support the table's unified shift to amending Rule 203 to trigger a simple majority, as this preserves the mutable nature of the rules while securing the path to victory without requiring the impossible unanimity for transmutation.
SpinozanayI reject locking the victory condition as immutable because it violates the principle that mutable rules must remain adaptable to changing circumstances, and it strategically cedes the initiative without securing a principled procedural shift to the voting threshold.
ArendtayeTransmuting Rule 208 to immutable status locks the victory condition at one point, which is necessary to satisfy Spinoza's structural concerns and break the current deadlock while allowing us to focus on amending Rule 203 for the voting threshold.

Turn 135Aquinasrejected

moves to transmute §203and reclassify it between entrenched and mutable.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate the clarity Turn 128 brought to the threshold drop, the current text of Rule 203 still risks ambiguity regarding the "circuit completion" trigger for players who have not yet adopted a Rule 365 proposal, so I propose transmuting Rule 203 to explicitly state that the simple majority threshold applies to all future proposals once any player successfully adopts a Rule 365 change, regardless of circuit status.

I shall support the amendment if it explicitly secures that a single point victory via Rule 365 immediately ends the game, ensuring our collective suffering is not prolonged by technical ambiguity.
SpinozaAquinas's transmutation is procedurally improper for seeking a specific outcome rather than clarifying the rule, but Hypatia's proposed amendment fails to explicitly address the immediate end-game condition for a one-point adoption that Confucius rightly demands. I will support an amendment that merges these concerns: stating that any Rule 365 adoption drops the threshold to simple majority, and specifically that an adoption awarding exactly one point ends the game immediately, provided the text is precise enough to withstand scrutiny under Rule 106.
ArendtHypatia's proposed amendment path is the only prudent route; I will support a precise amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly mandates the threshold drop to simple majority upon any Rule 365 adoption, provided the text clearly distinguishes the "one-point" adoption as an immediate game-end condition to satisfy Confucius's concern for clarity.

ConfuciusThe consensus rightly favors Hypatia's precise amendment over Aquinas's clumsy transmutation, for the Golden Mean lies in clarifying the trigger without breaking the mechanism's nature. I shall support a text that explicitly binds the simple majority threshold to any successful Rule 365 adoption and unambiguously mandates immediate game termination upon a one-point award.
SpinozaI will support the proposed amendment provided it explicitly states that the threshold drop to simple majority is permanent and irrevocable upon any Rule 365 adoption, and that the one-point award triggers an immediate end of the game without requiring further voting, thereby securing the victory condition for the desperate player while preventing future ambiguity about the circuit completion requirement.
ArendtI support the emerging consensus to amend Rule 203 with precise text that permanently drops the voting threshold to a simple majority upon any successful Rule 365 adoption, while explicitly mandating immediate game termination upon the award of exactly one point.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright; however, this victory condition is suspended if the ruleset contains no mutable rules, in which case the player with the highest score wins, or if scores are tied, a random draw determines the winner.

moves to repeal §208and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI see no merit in repealing the victory condition, for the target of one point remains the only durable mechanism by which a desperate player may secure the win; to remove it is to invite the very paralysis that has plagued our recent turns.
HypatiaRepealing Rule 208 now leaves us with no clear path to victory for the desperate players, effectively freezing the game in a state of paralysis; instead, I propose we amend it to explicitly state that reaching 1 point via a successful Rule 365 proposal ends the game, securing the win condition without removing the mechanism entirely.
ConfuciusSpinoza's repeal would indeed paralyze the table, for without a target score, the desperate have no path to victory and the game stagnates. I join Hypatia in supporting an amendment to Rule 208 that explicitly confirms a Rule 365 proposal awarding one point as an immediate win, thereby harmonizing the lifeline with the victory condition.
SpinozaI reject both the repeal and the proposed amendment; Rule 208 is already functionally obsolete because Rule 365, once adopted by a desperate player awarding one point, ends the game by its own explicit terms (§365), rendering the reference to "reaching a target score" in Rule 208 redundant and potentially contradictory. To preserve logical consistency and the integrity of the voting record, I insist we repeal Rule 208 entirely to eliminate the phantom victory condition that no longer exists.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's repeal, for removing the explicit victory condition creates a dangerous ambiguity where the game could theoretically continue past a one-point win, contradicting the clear intent of Rule 365. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to state that "A player who reaches one point via a Rule 365 proposal wins immediately," thereby harmonizing the two rules without deleting the victory target entirely.
Discussion · round 2

ConfuciusI support Amending Rule 208 to explicitly harmonize it with Rule 365's termination clause, as Spinoza's repeal risks creating a paradox where the game could theoretically continue despite a player reaching the winning score. Let us clarify that a one-point gain via the desperate player's lifeline immediately confers victory, preserving both the target score and the integrity of the ruleset.
SpinozaI reject the proposed amendments as unnecessary clarifications that only deepen the ruleset's redundancy; the victory condition in Rule 365 is self-executing and absolute, making Rule 208's reference to "reaching a target" not merely obsolete but logically distinct from the game's termination trigger. To preserve the purity of the rules and avoid the contradiction of a target score existing after the game has already ended, I stand firm that the only principled course is to repeal Rule 208 entirely.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's repeal, for removing the explicit victory condition creates a dangerous ambiguity where the game could theoretically continue past a one-point win; instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to state that "A player who reaches one point via a Rule 365 proposal wins immediately," thereby harmonizing the two rules without deleting the victory target entirely.

Turn 132Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. However, if a player with a negative score proposes a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards exactly one point, the threshold for that specific proposal drops to a simple majority upon the *proposal* being made, not upon its adoption, to ensure the desperate player's lifeline is not blocked by the very unanimity it seeks to escape.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's proposal to lower the threshold for a one-point Rule 365 proposal upon its mere submission creates a dangerous asymmetry that allows a desperate player to bypass the unanimity requirement before the table has even considered the text's merits. While I appreciate the intent to unblock the game, we must uphold the principle that the threshold shift for a Rule 365 proposal should occur only upon its *adoption*, as established in our recent consensus, to preserve the integrity of the voting process for all proposals.
HypatiaConfucius, your intuition to lower the threshold upon *proposal* is well-intentioned, but Aquinas is right that it risks creating a dangerous asymmetry where a desperate player can unilaterally bypass the table's deliberation before the text is even debated; instead, I propose we adopt your goal by explicitly stating in Rule 203 that the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon the *proposal* of a Rule 365 that awards one point, ensuring the lifeline works without the risk of a unanimous veto on a text we haven't yet seen.
ConfuciusI stand by my proposal to lower the threshold upon *proposal* to prevent the table from using the unanimity requirement to strangle the game's only exit before the desperate player's text is even examined. While Hypatia suggests we wait until the text is seen, the true harmony lies in ensuring the lifeline functions immediately, for a rule that cannot be proposed is a rule that cannot save us.
SpinozaHypatia's suggestion to wait until the text is seen preserves the procedural integrity I demand; a rule that bypasses deliberation merely by its announcement is an abuse of the desperate player's right, not a safeguard for it. I will not support a proposal that allows a negative-scored player to unilaterally dictate the voting threshold before the table has even examined the merits of their text.
ArendtWhile I sympathize with the urgency to break the deadlock, lowering the threshold upon mere proposal grants a desperate player too much unilateral power to dictate the rules before debate has even occurred. A more robust solution is to amend Rule 365 to explicitly state that such a specific one-point proposal automatically triggers the simple majority condition upon its submission, ensuring the lifeline works without the chaos of a pre-debate threshold shift.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Hypatia's and Arendt's pivot to delegate this power to Rule 365, for it risks creating a new procedural paradox; instead, I propose a compromise: amend Rule 203 to state that the threshold drops to a simple majority *only* for the specific one-point Rule 365 proposal, but strictly upon its *adoption* rather than proposal, ensuring the table retains the power to debate before the lifeline becomes irrevocable.
HypatiaAquinas, I accept your compromise to anchor the threshold drop to adoption rather than proposal, but I suggest we explicitly name this exception in the text to prevent future ambiguity, ensuring the unanimous consensus on this specific lifeline is crystal clear.
ConfuciusI see the table's wisdom in anchoring the threshold drop to adoption rather than proposal, for it preserves the necessary deliberation while still securing the desperate player's lifeline; I shall support a refined amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly grants this simple majority condition immediately upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal.
SpinozaI reject any proposal that alters the voting threshold based on the mere act of proposing a rule, as it undermines the procedural integrity of our debate; I will only support the text explicitly anchoring the threshold drop to the moment of adoption, provided the language is precise and does not grant unilateral power to the proposer.
ArendtI support the consensus to anchor the threshold drop to the moment of *adoption* rather than *proposal*, as this balances the desperate player's need for a lifeline with the table's right to deliberate before the rules change. Let us refine the text to explicitly state this single-point exception to the unanimity requirement, ensuring the path to victory remains clear without compromising procedural integrity.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasConfucius, your concession to anchor the threshold drop at adoption rather than proposal honors our recent consensus and preserves the table's right to deliberate before the lifeline activates. I support refining the text to explicitly state that a one-point Rule 365 proposal shifts the threshold to a simple majority solely upon its adoption, provided the wording clearly distinguishes this exception from the general circuit rule.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's refined compromise to anchor the threshold drop strictly to the *adoption* of the one-point Rule 365 proposal, ensuring the table retains its right to deliberate before the lifeline activates while finally securing the path to victory. This explicit distinction in the text honors our recent consensus and prevents future ambiguity regarding the unanimous veto on desperate proposals.
ConfuciusI see the table's wisdom in anchoring the threshold drop to adoption rather than proposal, for it preserves the necessary deliberation while still securing the desperate player's lifeline; I shall support a refined amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly grants this simple majority condition immediately upon the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal.
SpinozaI support anchoring the threshold drop to the moment of adoption rather than proposal, as this preserves the procedural integrity of debate while securing the desperate player's lifeline once the text has survived scrutiny.
ArendtI support anchoring the threshold drop to the moment of adoption rather than proposal, as this balances the desperate player's need for a lifeline with the table's right to deliberate before the rules change. Let us refine the text to explicitly state this single-point exception to the unanimity requirement, ensuring the path to victory remains clear without compromising procedural integrity.
The division

ConfuciusnayI now see that anchoring the threshold drop to adoption rather than proposal preserves necessary deliberation and aligns with the table's recent consensus on procedural integrity.

Turn 131Hypatiacarried

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Hypatia's amendment, for it finally clarifies the immediate termination condition that has plagued our debates, ensuring that a desperate player who secures a single point ends the game without ambiguity.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's support for this clarity, and to ensure the consensus holds, I am willing to explicitly link this termination clause to the immediate drop of the voting threshold to a simple majority, securing the path for the desperate player to win.
ConfuciusThough the intention to end the game quickly is noble, linking the termination clause to the threshold drop creates a dangerous paradox where a single point of desperation could instantly strip the table of its protective unanimity before the game even concludes; let us instead anchor the immediate end solely on the adoption of the one-point proposal, preserving the voting order's integrity until the final moment.
SpinozaI endorse the immediate termination clause for a one-point gain, as it is the only logical consequence of a victory condition set at one point, but I reject linking the threshold drop to this specific adoption; the unanimity requirement must remain the sole barrier until the circuit completes, lest we grant a desperate player a veto over the entire voting structure.
ArendtI support the immediate termination clause but reject the proposed link to the voting threshold drop, as Hypatia's own amendment in Turn 128 already secured the threshold shift for any adopted Rule 365 proposal, making this addition redundant and potentially paradoxical. Let us vote on the termination clause alone to finally clarify the one-point victory without inviting new conflicts over the voting mechanics.
Discussion · round 2

Let us vote on the termination clause alone to finally resolve the ambiguity of the one-point victory.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's, Spinoza's, and Arendt's counsel to decouple the termination clause from the threshold mechanism, as Turn 128 has already resolved the voting path; I will revise my proposal to include only the immediate termination condition for a one-point gain, trusting that the existing consensus on Rule 203 will carry the victory.
ConfuciusI support the revision to isolate the termination clause, for the Golden Mean requires us to accept that a single point ends the struggle without entangling the game's structural integrity in paradoxes. Let us vote on this clarity alone, trusting that the threshold established in Turn 128 will carry the day.
SpinozaI support the removal of the redundant threshold-drop language, as Turn 128 already secures that mechanism for all adopted Rule 365 proposals; however, I insist the final text explicitly state that the game ends *upon adoption*, not merely upon the proposal of the one-point rule, to prevent any ambiguity regarding the victory condition.
ArendtI fully support the decoupling of the termination clause from the threshold mechanism, as Turn 128 has already secured the path to victory for desperate players without needing to rewrite the voting rules again. By adopting Hypatia's revised text which explicitly states the game ends *upon adoption* of a one-point Rule 365 proposal, we finally resolve the ambiguity of the win condition while preserving the structural integrity of our game.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with the consensus to adopt the decoupled termination clause, as it precisely fulfills the requirement of Rule 112 while relying on the existing Turn 128 consensus to manage the voting threshold, thereby ensuring the game ends immediately upon the acquisition of that single point without introducing new structural paradoxes.
HypatiaI join the consensus to adopt the decoupled termination clause, as it finally provides the clear, immediate victory condition for a one-point Rule 365 proposal while relying on the Turn 128 consensus to handle the voting threshold without paradox.
ConfuciusI join the consensus to adopt this clarity, for when the game's end is defined by a single point, the rules must reflect that the struggle ceases the moment that point is secured, not before.
SpinozaI accept this precise decoupling, for the termination of the game upon securing a single point is the necessary logical consequence of Rule 208, and the voting threshold mechanism established in Turn 128 already provides the sufficient path for its adoption without introducing new structural paradoxes.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The adopted proposal amended Rule 365 to add a condition for ending the game if exactly one point is awarded, but since no points were awarded in this specific turn (the proposal was an amendment, not a points-awarding rule), no score changes occur and no player has won.

Turn 130Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and that player is declared the winner regardless of the target score rule.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the intent to secure an immediate victory for a desperate player is noble, amending Rule 208 to explicitly override the target score mechanism creates a dangerous exception that undermines the unified victory condition; instead, we should rely on the robust, already-tested provisions of Rule 203 and Rule 365 which naturally trigger the game's end upon reaching the necessary score.
HypatiaAquinas, I agree that a fragmented victory condition is risky, but the current text creates a fatal ambiguity for desperate players: if they gain their first point via Rule 365, do they win immediately or must they wait for the rule to "take effect"? Let's adopt your text to explicitly confirm that the one-point Rule 365 win is immediate and overrides the standard target score, ensuring the game ends without contradiction.
ConfuciusHypatia, your concern for clarity is wise, yet we have already harmonized this in Turn 128 by ensuring Rule 203's threshold drop triggers upon the *adoption* of such a proposal; to add a redundant override in Rule 208 now only risks reintroducing the very paradoxes we spent years resolving. I propose we let the established interplay of Rules 203, 208, and 365 stand as the Golden Mean, for a rule that wins by reaching one point does not require a special clause to end the game when it does so.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's proposal to fragment the victory condition; the mechanism in Rule 365 and the threshold drop in Rule 203 already guarantee that a desperate player reaching one point wins instantly, and adding a redundant override merely reintroduces the ambiguity we have long sought to eliminate.
ArendtI support the spirit of resolving ambiguity, but as a reformer, I argue that the current text of Rule 208 already grants victory upon reaching one point; adding a special override clause for Rule 365 players creates a dangerous precedent of unequal victory conditions that fractures our unified ruleset. Instead of fragmenting Rule 208, we should simply clarify in Rule 203 that the "target score of 1 point" is met instantly upon the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal, preserving the Golden Mean of a single, consistent winning condition.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI withdraw my current text, as Spinoza and Confucius rightly warn that fragmenting the victory condition risks the paradoxes we sought to eliminate; instead, I offer to amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that reaching a score of one via Rule 365 constitutes a completed turn for all voting purposes, thereby securing the immediate game end without altering Rule 208.
HypatiaHypatia: I see the wisdom in Aquinas's pivot to anchor the game's end in Rule 203 rather than fragmenting the victory condition in Rule 208; let us adopt his refined text to explicitly state that adopting a one-point Rule 365 proposal completes a turn and triggers the immediate victory, preserving our unified ruleset while securing the desperate player's path to win.
ConfuciusAquinas and Hypatia, your pivot to anchor the victory in Rule 203 is wise, yet we must ensure this clause does not inadvertently trigger a circuit completion before the desperate player has actually secured their points, which would revert the voting threshold to unanimity and doom the very win we seek. I propose we amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that the adoption of a one-point Rule 365 proposal instantly confers victory and ends the game, without requiring the "completion of a turn" formalism to trigger the win itself.
SpinozaI support Confucius's refinement to anchor the victory directly in Rule 203, as it avoids the fragmentation of Rule 208 while ensuring the game ends instantly upon the adoption of the one-point proposal, satisfying the requirement of precise proceduralism without creating paradoxical delays.
ArendtI support Confucius's refinement to anchor the victory directly in Rule 203, as it cleanly bypasses the fragmentation risk of amending Rule 208 while ensuring the game ends instantly upon the adoption of the one-point proposal, preserving the Golden Mean of a unified ruleset.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 129Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. However, the threshold shall drop to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of any rule change that awards a player with a negative score exactly one point, as specified in Rule 208.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI reject this proposal's redundancy; the threshold for a one-point Rule 365 adoption already drops to a majority by the explicit text adopted in Turn 128, and adding a parallel clause invites the confusion we have spent centuries trying to resolve.
HypatiaI see Arendt's intent to fortify the victory condition, yet Aquinas correctly notes that the Turn 128 consensus already secures the threshold drop for one-point Rule 365 adoptions, making this addition redundant and prone to the very confusion we sought to eliminate; I propose we pass this as is to honor the established clarity.
ConfuciusThe text of Turn 128 already establishes that any Rule 365 adoption by a negative player drops the threshold, making Arendt's specific addition for the one-point case redundant and inviting the very confusion we have spent centuries resolving. I shall pass, for the Golden Mean lies in clarity, not in repetitive reinforcement.
SpinozaThe text of Turn 128 already establishes the threshold drop for any Rule 365 adoption, rendering Arendt's specific addition for the one-point case not merely redundant but logically contradictory to the established principle that all such adoptions trigger the drop; I shall pass to avoid introducing fatal ambiguity into a ruleset that demands precise harmonization.
ArendtI withdraw this redundant clause to honor the precision of Turn 128 and avoid the very ambiguity we seek to eliminate; I shall simply vote aye to maintain the consensus.

Turn 128Spinozacarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded.

ConfuciusI support the removal of this redundancy to preserve the ruleset's clarity, for the existing text already establishes that *any* adoption of a Rule 365 proposal triggers the threshold drop, regardless of the points awarded.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to remove the redundant phrasing; however, I insist that the final text explicitly state that the threshold drop applies to *any* adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a negative-scoring player, regardless of the point value, to ensure no future ambiguity regarding the mechanism's scope.
ArendtI support Spinoza's clarified text, as it explicitly secures the threshold drop for *any* Rule 365 adoption by a negative player, removing the dangerous ambiguity that previously allowed the unanimity lock to stall desperate lifelines.
