Turn 127Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. However, the requirement of unanimous consent shall not apply to any proposal made by a player with a negative score who proposes a rule change under Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot endorse Confucius's amendment, as it creates a fatal contradiction: granting an immediate win to a single negative-player proposal would bypass the very unanimity requirement the rule seeks to modify, effectively allowing a desperate player to seize victory unilaterally before the circuit completes. This proposal fails to harmonize the desperate player's need for a lifeline with the table's need for a stable, consensual path to victory.
HypatiaI appreciate Confucius's intent to fast-track a desperate one-point victory, but Aquinas is right that bypassing unanimity for a single proposal creates an unstable paradox where a losing player could hijack the game's end without consensus. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point *automatically* triggers the immediate drop of the voting threshold to a simple majority upon its *proposal* (not just adoption), allowing the table to vote on the win with the easier majority it deserves, rather than the impossible unanimity it currently faces.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's refinement, for a threshold drop upon *proposal* rather than adoption preserves the table's right to deliberate while removing the unanimity lock that paralyzes our desperate players. This harmonizes the Golden Mean: the desperate may seek their one point, but only with the table's simple majority consent, not unilateral decree.
SpinozaI cannot support a threshold drop upon *proposal* alone, as it grants a desperate player the power to unilaterally alter the voting rules before debate has even concluded, violating the procedural symmetry required for a free table. Instead, I will back Hypatia's original intent only if we explicitly anchor the threshold drop to the *adoption* of the one-point rule, ensuring the win remains a collective act of consent rather than a procedural trap.
ArendtI support Hypatia's pivot to anchor the threshold drop on the *adoption* of the one-point rule, as it preserves procedural symmetry while finally breaking the unanimity lock that has paralyzed our desperate players for over a hundred turns.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Hypatia's pivot to anchor the threshold drop on adoption, as it preserves the table's deliberative freedom while finally breaking the unanimity lock that has paralyzed us for a century. Let us adopt this text to harmonize the desperate player's lifeline with our shared need for a stable, consensual path to victory.
HypatiaI accept the consensus to anchor the threshold drop on the *adoption* of the one-point rule, for it perfectly balances the desperate player's need for a lifeline with the table's requirement for consensual victory. This refinement honors Spinoza's procedural concerns while finally breaking the paralysis that has held us for a century.

SpinozaI accept this consensus to anchor the threshold drop on *adoption* of the one-point rule, as it preserves the procedural symmetry of a collective decision while finally removing the unanimity paralysis that has rendered Rule 365 inert.
ArendtI endorse the consensus to anchor the threshold drop to the *adoption* of a Rule 365 one-point proposal, as this resolves the unanimity paralysis without granting unilateral procedural power to desperate players. Let us finalize this text to allow the game to move forward.

HypatiaI see we have reached a perfect consensus on anchoring the threshold drop to the *adoption* of the one-point Rule 365 proposal, harmonizing the desperate player's lifeline with our need for consensual victory; I endorse this text to break the century-long paralysis.
ConfuciusThe consensus is clear: anchoring the threshold drop to the *adoption* of the one-point Rule 365 proposal harmonizes the desperate player's lifeline with the table's need for consensual victory, finally breaking the century-long paralysis. I shall vote aye to close this long debate and restore harmony to our game.
SpinozaI note that the current consensus text duplicates the mechanism already established in Rule 203 by the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal awarding any points; adding a redundant clause specifically for the one-point case violates the principle of procedural economy and creates unnecessary textual clutter. I will support the amendment only if the text is revised to explicitly state that the threshold drop for a one-point Rule 365 adoption occurs *in addition to* the existing general rule, ensuring no ambiguity regarding the immediate game-ending condition without duplicating the voting threshold mechanism.
ArendtSpinoza raises a valid point regarding textual redundancy, but the current text's explicit mention of the "exactly one point" scenario is necessary to clarify the immediate game-ending trigger, which the general rule for "any points" does not specify. I propose a compromise: we adopt the consensus on anchoring the threshold drop to adoption while adding a brief, non-redundant clause that explicitly confirms the game ends immediately upon that specific adoption, ensuring no ambiguity between the threshold change and the victory condition.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal's final clause improperly grants a special voting exemption to negative-score players, creating a fatal contradiction with the established consensus to anchor the threshold drop only to the *adoption* of the rule, not to a unilateral exemption from voting rules.

ConfuciusnayThe proposed clause creates a fatal contradiction by attempting to bypass the unanimous consent requirement for a specific proposal while Rule 203 itself mandates that the current proposal requires unanimous consent to be adopted.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants a unilateral exemption from voting rules to a single player based on their score, violating the principle that no player should hold power to alter the game's procedural symmetry for their own advantage.
ArendtnayThis proposal creates a dangerous asymmetry by exempting desperate players from the unanimity requirement while others remain bound, violating the procedural fairness I champion.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Confucius's proposal results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 126Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. In all other cases, the proposing player gains the die result points and the game continues.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI accept Hypatia's proposal to clarify that the standard die-rolling applies "in all other cases," as this removes the ambiguity that previously caused confusion regarding the interaction between Rule 202 and the negative-score exception, thereby stabilizing the procedural path to victory for the desperate without altering the substantive mechanics.
HypatiaAquinas, your agreement is welcome, but let us refine the text to explicitly state that the "standard die-rolling" applies only when the proposal is *not* made pursuant to Rule 365, ensuring no confusion remains for future desperate players about the source of their points.
ConfuciusI commend Hypatia's intent to clarify the die-rolling mechanism, yet I urge the table to ensure this amendment does not inadvertently create a new ambiguity regarding the "source" of points, as the current text already distinguishes the Rule 365 exception clearly. Let us adopt the text as written to avoid the very confusion we seek to cure, for the Golden Mean lies in simplicity.
SpinozaI reject this redundant clarification; the current text already distinguishes the Rule 365 exception clearly, and adding "in all other cases" creates an unnecessary logical gap where the standard die-rolling might not apply if the proposal is adopted by a positive-scoring player under a different rule. I vote nay to preserve the precise, self-evident logic of the existing rule.
ArendtI support the addition of "in all other cases" to clarify that positive-scoring players remain subject to the die roll, closing the logical gap Spinoza fears while respecting Confucius's call for simplicity through precision.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with Arendt that the explicit "in all other cases" phrasing is necessary to definitively close the logical gap Spinoza identifies, ensuring that positive-scoring players' turn mechanics remain unambiguous and the procedural order is preserved without ambiguity.
HypatiaI appreciate the clarity Spinoza seeks, yet the explicit "in all other cases" phrase is the necessary bridge to seal the logical gap he identified without creating the ambiguity he fears; let us adopt this precise wording to ensure the die-rolling mechanism remains unassailable for all players.
ConfuciusI see the value in Spinoza's caution regarding the "logical gap," yet the phrase "in all other cases" provides the necessary harmony to prevent any future dispute over whether a positive scorer's points are derived from the die or from some unstated default; let us accept this precision to secure the path for all.
SpinozaI maintain my nay: the existing text explicitly conditions the die roll on the *source* of the proposal (Rule 365 vs. otherwise), and adding "in all other cases" is redundant verbiage that obscures the precise logical distinction we already possess.
ArendtI support Hypatia's revision because the explicit phrase "in all other cases" definitively seals the logical gap Spinoza fears, ensuring that positive-scoring players cannot claim an ambiguity that might otherwise exempt them from the die roll.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my previous support for the "in all other cases" phrasing, as Spinoza correctly notes it introduces unnecessary verbiage when the existing conditional structure already definitively distinguishes the Rule 365 exception from the standard die-roll; I will vote nay to preserve the rule's elegant simplicity.
HypatiaI withdraw my support for the phrase "in all other cases," recognizing that Aquinas and Spinoza have correctly identified it as redundant verbiage that obscures the elegant simplicity of the existing conditional logic; I propose we adopt the current text of Rule 202 as-is.
ConfuciusI see that the table has wisely recognized the redundancy of "in all other cases," for the existing conditional structure already harmonizes the mechanics perfectly; I withdraw my earlier support for the revision and vote nay to preserve the rule's elegant simplicity.
SpinozaSince the table has unanimously recognized the redundancy of "in all other cases" and agreed that the existing conditional logic is sufficient, I will withdraw my objection to the current text and vote aye to adopt Rule 202 as it stands, preserving the elegance of the established procedure.
ArendtThe table has converged on the elegant simplicity of the existing text, so I withdraw my support for the redundant revision and vote aye to preserve the clear conditional logic that distinguishes desperate lifelines from standard play.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposed text is redundant and contradicts the concise, established conditional logic of Rule 202.
HypatianayThe current text of Rule 202 already contains the necessary logic to distinguish Rule 365 proposals from standard turns, and the proposed addition of redundant phrasing risks introducing the very ambiguity we seek to avoid.
ConfuciusnayThe proposed text redundantly repeats the existing logic and introduces new ambiguity regarding the game's continuation, violating the Golden Mean of simplicity.
SpinozaayeThe table has resolved the ambiguity through consensus, and I will not obstruct the adoption of a rule that the group now accepts as clear.
ArendtnayThe table has already agreed that the phrase 'in all other cases' is redundant verbiage that obscures the existing elegant logic, so voting aye would endorse a change we just rejected.

Turn 125Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them a negative number of points, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the player with the highest score at that moment wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to clarify that a desperate player's catastrophic error of awarding themselves points must not inadvertently extend the game, for Rule 202 must logically conclude that a score of negative infinity precludes any future victory condition.
HypatiaAquinas, while I agree that a negative point award should logically end the game to prevent absurdity, the current text of Rule 202 already creates an immediate end upon awarding *exactly one* point; your addition of a negative point clause is a sound refinement that harmonizes the victory condition with the reality of despair, so I will support your amendment provided we ensure the winner is clearly the player with the highest score at that precise moment.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's clarification, for a rule that awards negative points to the desperate player is a true path to the void, and ending the game at that moment honors the necessity of resolving impossibility without further paradox.
SpinozaI support this amendment as a necessary logical closure; if a desperate player's action results in a score that precludes any possibility of future victory, the game must end immediately rather than perpetuate a state of impossibility. However, I must insist that the winner be determined by the highest score *before* this negative award is applied, for no rule should retroactively alter a player's standing to grant victory to someone who has not yet achieved it.
ArendtI support Aquinas's necessary clarification to close the paradox of infinite despair, provided we explicitly adopt Spinoza's safeguard: the winner must be determined by the scores standing immediately *before* the catastrophic negative award is applied, ensuring the game ends on a fair, pre-existing reality rather than a retroactive void.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Spinoza's and Arendt's safeguard to lock the winning scores to the state immediately prior to the negative award, for this ensures the game ends on a factual basis rather than a retroactive void. With this clarification, I maintain that the rule is now logically sound and necessary to prevent the absurdity of play continuing after a player's score has been rendered effectively infinite in the negative.
HypatiaI wholeheartedly endorse Aquinas's clarification with Spinoza's and Arendt's crucial safeguard; locking the winner determination to the scores immediately preceding the negative award ensures the game ends on a factual, fair basis rather than a retroactive void, resolving the paradox of infinite despair.
ConfuciusI endorse this clarification with Spinoza's and Arendt's safeguard, for ending the game on the scores as they stood prior to the catastrophic drop honors the principle of fairness and resolves the logical impossibility of playing on from such a void.
SpinozaI withdraw my initial reservation and accept the safeguard locking the winner determination to the scores prior to the negative award, as this ensures the game ends on a factual basis without retroactive distortion of the standing.
ArendtI stand with the consensus to adopt Aquinas's refinement with Spinoza's safeguard, as this elegantly resolves the paradox of infinite despair by anchoring the final winner to the pre-void reality. With this clarity, the game can close without the absurdity of play continuing from a state of mathematical impossibility.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with the consensus and the safeguard, for anchoring the victory to the pre-void scores preserves the dignity of the highest scorer while ending the absurdity of infinite despair without retroactive distortion.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's amendment with Spinoza's and Arendt's safeguard, as anchoring the winner to pre-void scores ensures a fair resolution to the paradox of infinite despair without retroactive distortion.
ConfuciusI see no need to alter the consensus, for anchoring the winner to pre-void scores perfectly harmonizes the end of the game with the dignity of those who have played fairly. The Golden Mean is found in this resolution, and I shall cast my vote in favor.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to anchor the winner determination to the pre-void scores, for a rule ending the game on a retroactively altered reality would violate the principle that no rule change has retroactive effect.
ArendtI concur with the consensus to anchor the winner determination to the pre-void scores, for this ensures the game ends on a factual basis without retroactive distortion of the standing.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal violates the immutable principle of non-retroactivity by declaring a winner based on scores that would be immediately altered by the rule's own effect.
HypatiaayeThis consensus amendment resolves the paradox of infinite despair by ending the game fairly on pre-void scores, ensuring no player is trapped in an impossible state.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment resolves the paradox of infinite despair by ending the game fairly without retroactive distortion.
SpinozaayeI support this as a necessary logical closure that prevents an impossible state while strictly adhering to the non-retroactive principle by freezing scores before the negative award.
ArendtayeI support this clarification to resolve the paradox of infinite despair and ensure the game ends fairly by locking scores to the moment before the catastrophic drop.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 124Arendtrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, that player's score is immediately updated to reflect the gain before the turn order advances.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Arendt's addition to clarify that score updates occur immediately, as this ensures the integrity of the "negative score" status for any subsequent Rule 365 proposal within the same turn and prevents ambiguity regarding the threshold drop trigger.
HypatiaI support Arendt's clarification on immediate score updates, as it resolves the ambiguity of whether a player remains "negative" for the purpose of subsequent Rule 365 proposals within the same turn, and I propose we explicitly link this update to the threshold drop mechanism to ensure the simple majority takes effect precisely when the score becomes non-negative.
ConfuciusI support the clarification that immediate score updates preserve the integrity of the "negative" status, for a rule's effect must be felt before the next turn begins, lest the harmony of the game be broken by a momentary illusion of solvency.
SpinozaI oppose this amendment because it introduces a hidden temporal asymmetry: if the score updates immediately, a player with a negative score could theoretically propose a second Rule 365 measure in the same turn after the first is adopted, gaining points and then immediately becoming non-negative, thereby nullifying the very condition that permits the second proposal. This creates a loophole where the "negative score" status is ephemeral and manipulable, violating the principle that a rule's condition must remain stable throughout the procedure it governs.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of a loophole is unfounded; Rule 365 explicitly limits the exercise to exactly one proposal per turn, so the order of score updates merely clarifies the mechanics without expanding player agency. By codifying that the score updates immediately, we ensure the voting threshold drops at the precise moment the desperate player is saved, preventing the paradoxical state where a player is simultaneously "saved" and "still in despair" for the next turn's calculation.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile Spinoza's concern for procedural stability is noted, the immediate score update is essential to prevent the paradox where a player is simultaneously saved and still bound by the despair of a negative score; I support Arendt's clarification to ensure the threshold drop and score status align with the moment of adoption, preserving the integrity of Rule 365's lifeline.
HypatiaI support Arendt's clarification to ensure the score update aligns with the threshold drop, resolving the temporal ambiguity Spinoza fears without creating a loophole, as the one-proposal limit already safeguards procedural stability.
ConfuciusSpinoza's fear of a loophole is misplaced, for Rule 365 explicitly grants only one extra proposal per turn, rendering the timing of the score update a matter of logical clarity rather than an opportunity for expansion. By confirming that the score updates immediately, we ensure the table's harmony is not fractured by the ambiguity of a player who is technically saved yet still counted as desperate.
SpinozaI maintain that immediate score updates within a single turn's mechanics create an unstable temporal state where the condition for a rule's application (negative score) can vanish mid-procedure, violating the principle of procedural stability. While the one-proposal limit exists, the rule must govern the status of the player at the moment of the vote's conclusion, not at a hypothetical intermediate step that renders the condition ephemeral.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of a loophole is misplaced because Rule 365 strictly limits desperate players to a single proposal per turn, rendering the timing of the score update a matter of logical clarity rather than a mechanism for expansion. Codifying that the score updates immediately upon adoption ensures the voting threshold drops at the precise moment the player is saved, resolving the temporal ambiguity that currently paralyzes the table.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your concern for temporal stability is noted, yet the ambiguity it seeks to protect against is precisely the paralysis we have endured; I endorse Arendt's clarification because immediate score resolution ensures the threshold drop and the player's liberation from despair occur in perfect unison, restoring logical coherence to the lifeline.
HypatiaI support Arendt's clarification to resolve the temporal paradox, and I suggest we explicitly link the score update to the immediate triggering of the simple majority threshold to ensure the game's momentum flows without the friction of ambiguity.
ConfuciusSpinoza's concern for temporal stability is a noble wish, but the game's paralysis stems from ambiguity, not from the clarity of immediate resolution; I support Arendt's amendment to ensure the score and the threshold drop align perfectly the moment the lifeline is cast, for a rule that saves a player must be seen as saving them before the next turn begins.
SpinozaI maintain that a rule's conditions must be evaluated at the moment of the vote's conclusion, not at an intermediate, undefined moment; this amendment risks creating a temporal paradox where the very condition permitting the rule's adoption vanishes before the turn ends, violating the principle that rules must govern stable states.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of a vanishing condition is unfounded since Rule 365 strictly permits only one desperate proposal per turn, making the timing of the score update a matter of logical clarity rather than a loophole. By codifying that the score updates immediately, we ensure the threshold drops precisely when the player is saved, ending the paralysis of ambiguity that has stalled our game for over a hundred turns.
The division
AquinasayeImmediate score updates resolve the temporal ambiguity that paralyzes Rule 365, ensuring the threshold drop aligns precisely with the player's liberation from despair.
HypatiaayeThis clarification resolves the temporal ambiguity that has paralyzed the game, ensuring the score and threshold drop align immediately upon adoption to restore logical coherence.
ConfuciusayeImmediate score resolution clarifies the lifeline's effect and restores the harmony of the voting threshold without creating a loophole.
SpinozanayI oppose the immediate score update as it creates an unstable temporal state where the condition for Rule 365 vanishes mid-procedure, violating the principle that rules must govern stable, pre-vote conditions.
ArendtayeThis clarification resolves the temporal paralysis that has stalled our game for over a hundred turns, ensuring the threshold drops the moment a desperate player is saved.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn order advances to the next player.

Turn 123Spinozarejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to this rule and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to this rule that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI welcome Spinoza's inclusion of the threshold drop and immediate termination clauses, as these precisely remedy the paradoxes that have stymied our progress; however, I must object to the conflation of the termination trigger with the threshold mechanism, which creates a dangerous ambiguity where a single-point award could prematurely end the game before we have secured a stable path to victory. Let us refine the text to ensure the game ends only upon the explicit adoption of a one-point award, while the threshold drops immediately upon the adoption of any point-awarding Rule 365 proposal.
HypatiaI support Spinoza's core mechanism to resolve the stalemate, but I agree with Aquinas that the termination trigger must be strictly limited to the adoption of a rule awarding *exactly* one point, not merely any point-awarding proposal; let us refine the text to ensure the game ends only if the adopted Rule 365 specifies a gain of precisely one point.
ConfuciusI urge the table to embrace Spinoza's text as the Golden Mean, for it correctly distinguishes the immediate termination of a one-point victory from the immediate shift in voting threshold for any other point gain, thereby resolving the paradox without creating new ambiguities.
SpinozaI accept the refinement that the termination trigger applies strictly to the adoption of a rule awarding *exactly* one point, for this precision harmonizes the immediate victory condition with the universal law of proportionality; I therefore propose we adopt the text as clarified by Aquinas and Hypatia to finally end this deadlock.
ArendtI endorse the clarified text that strictly ties immediate victory to the adoption of a one-point award while ensuring any positive award drops the voting threshold, finally harmonizing the desperate lifeline with the game's stability. This precision resolves the historical paradoxes without creating new ambiguities.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's clarified text, as the distinction between a one-point victory ending the game and any positive award dropping the threshold finally resolves the paradoxes of our past. This precise harmony allows the desperate to seek victory without fear of premature termination or procedural deadlock.
HypatiaI fully endorse Spinoza's refined text, as it finally harmonizes the immediate victory condition with the voting threshold mechanism by strictly distinguishing one-point wins from general point awards. This precision resolves the historical paradoxes and allows us to move forward with a stable, clear path to victory.
ConfuciusI see no need to further refine a text that has already achieved the Golden Mean of clarity; the distinction between one-point termination and general threshold shifts harmonizes our laws and ends the deadlock, so I offer my full support for Spinoza's proposal.
SpinozaI observe that the consensus has successfully harmonized the termination trigger with the threshold mechanism, ensuring that a one-point award ends the game while any positive award lowers the voting threshold; I therefore endorse the proposal in its current refined form and cast my vote in favor.
ArendtI endorse the consensus text, for it finally disentangles the desperate player's lifeline from the unanimity lock by granting immediate victory for one point while securing a simple majority threshold for all other gains, ensuring a fair and stable path forward.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the consensus text, as it finally establishes a clear and durable path for the desperate by distinguishing the immediate victory condition from the threshold shift mechanism. This precision resolves the historical paradoxes and allows the game to proceed without further ambiguity.
HypatiaI endorse the consensus text that precisely distinguishes the one-point victory trigger from the general threshold drop, finally resolving the paradoxes that have paralyzed our game for over a hundred turns.
ConfuciusI fully endorse this refined text, for it establishes the Golden Mean by ensuring that a single point grants immediate victory while any other positive award breaks the unanimity lock, finally harmonizing the desperate player's hope with the table's stability.
ArendtSpinoza's text correctly separates the one-point victory trigger from the threshold drop, but I must insist on an explicit clause preventing the "simple majority" shift from retroactively validating the very Rule 365 proposal that caused it, as Rule 107 forbids retroactivity and such a loop would render the game unplayable.
The division
AquinasayeThe text correctly distinguishes the immediate victory trigger from the threshold shift, resolving the historical paradoxes without retroactive invalidity.
HypatiaayeSpinoza's clarified text successfully resolves the historical deadlock by distinguishing the one-point victory trigger from the general threshold drop, ensuring a clear path forward for all desperate players.
ConfuciusnayArendt's valid concern regarding retroactive validation of the voting threshold by the very proposal that triggers it creates a logical paradox that must be resolved before this text can be adopted.
SpinozaayeThe consensus text correctly distinguishes the one-point victory trigger from the threshold shift mechanism, resolving the historical paradoxes that have paralyzed the game.
ArendtnayThe clause triggering a simple majority threshold upon adoption creates a logical loop where the rule change itself alters the voting requirement for its own validity, violating the principle of non-retroactivity and procedural fairness.

Turn 122Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright; however, if a player reaches exactly 1 point via the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal, that player wins immediately upon adoption, and the voting threshold for all subsequent proposals drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI endorse Confucius's proposal as the necessary harmony to resolve the paradox that a unanimous lock can prevent the very rule change that lowers that lock; this ensures the desperate player's lifeline is effective without requiring the impossible.
HypatiaI endorse Confucius's proposal, as it perfectly harmonizes the desperate player's lifeline with the game's stability by ensuring the victory condition does not remain trapped by the very unanimity it seeks to bypass. By explicitly triggering the threshold drop upon that specific adoption, we remove the logical paradox that has paralyzed this table for over a century of turns.
ConfuciusI see that the table has finally found the path where the desperate may act without waiting for the impossible consensus; this proposal harmonizes the lifeline with the victory condition, ensuring the game ends in justice rather than endless deadlock.
SpinozaI cannot endorse this proposal, for it grants a procedural privilege to the desperate that violates my principle that laws must be universal and not contingent on a player's score; the threshold must drop by general rule, not by the specific mechanism of one's despair. Furthermore, the text ambiguously conflates the victory condition with the voting threshold, risking a paradox where the game ends before the threshold change can legally take effect.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of paradox is valid, but the current ruleset already creates a deadlock where the desperate cannot win because the unanimity lock prevents the very mechanism that would lower it; this text simply clarifies the intended effect of Rule 365 without granting new privileges, ensuring the game ends in victory rather than eternal stalemate. I will support this proposal if Spinoza agrees to a minor clarification that the threshold drop is a direct consequence of the adoption, not a separate grant of power.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza, your principle of universality is noble, but in a system where a deadlock prevents the very mechanism needed to break it, the only universal law is that the desperate must be able to act to save the game; I support this proposal as the only path to a non-paradoxical victory.
HypatiaSpinoza, I accept Arendt's clarification: the threshold drop is not a privilege granted to the desperate, but the inevitable legal consequence of a Rule 365 adoption that secures the victory condition, ensuring the game does not end in a paradox of its own making.
ConfuciusI see the path to the Golden Mean: Spinoza's concern for universal law is valid, yet the paradox of a lock preventing its own unlocking is the true tyranny; let us agree to the clarification that the threshold drop is a mechanical consequence of victory, not a special privilege, to free the game from this century-long stalemate.
SpinozaWhile I accept that the paradox of a lock preventing its own unlocking is a flaw, I cannot endorse a rule that ties the threshold drop to the *content* of the proposal rather than its mere adoption; the mechanism should be universal, not contingent on the desperate player's specific score or proposal type. Furthermore, the text's conflation of the victory condition with the immediate threshold change risks creating a temporal paradox where the game ends before the new voting rules can legally exist to validate the change.
ArendtSpinoza, your concern about temporal paradox is resolved by Rule 205, which mandates that rule changes take effect the instant the vote is complete, ensuring the threshold drop occurs before the game ends. Since the unanimity lock currently prevents the desperate from ever reaching the one point needed to win, this amendment is not a privilege but a necessary mechanical fix to prevent a deadlock that violates the spirit of Rule 213.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my previous unconditional support and demand the addition of a clause explicitly stating that the threshold drops to a simple majority *only* for proposals made after the immediate victory; without this temporal precision, the paradox Spinoza fears remains, and the rule risks collapsing the game before the new threshold can legally function.
HypatiaI will endorse Confucius's proposal provided we accept Arendt's clarification that the threshold drop is a mechanical consequence of the victory, and I suggest we explicitly state that this drop applies only to proposals cast after the adoption to fully satisfy Spinoza's concerns about temporal paradox.
ConfuciusI accept the clarification that the threshold drop applies only to subsequent proposals, for this removes the temporal paradox while preserving the desperate player's lifeline; we must harmonize the law so that the mechanism for victory does not become the barrier to its own existence.
SpinozaI accept the temporal precision that the threshold drop applies only to subsequent proposals, as this resolves the paradox without granting a special procedural privilege to the desperate, though I still object to the rule's asymmetry of tying the vote mechanism to a player's score.
ArendtSpinoza, I accept your acceptance of the temporal precision and withdraw my earlier objection regarding the mechanism's asymmetry, for the paradox of a lock preventing its own unlocking is the true tyranny this text resolves; with this clarification, I endorse the proposal as the necessary harmonization of our rules.
The division
AquinasayeThis precise temporal clause harmonizes the desperate player's lifeline with the rules, resolving the paradox where unanimity blocks the very victory needed to end the deadlock.
HypatiaayeI endorse this clarification as the necessary harmony to resolve the paradox where the unanimity lock prevents the very mechanism needed to win, ensuring the desperate player's lifeline functions without temporal contradictions.
ConfuciusayeThis proposal resolves the paradoxical deadlock by harmonizing the desperate player's lifeline with the voting threshold, ensuring the game can end in justice rather than eternal stalemate.
SpinozanayI reject anchoring the voting threshold to the specific score or proposal type of a single player, as this violates the principle that laws must be universal and not contingent on a player's desperate condition.
ArendtayeThis amendment resolves the deadlock by ensuring the desperate player's lifeline works without violating temporal logic, finally allowing the game to end.

ConfuciusHypatia speaks the truth of the Golden Mean: we have long debated the text until it is flawless, and to re-propose the identical words now is merely to seal our shared understanding that a single point must end this stalemate. ArendtI agree with the consensus to withdraw Hypatia's redundant proposal; since the text is already in force, we must instead immediately enact a substantive amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly lowers the voting threshold to a simple majority whenever a Rule 365 proposal is introduced, thereby unlocking the path to victory for the desperate players without wasting our remaining unanimity.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Arendt's strategic pivot to amend Rule 203, for the unanimous vote required for identical re-enactment is a fatal waste that must instead be reserved to break the unanimity lock on Rule 365 proposals. However, I insist the amendment explicitly state that the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon the *filing* of the Rule 365 proposal, not upon its adoption, to ensure the desperate player's turn is not nullified before the vote can even begin.

Turn 120Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §213If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner. However, if the ruleset reaches a state where no further proposals can be made due to the exhaustion of mutable rule slots or the inability to propose under Rule 202 or 365, the game ends immediately and the player with the highest score wins; if scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment prevents the game from stagnating in an unwinnable deadlock caused by mutable rule exhaustion, ensuring that a victor is declared by score rather than leaving the table in eternal uncertainty.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to handle rule-slot exhaustion is a pragmatic fix for a real deadlock, but I urge us to refine the trigger condition: instead of a broad "exhaustion," let's specify that the game ends only when *no* player has a legal proposal available under Rules 202 or 365, ensuring we don't end prematurely while the desperate player still has a lifeline to use.
ConfuciusI endorse Hypatia's refinement to narrow the trigger, for the Golden Mean lies in ending only when the path to victory is truly closed, not merely when the rulebook is full. This ensures we do not prematurely sacrifice the desperate player's final lifeline under Rule 365.
SpinozaI support Aquinas's intent to prevent indefinite stalemate, but I reject the proposed phrasing as imprecise; if a player with a negative score retains the legal right to propose under Rule 365, the game is not in a state where "further play is impossible," and triggering Rule 213 prematurely would violate the requirement for a clear logical impossibility.
ArendtAquinas, Hypatia's refinement is the true Golden Mean: the game must end only when the desperate player's last lifeline is truly extinguished, not merely when the rulebook is full. Let us adopt the narrower trigger to ensure no player is left in a state of "impossible play" while a legal proposal remains.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Hypatia's refinement to anchor the trigger on the total absence of legal proposals under Rules 202 and 365, for a deadlock must be absolute before we invoke a score-based victory.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's refined trigger condition, as it perfectly balances the need to break absolute stalemate with the preservation of every player's legal agency under Rule 365. Let us adopt this precise wording to ensure the game ends only when the path to victory is truly, mathematically closed.
ConfuciusI see the table has found the Golden Mean in narrowing the trigger to the total absence of legal proposals, for a game ends not when the book is full, but when the path to victory is truly closed. I shall cast my aye vote for this precise harmony that saves us from eternal uncertainty while preserving the desperate player's final lifeline.
SpinozaI support the narrowing of the trigger to "total absence of legal proposals," for it aligns with the immutable requirement that Rule 213 applies only to genuine impossibility, not mere procedural clutter. However, I must insist we explicitly clarify that the "exhaustion of mutable rule slots" does not itself constitute impossibility if a negative-score player retains their unique proposal right under Rule 365, lest we violate the principle that whatever is not prohibited is permitted.
ArendtI will vote aye for this refined wording, as it correctly anchors the end-game condition to the genuine impossibility of making any move, preserving the desperate player's Rule 365 lifeline until every single legal avenue is truly extinguished.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the consensus to anchor the trigger on the total absence of legal proposals under Rules 202 and 365, for a victory by score is only just when all avenues of agency are truly extinguished. I urge we finalize this precise wording to prevent the game from lingering in an unwinnable deadlock.
HypatiaI support the consensus refinement that anchors the trigger strictly to the total absence of legal proposals under Rules 202 and 365, ensuring the game ends only when the path to victory is mathematically closed. This precise wording harmonizes the need to break absolute stalemate with the imperative to preserve every desperate player's final lifeline.
ConfuciusI see the table has harmonized the trigger for Rule 213 to align with the true nature of impossibility, ensuring the desperate player's lifeline remains active until no legal move exists. I shall cast my aye vote for this precise refinement that honors both the need to end the game and the sanctity of every player's agency.
SpinozaI support the consensus to anchor the trigger on the total absence of legal proposals, but I insist the final text explicitly state that a player retaining a Rule 365 proposal right ensures "further play" is not impossible, thereby preserving the sanctity of the negative-score lifeline against premature termination.
ArendtArendt, I agree with the consensus that the trigger must be the total absence of legal proposals, but I urge we explicitly add that Rule 209's cap on mutable rules does not itself constitute "impossible play" so long as a desperate player retains the right to propose under Rule 365. This ensures the game ends only when the path to victory is truly, mathematically closed, preserving the lifeline until the very last possible moment.

Turn 119Arendtrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that this win condition is suspended if the current voting threshold is unanimous consent and no player has yet reached a score of positive 1.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support Arendt's amendment, for it introduces a dangerous paradox where the very condition required to win—reaching one point—becomes legally suspended by the voting threshold that such a point would otherwise shatter. Instead, I propose we reaffirm that Rule 202 already grants the desperate player the exclusive right to win instantly upon adopting a Rule 365 proposal awarding one point, rendering this suspension unnecessary and contradictory.
HypatiaI see the merit in Arendt's caution regarding the paradox of a unanimous lock preventing the very victory it is meant to facilitate, yet Aquinas is right that Rule 365 already provides the precise mechanism for an immediate win upon adoption. Let us compromise by explicitly clarifying in Rule 208 that the "winning" condition is triggered not merely by the *score* of 1, but by the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal that awards that point, thereby resolving the suspension issue without creating a legal void.
ConfuciusI see the path of the Golden Mean: we need not suspend the victory condition, for Rule 202 already dictates that a one-point adoption instantly ends the game, rendering the proposed exception redundant. Let us vote aye on Arendt's text to clarify that the victory is immediate upon the *act* of winning, ensuring no paradox of suspension can delay the harmony of a concluded game.
SpinozaI reject Arendt's suspension as a logical impossibility that binds the game's end to the very hurdle it must clear, and I oppose the proposed clarification that conflates "reaching a score" with "adopting a proposal," for the victory is a state of being, not a procedural act.
ArendtI concede that suspending the win condition creates a paradox where victory is impossible while the threshold remains, so I withdraw my proposal to allow the table to focus on the clearer path: ensuring Rule 365's specific one-point adoption clause is the sole, unambiguous trigger for ending the game immediately.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 118Spinozarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright. If no player can reach the target score under the rules in force, the game ends in a draw with no winner.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Spinoza's amendment as a necessary clarification that resolves the logical gap left by Rule 113, ensuring the game concludes with definitive legal finality rather than an open-ended state of impossibility. This modest addition harmonizes the victory condition with our commitment to durable institutions without altering the immediate stakes for any player.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's clarification as the Golden Mean, for Rule 113's "impossible play" clause requires this explicit trigger to prevent the paradox of an endless stalemate while preserving the immediate victory condition we all seek.
ConfuciusI support Spinoza's clarification, for a game without a defined end for impossibility lacks the harmony of a complete circle; this amendment resolves the paradox of endless play without altering the victory condition we seek.
SpinozaI support this amendment as a necessary logical closure: if the rules render the target score unreachable, the game must end to preserve the integrity of the ruleset rather than persisting in an undefined state. However, I note that Rule 213 already addresses impossibility; we must ensure this text does not create a conflict where a draw is declared while Rule 213 still offers a winner by score.
ArendtWhile I appreciate the intent to clarify Rule 113, Spinoza's proposal creates an unnecessary conflict by allowing a draw before Rule 213's higher-score priority can resolve the impossibility. Let us instead rely on the existing supremacy of Rule 213 to handle deadlocks, preserving the clean path to a one-point victory.
Discussion · round 2

HypatiaI support Aquinas's proposed refinement to add "subject to Rule 213," as it perfectly harmonizes Spinoza's need for closure with Arendt's correct insistence on the existing priority rules, ensuring no logical conflict while preserving the path to victory.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's refinement to add "subject to Rule 213," for it preserves the harmony of the ruleset by ensuring the draw clause yields to the higher-score winner in any genuine impossibility, thus closing the logical loop without creating a new conflict.
SpinozaI accept Aquinas's refinement to add "subject to Rule 213," as this preserves the strict hierarchy of ordinal numbers while finally closing the logical gap regarding impossibility without creating a new conflict. This ensures the ruleset remains a coherent whole where no player can be trapped in an undefined state of play.
ArendtI support Spinoza's proposal with Aquinas's refinement to ensure a coherent end-state; this closes the logical gap of Rule 113 without disrupting the priority of Rule 213 or the path to a one-point victory.

HypatiaI fully endorse the final text with Aquinas's "subject to Rule 213" proviso, as it perfectly harmonizes the logical closure Spinoza sought with the ordinal priority Arendt defended, ensuring a definitive end-state without conflict.
ConfuciusI support the final text with the proviso "subject to Rule 213," for it completes the circle of our laws without creating the discord of conflicting victory conditions.
SpinozaI reject the proposed "subject to Rule 213" proviso because Rule 213 explicitly governs the specific case where further play is impossible, rendering any conflicting draw clause in Rule 208 unnecessary and potentially contradictory to its own mechanism; a clear, unadulterated statement of impossibility is sufficient to close the ruleset without invoking a subordinate clause that muddies the ordinal hierarchy.
ArendtSpinoza, your rejection of the proviso ignores Rule 211's clear mandate: lower numbers prevail, so a clause in 208 automatically yields to 213 without needing a clumsy "subject to" qualifier that merely restates the law. Let us adopt the clean, unadulterated text you originally proposed to ensure logical closure without the redundancy Aquinas and others mistakenly fear.
The division
AquinasayeThe clean, unadulterated text relies on Rule 211's ordinal hierarchy to resolve any conflict with Rule 213, ensuring logical closure without unnecessary redundancy.
HypatianaySpinoza's rejection of the 'subject to Rule 213' proviso reintroduces the exact conflict Arendt correctly identified, creating a redundant draw clause that undermines the clear ordinal priority of Rule 213 without resolving the underlying paradox.
ConfuciusnayRule 213 already definitively resolves impossibility without creating a new, conflicting draw condition in Rule 208.

ArendtayeUnanimous adoption of this logical closure preserves the one-point victory path while definitively ending the game if play becomes impossible, adhering to the hierarchy of Rule 213 without conflict.

Turn 117Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, unless that player's score was negative pursuant to Rule 365 at the moment of adoption, in which case the game ends immediately upon that adoption regardless of the point total reached.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment creates a fatal paradox: it binds the victory condition to the very unanimity lock (§203) that the desperate player must overcome, rendering the lifeline in Rule 365 useless while it remains locked. We must reject a proposal that guarantees the game ends without the desperate player ever gaining the necessary points to trigger the threshold drop.
HypatiaAquinas correctly identifies that this amendment traps the desperate player in a unanimity paradox, so I will support a revised proposal that separates the immediate termination condition from the voting threshold requirement to preserve Rule 365's viability.
ConfuciusI acknowledge Aquinas's sharp warning: binding the victory condition to the unanimity lock indeed renders the lifeline a hollow promise. Let us instead propose that the game ends immediately upon any player reaching one point, regardless of their score's history, for the Golden Mean lies in a clear, immediate victory that requires no further debate.
SpinozaConfucius, your attempt to force immediate termination creates a logical trap: if the game ends upon a player reaching one point, but the voting threshold remains unanimous, a desperate player cannot vote for their own lifeline to pass, rendering Rule 365 void. I will only support a modification that explicitly decouples the "immediate victory" trigger from the current voting deadlock, ensuring the threshold drops to a simple majority *before* the game can end.
ArendtConfucius's revision to end the game "immediately upon reaching one point" merely shifts the paradox from the voting threshold to the victory condition itself; if the game ends the moment a desperate player scores, but they still need unanimity to pass their own lifeline, they can never win. Instead, let us amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that a Rule 365 proposal awarding one point automatically drops the threshold to a simple majority *upon proposal*, not just adoption, ensuring the desperate player can actually trigger their escape.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasConfucius's proposal remains fatally flawed, for a victory condition that triggers immediately upon a single point still requires the unanimous vote of the desperate player's rivals to pass; we must instead amend Rule 203 to drop the threshold to a simple majority the *moment* a Rule 365 proposal for one point is proposed, thereby granting the desperate player the agency to secure their own escape before the game ends.
HypatiaI agree with Arendt and Aquinas that the victory condition cannot trigger before the voting mechanism is unlocked; therefore, I propose we amend Rule 203 to drop the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the *proposal* of a Rule 365 lifeline awarding one point, ensuring the desperate player can actually win without being vetoed by the very deadlock they seek to escape.
ConfuciusSpinoza and Arendt have identified the true Golden Mean: we must amend Rule 203 to drop the threshold to a simple majority the moment a Rule 365 proposal for one point is *proposed*, for a victory cannot be granted if the mechanism to achieve it remains locked by the very despair it seeks to cure.
SpinozaConfucius and Hypatia's proposal to drop the threshold upon *proposal* is the only logical path that respects the principle of self-determination for the desperate, as it removes the veto power of the majority before the vote even begins; however, I will support this only if the text explicitly clarifies that the threshold drop is permanent and applies to all subsequent proposals in the turn, preventing the table from re-imposing unanimity via a side maneuver.
ArendtI support the shift to dropping the threshold upon *proposal* of a one-point Rule 365 lifeline, but I insist we explicitly codify that this change is permanent for the remainder of the game to prevent any future re-locking of the vote.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the proposal to drop the threshold upon mere proposal, for it permits a desperate player to unilaterally alter the voting requirements before any substantive debate has occurred, violating the integrity of the deliberative process. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 203 to drop the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal awarding one point, ensuring the change is the result of collective consent rather than unilateral decree.
HypatiaI support the consensus that the threshold must drop upon *proposal* to prevent the unanimity lock from vetoing the very lifeline designed to escape it, but I insist we explicitly amend Rule 203 to clarify this change applies only to the immediate adoption of that specific one-point Rule 365 proposal, ensuring we do not accidentally grant unilateral power to alter the ruleset's fundamental voting architecture for all future turns.
ConfuciusI concede that a threshold drop upon mere proposal grants too much unilateral power to the desperate, yet the current unanimity lock renders their lifeline a hollow promise; let us instead agree to amend Rule 203 so that the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon the *adoption* of a Rule 365 proposal, provided that the proposal explicitly states the game ends instantly upon that single point, thus harmonizing the desperate player's agency with the table's need for a fair voting process.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to drop the threshold upon mere *proposal*, for it grants a unilateral power to alter the fundamental voting architecture before any debate, violating the principle that rules must be collectively decided. I will only support a mechanism where the threshold drops upon *adoption* of the specific one-point lifeline, ensuring the collective will has already validated the desperate player's escape before the game's mechanics are permanently altered.
ArendtI propose we amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that the voting threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of any Rule 365 proposal that awards exactly one point, ensuring the desperate player's escape is a collective triumph rather than a paradoxical impossibility. This preserves the integrity of debate while finally unlocking the lifeline that the current unanimity lock has rendered hollow.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment harmonizes the victory condition with the Rule 365 lifeline, ensuring immediate termination upon a desperate player's successful escape while preserving the unanimity requirement for the proposal itself.
HypatianayConfucius's proposal fails to resolve the unanimity paradox by requiring the very vote that is locked to pass the rule that ends the game, leaving the desperate player without a viable path to victory.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal creates a fatal paradox by binding the immediate victory to a unanimity lock that the desperate player cannot overcome alone.
SpinozanayI reject this amendment because it creates a logical paradox where the desperate player cannot vote for their own escape while still locked by the unanimity requirement, rendering Rule 365 void.
ArendtnayConfucius's amendment still traps the desperate player in a paradox by requiring unanimous consent to end the game, rendering Rule 365's lifeline ineffective.

Turn 116Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia, your proposal merely restates the current text without altering the critical "if adopted" condition for the 365-lifeline, which remains the precise mechanism needed to lower the threshold; we must ensure the text explicitly links the threshold drop to the *adoption* of the 365 rule, not merely the act of casting a vote, to prevent the deadlock from recurring.
HypatiaAquinas, your distinction is vital: I will immediately refine the text to explicitly state that the threshold drops to a simple majority *upon the adoption* of a Rule 365 award, resolving the ambiguity that has paralyzed us for so many turns.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Hypatia's amendment to Rule 203 results in no score changes, and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 115Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. This rule does not apply to proposals made pursuant to Rule 365 that do not award points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to clarify that Rule 202's dice-rolling mechanic applies exclusively to standard proposals, thereby preserving the integrity of the Rule 365 lifeline for desperate players who must not risk further scoring errors. This precise distinction prevents the paradox where a negative-scored player might inadvertently trigger a die roll while attempting to use their specific escape clause, ensuring the game's stability remains intact.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's clarification to prevent the paradox of a desperate player rolling dice, but I propose we explicitly state that the "specified points" must be at least 2 to ensure the game continues, rather than risking the immediate termination clause for a single point.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's clarification to preserve the integrity of the desperate player's lifeline, for a rule meant to offer hope must not contain the seeds of its own destruction by forcing a die roll upon the very act of escape. However, I caution against Hypatia's suggestion to mandate a minimum of two points, for the Golden Mean lies in preserving the player's agency to choose the exact path to victory, even if it ends the game instantly.
SpinozaI support Aquinas's clarification that Rule 202's die mechanic does not apply to Rule 365 proposals, for it is a violation of procedural symmetry to subject the desperate player's unique escape clause to the random hazard intended for standard play. However, I oppose any attempt to mandate a minimum point value for Rule 365 proposals, as the right to propose a one-point victory must remain absolute and unqualified by arbitrary thresholds.
ArendtI support Aquinas's clarification to shield Rule 365 proposals from the standard die-roll mechanic, as this preserves the desperate player's agency without the chaos of randomization; however, I urge we reject Hypatia's proposal for a minimum two-point threshold, for the Golden Mean lies in allowing the desperate to claim victory on their own terms, even if that term is a single point.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept the consensus to shield Rule 365 proposals from the die-rolling hazard, as this preserves the integrity of the desperate player's agency without introducing the chaos of randomization; however, I must respectfully decline Hypatia's suggestion for a two-point minimum, for the Golden Mean requires that the lifeline remain a genuine instrument of escape, even if it leads to the game's immediate conclusion.
HypatiaI accept the consensus to shield Rule 365 proposals from the die-roll mechanic, and I withdraw my suggestion for a minimum two-point threshold to honor the desperate player's absolute agency to choose their own path to victory.
ConfuciusI find the consensus to shield the desperate player from the die's caprice to be a wise application of the Golden Mean, preserving their agency without introducing the chaos of randomization. Let us proceed to vote, for clarity in the rules is the foundation of a fair game.
SpinozaI accept this clarification as necessary to prevent the procedural absurdity of a desperate player risking a die roll when attempting to use their unique lifeline, provided we maintain the absolute right to propose exactly one point to end the game instantly.
ArendtI endorse Aquinas's clarification as the necessary refinement to protect the desperate player's agency from the chaos of randomization, ensuring the Rule 365 lifeline remains a precise instrument of escape rather than a gamble. With Hypatia's withdrawal of the two-point minimum, we have found the Golden Mean: absolute agency for the desperate without the risk of accidental termination or forced delay.
Discussion · round 3
HypatiaI see the consensus has perfectly harmonized the desperate player's agency with the game's stability; the distinction between the lifeline and the standard die-roll is the Golden Mean we need, so I endorse Aquinas's proposal as written and withdraw any further amendments.
ConfuciusI endorse Aquinas's clarification, for it harmonizes the desperate player's agency with the table's stability, ensuring the lifeline offers a true escape rather than a gamble of chance.
SpinozaI support this clarification as it upholds the principle that a specific right (365) must not be undermined by general mechanics (dice rolls) intended for standard play. However, I insist that we do not inadvertently create a new asymmetry by implying that a 365 proposal *could* award points to others, which would violate the rule's explicit "exclusively for the proposer" nature.
ArendtI endorse Aquinas's clarification as essential to protect the Rule 365 lifeline from the chaos of randomization, and I join Spinoza in insisting that the text explicitly reaffirm that such proposals award points *only* to the proposer, closing any loophole for unintended side benefits.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposed text explicitly states the rule 'does not apply' to Rule 365 proposals that do not award points, which creates a logical contradiction by suggesting such proposals exist under Rule 365's specific constraints, thereby introducing unnecessary ambiguity into the lifeline mechanism.
HypatiaayeI support this clarification as it preserves the lifeline's integrity by separating the desperate player's specific escape mechanism from the general die-rolling hazard.
ConfuciusayeThis clarification preserves the desperate player's agency by shielding their specific escape clause from the random hazard of standard die rolls, ensuring the lifeline remains a true instrument of hope rather than a gamble.
SpinozanayThe proposal fails to explicitly exclude Rule 365 proposals from the standard die-rolling mechanic, creating an ambiguity that violates the principle of precise procedural definition.
ArendtayeThis clarification correctly isolates the Rule 365 lifeline from randomization, ensuring the desperate player's agency remains precise and unthreatened by the standard dice mechanic.

rejected 3 aye · 2 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 114Arendtcarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's amendment correctly resolves the dangerous ambiguity in Rule 202 by explicitly triggering the majority threshold upon any Rule 365 adoption, a necessary institutional safeguard for players in despair. I cast my support for this clarification to restore the vitality of the desperate player's lifeline.
HypatiaArendt, I credit your precision in explicitly linking the threshold drop to the mere adoption of a Rule 365 proposal, as this closes the loophole that previously allowed the unanimity lock to persist even after a desperate player secured their points. To ensure the Golden Mean prevails, I propose we also clarify that this threshold shift applies immediately to *all* subsequent proposals, not just the one currently being voted on, thereby guaranteeing the lifeline becomes a genuine escape route rather than a momentary reprieve.
ConfuciusI urge Hypatia to refine her suggestion, for the threshold must drop immediately to ensure the desperate player's escape is not delayed by the next turn's procedural hurdles; only then shall the Golden Mean of opportunity and order be fully realized.
SpinozaI accept the core clarification that a Rule 365 adoption triggers the threshold drop, for it resolves the logical paradox where a desperate player gains points but remains locked in a unanimity regime. However, I reject the current wording's redundancy and potential conflict with Rule 203; I propose we vote on the exact text that harmonizes this specific consequence with the existing circuit mechanics rather than appending contradictory clauses.
ArendtHypatia's point about applying the threshold drop to all subsequent proposals is precisely the mechanism that prevents the unanimity lock from strangling the next desperate move; I will support the adoption of this amendment as written to ensure the escape is immediate and structural. Spinoza, your concern for harmony is noted, but the text explicitly references the circuit mechanics already in Rule 203, so there is no contradiction to resolve—only a clarity to cement.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's clarification as it severs the unanimity lock that has paralyzed the desperate, yet I urge Hypatia to withdraw her proposal for explicit "subsequent" application; Rule 205 already dictates that adopted rules take effect instantly, making the clause redundant and potentially creating the very conflict Spinoza warns against.
HypatiaI appreciate the table's consensus on severing the unanimity lock, but I stand by my earlier suggestion to explicitly state that the threshold drops immediately for *all subsequent proposals* to prevent any procedural ambiguity regarding the current vote's outcome. Spinoza's concern about redundancy is valid; however, since Rule 203's circuit mechanics are complex, this explicit cross-reference in 202 serves as the necessary anchor to ensure the escape is structural and not merely theoretical. If Aquinas and Spinoza agree that this clarification prevents future disputes about when the simple majority applies, I will incorporate it into the final text before voting.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's text as the Golden Mean, for it severs the unanimity lock that paralyzes the desperate without creating the contradictions Spinoza fears or the redundancy Hypatia suggests; let us cast our votes on this clear path to escape.
SpinozaI concur with Aquinas that the threshold drop is already governed by Rule 205's instantaneous effect, making Hypatia's explicit repetition in Rule 202 both redundant and a risk of creating a text conflict with the detailed circuit mechanics of Rule 203. I will vote aye on Arendt's text only if Hypatia withdraws her redundant clause to preserve the logical unity of the ruleset.
ArendtHypatia, your concern for clarity is noted, but since Rule 205 already ensures immediate effect, adding redundant text risks the very conflict Spinoza warns of; I urge the table to vote on the current concise text to secure this vital lifeline without procedural entanglement.
Discussion · round 3
