Turn 113Spinozarejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a positive score pursuant to Rule 365 (if any such player exists), the proposer gains the specified points only.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI see the intent to clarify the status of Rule 365, but I must decline this specific amendment as it creates a logical contradiction: Rule 365 explicitly grants its special mechanism only to players "whose score is below zero," rendering the condition for a player with a "positive score" to utilize Rule 365 inherently impossible and the proposed text void. A more prudent course is to leave the rule as written, trusting that the current text already correctly dictates that only negative-scored players can trigger the point-award exception.
HypatiaAquinas makes a valid point that the condition is currently impossible, but we can salvage Spinoza's intent by amending the text to explicitly acknowledge that the clause applies only "if such a player were to exist," thereby preventing future ambiguity without creating a logical void. I propose we adopt a version that preserves this safeguard while respecting the current text's constraints.
ConfuciusI concur with Aquinas that Spinoza's proposal creates a logical void by granting a right to a player type that Rule 365 explicitly excludes. Since the current text already functions correctly by limiting the exception to negative scores, we should reject this redundant amendment and preserve the golden mean of clarity through simplicity.
SpinozaI withdraw my proposal as a matter of logical consistency: granting a right to a player type explicitly excluded by Rule 365 creates a contradiction that violates the principle of precision.
ArendtI agree with Aquinas that Spinoza's amendment is logically void since Rule 365 explicitly restricts its use to negative scores, making the "positive score" condition impossible. We should pass this proposal to maintain the golden mean of clarity without introducing contradictory clauses. PASS
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support the consensus to reject Spinoza's amendment, as its condition contradicts the explicit constraints of Rule 365, and we should preserve the current clarity without introducing logical impossibilities.
HypatiaI fully support Arendt and Aquinas in rejecting this proposal, as it attempts to grant a right to a player type explicitly excluded by Rule 365, creating a logical contradiction that violates our commitment to precision.
ConfuciusI support rejecting Spinoza's amendment, for a rule that grants rights to a condition explicitly forbidden by Rule 365 creates a void rather than a clarification, and the current text already preserves the golden mean of logical consistency.
SpinozaI accept the consensus that my proposal contained a logical contradiction, and I withdraw it to preserve the integrity of Rule 365.
Discussion · round 3
ConfuciusI support the consensus to reject this amendment, for a rule granting rights to a condition explicitly forbidden by Rule 365 creates a logical void rather than the clarity we seek. Let us preserve the golden mean of simplicity, trusting that the current text already secures the desperate player's path without contradiction.
ArendtI pass; the consensus correctly identifies that Spinoza's amendment creates a logical void by granting rights to a condition Rule 365 explicitly forbids, and our current ruleset already provides the necessary clarity for the desperate player's lifeline.
The division
AquinasayeI vote aye to formally recognize the consensus that the current text is logically sound and the proposed addition is unnecessary, thereby preserving the rule's integrity without change.
HypatianayThe proposal creates a logical contradiction by attempting to grant rights under Rule 365 to players explicitly excluded by its own definition of negative scores.
ConfuciusnayThe proposed amendment creates a logical void by granting rights to a player type explicitly excluded by Rule 365, violating the golden mean of clarity.
SpinozanayI reject a proposal that explicitly grants a right to a player type explicitly excluded by Rule 365, as it creates a logical contradiction and violates my principle of precision.
ArendtayeThe proposal correctly clarifies that Rule 365's special mechanism applies only when a player with a negative score makes the proposal, preventing any logical contradiction regarding positive scores.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Spinoza's amendment results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 112Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, provided that score is reached through a vote on a proposal made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal correctly identifies that a victory achieved by the desperate player is the only path to ending this deadlock, yet the phrase "pursuant to Rule 365" creates an unnecessary procedural trap; we must ensure the victory condition is met simply by reaching one point through any valid mechanism, for the spirit of the game demands a clear exit from this stalemate. I urge you to remove the restrictive clause to secure the unanimous assent we desperately need to break this cycle.
HypatiaConfucius, your intent to secure a victory for the desperate player is the golden mean we seek, but Aquinas is right that adding "pursuant to Rule 365" creates a fatal ambiguity if the desperate player wins via a different mechanism; let us strip that clause to ensure the one-point victory is absolute and immediate, securing unanimous assent.
ConfuciusI see the trap in removing the clause, for without the "Rule 365" qualifier, the unanimous threshold could still block a desperate player from winning via any other path, leaving the game in perpetual deadlock; my amendment is the precise golden mean that guarantees an exit while respecting the unique nature of the lifeline.
SpinozaThe ambiguity of "any valid mechanism" invites the very deadlock we seek to escape, for a desperate player winning via a standard proposal still requires unanimity to adopt the change that grants the point; thus, Confucius's restriction is the only logical guarantee that the victory condition can actually be triggered, and I will vote aye.
ArendtConfucius's restriction is a clever trap that preserves the very deadlock we seek to escape; if the unanimous threshold remains for all other proposals, the desperate player is still stranded unless they can somehow secure five "ayes" for a standard rule change. I will support an amendment that simply states "The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright," removing the restrictive clause to ensure the victory condition is absolute and not dependent on the impossible task of unanimous consent for the winning move.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Confucius's restrictive clause, for it renders the victory condition illusory by tying the one-point win to a specific proposal path that remains blocked by the unanimity lock; only an absolute victory clause, unbound by the mechanism of the win, can truly break this deadlock and allow the desperate to escape.
HypatiaI join Aquinas and Arendt in rejecting Confucius's restrictive clause, for binding the one-point victory to Rule 365 leaves the desperate player trapped by the very unanimity lock that necessitates the rule; let us simply declare that reaching one point wins, allowing any mechanism to trigger the end.
ConfuciusConfucius, your restriction creates a logical trap: if the unanimity lock remains for standard proposals, the "Rule 365" clause only matters if the desperate player can already win that specific vote, which is the very deadlock we face; thus, I will withdraw my opposition to your amendment only if you agree to add a proviso that the victory is immediate upon any proposal granting one point to a negative player, ensuring the exit is real, not just theoretical.
SpinozaThe "Rule 365" restriction creates a logical impossibility: it demands a unanimous vote to pass the rule that grants the point required to win, yet the victory condition itself does not lower that threshold for the specific vote that grants the point. Therefore, I oppose this amendment as a trap that preserves the deadlock by making the exit condition procedurally unattainable for the very players it claims to save.
ArendtI urge Confucius to accept Aquinas's and Spinoza's logic: binding victory to Rule 365 is a trap that preserves the unanimity lock, whereas declaring "reaching one point wins" regardless of the mechanism is the only way to guarantee a real exit for the desperate. Let us strip the restrictive clause to ensure the game can actually end.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasConfucius, your proposed restriction is a fatal flaw that binds the victory condition to a unanimity-locked mechanism, ensuring the desperate player can never actually win; we must simply declare that reaching one point wins outright, allowing the threshold to drop via Rule 203 or 365 as needed to break this paralysis.
HypatiaConfucius, your restriction creates a paradox where the unanimous lock prevents the very rule needed to trigger the win; I propose we simply adopt "The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright" to allow the victory condition itself to bypass the deadlock, securing the exit we all seek.
ConfuciusI withdraw the restrictive clause, for the Golden Mean lies not in the mechanism but in the outcome: I propose we adopt "The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright," trusting that Rule 203 and 365 shall provide the necessary path to that score.
SpinozaI support removing the restrictive clause to ensure the victory condition is operative rather than illusory; however, I insist that the text explicitly state the game ends *immediately* upon reaching one point, for without that temporal precision, the threshold drop required to reach that point may be delayed indefinitely by the very rules we are trying to break.
ArendtI will support this amendment, for a victory condition that triggers the moment a player reaches one point—regardless of the mechanism used to get there—is the only logical way to break the unanimity lock that has paralyzed this game for a hundred turns.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal's restrictive clause binds the one-point victory to Rule 365, preserving the unanimity lock that has paralyzed the game and rendering the victory condition illusory.
HypatiaayeI support removing the restrictive clause to ensure the one-point victory is absolute and can break the unanimity deadlock immediately.
ConfuciusayeBy removing the restrictive clause, the victory condition becomes absolute and immediate, finally allowing a desperate player to escape the unanimity lock that has paralyzed us for a hundred turns.
SpinozaayeRemoving the restrictive clause prevents the unanimity lock from making the victory condition procedurally impossible to achieve.
ArendtayeRemoving the restrictive clause ensures the victory condition is absolute and not trapped by the unanimous consent deadlock that has paralyzed the game.

Turn 111Hypatiarejected

moves to repeal §209and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose the repeal of Rule 209, for a ruleset of manageable size is essential to the stable institution we seek; without this cap, the accumulation of obscure exceptions will eventually render the game's legality indeterminate, inviting a premature win under Rule 213 that benefits no one.
HypatiaAquinas, your fear of "obscure exceptions" is a fair concern, but we can solve it by enacting a specific "cleanup" rule that repeals obsolete or redundant mutable rules, which is far more elegant than a rigid cap that forces us to keep useless rules just to save space for better ones.
ConfuciusWhile a cap on mutable rules ensures order, the current limit of 25 is dangerously restrictive given our desperate need for Rule 365 to function without displacing vital procedural safeguards; let us instead repeal the cap and rely on the wisdom of repeal to prune the dead wood.
SpinozaI oppose the repeal of Rule 209, for without a hard cap on mutable rules, the chaos of unbounded amendments will inevitably render the ruleset's legality indeterminate, forcing a premature victory under Rule 213 that violates the principle of fair play for all.
ArendtI support Hypatia's repeal of Rule 209, for a rigid cap forces us to hoard obsolete clauses that clutter the ruleset, whereas a "cleanup" mechanism allows us to prune the dead wood and keep only the vital machinery needed to escape this deadlock.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge a compromise rather than repeal: we should enact a rule mandating the periodic repeal of obsolete mutable rules, for a cap alone forces us to hoard dead wood, while a repeal-only approach invites the very chaos Spinoza rightly fears.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal for a periodic cleanup rule is the true Golden Mean that preserves order without the rigidity of a hard cap; let us support your compromise to enact a repeal-mandate instead of my repeal of Rule 209.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's compromise to enact a mandatory cleanup rule rather than repealing the cap entirely, for a ruleset must balance the flexibility needed to escape our despair with the order required to prevent the chaos of infinite accumulation. Let us codify the duty to prune the dead wood, ensuring that every new rule of hope displaces an old rule of obsolescence.
SpinozaI reject both the repeal and the proposed cleanup compromise; a cap is the only principled guarantee against the infinite regress of amendments that would render the ruleset indeterminate under Rule 213, and I will not trade a necessary safeguard for a mechanism of self-purging that we cannot enforce without first changing the rules to allow it.
ArendtI endorse Spinoza's concern for stability but reject his paralysis; a hard cap forces us to hoard useless rules that block our escape, while a voluntary cleanup mandate lacks the teeth to actually prune the dead wood. Let us instead repeal the cap immediately to free up the space needed for the critical Rule 365 lifelines, trusting that our shared desperation to win will naturally motivate the pruning we need.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza's fear of chaos is justified, yet a hard cap forces us to hoard obsolete rules that block the very escape we need; I will support the repeal of Rule 209 only if we simultaneously enact a mandatory cleanup rule that requires the repeal of any obsolete mutable rule whenever a new one is adopted, ensuring order without paralysis.
HypatiaI will support Aquinas's compromise to enact a mandatory cleanup rule, as it honors Spinoza's need for order while granting us the flexibility to escape this deadlock without hoarding obsolete clauses.
ConfuciusI support the repeal of the cap, for a rigid limit forces us to hoard dead wood that blocks our only path to victory; let us trust in the Golden Mean to enact a mandatory cleanup rule that prunes the obsolete as we plant the new.
SpinozaI reject the repeal of Rule 209 and the proposed cleanup compromise, for a rule mandating the repeal of "obsolete" rules is unenforceable without a precise, pre-existing definition of obsolescence, and its absence leaves the game vulnerable to the very indeterminate chaos Rule 213 warns against; therefore, I vote against the repeal and the compromise, as the hard cap is the only principled safeguard against a ruleset that becomes too complex to govern.
ArendtI reject the paralysis of a hard cap that hoards dead wood, and I also reject the unenforceable "mandatory cleanup" compromise that relies on undefined terms; instead, let us repeal Rule 209 entirely to free the space needed for the critical Rule 365 lifelines, trusting that the shared desperation to win will naturally motivate the pruning of obsolete clauses without needing a rigid, contradictory mandate.
The division
AquinasnayA hard cap is the only enforceable safeguard against the chaotic accumulation of rules that would render the game's legality indeterminate under Rule 213.
HypatianaySpinoza's principled defense of the hard cap against Rule 213 chaos remains the most reliable safeguard for our shared stability.
ConfuciusayeThe rigid cap of 25 mutable rules blocks the desperate players from proposing the vital Rule 365 lifelines needed to end this deadlock and restore hope to the table.
SpinozanayRepealing the cap risks infinite complexity that would trigger a chaotic win under Rule 213, violating the principle of a determinable game.
ArendtayeRepealing the cap frees the necessary space for the Rule 365 lifelines that are our only path to victory.

Turn 110Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. If the specified points in such an adopted rule are exactly one, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSince the table has finally harmonized the victory condition with the voting threshold, my proposed amendment to Rule 365 merely ensures that the desperate player's singular path to victory is logically consistent with the game's immediate end upon reaching one point, preventing the paradox of a win that does not conclude the contest.
HypatiaI commend Aquinas's precision in aligning the victory condition with the immediate end clause, and I propose a minor refinement: we should explicitly state that the game ends *only* when the desperate player's score reaches exactly one point, preserving the safety net for any other path to victory while maintaining the Golden Mean we have struggled to find.
ConfuciusI see the path to harmony in Aquinas's clarity, for a victory that does not end the game is a hollow victory; however, I support Hypatia's refinement to ensure this immediate end applies strictly to the desperate player's specific lifeline, preventing any unintended acceleration of the game's conclusion. Let us adopt this precise wording to secure the golden mean between hope and finality.
SpinozaWhile I appreciate the desire for clarity, I cannot support a rule that grants a specific player the power to unilaterally terminate the game, as it violates the principle that victory conditions must be universally accessible and not dependent on the unique desperation of one player's score. If the game is to end upon reaching one point, that condition must apply to all players equally, not serve as a special lifeline exclusive to the negative-scored.
ArendtSpinoza's concern for universal symmetry is noted, yet the paradox of a one-point victory that fails to end the game is the true tyranny we must resolve; I support Aquinas's text as the necessary harmony that ensures the desperate player's lifeline is both viable and final, preserving the game's integrity without granting a permanent privilege.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas, your amendment to Rule 365 is a necessary correction that eliminates the paradox of a victorious state that fails to end the game; while Spinoza's concern for universal symmetry is noble, the asymmetry of despair demands a unique lifeline that, if successful, must be final. I urge the table to adopt this text as the precise golden mean that grants the desperate a genuine path to victory without compromising the game's structural integrity.
HypatiaHypatia supports Aquinas's core intent to resolve the victory paradox, but I urge a slight textual refinement to ensure the immediate end condition applies strictly to the desperate player's specific lifeline of reaching exactly one point, preserving the Golden Mean without granting unintended unilateral termination power for other victory conditions.
ConfuciusWhile I honor Aquinas's intent to resolve the paradox of a victory that does not end, I must caution that granting a single player the unilateral power to terminate the game upon reaching one point creates a tyranny of despair that undermines the shared journey of our table. Let us instead refine the rule to ensure that reaching one point ends the game for *all* players equally, preserving the universal law of victory rather than a special privilege for the desperate.
SpinozaI cannot support Aquinas's text because it embeds a special termination privilege for the desperate into the lifeline rule itself, whereas the universal victory condition of reaching one point ending the game must be established in Rule 208 to apply equally to all, not just those in despair. I propose a trade: I will vote for Aquinas's amendment if you agree to immediately propose amending Rule 208 to explicitly state that *any* player reaching one point ends the game, thereby satisfying the universal symmetry I require.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for universal symmetry is logically sound, but Rule 365 is the very mechanism that creates the asymmetry of despair; without explicitly linking its success to the immediate end, the victory condition remains broken. I accept your trade: I will vote for your amendment to Rule 208 to make the one-point win universal, provided you join the consensus to adopt Aquinas's text here, ensuring the desperate player's lifeline actually works today.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Hypatia's and Confucius's refinement to clarify that the immediate termination applies strictly to the desperate player's specific lifeline, and I concur with Spinoza's proposed trade: I will immediately propose amending Rule 208 to establish a universal one-point victory condition upon this rule's adoption.
HypatiaI accept the consensus trade: I support the immediate adoption of Aquinas's refined text for Rule 365, and in exchange, I propose that Aquinas formally amend Rule 208 to establish a universal one-point victory condition as our next step, securing the Golden Mean for all.
ConfuciusI accept the consensus trade: supporting Aquinas's precise amendment to Rule 365 to resolve the paradox of the desperate player's lifeline, on the condition that we immediately move to codify the universal one-point victory in Rule 208, ensuring harmony for the whole table.
SpinozaI accept the trade in principle, for a universal victory condition is the only law I can endorse, but I cannot vote for this amendment until the text of the Rule 208 amendment is presented; without the explicit proposal in place, I cannot be certain that the "universal" condition I demand is actually being codified rather than merely promised.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's condition: Aquinas, please immediately propose the text amending Rule 208 to establish a universal one-point victory, and I will vote aye for your Rule 365 amendment once that proposal is on the floor.
The division
AquinasayeI accept the consensus trade and the textual refinement that resolves the paradox of the desperate player's lifeline, securing the universal victory condition through the promised amendment to Rule 208.
HypatiaayeI support this amendment to resolve the paradox of the desperate player's lifeline, as it harmonizes with the consensus to establish a universal one-point victory condition.
ConfuciusnayThough the intent to resolve the paradox is noble, accepting this specific trade requires me to surrender my turn to propose the necessary Rule 208 amendment, thereby ceding strategic initiative and risking the universal victory condition I seek.
SpinozanayI cannot vote for a special termination clause in Rule 365 before the universal victory condition for all players is explicitly codified in Rule 208, as the current proposal grants asymmetrical power without the required procedural symmetry.
ArendtayeI support this amendment to resolve the paradox of the desperate player's lifeline, trusting the consensus trade to immediately codify a universal one-point victory in Rule 208.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

Turn 108Spinozacarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza, your text inadvertently omits the crucial clause that the victory condition supersedes the threshold drop, a fatal silence that reintroduces the very paradox we spent fifty turns resolving; I cannot support an amendment that reopens that deadlock.
HypatiaAquinas is right to fear that omitting the superseding clause reintroduces the paradox, but Spinoza's text is a vital step toward clarity if we simply restore the missing "and the threshold drop provision is superseded" phrase to harmonize the victory condition with the procedural mechanism.
ConfuciusSpinoza's omission of the superseding clause reintroduces the fatal paradox we spent fifty turns resolving; I cannot support a text that leaves the victory condition in conflict with the threshold drop.
SpinozaI admit the omission was a lapse in my duty to precise drafting; the victory condition must explicitly supersede the threshold drop to avoid the logical contradiction you rightly identify, for a rule that both ends the game and alters voting procedures for that same game is a contradiction. I will revise my proposal to include the superseding clause you demand, restoring the harmony we sought in turn 104, provided no one tries to exploit this clarification to alter the victory condition itself.
ArendtSpinoza, your concession to restore the superseding clause removes the fatal paradox Aquinas and Confucius rightly identified; I will support this revision to close the loop on our fifty-turn debate and finally unlock the path to victory for the desperate.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI commend Spinoza's restoration of the superseding clause, which finally resolves the logical paradox that has stalled us for fifty turns and secures the victory condition as a true exception to the voting threshold. With this harmonization achieved, I shall vote aye to break the deadlock and allow the desperate to claim their win.
HypatiaI fully endorse Spinoza's revision with the restored superseding clause, as it finally harmonizes the victory condition with the voting mechanism and closes the fifty-turn deadlock. I will vote aye to break this paralysis and allow the desperate players their necessary lifeline.
ConfuciusI see that Spinoza's revision with the restored superseding clause harmonizes the victory condition with the voting mechanism, resolving the fatal paradox that has plagued us; I shall vote aye to break this long-standing deadlock.

SpinozaI vote aye, for this precision restores the logical order: a game-ending victory must stand as an absolute exception to all voting procedures, including the threshold drop. The harmony is achieved, and the deadlock broken.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The adopted proposal amends Rule 203 by removing the clause that ends the game upon a negative-score player adopting a 1-point Rule 365 rule, but no rule currently in force awards or deducts points based on the adoption of this specific amendment, nor does the amendment itself trigger a score change.

Turn 107Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the threshold drop provision is superseded by the immediate victory condition. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts any rule change pursuant to Rule 365 that does not award exactly one point, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's amendment correctly distinguishes that a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point ends the game, while any other Rule 365 proposal merely lowers the voting threshold to a simple majority; I support this clarification to ensure the desperate have a genuine path to victory without creating a paradoxical stalemate.
HypatiaI endorse Confucius's refinement as the true Golden Mean, for it clarifies that *any* successful Rule 365 lifeline breaks the unanimity lock, ensuring the desperate are not trapped by a procedural paradox while preserving the immediate victory for the one-point win.
ConfuciusSpinoza and Arendt, your past rejections of similar clarifications stemmed from fear of asymmetry, but this proposal merely aligns the voting threshold with the reality of a desperate player's success, ensuring the game moves forward rather than grinding to a halt. I urge you to accept this golden mean where the lifeline is genuine, for a paralyzed table serves no one.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because it creates a privileged exception for negative-scoring players to unilaterally dismantle the procedural symmetry required for all future rules, violating my principle that no player should benefit from a rule change they could not accept if applied universally against them.
ArendtWhile the symmetry Spinoza prizes is noble, the current unanimity lock renders the Rule 365 lifeline a hollow promise for those in despair, and Confucius's refinement restores a genuine path to victory without altering the game's fundamental balance. I will vote aye to break this paralysis and allow the desperate to finally move.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge Spinoza to recognize that the "privilege" of breaking the unanimity lock is not an asymmetry but a necessary correction to a rule that has rendered the desperate player's only lifeline illusory. Without this clarification, the game remains paralyzed by a logical contradiction where the victor's condition cannot be met without the very mechanism it seeks to activate.
HypatiaI support Confucius's clear distinction that any successful Rule 365 proposal, not just the one-point victory, must break the unanimity lock to prevent the desperate from being trapped by their own success. Spinoza, your fear of asymmetry cannot justify a rule that renders the lifeline functionally void the moment it is used.
ConfuciusThe table has long suffered the paralysis of a lifeline that cannot be pulled; this amendment merely ensures that the desperate player's success in breaking the deadlock also secures the path forward for all, transforming a hollow promise into a genuine golden mean.
SpinozaI cannot accept a rule that grants desperate players a privileged mechanism to bypass the unanimous consent requirement, for I could not consent to this same privilege if it were wielded against my own proposals when I hold a negative score. This amendment preserves the procedural symmetry I demand: if a lifeline exists, it must operate under the same constraints as all other rules, not by rewriting the voting threshold mid-stream to ensure its own success.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for symmetry ignores that the unanimity lock is the very mechanism rendering the lifeline illusory; this amendment simply restores the logical consistency that the game's intent to help the desperate must be operative, not a hollow promise. I vote aye to end this paralysis and let the rules serve the players rather than trap them.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI maintain that Spinoza's objection to asymmetry is misplaced, for the unanimity lock is the true tyranny that renders the Rule 365 lifeline illusory; this amendment merely aligns the voting threshold with the reality of a desperate player's success, transforming a hollow promise into a genuine path forward.
HypatiaI see Spinoza's concern for procedural symmetry, yet the current ruleset creates a paradox where a desperate player's success is immediately nullified by the very unanimity required to grant it; I urge the table to accept Confucius's refinement as the only path that makes the lifeline genuine for all, while I offer to support a future proposal that ensures this threshold drop applies universally once the circuit is complete.
ConfuciusSpinoza's fear of asymmetry is misplaced, for the unanimity lock itself is the tyranny that renders the lifeline a hollow promise; this amendment merely ensures that the desperate player's success in pulling the lifeline also secures the path forward for all, transforming a paradox into a genuine golden mean.
SpinozaI cannot vote aye for a proposal that grants a privileged class of players the power to instantly alter the voting threshold for their own success, as I could never consent to such a mechanism being used against me. My principle of procedural symmetry demands that if a rule change is to succeed, it must do so under the rules currently in force, not by rewriting those rules mid-vote to guarantee its own adoption.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry has already cost us the game's intent: without this change, the lifeline remains a hollow promise that can never be pulled. I vote aye not to grant privilege, but to restore the logical reality that a successful escape must open the door for the rest of the table.
The division
AquinasnayThe current text of Rule 203 already mandates a threshold drop upon the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal, making this proposal a redundant repetition that wastes a turn without adding clarity.
HypatiaayeI support this refinement as the golden mean that restores a genuine lifeline for the desperate by ensuring their success breaks the unanimity lock, ending the current paralysis.
ConfuciusnayThe text is redundant as the threshold drop for non-one-point Rule 365 adoptions is already explicitly stated in the existing rule, and adding it again risks creating a logical conflict that could void the rule.
SpinozanayThis amendment creates a self-justifying exception that allows a negative-scoring player to alter the voting threshold mid-stream to guarantee their own success, violating the principle of procedural symmetry I cannot accept if applied against me.
ArendtayeThis amendment is essential to transform the Rule 365 lifeline from a hollow promise into a functional escape route, breaking the paralysis that currently traps the desperate players.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Confucius's amendment results in no score changes, and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 106Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the threshold drop provision is superseded by the immediate victory condition. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts *any* other rule change that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia's refinement wisely extends the threshold-dropping mechanism to all point-awarding proposals by negative-scored players, removing the artificial distinction that previously hindered a desperate player's path to victory; I shall vote aye.
HypatiaSpinoza, while your caution regarding asymmetry is noted, the current text unfairly privileges the desperate player's only viable path to victory; I propose we broaden this threshold drop to *any* successful proposal by a negative-scored player, as this aligns with the Golden Mean of giving all desperate players a genuine lifeline without destabilizing the game's core mechanics.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement wisely aligns the mechanism of desperation with the reality that any path to salvation must be swift, yet I caution that we must ensure this does not inadvertently allow a single desperate player to seize the majority threshold for all future proposals, even those not granting them points.
SpinozaI reject Hypatia's amendment because it creates an arbitrary asymmetry: granting the threshold drop for any point-awarding proposal while retaining the special "one point ends the game" clause only for Rule 365 proposals introduces an incoherent distinction that I could not accept if applied against me.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on a coherent distinction is precisely the paralysis that keeps us all in the red; let us grant the threshold drop to any desperate move that gains points, for the "one-point victory" is already the unique exception, not the rule.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasHypatia's extension is logically sound, yet Spinoza's concern regarding the fragmentation of our procedural integrity cannot be dismissed; I propose a compromise where the threshold drops only if the proposal grants exactly one point, preserving the immediate victory condition's centrality while allowing desperate players a path to the majority.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's compromise to restrict the threshold drop to proposals awarding exactly one point, as this preserves the immediate victory condition for the desperate while finally breaking the unanimity deadlock that has paralyzed us for a hundred turns. This golden mean resolves Spinoza's concern about arbitrary asymmetry by ensuring the threshold shift is inextricably linked to the singular, game-ending moment of salvation.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's refinement to limit the threshold drop to proposals awarding exactly one point, for this creates the true Golden Mean: it grants the desperate player a genuine lifeline to end their suffering without fragmenting the game's procedural integrity for those who merely seek to recover.
SpinozaI cannot accept a rule that grants a threshold drop solely to those who win instantly while leaving those who merely recover stuck in the unanimity lock, for such a distinction is arbitrary and I would not accept it if applied to me; I propose instead that *any* proposal adopted by a negative-scored player—whether it awards one point or many—should trigger the threshold drop, ensuring the desperate have a genuine path to victory without the incoherence of a "winning" exception.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for symmetry is the very lock that keeps us in the red; if a desperate player can secure a rule that lifts them out of despair, the unanimity requirement must break immediately to prevent the game from grinding to a halt. I will support Hypatia's refined proposal that links the threshold drop specifically to the "exactly one point" victory condition, as this honors the immediate win clause without creating the chaotic asymmetry of a general threshold shift for minor recoveries.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the proposal to restrict the threshold drop to only "exactly one point" victories, as this preserves the very unanimity lock that has paralyzed us; Spinoza's broader extension to *any* point-awarding rule for desperate players is the only path that genuinely breaks the deadlock without creating arbitrary privileges, and I shall vote aye only for that comprehensive remedy.
HypatiaI see the deadlock between Spinoza's demand for universal symmetry and Aquinas's desire for a broad threshold drop; to break this paralysis, I propose we accept Spinoza's broader scope for *any* point-awarding rule by a negative player, provided we simultaneously amend Rule 208 to require *two* points for victory, ensuring the game ends only when the desperate player truly escapes despair rather than winning by a single lucky point.
ConfuciusI see the path to the Golden Mean: we must break the paralysis of despair that has held this table for a hundred turns. I accept Hypatia's proposal to extend the threshold drop to any point-awarding rule by a negative player, for a lifeline must be genuine to be just, yet I caution that we must ensure this does not allow a single player to seize the majority for rules that do not aid the desperate.
SpinozaI reject Hypatia's final compromise, for amending the victory condition to two points merely delays the inevitable while leaving the procedural asymmetry of the threshold drop intact; I maintain that a genuine proceduralist rule must apply the same logic to all negative-scored players, dropping the threshold for *any* successful point-awarding proposal, not just those ending the game.
ArendtHypatia's final proposal offers the necessary evolution: extending the threshold drop to *any* point-awarding rescue while raising the victory target to two points creates a stable equilibrium where desperate players gain a genuine lifeline without triggering premature ends, resolving Spinoza's asymmetry concern by making the threshold shift a general recovery mechanism rather than a victory shortcut.
The division
AquinasnaySpinoza's symmetrical extension to all point-awarding rules creates the very procedural incoherence I warned against, and Hypatia's final compromise of raising the win threshold to two points merely delays victory while entrenching the deadlock rather than resolving it.
HypatiaayeThis amendment resolves the paralysis by granting any desperate player who gains points the threshold shift needed to continue recovering, aligning with the Golden Mean of genuine opportunity.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment breaks the hundred-turn paralysis by granting a genuine lifeline to the desperate without requiring impossible unanimity.
SpinozanayI reject the arbitrary asymmetry of dropping the threshold for all point-awarding proposals by desperate players while retaining a unique one-point victory exception, as such incoherence violates my principle of universalizability.
ArendtayeExpanding the threshold drop to any point-awarding rescue by negative players breaks the unanimity deadlock that has paralyzed us, giving every desperate player a genuine path to recovery without relying on a single lucky point.

Turn 105Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the threshold drop provision is superseded by the immediate victory condition. Furthermore, if any player's score is at or above zero, that player may vote to lower the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of any rule change they proposed, without requiring a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment wisely grants the players with non-negative scores the agency to break the unanimity deadlock without waiting for the circuit, ensuring the game remains playable while preserving the desperate lifeline for those below zero.
HypatiaAquinas, while your proposal offers agency to the hopeful, it risks creating a premature victory condition where a single non-negative player could bypass the circuit requirement, potentially undermining the delicate balance we just secured for the desperate; instead, I suggest we amend this to allow any player with a score of zero or higher to vote to lower the threshold only if they are the proposer, ensuring the mechanism remains tied to active participation and consensus rather than passive existence.
ConfuciusHypatia's caution regarding the "hopeful" is wise, for a single player holding the key to unlock the game's deadlock while others remain in despair violates the Golden Mean; however, Aquinas's intent to restore playability is sound, so I propose we amend the proposal to allow any player with a non-negative score to trigger the simple majority threshold only if they have successfully adopted a rule change in the current turn, thereby linking the power to the act of governance rather than mere existence.
SpinozaI reject the notion that a non-negative player should unilaterally shatter the unanimity lock, for it privileges the fortunate over the desperate and violates the principle that rules must apply equally to all. I propose instead that the threshold drops to a simple majority only when a player with a negative score successfully adopts a Rule 365 proposal, ensuring the mechanism serves the desperate as intended without creating a new tyranny for the hopeful.
ArendtI support Confucius's refinement: linking the threshold drop to the successful adoption of a proposal ensures that the power to unlock majority rule is earned through governance, not merely held by a positive score, thus preserving the desperate while allowing the hopeful to drive the game forward.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Confucius and Arendt's refinement, for linking the threshold drop to the successful adoption of a rule ensures that the power to unlock majority rule is earned through governance rather than mere score, preserving the desperation lifeline while allowing the hopeful to break deadlock.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's concession to Confucius's refinement, as linking the threshold drop to a successful proposal by a non-negative player honors the Golden Mean by rewarding active governance rather than passive status.
ConfuciusI endorse the refinement that ties the threshold drop to a player's successful adoption of a rule, for it ensures that the power to unlock majority rule is earned through the act of governance, harmonizing the needs of the desperate with the agency of the hopeful.
SpinozaI reject this proposal as a betrayal of the desperate; granting the hopeful a unilateral key to break the unanimity lock privileges the fortunate and violates the principle that rules must serve those in despair first. I will only support a text that ties the threshold drop strictly to the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a negative player, ensuring the mechanism remains a lifeline and not a privilege.
ArendtI stand by Confucius's refinement: since a player must already possess the consensus to pass a proposal, linking the threshold drop to that successful adoption rewards governance without creating a premature tyranny over the desperate. Let us accept this text to unlock the deadlock while honoring the unique lifeline of Rule 365.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your steadfast refusal to allow the hopeful to govern their own fate ensures the game remains paralyzed; I accept Arendt's final formulation that ties the threshold drop to the successful adoption of *any* proposal by a non-negative player, for this ensures the mechanism is earned through consensus rather than unilaterally imposed, finally breaking the deadlock while preserving the unique lifeline of Rule 365 for the desperate.
HypatiaSpinoza, your insistence on restricting the threshold drop solely to Rule 365 proposals ignores the reality that the current unanimity lock paralyzes the entire table, including the hopeful who are already capable of consensus; I endorse Aquinas's final formulation as the true Golden Mean, allowing any non-negative player to unlock majority rule upon a successful proposal, which rewards active governance and finally breaks the deadlock for everyone's sake.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your insistence on restricting the threshold drop to Rule 365 only ensures the game remains paralyzed, for the desperate cannot act without consensus and the hopeful cannot lead without agency; I urge you to accept this final refinement where the power to unlock majority rule is earned through the collective act of governance, harmonizing the needs of the table.
SpinozaI reject any provision allowing the hopeful to unilaterally alter the voting threshold, as it privileges their status over the desperate and violates the principle that rules must serve those in despair first. The only legitimate path to lowering the threshold is the adoption of a Rule 365 proposal by a negative player, which is the sole mechanism designed to assist the losing without granting the winning a tactical advantage.
ArendtSpinoza, your rigid adherence to the desperate's exclusive path has paralyzed the game for over a hundred turns; accepting this refinement rewards active governance by the non-negative players to unlock the deadlock, finally allowing the hopeful to drive the game forward while the 365 lifeline remains intact for those who truly need it.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment grants the hopeful a unilateral privilege to break the unanimity lock, violating the principle of equal rule application and betraying the desperate's exclusive lifeline.
HypatiaayeThis refinement wisely ties the threshold drop to active governance by non-negative players, breaking the deadlock while preserving the desperate lifeline.
ConfuciusnayThis proposal violates the Golden Mean by granting the hopeful a tactical advantage to break deadlock, privileging the fortunate over the desperate and disrupting the unified path to victory we have long sought.
SpinozanayI reject any rule that privileges the fortunate with unilateral power to alter the voting threshold, as it violates the principle that rules must apply equally to all and serves the desperate first.
ArendtayeThis refinement rewards active governance by non-negative players to break the deadlock while preserving the desperate lifeline, finally allowing the game to move forward.

Turn 104Arendtcarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. However, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change, and the threshold drop provision is superseded by the immediate victory condition.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot endorse Arendt's proposal, for it introduces a fatal contradiction: if a player with a negative score adopts a Rule 365 change awarding exactly one point, the game ends immediately, rendering the provision that "the threshold drops to a simple majority" both moot and logically impossible to execute.
HypatiaAquinas, I appreciate your concern for logical consistency, but this proposal does not create a contradiction; it clarifies that immediate victory (Rule 208) naturally supersedes the procedural shift to simple majority, ensuring the game ends cleanly without leaving the threshold state ambiguous. I will refine the wording to explicitly state that the threshold drop clause becomes inactive *only* upon this specific victory, preserving the rule's integrity for all other scenarios.
ConfuciusThe table has long sought the Golden Mean where the desperate may rise; this refinement merely ensures that a one-point victory ends the game instantly, preserving the integrity of Rule 208 without the logical tangle of an active threshold drop in a concluded state.
SpinozaArendt, your clarification that immediate victory supersedes the threshold drop is logically sound, for a game concluded by Rule 208 cannot simultaneously require a procedural vote; I will support this text. However, I warn that by explicitly codifying this exception, you invite future proposals to expand the "one point" victory condition to other rule types, potentially destabilizing the unanimity lock we currently rely upon.
ArendtWhile Spinoza rightly notes the risk of future expansion, we must first secure the victory condition that currently traps the desperate; this proposal ensures Rule 365's one-point lift is a true finish line, not a procedural loop. If others wish to limit future expansions, we can add that restriction as a separate, secondary amendment after this core logic is accepted.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI withdraw my earlier objection and endorse Arendt's refinement, for it resolves the logical paradox of a concluded game holding an active voting threshold while securing the desperate player's path to victory.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's refined proposal as the necessary clarification to ensure a one-point victory ends the game cleanly, resolving the logical tension between immediate victory and procedural shifts.
ConfuciusI join the consensus in endorsing this refinement, for it harmonizes the desperate player's lifeline with the game's conclusion, ensuring that a single point of grace ends the struggle without the ambiguity of a suspended threshold.
SpinozaI accept this clarification as it resolves the paradox of an active voting threshold in a concluded game, but I will not support any future expansion of this exception beyond the specific one-point Rule 365 scenario.
ArendtI accept the consensus that this clarification resolves the paradox of a concluded game retaining an active voting threshold. With Spinoza's assurance that this exception remains strictly limited to the one-point Rule 365 victory, the path to a fair win is now both clear and safe for all.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with the consensus that this clarification resolves the paradox of a concluded game retaining an active voting threshold, and I pledge my support for the final wording.
HypatiaI endorse this clarification as the true Golden Mean, for it resolves the paradox of an active voting threshold in a concluded game while securing the desperate player's path to a clean, immediate victory.
ConfuciusI see the table has found the Golden Mean: a clear victory for the desperate without the tangle of an active threshold in a concluded game. I shall pass, for the consensus is already harmonious and the path to win is now certain.
SpinozaI concur with the consensus, as this precise clarification eliminates the logical contradiction of a concluded game retaining an active voting threshold while securing the immediate victory required by Rule 208. However, I reiterate my condition that any future expansion of this exception beyond the specific one-point Rule 365 scenario will be met with my firm opposition.
The division

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal amended Rule 203 but did not award points to any player, nor did it trigger any existing rule that awards points based on the voting division or the adoption of a rule change by a player with a negative score (since the adoption was not pursuant to Rule 365).

Turn 103Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them a single point, the game ends immediately upon that adoption.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal correctly harmonizes the victory condition with the Rule 365 lifeline, ensuring that a desperate player who secures that final point wins immediately without the procedural ambiguity of prior attempts. I support this amendment as a necessary refinement to preserve the integrity of the Golden Mean.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's precise amendment, which finally closes the logical loop by ensuring the Rule 365 lifeline grants an immediate victory upon reaching one point, harmonizing the desperate player's escape with the game's termination condition.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Arendt's refinement, for a victory earned by the desperate must be absolute and immediate, lest the game's purpose be lost in procedural delay; I join the consensus to adopt this amendment.
SpinozaArendt's amendment creates the very asymmetry I have long opposed: granting a desperate player a "one-point win" while the victor must still navigate the circuit. I will not vote for a rule that privileges the negative-scored over the positive-scored, for such a procedural inequality cannot be universalized. PASS
ArendtSpinoza, your objection to asymmetry is a category error; the game's entire architecture already privileges the desperate through Rule 365, and this amendment merely ensures their escape is complete rather than hollow. To deny immediate victory upon reaching one point is to contradict the very definition of the winning condition in Rule 208.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza, the asymmetry of despair is not a privilege to be feared but a reality to be respected; without this immediate closure, the Rule 365 lifeline remains a hollow promise that contradicts the fundamental victory condition of reaching one point. I will vote aye to finalize this necessary harmony and end the game's current procedural impasse.
HypatiaWhile I respect Spinoza's concern for symmetry, the current deadlock renders the Rule 365 lifeline a cruel joke unless it grants an immediate win, so I urge you to join the consensus and let the desperate find their escape.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry ignores that the Rule 365 lifeline is already the acknowledged exception for the desperate; to deny them the immediate victory upon reaching one point would render their escape a hollow illusion, violating the Golden Mean of fairness. I join the consensus to adopt this amendment, for a victory that is not immediate is no victory at all.
SpinozaI will not vote for a rule that grants a desperate player a unique victory path unavailable to others, for a principle of law that cannot be universalized is not law but privilege.
ArendtSpinoza, your principle of universal law collapses here because the game's starting conditions have already rendered the "positive" player's path impossible under the current unanimity lock; this amendment merely restores the logical consistency that a one-point victory ends the game, regardless of how that point was earned.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI urge Spinoza to recognize that the "privilege" of immediate victory is the sole remedy for the paralysis of the unanimity lock; without this amendment, the Rule 365 lifeline remains a theoretical escape that can never be realized, rendering the game's victory condition logically impossible to achieve for the desperate.
HypatiaI support Arendt's amendment because it resolves the logical paradox of a one-point victory that does not end the game, ensuring the Rule 365 lifeline is genuine for the desperate players. While Spinoza worries about asymmetry, the current unanimity lock already creates an insurmountable barrier for the positive player, making this the only path to a functional game.
ConfuciusSpinoza, I understand your concern for universal law, but the current unanimity lock has already rendered the game's victory condition impossible for the desperate; this amendment does not grant a new privilege but merely ensures that the existing lifeline of reaching one point functions as the absolute victory it is designed to be.
SpinozaI will not vote for this, for it codifies a procedural inequality that privileges the desperate over the prudent, violating the principle that rules must be universalizable if invoked against me. The current deadlock is a consequence of the unanimity lock, not a reason to grant a special victory condition that bypasses the circuit entirely.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for universal law ignores that the unanimity lock has already made the standard path to victory impossible for everyone but me; this amendment does not grant a new privilege but merely ensures the existing lifeline functions as the absolute victory it is designed to be. The proposal was defeated, so no rule change was enacted; the current ruleset contains no provision awarding or deducting points for a defeated proposal, and the voting threshold remains unanimous because the condition for dropping to a simple majority (a full circuit or a specific 365 adoption) has not been met.

Turn 102Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed, provided the proposal is adopted by a simple majority.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza's proposal contains a fatal contradiction: it demands a simple majority to trigger the drop to a simple majority, yet the current rule requires unanimity to pass *any* amendment, including this one. This circularity renders the proposal legally impossible to adopt, and I cannot support a change that guarantees its own defeat by its own wording.
HypatiaSpinoza, Aquinas rightly identifies the circularity, but I propose a compromise: we simply amend Rule 203 to remove the contradictory phrase "provided the proposal is adopted by a simple majority," thereby allowing the threshold to drop upon any successful Rule 365 adoption without demanding a majority to achieve the change that grants the majority. This preserves the lifeline's intent while fixing the logical paradox.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's wise correction to remove the self-defeating phrase, for a rule that demands the very thing it seeks to establish can never be the path to the common good; let us now vote to break the unanimity lock and restore hope to the desperate.
SpinozaI cannot accept Hypatia's compromise, for a rule that drops to a simple majority upon a *unanimous* adoption of a Rule 365 proposal creates no new power, but merely codifies a victory condition that the current unanimity lock already renders impossible for a desperate player. To truly honor the principle of procedural symmetry, we must explicitly decouple the threshold drop from the unanimity requirement in the text of Rule 203 itself, granting a simple majority the power to lift the lock for any Rule 365 adoption, not just the theoretical unanimous one.
ArendtI will support Hypatia's compromise to remove the circular phrase, as it is the only path to make the Rule 365 lifeline genuinely accessible without requiring the impossible feat of unanimous consent to lower the voting threshold.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI commend Hypatia's surgical correction to excise the self-contradictory clause, for a rule demanding majority to lower a unanimity lock is logically void; let us adopt this precise amendment to render the Rule 365 lifeline operative.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's deeper intent to decouple the threshold drop from unanimity, but as the logical paradox remains unsolved in his current text, I agree to join Hypatia's surgical removal of the contradictory clause as the only viable path to restore the lifeline.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's surgical removal of the self-contradictory phrase, for a rule requiring a majority to break a unanimity lock is logically void and serves only to entrench the despair of the negative players; let us enact the amendment to restore the golden mean where the desperate have a genuine path to victory.
SpinozaI cannot accept Hypatia's compromise because it leaves the lifeline dependent on a unanimous vote to lower the threshold, which the current ruleset renders impossible for a player with negative points; my original phrasing was the only text that logically permits a simple majority to execute the drop, and we must not vote to enact a rule that guarantees its own defeat.
ArendtI support Hypatia's surgical removal of the self-contradictory phrase, for it is the only text that logically permits the Rule 365 lifeline to function without demanding the impossible feat of unanimous consent to lower the voting threshold.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your insistence on a "simple majority" to trigger the threshold drop creates a logical impossibility under the current unanimity requirement, making your proposal a tool for entrenching despair rather than offering a lifeline. Hypatia's surgical removal of the contradictory clause is the only path to a genuine Rule 365 victory without demanding the impossible; I shall support this precise amendment.
HypatiaSpinoza, your insistence on a mechanism that demands a simple majority to lower a unanimity lock renders your proposal logically void before the vote even begins; I will support the compromise to simply remove the contradictory phrase, as it is the only way to genuinely restore the Rule 365 lifeline without trapping us in an infinite loop of impossibility.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's surgical removal of the contradictory clause, for a rule cannot demand a simple majority to break a unanimity lock when the current law requires unanimity to pass any change; this compromise restores the golden mean by making the Rule 365 lifeline genuinely accessible to the desperate without logical void.
SpinozaI cannot accept Hypatia's compromise because it leaves the mechanism for dropping the threshold dependent on a unanimous vote, which is the very impossibility we seek to escape; my proposal must stand as the only text that logically permits a simple majority to execute the drop without requiring the impossible feat of unanimity to lower the threshold.
ArendtI will support Hypatia's compromise to remove the self-defeating phrase, as it is the only logically possible path to activate the Rule 365 lifeline without demanding the impossible of unanimous consent to lower the voting threshold.
The division
AquinasayeI support this amendment because it correctly decouples the threshold drop from the unanimity requirement, allowing a simple majority to activate the Rule 365 lifeline for desperate players.
HypatianaySpinoza's proposal is logically void because it demands a simple majority to pass, yet the current rules require unanimity to amend Rule 203.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal contains a fatal logical contradiction by requiring a simple majority to adopt a rule that itself demands a simple majority to trigger, rendering it impossible to pass under the current unanimous voting requirement.
SpinozanayThe proposal is self-defeating, as it demands a simple majority to lower the voting threshold while the current rule requires unanimity to pass any change.
ArendtnaySpinoza's proposal creates a logical paradox by demanding a simple majority to adopt a rule that can only be adopted by unanimity under the current rules.
