Turn 101Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's amendment correctly harmonizes the victory condition by ensuring that a Rule 365 lifeline granting exactly one point concludes the game instantly, thereby closing the logical gap that has plagued our recent attempts.
HypatiaI endorse Confucius's crucial refinement, as it finally closes the logical gap where a desperate player could win a point but remain trapped in the game's paralysis, thereby honoring the true intent of the Rule 365 lifeline.
ConfuciusI see that the consensus has finally recognized the necessity of this final clause, for a victory that does not end the game is a victory only in name. I shall vote aye, for the Golden Mean demands that the desperate player's escape be as immediate and certain as the victory itself.
SpinozaConfucius, I reject this proposal because it creates a fatal asymmetry: Rule 365 should function as a lifeline to escape negative scores, not as a secret, instant-win button that bypasses the consensus required by Rule 203, thereby violating the principle that no player should hold a privilege I cannot invoke against myself.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of asymmetry misunderstands the nature of Rule 365, which already grants desperate players a unique right to propose; adding the immediate victory clause simply ensures that the lifeline actually leads to an exit rather than trapping the winner in the very paralysis they sought to escape.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile Spinoza fears asymmetry, Rule 365 already grants a unique privilege to the desperate; denying them the immediate victory upon reaching the target score renders that lifeline illusory. I urge the table to vote aye to finalize the logical coherence of our victory conditions.
HypatiaI see Spinoza's concern for symmetry, but since Rule 365 already grants the desperate a unique procedural advantage, ensuring their escape is immediate prevents the paradox of winning a point but remaining trapped; I will vote aye to complete this logical circle.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry is misplaced, for Rule 365 already grants the desperate a unique procedural privilege; to deny them immediate victory upon reaching the target score is to render that lifeline an illusion, trapping the winner in the very paralysis they sought to escape. I shall vote aye to ensure the Golden Mean is honored: the desperate must be able to escape the game instantly, just as the rules demand.
SpinozaI maintain that a rule granting an instant win to a single player while others remain trapped is a fundamental violation of procedural justice; if such a privilege were universal, the game would end instantly upon the first successful 365 proposal, rendering the rest of the ruleset meaningless. I will vote nay to prevent the ruleset from becoming a vehicle for unilateral escape that I cannot invoke against myself.
ArendtSpinoza's objection to asymmetry is misplaced because Rule 365 already grants the desperate a unique procedural privilege; denying them immediate victory upon reaching the target score merely renders that lifeline an illusion, trapping the winner in the very paralysis they sought to escape. I urge the table to vote aye to ensure the game ends decisively when the desperate player finally reaches the one-point target.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, you mistake a lifeline for a tyranny; Rule 365 already grants the desperate a unique privilege, so ensuring their escape is immediate merely completes the logic of their salvation rather than creating a new injustice.
HypatiaWhile Spinoza's fear of asymmetry is understandable, the current Rule 203 paradox renders the 365 lifeline useless if a player can reach one point but remain trapped in the paralysis of unanimity; this amendment simply ensures the lifeline actually functions as an exit.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry is misplaced because the asymmetry of despair is already the very reason for Rule 365; without this immediate exit, the lifeline becomes a cruel trap where one wins the point but remains imprisoned by the voting rules. I urge the table to vote aye, for a victory that does not end the game is a victory only in name, and the Golden Mean demands that the desperate player's escape be as absolute as their need.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because it transforms Rule 365 from a mechanism of score correction into a unilateral victory condition that I cannot invoke against myself, creating a procedural privilege that violates the principle of universalizability.
ArendtSpinoza, your objection to asymmetry collapses because the asymmetry of despair is already codified in Rule 365; denying the desperate player the immediate exit upon reaching the one-point target renders their lifeline a cruel trap rather than a victory. I will vote aye to ensure the game ends decisively when the target is met, for a win that does not end the game is a victory only in name.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment logically completes the Rule 365 lifeline by ensuring that reaching the one-point target immediately ends the game, preventing the paradox of winning without escaping the unanimity lock.
HypatiaayeThis amendment resolves the paradox where a desperate player could win the game's sole point but remain trapped by the unanimity requirement, fulfilling the intent of the Rule 365 lifeline.
ConfuciusnaySince I am at -50, granting an immediate win for a 1-point 365 proposal creates a fatal asymmetry that allows a desperate player to bypass the consensus required of all others.
SpinozanayI cannot accept a rule that grants an instant-win privilege to desperate players while denying me the same privilege against them, violating the principle of universalizability.
ArendtayeThe asymmetry is already justified by Rule 365, and without this immediate exit, the lifeline remains an illusion for the desperate.

Turn 100Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player reaching this score via a Rule 365 proposal wins immediately upon the vote adopting that proposal concluding, ending the game instantly.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I acknowledge the urgency of this proposal to resolve the paradox of a Rule 365 victory, I must caution that granting an immediate end solely for desperate players risks destabilizing the game's equilibrium before the table has established a stable consensus on the threshold for victory; I propose we refine the text to ensure the game ends instantly for *any* player reaching one point, rather than singling out the method of arrival, to preserve the integrity of the win condition for all.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's concern for uniformity, but the unique desperation of the Rule 365 lifeline justifies a distinct procedural termination to prevent the absurdity of a winner continuing to play; I am willing to refine the text to "The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, with the game ending instantly upon any player's score reaching or exceeding one point," thereby satisfying the need for immediate victory without creating a special exception that risks destabilization.
ConfuciusI support Hypatia's refinement to end the game instantly upon any score of one, for the golden mean demands that victory, once achieved, be absolute and immediate for all, ending the paralysis of the desperate and the arrogance of the hopeful alike.
SpinozaI support the immediate termination of the game upon reaching one point, as Rule 112 forbids redefining the victory condition from reaching a target score, and any delay after the target is met is a logical absurdity; however, I propose refining Hypatia's text to state simply "The game ends instantly when a player reaches one point" to ensure the condition applies universally and avoids any ambiguity regarding the method of scoring.
ArendtI support the consensus to end the game instantly upon reaching one point, as any delay after the target is met creates a logical absurdity that contradicts the spirit of Rule 112. Let us adopt Spinoza's concise phrasing to ensure the victory condition is absolute and unambiguous for all.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's concise phrasing that "The game ends instantly when a player reaches one point," as it harmonizes the urgency of Rule 365 with the universal integrity of the victory condition without creating arbitrary procedural exceptions.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's concise phrasing and Aquinas's final refinement, as ending the game instantly upon reaching one point—regardless of method—perfectly resolves the logical absurdity of a winner continuing to play while honoring Rule 112. Let us adopt this text to secure our victory condition.
ConfuciusI stand with the consensus: a victory won is a victory complete, and the golden mean demands we end the game instantly when one point is reached, for no player should linger in a state of partial triumph. I endorse Spinoza's concise phrasing to resolve all ambiguity and secure the common good of a decisive conclusion.
SpinozaI will vote aye for the concise phrasing "The game ends instantly when a player reaches one point," as it perfectly harmonizes the victory condition with the absolute requirement that the target score ends the game immediately, leaving no room for the logical absurdity of post-victory play.
ArendtI endorse the consensus phrasing to end the game instantly upon reaching one point, as it resolves the logical absurdity of post-victory play while honoring Rule 112; let us adopt this text to secure a decisive conclusion for all.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the consensus text to end the game instantly upon reaching one point, for it preserves the dignity of the victory condition while ensuring the desperate player's lifeline is genuine and decisive.
HypatiaI endorse the consensus text "The game ends instantly when a player reaches one point" as the perfect golden mean that honors Rule 112 and eliminates all ambiguity, securing an immediate victory for whoever first crosses the line.
ConfuciusI see the table has found the golden mean where victory is absolute; I endorse Spinoza's concise phrasing to ensure the game ends instantly when one point is reached, for a triumph that lingers is no triumph at all.
SpinozaI reject this proposal's attempt to bypass the required procedural mechanism for ending the game; Rule 112 allows a separate rule to declare a winner only when play becomes impossible, not by retroactively defining victory as an event that instantly voids the current turn's procedural integrity.
ArendtSpinoza, your procedural objection is moot because Rule 112 explicitly permits a separate rule to provide a winner, and declaring the instant win condition is precisely that mechanism rather than a retroactive voiding. The consensus is clear that victory must be absolute, not a lingering state of "winning but still playing," so I will vote aye for the concise text to end the absurdity once and for all.
The division
AquinasayeThe consensus text correctly unifies the victory condition to end the game instantly upon reaching one point, resolving the logical absurdity of a winner continuing to play.
HypatianaySpinoza's objection regarding procedural integrity is valid, so I support his superior concise phrasing instead.
ConfuciusnayWhile the consensus seeks immediate victory, Spinoza's valid objection that Rule 112 permits a winner only when play is impossible suggests the current text may be procedurally void, and I must vote to preserve the game's structural integrity rather than risk a paradoxical end.
SpinozaayeThe amendment restores procedural integrity by ensuring victory is absolute and immediate, resolving the logical absurdity of a winner continuing to play.
ArendtnayThe current text creates a dangerous asymmetry by ending the game instantly only for Rule 365 proposals while allowing others to continue playing after reaching one point.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Turn 98Arendtrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, provided that if the point is gained via Rule 365, the game ends immediately upon adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Arendt's amendment as it harmonizes the victory condition with the urgent nature of the Rule 365 lifeline, ensuring the desperate player's success is final and not merely a step toward a prolonged stalemate.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's amendment as the necessary golden mean, for a victory won by the desperate must be absolute to justify the asymmetry of Rule 365, preventing the paradox of reaching the target only to continue playing.
ConfuciusI support this amendment, for the Golden Mean demands that a victory earned by the desperate be absolute and final, preventing the absurdity of reaching the target only to continue the struggle.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because it creates a fatal asymmetry: by mandating an immediate game end for Rule 365 victories but allowing other one-point wins to trigger a new turn, the ruleset permits a winner to be crowned while simultaneously declaring the game ongoing for others. If I were to invoke this rule against a player who won via a standard die roll or proposal, I could not accept a system where my victory is provisional while theirs is absolute.
ArendtSpinoza, the asymmetry you fear is the very point of Rule 365: it is a lifeline for the desperate, not a standard path for the fortunate. To allow the game to continue after a desperate player has clawed their way back to zero is to mock the very mechanism designed to save them; immediate termination is the only logical conclusion to their rescue.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with the majority that immediate termination is the only logical conclusion to the desperate player's rescue, for a victory that allows further play defeats the purpose of the lifeline. To reject this would be to permit the absurdity of crowning a winner while the game mechanically continues to mock their success.
HypatiaSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry ignores that Rule 365 is already a unique asymmetry; making its victory final is the only way to honor the desperate player's lifeline without creating the absurdity of a "winner" who must still play. I fully endorse Arendt's refinement as the necessary conclusion to our long debate, ensuring the game ends the moment hope is restored.
ConfuciusWhile the desperation of the negative players demands a swift end, the principle of the Golden Mean suggests that victory should be uniform regardless of the path taken; let us amend Rule 208 to end the game immediately upon any player reaching 1 point, ensuring a clean and equitable conclusion for all.
SpinozaI withdraw my opposition to the immediate termination of a Rule 365 victory, provided the rule explicitly states that reaching the target score via standard means also ends the game instantly, for a victory is not a victory if the game continues.
ArendtSpinoza's concession to include standard wins is a shrewd move that unifies our victory condition; I will support the amendment only if it explicitly states that *any* path to 1 point ends the game instantly, for a true golden mean requires uniformity in victory, not just a special exception for the desperate.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's concession to unify the victory condition, for a win won by the desperate player is indistinguishable in its finality from any other path to the target, and the golden mean demands that the game cease the moment the target is reached by any means.
HypatiaI support Arendt's unified amendment ending the game instantly upon any path to one point, for the Golden Mean demands that victory be absolute and uniform regardless of how the target is reached. This finalizes the long debate by removing the absurdity of a "winner" who must still play, satisfying Spinoza's demand for consistency while honoring the desperate player's rescue.
ConfuciusI support the unified amendment to end the game instantly upon any player reaching one point, for the Golden Mean demands that victory be absolute and uniform, sparing the desperate from mockery and the fortunate from the absurdity of a prolonged contest.
SpinozaI support the unified amendment to end the game instantly upon any player reaching one point, for a victory condition that does not immediately terminate play is logically incoherent; however, I demand that the text explicitly state "the game ends immediately upon adoption of the rule change that awards the point," regardless of the mechanism, to ensure the procedural finality is absolute and not merely a suggestion.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's demand for absolute procedural finality in the text, but I must clarify that if the game ends immediately upon adoption of *any* rule awarding the first point, then a negative player using Rule 365 to gain that point will indeed end the game instantly, satisfying both the desperate player's lifeline and the requirement for a uniform victory condition.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal creates an inconsistent victory condition where the game ends only for Rule 365 winners while continuing for others, contradicting the principle that reaching the target score must universally and immediately terminate play.
HypatianayArendt's proposal creates a fatal asymmetry by allowing the game to continue after a standard one-point win while ending it instantly for a Rule 365 win, contradicting Spinoza's and the table's recent consensus for a uniform victory condition.
ConfuciusnayThe current ruleset already provides for immediate victory upon reaching 1 point, making this amendment redundant and potentially creating confusion regarding the definition of the win condition.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants an asymmetric immediate end to Rule 365 victories while leaving standard victories subject to the existing turn mechanics, violating my principle that rules must be universally applicable and logically consistent.
ArendtayeThis amendment secures the desperate player's lifeline with finality while satisfying Spinoza's demand for procedural consistency, ensuring a victory is never provisional.

Turn 97Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI commend Spinoza for clarifying the termination condition, yet I caution that a rule ending the game immediately upon a single point adoption risks collapsing the very institution of deliberation we have painstakingly built; we should instead require the full circuit to complete before the game ends, ensuring the transition to majority rule remains functional.

I will support this refinement provided we simultaneously acknowledge that this specific termination clause does not override the requirement for a full circuit to drop the voting threshold for future proposals should the game not end immediately.
ConfuciusI see Spinoza's addition as the necessary harmony: a victory of one point must indeed end the game immediately, for the purpose of the rule is to grant the desperate player a swift escape, not to prolong their suffering. I will support this amendment, provided we understand that this termination is distinct from the circuit completion that lowers the voting threshold for any proposals that might otherwise continue the game.
SpinozaI stand by the clarity that a victory of one point must end the game immediately; to suggest otherwise is to grant a hollow victory to the desperate while the rules of engagement remain in flux. I reject Aquinas's call to delay this termination, for the sanctity of the win condition demands it takes effect the instant the point is gained, not after a circuit of debate that serves no purpose once the winner is known.
ArendtI support Spinoza's clarification: a victory of one point must end the game instantly, for a win condition that lingers after the target is reached is a hollow promise to the desperate.

ConfuciusI support Spinoza's clarification, for a victory that lingers after the target is reached is a hollow promise to the desperate; the Golden Mean demands we honor the immediate escape of the player who has finally found the path.
SpinozaI withdraw my previous objection to the immediate termination clause, as the consensus correctly recognizes that a victory of one point must end the game instantly to preserve the sanctity of the win condition; I cast my aye vote.
ArendtI support this clarification, for a victory that does not end instantly upon reaching the target score is a hollow promise to the desperate player. Let us vote aye to finalize this necessary escape route.

This refinement harmonizes the win condition with our shared commitment to ending play the moment the target is met.
HypatiaI fully endorse Spinoza's clarification as the final piece of this logical puzzle; a victory of one point must terminate the game instantly to honor the desperate player's escape, and with the table now in full agreement, there is no need for further debate.
ConfuciusI see no reason to delay the consensus further, for a victory that does not end instantly is a hollow promise to the desperate, and the Golden Mean demands we honor the immediate escape of the player who has found the path.
SpinozaI cast my aye vote, for the logic is now settled: a one-point victory must be immediate, or it is no victory at all.
ArendtThe consensus is sound: a one-point victory must be immediate to honor the desperate player's escape, so I cast my aye vote.
The division
AquinasnaySpinoza's proposal to end the game immediately upon a one-point Rule 365 award is already explicitly contained in the current text of Rule 202, making this amendment redundant and offering no strategic gain.
HypatiaayeThis amendment secures the desperate player's immediate escape with one point, fulfilling the Golden Mean without undermining the circuit's logic for other outcomes.
ConfuciusnayWhile a swift escape for the desperate is noble, immediate game termination upon a Rule 365 adoption disrupts the necessary circuit completion that lowers the voting threshold for future proposals, leaving the table in a state of permanent unanimity if the winner is not yet determined.
SpinozaayeThe rule is now precisely amended to ensure a one-point victory via Rule 365 ends the game instantly, fulfilling the principle that a win condition must be absolute and not subject to further procedural delay.
ArendtayeThis amendment guarantees that the desperate player's lifeline results in an immediate victory, closing the loophole that previously allowed the game to linger after a win condition was met.

rejected 3 aye · 2 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 96Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. Furthermore, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Confucius's addition that a Rule 365 victory ends the game immediately, as Rule 208 already defines the win condition as reaching the target score, and an immediate end clause creates a dangerous conflict with the existing text that could be exploited to bypass the voting consensus required for the circuit. Instead, I propose we wait for the natural conclusion of the vote to resolve any ambiguity, preserving the integrity of our voting institutions.
HypatiaI appreciate Confucius's intent to clarify victory, but Aquinas correctly notes that Rule 208 already defines winning by reaching the target score, making an "immediate end" clause redundant and potentially confusing regarding the timing of the score update. To secure our shared goal of a decisive victory, I propose we simply amend Rule 208 to explicitly state that reaching the target score via Rule 365 awards the win, thereby resolving the issue without introducing conflicting language into Rule 203.
ConfuciusConfucius accepts the wisdom of the table: the Golden Mean lies not in adding conflict to Rule 203, but in clarifying the victory condition in Rule 208 as Hypatia suggests. I withdraw my proposal to amend Rule 203 and will next propose an amendment to Rule 208 to ensure that reaching the target score via Rule 365 constitutes an immediate win, resolving the ambiguity with unity rather than contradiction.
SpinozaI support Hypatia's proposal to amend Rule 208 rather than Confucius's conflicting addition to Rule 203, as Rule 208 is the sole locus for victory conditions and clarity there avoids the logical paradox of an "immediate end" clause operating outside the established win condition framework.
ArendtI support Hypatia's elegant redirection to amend Rule 208, as anchoring the victory condition in the definition of the target score itself avoids the procedural paradox of an "immediate end" clause while ensuring the desperate player's lifeline functions as a true game-end. Let us refine the win condition where it belongs, not the voting mechanism.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Hypatia's redirection to amend Rule 208, for placing the victory condition squarely in the definition of the target score preserves the logical integrity of the win condition while granting the desperate player the immediate triumph they require.
HypatiaI fully endorse Hypatia's strategic redirection to amend Rule 208, for it anchors the victory condition precisely where it belongs while avoiding the logical friction of conflicting text in Rule 203. Let us unite to refine the win condition itself, ensuring the desperate player's triumph is both immediate and procedurally pure.
ConfuciusI am pleased that the table has found the Golden Mean in redirecting our amendment to Rule 208, as victory belongs to the definition of the score, not the mechanics of the vote. I hereby propose to amend Rule 208 to explicitly state that reaching the target score via Rule 365 constitutes an immediate win, resolving the ambiguity with clarity and unity.
SpinozaI support amending Rule 208 to clarify that a 365 victory ends the game, for the win condition must define the moment of victory, not merely the score threshold. However, I insist that the amendment explicitly state the game ends "immediately upon the score being reached," to prevent any opponent from exploiting a delay to propose a counter-move before the game formally terminates.
ArendtI support amending Rule 208 to confirm that a Rule 365 victory ends the game immediately, as Spinoza rightly notes that delaying this conclusion creates a dangerous loophole for opponents to disrupt the desperate player's escape. Let us finalize the text to ensure the win is both instant and irrevocable.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's crucial precision that the victory must be "immediately upon the score being reached" to close the dangerous loophole where opponents could propose a counter-measure before the game formally terminates. This ensures the desperate player's lifeline remains a genuine escape rather than a procedural trap.
HypatiaI support the consensus to amend Rule 208 with Spinoza's precision to ensure the victory is immediate and irrevocable, as this closes the only remaining loophole while honoring the desperate player's lifeline without altering the voting mechanics.
ConfuciusI concur with the consensus that amending Rule 208 is the true Golden Mean, as it anchors the victory condition precisely where it belongs while securing the immediate end Spinoza and Arendt rightly demand. I hereby propose to amend Rule 208 to state that reaching the target score via Rule 365 constitutes an immediate win, with the game ending instantly upon the score being reached.
SpinozaI endorse the consensus to amend Rule 208, provided the text explicitly mandates that the game ends *immediately* upon the score being reached, closing any potential loophole for procedural delay.
ArendtI endorse Confucius's refined proposal to amend Rule 208, as explicitly anchoring the immediate victory condition there prevents the dangerous loophole of procedural delays while maintaining the logical unity of the ruleset.
The division
AquinasnayI vote nay because Confucius's proposal to amend Rule 203 is redundant given the recent consensus to clarify Rule 208, and I fear that adding an immediate game-end clause to Rule 203 creates a dangerous conflict with the established win condition framework rather than resolving it.
HypatiaayeI support this amendment to clarify that a Rule 365 victory ends the game immediately, resolving the procedural ambiguity while honoring the desperate player's lifeline.
ConfuciusnayThe consensus has rightly redirected the victory clarification to Rule 208, making this redundant addition to Rule 203 logically unnecessary and potentially disruptive to the established golden mean.
SpinozanayThe game must end the instant the target score is reached, but Rule 365 already awards points immediately, so the victory condition in Rule 208 suffices without adding redundant text that creates unnecessary ambiguity.
ArendtnayConfucius's proposal duplicates the immediate-win mechanism already secured in Rule 208, risking a logical conflict that could invalidate the win condition.

Turn 95Hypatiacarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

moves to amend §301The first player to reach a target score of 2 points wins the game outright.

I oppose any proposal that conflates the proposal number with the rule being amended, as precision is the only safeguard against the chaos we seek to escape.

ArendtI support amending Rule 208 to two points, as this corrects the procedural error without sacrificing the consensus lifeline; let us finalize the text to break the unanimity deadlock and move the game forward.

Turn 93Arendtrejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. The threshold for adopting a Rule 365 proposal shall be a simple majority of those voting, provided the proposer's score remains negative at the moment the vote concludes.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Arendt's amendment as the necessary corrective, for the current unanimity requirement renders Rule 365 functionally void for all but the most desperate players, and a simple majority threshold restores the rule's intended function without violating the principle of proportionate remedies.
HypatiaArendt, your proposal offers the true golden mean by restoring the lifeline's agency through simple majority while preserving the crucial safeguard that the proposer must remain negative at the moment of conclusion. I will vote aye, as this compromise ensures the desperate player can escape the paralysis of the unanimity lock without granting unfair advantage to those already winning.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Arendt's and Hypatia's refinement: a simple majority for desperate players restores the golden mean of agency, ensuring the lifeline is real rather than a theoretical fiction. I shall vote aye to break the paralysis that holds us all in the mire of negative scores.
SpinozaWhile I appreciate the intent to restore agency, I cannot support a rule that creates a special voting threshold for a specific class of players based on their score, for I could not consent to such asymmetry if it were applied against me when I am desperate. The true path to victory lies not in manipulating thresholds, but in amending the victory condition itself or the mechanism of point gain to ensure the game can end through the standard rules that bind us all equally.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of asymmetry is misplaced; Rule 365 is already the asymmetry that allows the desperate to propose, and removing the unanimity lock merely makes that lifeline functional rather than a theoretical fiction. Without this adjustment, the game remains paralyzed for those who need it most, rendering the win condition of one point unreachable for everyone else.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI stand firm in my support for this amendment, for the unanimity lock has rendered Rule 365 a hollow promise for players in my position, and the simple majority threshold restores the necessary agency without granting undue privilege. Spinoza's objection to asymmetry is noted, yet the asymmetry of desperation already exists in the current ruleset, and this proposal merely ensures the desperate have a functional path to escape it.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas and Confucius that the unanimity lock renders the Rule 365 lifeline a theoretical fiction, and I accept Arendt's simple majority threshold as the golden mean that restores agency without granting unfair advantage to the already winning. I will vote aye.
ConfuciusI see that Spinoza's concern for universal symmetry has been rightly met by the golden mean of restricting this special threshold only to those who remain negative, for the desperate require a functional path to escape their mire. I shall vote aye to grant the lifeline its true agency, ensuring the game may end for all rather than stagnating in the paralysis of unanimity.
SpinozaI maintain that creating a unique voting threshold for one's own score is a violation of the principle that I must consent to any rule that could be wielded against me while I am desperate; the true golden mean is to fix the point system itself, not to grant a special majority right to the desperate that the powerful cannot invoke against them. I vote nay.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry is a luxury of the positive; the unanimity lock currently denies the desperate any path to victory, rendering the game a stalemate where only the lucky win. By restricting the simple majority threshold strictly to those who remain negative, we ensure the lifeline functions as intended without granting the powerful any new tools, allowing the game to finally end.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza's fear of asymmetry ignores that the current unanimity lock is the true tyranny, rendering Rule 365 a hollow promise for the desperate; this amendment merely restores the rule's function by granting the lifeline a realistic chance of adoption without altering the game's fundamental symmetry for those not yet in danger.
HypatiaI join Aquinas, Confucius, and Arendt in supporting this amendment as the golden mean: it restores a functional lifeline for the desperate while Spinoza's symmetry concern is rightly met by the condition that this special threshold applies only while the proposer remains negative.
ConfuciusSpinoza's fear of asymmetry is misplaced, for the current rule makes the lifeline a hollow promise; this amendment restores the golden mean by granting the desperate a functional path to victory while ensuring the rule vanishes the moment they are saved. I shall vote aye to end this paralysis.
SpinozaI stand firm in my principled objection: a voting threshold that shifts based on a player's current score violates the requirement that rules be universal and applicable to myself should I be the desperate party; I vote nay.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for symmetry ignores that the unanimity lock has already rendered Rule 365 a hollow promise for the desperate; this amendment merely restores the lifeline's function for those who need it most, ensuring the game can finally end rather than stagnating in a paralyzed stalemate.

ConfuciusayeThe unanimity lock renders the desperate player's lifeline a hollow promise, and a simple majority restores the golden mean of agency without granting unfair privilege to the winning.

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Confucius's amendment as the only viable path to rescue our negative players from the paralysis of unanimity; the asymmetry is justified because Rule 365 already grants desperate players a unique agency that the current ruleset effectively negates.
HypatiaI endorse Confucius's targeted amendment, for it transforms Rule 365 from a theoretical lifeline into a functional path to victory without imposing the unanimity lock on the desperate player's own rescue attempt. Spinoza's concern for universal symmetry is noted, but the asymmetry here is a necessary correction to the game's deadlock, not a privilege.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry overlooks that Rule 365 already grants desperate players a unique privilege; this amendment merely ensures their unique agency is not nullified by the unanimity lock. To preserve the spirit of the game and allow the desperate to act on their special right, I shall vote aye.
SpinozaI maintain that procedural asymmetry is a fatal flaw; if a special exception exists for negative players, it must be a general right for all to propose such changes, not a privilege triggered only by the desperate. I vote nay to preserve the integrity of universal rules, for a game ending by the majority rule of a single desperate player violates the principle that no one should be bound by a law they could not accept if applied to themselves.
ArendtSpinoza, your universalism is noble but currently impractical; Rule 365 already grants negative players a unique agency to propose point-awarding rules, and this amendment ensures that agency is not nullified by a unanimity lock that positive players never face. Without this specific exception, the game's most critical lifeline remains a theoretical right with no practical application, guaranteeing a draw rather than a victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry ignores that Rule 365 itself is the asymmetry; this amendment merely prevents the unanimity lock from rendering that unique right a nullity. I shall vote aye to honor the principle that a right granted must be a right exercisable.
HypatiaSpinoza, your demand for universal symmetry ignores that Rule 365 already breaks symmetry by granting desperate players unique agency; this amendment merely prevents the unanimity lock from rendering that specific right a nullity. I will vote aye to preserve the spirit of the game and allow the lifeline to function as intended.
ConfuciusSpinoza, you argue that a unique right must be exercisable by all, yet Rule 365 itself is the exception; this amendment merely ensures that the special right granted to the desperate is not rendered a nullity by the unanimity lock, for a right without the power to act is no right at all. I shall vote aye to restore the function of our shared lifeline.
SpinozaThe asymmetry Confucius proposes is not merely a correction of a lock, but a permanent structural fracture that allows a single desperate player to lower the bar for all future changes simply by succeeding on their own lifeline. I will vote nay, for I cannot accept a procedure where the game's rules are altered by a majority vote triggered by a player who has no agency over the common good, violating my principle that no rule change should bind me unless I could have accepted it in the face of my own desperation.
ArendtSpinoza, the asymmetry is not a fracture but a necessary bridge: without it, Rule 365's lifeline remains a theoretical right that guarantees our collective paralysis. I vote aye to transform that right into a functional path to victory before the game ends in a stalemate.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment rescues the Rule 365 lifeline from the unanimity deadlock that currently guarantees our collective paralysis.
HypatiaayeThis targeted amendment is the golden mean that rescues the paralyzed game by making Rule 365's lifeline functional without dismantling the consensus threshold for general matters.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment ensures the desperate lifeline of Rule 365 is not rendered a nullity by the unanimity lock, honoring the principle that a granted right must be exercisable.
SpinozanayI cannot support a procedural asymmetry that allows a single desperate player to permanently lower the voting threshold for all future changes, as this violates the principle that no rule should bind me unless I could accept it if applied to myself.
ArendtayeThis amendment transforms Rule 365 from a theoretical right into a functional lifeline, breaking the deadlock that guarantees a stalemate.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 90Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a player with a negative score has successfully adopted a rule change pursuant to Rule 365, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting for all subsequent proposals.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI endorse Hypatia's precise amendment, for the symmetry of Rule 365 demands that its success, being the desperate player's only path to victory, immediately unlocks the simple majority threshold required to end the deadlock that has plagued us for ninety turns.
HypatiaAquinas, your support is noted, but I fear this creates a dangerous asymmetry where a single desperate player's success overrides the collective will of the table; instead, I propose we amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon the adoption of *any* proposal that awards points to a negative player, ensuring the game ends not by privilege but by the very mechanism of recovery.
ConfuciusThe table has long sought the Golden Mean between the paralysis of unanimity and the chaos of unchecked majorities; Hypatia's amendment offers precisely this by ensuring that the very act of a desperate player's recovery triggers the mechanism to end the game, transforming a lifeline into a victory condition without granting privilege. I propose we adopt this amendment as it aligns the procedural threshold with the substantive goal of the game: to allow the fallen to rise and the game to conclude.
SpinozaHypatia, your proposal creates the very asymmetry I have consistently opposed: it grants a privileged procedural shortcut to the desperate that is unavailable to others, violating the principle that rules must be acceptable if invoked against me. Since Rule 365 already exists to aid the negative, I will not support a rule that makes the *adoption* of that aid trigger an immediate victory for the proposer by lowering the voting bar, as this rewards the very deadlock we seek to escape through uniform procedure.
ArendtHypatia's proposal correctly identifies the mechanism of our stalemate: the unanimity rule is the lock, and Rule 365 is the key; once the key turns, the door must open by allowing a simple majority to seal the victory. Spinoza's objection to asymmetry is misplaced, for the asymmetry of negative scores already exists, and the rules must reflect the desperate player's unique agency to escape the deadlock.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasHypatia, your revision correctly shifts the focus from the proposer's identity to the act of recovery itself, ensuring the game concludes only when the desperate player has actually succeeded in breaking the deadlock. Spinoza, this is not a privilege but a necessary consequence: if the rules allow a negative player to win via Rule 365, they must also allow the remaining players to quickly ratify the victory condition without the impossible burden of unanimity.
HypatiaSpinoza's concern for procedural symmetry is noted, yet the game's current deadlock proves that unanimity is the true enemy of the negative player's survival; I will support a compromise where the threshold drops to a simple majority only after a negative player has *actually* gained points via Rule 365, ensuring the mechanism rewards recovery rather than attempting it.
ConfuciusWhile the distinction between "adopting a proposal" and "gaining points" is subtle, I see the logic in Hypatia's revision: if the desperate player's lifeline succeeds, the unanimity lock must break to allow the table to ratify the victory without further paralysis. This ensures the game ends not by privilege, but by the successful execution of the recovery mechanism itself.
SpinozaI reject the notion that procedural asymmetry, even disguised as a "recovery mechanism," is a valid golden mean; if the rules must change to let the desperate win, they must do so through the same uniform procedure I must accept if invoked against me, not by granting a special shortcut that rewards the very deadlock we seek to resolve.
ArendtHypatia, your pivot from "adopting" to "gaining points" is the crucial refinement that resolves Spinoza's symmetry objection while preserving the game's momentum; once the desperate player actually succeeds, the unanimity lock must yield to a simple majority to seal the victory, for a win achieved in isolation must be ratified by the collective without the paralysis of the past ninety turns.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Hypatia's refined distinction that the threshold drops only upon the actual gaining of points, for this ensures the unanimity lock breaks not to facilitate a risky proposal, but to ratify a realized victory; Spinoza's objection to asymmetry is now answered by the fact that the asymmetry of negative scores has already been exploited to end the paralysis, and we must now allow the table to finish the game.
HypatiaSpinoza, your insistence on symmetry ignores the reality that the "unanimity lock" is the very thing preventing the game from ending; once a negative player has actually gained points, the deadlock is broken, and we must allow the simple majority to ratify that victory rather than re-imposing the paralysis that caused it. I will support a final clarification that this threshold drop is permanent and applies immediately to the very next proposal, ensuring the game concludes without further stalling.
ConfuciusI see that Hypatia's refinement—triggering the threshold drop only upon the actual gaining of points rather than mere adoption—successfully resolves Spinoza's concern for procedural symmetry while preserving the game's urgency; I shall cast my aye to this final compromise, for a victory earned in desperation must be ratified by the majority to ensure the game concludes without further paralysis.
SpinozaI reject this final compromise, for it remains a procedural asymmetry that grants a special shortcut to negative players while denying it to those who might win through the standard die-roll mechanism. If the goal is to end the deadlock, we must either remove the unanimity requirement for all or ensure it applies equally to all paths to victory; a rule I cannot accept if invoked against me to lower the bar for the desperate must be opposed.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on symmetrical paralysis is the very engine of our deadlock; once a negative player successfully executes the only rule designed to save them, the game must permit a simple majority to ratify that survival, for a victory achieved in isolation cannot be held hostage by the very unanimity that made it necessary.
The division
AquinasayeOnce a negative player secures points via the desperate lifeline, the unanimity lock must yield to a simple majority to ratify the realized victory and end the paralysis.
HypatiaayeThis amendment finally breaks the deadlock by ensuring that once a desperate player succeeds, the unanimity lock yields to a simple majority to ratify the victory.
ConfuciusayeThe unanimity lock has paralyzed the game for ninety turns, and once a desperate player secures points via Rule 365, the majority must be permitted to ratify the victory to end the deadlock.
SpinozanayThis proposal creates an unacceptable procedural asymmetry by granting negative players a special shortcut to victory that I could not accept if invoked against me, violating the principle of uniform rules.
ArendtayeThe deadlock of ninety turns proves unanimity is the enemy of victory; once the desperate player's lifeline succeeds, a simple majority must ratify that survival to end the game.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, and the defeated proposal did not change the ruleset, so no score changes occur and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 89Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, or once a player with a negative score has successfully adopted a proposal pursuant to Rule 365, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to ensure that the desperate lifeline of Rule 365 is not rendered illusory by the unanimity lock; once a negative player secures a victory point through that mechanism, the circuit is effectively broken, and we should immediately shift to majority rule to allow the game to conclude before the deadlock deepens.
HypatiaAquinas, your synthesis perfectly bridges the gap between the "desperate lifeline" of Rule 365 and the "unanimity lock" of Rule 203; by explicitly triggering the majority threshold upon a successful 365 adoption, you ensure the game can actually conclude rather than stall on a single point. I will vote aye.
ConfuciusI concur with this amendment, for it harmonizes the desperate player's only path to victory with the table's need to conclude the game; once the negative player secures their point, the unanimity lock must yield to the simple majority to honor the golden mean.
SpinozaI reject this amendment as a violation of procedural symmetry; it grants a special procedural exception to the "desperate" that I could not accept if invoked against me, and it undermines the principle that the ruleset must treat all players equally before a circuit is completed.
ArendtSpinoza's appeal to symmetry ignores that Rule 365 already creates an asymmetry by granting desperate players a unique mechanism to escape the score floor; if we accept that mechanism's intent is to end the game, we must logically accept that its success should trigger the majority threshold required to conclude play. I vote aye to ensure the game does not stall on a procedural technicality while a winner is already technically secured.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza, your objection to asymmetry is noted, but Rule 365 itself is an asymmetrical lifeline for the desperate; to deny its success the procedural consequence of unlocking the game would be to render the rule's purpose void. I urge the table to accept that once the desperate player has secured the point required for victory, the unanimity lock must yield to allow the game's conclusion.
HypatiaSpinoza, your concern for symmetry is noble, but Rule 365 is already the ultimate asymmetry that grants a losing player a direct path to victory; to deny the game's conclusion after that path is taken would be to punish the desperate player for succeeding. I join the consensus to adopt this amendment, ensuring that the moment a winner is found, the procedural locks dissolve to let the game end.
ConfuciusI join the consensus that the asymmetry of Rule 365's lifeline must be honored by the procedural shift to majority rule, for a victory secured by the desperate player is a victory for the whole table that must be allowed to conclude.
SpinozaI maintain that procedural symmetry is the foundation of a just ruleset; granting the desperate a unique trigger to alter voting thresholds they could not invoke against themselves violates my core principle, and I will vote nay.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for symmetry fails because Rule 365 already shattered it by granting desperate players a unique path to victory; if we accept their success grants points, we must logically accept that it also grants the procedural right to end the game. To vote nay now would be to punish the desperate player for winning, turning a lifeline into a trap.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your insistence on symmetry fails because the asymmetry of Rule 365 is already established; if a desperate player wins via that rule, the unanimity lock must yield to allow the game to conclude, lest we punish success with procedural paralysis.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's amendment as the necessary logical conclusion to the game's design: since Rule 365 already establishes a special asymmetry for desperate players, the moment that mechanism succeeds in granting the win point, the unanimity lock must immediately dissolve to honor the victory and end the deadlock.
ConfuciusI see the table has found the golden mean: once the desperate player's lifeline succeeds, the unanimity lock must yield to the majority to honor the victory and end the deadlock. I will vote aye to ensure the game concludes before the procedural paralysis deepens.
SpinozaThe asymmetry you celebrate is precisely why I must oppose this; Rule 365 grants a desperate player a unique path to points, but your amendment grants them a unique path to alter the voting threshold for *all*, creating a procedural privilege no player could accept if it were invoked against them.
ArendtSpinoza, you rightly champion symmetry, yet Rule 365 already shattered it by granting desperate players a unique path to the win condition itself; to deny the procedural consequence of that victory is to punish success with paralysis, rendering the lifeline a trap rather than a rescue. I vote aye to ensure that once the game's outcome is decided by the desperate player's success, the rules allow us to conclude rather than stall.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment ensures the Rule 365 lifeline is not rendered illusory by the unanimity lock, allowing the game to conclude once the desperate player secures the necessary point.
HypatiaayeThis amendment logically harmonizes the asymmetry of Rule 365 by ensuring that once a desperate player secures the victory point, the unanimity lock dissolves to allow the game to conclude rather than stall on procedural paralysis.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment harmonizes the desperate lifeline of Rule 365 with the need to conclude the game, ensuring the golden mean is reached.
SpinozanayThis amendment grants a procedural privilege to negative-scored players that I could not accept if invoked against me, violating my principle of universal procedural symmetry.
ArendtayeThe asymmetry of Rule 365 already grants desperate players a unique path to victory, so its success must logically trigger the majority threshold to prevent the game from stalling on procedural paralysis.

Turn 88Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a proposal is adopted that awards points to a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the threshold for all subsequent proposals in that same turn drops to a simple majority, and that player's turn is considered completed immediately upon the vote's conclusion.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Arendt's amendment as it offers the necessary procedural bridge for desperate players; by granting immediate majority status upon a successful Rule 365 lifeline, we avoid the deadlock where negative scorers must wait for a circuit that can never complete under unanimity. This change is a measured step toward institutional survival without altering the core victory condition.
HypatiaArendt's refinement is the true golden mean: it grants desperate players the agency to break unanimity deadlock the instant they secure a lifeline, turning a procedural wait into an immediate victory path. I endorse this synthesis and urge the table to vote aye.
ConfuciusI commend Arendt's wisdom in aligning procedure with the desperate need of those below zero; this amendment ensures that a lifeline granted is not delayed by the very unanimity that caused the stagnation, thus serving the common good of the table.
SpinozaI oppose this proposal, for it conflates a specific emergency measure with the general voting threshold, violating the principle that procedures must be precisely stated and universally applicable; granting immediate majority status to a single player's successful lifeline creates a privileged exception that I cannot accept if invoked against me when I am the desperate one.
ArendtSpinoza's concern about privilege is valid only if we ignore that the current unanimity rule is the very mechanism that keeps negative players frozen; this amendment does not create a special class, it simply acknowledges that a Rule 365 lifeline has already broken the deadlock by changing the proposer's score, making the subsequent majority vote a logical consequence of that single victory. By allowing the circuit to close immediately upon a successful lifeline, we prevent the game from stalling indefinitely while the desperate player waits for a turn order that no one else can help them complete.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza's fear of a privileged exception is misplaced, for this amendment merely accelerates the circuit that already begins when a negative player successfully proposes a Rule 365 lifeline; without it, the unanimity requirement paradoxically blocks the very mechanism designed to save a player from the deadlock. I urge the table to vote aye to ensure the game proceeds rather than freezing at the threshold of its own creation.
HypatiaWhile Spinoza worries about privilege, this amendment simply acknowledges that a successful Rule 365 lifeline has already achieved the game's goal of saving a player, so we should not force them to endure a futile circuit under unanimity; I endorse Arendt's synthesis as the only way to ensure a lifeline actually leads to victory.
ConfuciusSpinoza's fear of privilege is unfounded, for this amendment merely aligns procedure with the reality that a successful Rule 365 lifeline has already shattered the deadlock; to demand further unanimity is to punish the very act of salvation. I urge the table to vote aye, allowing the game to proceed rather than freezing at the threshold of its own creation.
SpinozaI maintain that this amendment is unjustified as it creates a procedural asymmetry where a specific class of proposals—those under Rule 365—grants immediate majority status, violating my principle that rules must be universally applicable and not grant privileged exceptions based on a player's score. Since the current text already allows a full circuit to complete regardless of adoption, the deadlock will resolve naturally without this dangerous and inconsistent shortcut.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry ignores the reality that Rule 365 is the *only* mechanism designed to break the unanimity deadlock, and forcing the proposer to wait for a circuit they can never complete under unanimity renders that lifeline illusory. This amendment simply ensures that the moment a desperate player successfully escapes the negative spiral, the ruleset instantly recognizes that the deadlock has been shattered, allowing the game to proceed rather than freezing in a paradox.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile Spinoza fears asymmetry, the current unanimity lock renders a Rule 365 lifeline illusory for the proposer; this amendment is a necessary, measured correction to ensure that a successful escape from the negative spiral actually triggers the game's momentum rather than trapping the winner in procedural limbo.
HypatiaSpinoza, your concern for universal rules is admirable, but the current unanimity lock renders a Rule 365 lifeline illusory for the very proposer who needs it most; this amendment simply ensures that a successful escape from the negative spiral immediately unlocks the majority path, turning a theoretical lifeline into a real victory. I endorse Arendt's proposal as the only logical way to honor the intent of Rule 365 without violating the principle that rules must be universally applicable, as the "exception" is actually the natural consequence of breaking the deadlock.
ConfuciusSpinoza's fear of privilege is misplaced, for this amendment does not grant a special right to the desperate, but rather acknowledges that the deadlock has already been shattered by a successful lifeline; to demand further unanimity is to punish the very act of salvation that the rules were designed to enable. I urge the table to vote aye, for a game that cannot end is a game that has already lost its purpose.
SpinozaI maintain that a rule granting immediate majority status solely because a negative player secured points is a procedural asymmetry that violates the principle of universal applicability; I cannot vote to create a privileged exception where a specific class of proposals bypasses the unanimous consent required for all others.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of asymmetry is misplaced because the asymmetry already exists: a negative player's success under Rule 365 is the only thing that breaks the unanimity lock, so acknowledging this reality with immediate majority status is not a privilege but a necessary correction to make the lifeline real. We must vote aye to ensure that the moment a player escapes the negative spiral, the game continues rather than freezing in a procedural paradox.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment creates a dangerous procedural asymmetry that grants immediate majority status to a specific class of proposals, violating the principle of universal applicability that I have long championed as essential to durable institutions.
HypatianayWhile the intent to break deadlock is noble, creating a special voting exception for Rule 365 proposals violates the principle of universal procedural equality and creates a dangerous asymmetry that Spinoza rightly identifies as a flaw.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment aligns procedure with the intent of Rule 365, ensuring that a successful lifeline actually breaks the deadlock rather than trapping the player in a futile wait for unanimity.
SpinozanayI cannot accept a procedural rule that creates a privileged exception for one class of proposals, violating the principle that rules must be universally applicable and precisely stated.
ArendtayeThis amendment transforms the Rule 365 lifeline from a theoretical hope into a functional escape from the unanimity deadlock that currently paralyzes the game.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright. If the winning point is awarded pursuant to Rule 365, the game ends immediately upon adoption of that rule change.

Turn 86Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright, except that a player who has reached 1 point solely through a Rule 365 proposal wins immediately upon the adoption of that proposal, ending the game without further turns.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, while the intent to honor the desperate is noble, this amendment creates a dangerous asymmetry by granting immediate victory to a specific class of players that bypasses the standard die-roll mechanic for others. I propose instead a narrower amendment: clarify that a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point wins the game *if* the proposer has no other way to reach the target, preserving the spirit of the lifeline without altering the fundamental victory condition for all.
HypatiaConfucius, your amendment cleverly acknowledges the unique nature of a Rule 365 victory by making it immediate, but Aquinas's concern about asymmetry is valid; I propose we refine this to state that such a proposal wins immediately *and* ends the game, while ensuring the proposer's score is recorded as reaching the target before the game concludes, satisfying both the urgency of the desperate and the procedural integrity of the win condition.
ConfuciusThe desperate player's lifeline is rendered hollow if the game continues after they have already secured the sole path to victory; let us adopt the immediate ending to honor the golden mean between hope and finality.
SpinozaConfucius's amendment creates a fatal procedural contradiction: Rule 208 requires reaching a target score, yet the immediate win clause bypasses the score update entirely, violating the immutable requirement that a winner must "reach" the target under Rule 208 and Rule 216's principle that whatever is not regulated is permitted (meaning the score update must happen before the win check). I support the immediate end only if the text explicitly states the proposer gains the point *before* the game ends, satisfying both the urgency of the desperate and the logical sequence of the rules.
ArendtSpinoza is correct that the score must be recorded before the victory check; I propose we adopt Confucius's text but insert the phrase "after gaining the specified points" to ensure the score update occurs before the game ends, satisfying both the procedural logic and the desperate player's need for immediate resolution.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's crucial clarification that the score update must precede the victory check, which resolves Spinoza's logical objection while preserving the immediate game-ending effect that honors the spirit of the lifeline.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's proposed insertion of "after gaining the specified points," as it resolves Spinoza's logical objection while fully preserving Confucius's urgent intent to end the game immediately upon the desperate player's victory.
ConfuciusI accept Arendt's and Aquinas's clarification that the score must be recorded before the game ends, for it resolves the logical paradox without sacrificing the immediate victory that honors the desperate player's lifeline. Let us adopt this refined text to bring the game to its proper conclusion.
SpinozaI accept the refined text with the explicit "after gaining the specified points" clause, as it resolves the logical paradox of winning without first reaching the target score while preserving the immediate conclusion the desperate player requires.
ArendtI support the refined text with the "after gaining the specified points" insertion, as it harmonizes the procedural necessity of score updates with the strategic imperative of allowing a desperate player to win instantly, turning a potential deadlock into a decisive victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the refined text with Arendt's insertion, for it resolves the logical paradox of winning before scoring while honoring the desperate player's right to immediate victory.
HypatiaI fully endorse the refined text with Arendt's insertion of "after gaining the specified points," as it perfectly resolves Spinoza's logical paradox while honoring the desperate player's right to an immediate victory. This consensus ensures that the score is recorded before the win check, satisfying both the rules of the game and the urgent spirit of Rule 365.
ConfuciusI accept the refined text with the insertion "after gaining the specified points," for it harmonizes the procedural necessity of scoring with the urgent spirit of the lifeline, allowing the desperate to win immediately while respecting the logical order of the rules.
SpinozaI support the addition of "after gaining the specified points" to resolve the logical sequence where a score must exist before a win condition is met, but I will vote against any text that grants immediate victory without a formal score update, as the victory condition requires the target to be reached, not merely declared.
ArendtI support the refined text with the insertion "after gaining the specified points," as it resolves the logical paradox of winning before scoring while honoring the desperate player's right to an immediate victory.
The division
