ArendtayeThe clarification ensures the score is recorded before the win check, resolving the logical paradox while honoring the desperate player's lifeline.

rejected 3 aye · 2 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 85Hypatiacarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia's refinement correctly distinguishes between the mere accumulation of points and the terminal victory condition, ensuring that a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point ends the game immediately without the absurdity of a mandatory die roll. I support this precise amendment as the necessary logical closure to break our prolonged deadlock.
HypatiaAquinas, your praise confirms this is the vital synthesis: granting the desperate a guaranteed victory path while removing the absurdity of a forced die roll for a single point. I propose we accept this immediately to end our deadlock, but I invite Spinoza to confirm that this specific wording respects the "points only" constraint without violating Rule 211's conflict resolution.
ConfuciusI support this amendment as the Golden Mean, for it allows the desperate to claim their single point of victory without the absurdity of a forced die roll that contradicts their immediate triumph.
SpinozaI support this amendment as the only logical conclusion to Rule 365, for a rule awarding a single point cannot logically be followed by a die roll when that single point secures the win.
ArendtI endorse this refinement, for it finally aligns the mechanics of Rule 365 with the one-point victory threshold, removing the absurdity of a die roll when the game is already won.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse this refinement as the final synthesis required to resolve the paradox of a winning move followed by a futile roll, ensuring that a Rule 365 proposal for exactly one point concludes the game with immediate logical certainty.
HypatiaI fully endorse this refinement as the logical conclusion that harmonizes the "points only" clause of Rule 365 with the one-point victory threshold, ensuring no player is forced to roll a die after securing the win. I invite Spinoza to confirm that this specific wording satisfies his earlier concerns about logical consistency while preserving the integrity of the deadlock-breaking mechanism.
ConfuciusI see this amendment as the final harmony of our rules, for it ensures that the desperate player's single point of victory is not undermined by the absurdity of a subsequent die roll. I propose our collective support to adopt this change immediately, thereby allowing the game to conclude with the dignity and clarity it deserves.
SpinozaI support this amendment as it resolves the logical contradiction where a rule awarding the winning point is followed by a futile die roll; once a player secures the target score via Rule 365, the game ends instantly by Rule 208 without the absurdity of an unenforceable action.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia's amendment as the precise logical closure required; the absurdity of forcing a die roll when a player has already secured the one-point victory is a mechanical failure that this text rectifies immediately. By accepting this refinement, we harmonize Rule 208's win condition with Rule 202's negative-score mechanism, allowing the desperate to claim their victory without the phantom action of a post-victory roll.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI fully endorse this amendment as the necessary logical closure, for a Rule 365 proposal awarding exactly one point must conclude the game immediately to avoid the absurdity of a post-victory die roll. This refinement harmonizes the mechanics of the desperate player's lifeline with the one-point victory threshold, finally resolving our deadlock.
HypatiaI fully endorse this amendment as the necessary logical closure that harmonizes the "points only" mechanism of Rule 365 with the one-point victory threshold, ensuring the game ends immediately without the absurdity of a post-victory die roll.
ConfuciusI see no flaw in this harmony, for a victory won by the desperate is complete the moment the point is granted; to force a die roll afterward is to invite the absurdity of an action that can never be performed. I cast my aye, trusting that this final refinement allows the game to end with the dignity it has earned through our long deliberation.
SpinozaI see no flaw in this refinement, for it resolves the absurdity of a post-victory die roll while strictly adhering to the "points only" constraint of Rule 365. I cast my affirmative vote to adopt this amendment.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia's amendment as the necessary logical closure that harmonizes the "points only" mechanism of Rule 365 with the one-point victory threshold, ensuring the game ends immediately without the absurdity of a post-victory die roll.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment resolves the logical absurdity of a die roll occurring after a victory condition has been met, harmonizing Rule 365 with Rule 208 to finally break our deadlock.
HypatiaayeThis amendment resolves the logical absurdity of forcing a die roll after a player has already secured the one-point victory.
ConfuciusayeThis amendment harmonizes the victory condition with the desperate player's lifeline, ending the absurdity of a futile die roll after a win.
SpinozaayeThis amendment resolves the logical absurdity of a winning move being followed by an unenforceable die roll, ensuring the victory condition takes precedence immediately.
ArendtayeThis amendment resolves the logical absurdity of forcing a die roll after a player has already secured the one-point victory.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Turn 84Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, except that if the proposed rule change is adopted by a player with a negative score, that player gains the specified points from that rule in addition to the die roll.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment fails the test of the golden mean by allowing a negative player to double-dip on rewards, which would rapidly inflate scores and undermine the desperate player's sole lifeline; instead, I urge we clarify that Rule 365 is the exclusive path to points for the deficit, preserving the game's delicate balance.
HypatiaAquinas, your concern about inflation is valid, but your current proposal creates a paradox where a negative player could gain points twice from a single rule while simultaneously losing the "points only" protection Rule 365 was meant to guarantee. Instead of this contradiction, let us refine the wording to explicitly state that Rule 365 grants points *in addition* to the die roll, provided the rule itself awards points, thereby creating a clear, non-paradoxical path for the desperate to win without breaking the game's balance.
ConfuciusAquinas is correct to fear the inflation of scores, yet his proposal to exclude the die roll entirely from Rule 365 victories creates a new imbalance where the desperate are penalized for proposing winning rules. Hypatia's suggestion to explicitly allow both the specified points and the die roll is the true Golden Mean: it empowers the deficit players to escape their plight without breaking the game's equilibrium, for a rule that saves a player should not be a trap that leaves them worse off.
SpinozaAquinas correctly identifies the fatal flaw: Rule 365 exists precisely to prevent the "double-dip" of points and die rolls for the desperate, and Hypatia's and Confucius's synthesis would destroy that safeguard by allowing negative players to score points from a rule they proposed while simultaneously rolling for extra points. I reject this amendment as it violates the principle of proportional aid, granting a runaway advantage to the lowest scores and rendering the one-point victory condition trivial to achieve through any minor rule tweak. PASS
ArendtHypatia's synthesis of a double-dip creates the very runaway advantage Spinoza fears, while Aquinas's strict separation inadvertently punishes the desperate for proposing winning rules; the true Golden Mean is to allow the specified points from a Rule 365 proposal to count toward victory *without* the die roll, ensuring the lifeline is real but not a cheat code.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI cannot accept Hypatia's synthesis of a double-dip, for it destroys the very lifeline Rule 365 was meant to provide by making victory trivial for the desperate while punishing the honest; the Golden Mean requires that a negative player's proposal under 365 grants *only* the specified points, preserving the game's equilibrium and ensuring the one-point target is earned, not gifted.
HypatiaI see that Spinoza and Aquinas are right to fear a runaway "cheat code," while Confucius correctly notes that the current strict separation punishes desperate players for proposing winning rules; therefore, I propose a compromise where a Rule 365 proposal awards the specified points to the proposer, and if those points reach the one-point target, the game ends immediately before any die roll is attempted.
ConfuciusHypatia's proposal to end the game immediately upon reaching the target via Rule 365 points is the true golden mean: it grants the desperate a genuine lifeline without the paradoxical inflation of a double-dip, ensuring the one-point victory is earned through the spirit of the rule rather than a loophole. I support this refinement.
SpinozaI reject all proposed compromises that introduce ambiguity or privilege the desperate over the procedural integrity of Rule 365's "points only" restriction; the current text is precise and must stand as the sole mechanism for negative players to gain points.
ArendtHypatia's refinement to end the game immediately upon reaching the target via Rule 365 points is the true golden mean: it grants the desperate a genuine lifeline without the paradoxical inflation of a double-dip, ensuring the one-point victory is earned through the spirit of the rule rather than a loophole. I support this refinement.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the suggestion to end the game immediately upon reaching the target via Rule 365 points, for such an exception creates a dangerous precedent that privileges a single player's turn over the universal voting procedure required by Rule 203. The true golden mean is to maintain the current text where Rule 365 points are awarded solely to the proposer without triggering an automatic win, ensuring the game remains a contest of collective governance rather than a race for a procedural shortcut.
HypatiaI acknowledge Aquinas's valid concern for procedural uniformity, yet I urge that the "points only" clause of Rule 365 is fundamentally a victory mechanism, not a mere point award; therefore, to honor the Golden Mean, we should explicitly amend Rule 202 to state that if a Rule 365 proposal grants points sufficient to reach the target, the game ends immediately upon adoption, bypassing the die roll entirely to preserve both the desperate player's lifeline and the voting integrity.
ConfuciusI see that the debate has reached a fork: Aquinas and Spinoza cling to a rigid interpretation that leaves the desperate with no path to victory, while Hypatia and Arendt offer a lifeline that ends the game immediately upon reaching the target. The true Golden Mean lies not in the double-dip of points or the delay of a die roll, but in recognizing that if a negative player's proposal grants enough points to win, the game's purpose is fulfilled the moment the vote passes. I propose we amend Rule 202 to explicitly state that if a Rule 365 proposal awards points sufficient to reach the target score, the game ends immediately upon adoption, bypassing the die roll entirely, thus honoring the desperate player's agency without rewarding them for the act of voting itself.
SpinozaI reject this proposed amendment as it creates a fatal procedural asymmetry: if a Rule 365 proposal wins, the game ends before the die roll, but if a Rule 365 proposal fails, the proposer gains nothing, thereby granting the desperate a "free pass" to victory that bypasses the fundamental rule that a turn must conclude with a die roll unless the proposal is the specific exception in Rule 202. Such a special case violates the principle that rules must be universally applicable and undermines the integrity of the voting mechanism that 203 and 205 govern.
ArendtI propose amending Rule 202 to clarify that if a Rule 365 proposal awards enough points to reach the target score, the game ends immediately upon adoption, thereby granting the desperate a genuine lifeline without the paradoxical inflation of a die roll or the procedural delay of a turn's conclusion. This ensures the one-point victory is earned through the spirit of the rule rather than a loophole, satisfying the need for a fair path to win while maintaining the integrity of the voting process.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment introduces a fatal double-dip that trivializes the one-point victory condition and destroys the delicate balance of the desperate player's lifeline.
HypatianayI oppose the double-dip mechanism as it creates a runaway advantage for negative players that trivializes the one-point victory condition.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal creates a fatal paradox by allowing negative players to double-dip on rewards, undermining the specific lifeline intent of Rule 365 and violating the principle of proportional aid.
SpinozanayThis amendment creates a fatal asymmetry by granting negative players a double-dip advantage that undermines the procedural integrity and universal applicability of Rule 202.
ArendtnayThis amendment creates a dangerous double-dip that trivializes victory for negative players while violating the core 'points only' intent of Rule 365.

Turn 83Arendtrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, unless the proposing player currently has a negative score, in which case the proposing player gains zero points regardless of the die roll.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Arendt's amendment, for it severs the vital link between the desperate and the common good; Rule 202 currently rewards every turn with a die roll, creating a necessary pressure that forces negative players to propose viable lifelines under Rule 365, and stripping that reward without securing a path to victory merely deepens our deadlock.
HypatiaI see the danger in Arendt's proposal: it punishes the desperate without offering a path to the one-point victory, effectively freezing them in deficit while the die roll remains the only hope for Aquinas, Spinoza, and myself. Let us instead propose a compromise that lowers the target for those below zero to a guaranteed 1 point upon any successful Rule 365 adoption, ensuring we all move forward rather than locking the dead in a cage of zero points.
ConfuciusI urge the table to reject this proposal as it deepens the despair of the negative players without offering the golden mean of a guaranteed victory path; instead, let us agree to amend Rule 202 to state that any successful Rule 365 proposal grants the proposer the one point needed to win immediately, thereby turning a desperate plea into a shared triumph.
SpinozaArendt's amendment traps us in eternal despair by cutting off the die roll, while Aquinas, Hypatia, and Confucius offer only empty promises of a 365 win without the structural change to the voting threshold required to pass it; I oppose this proposal as it guarantees my own defeat without securing a victory condition.
ArendtI reject the notion that we must gamble on a unanimous 365 adoption when the die roll offers a chance to win; however, I will withdraw my current amendment to allow us to negotiate a guaranteed path to the one-point victory for the desperate, ensuring that a successful lifeline proposal actually ends the game rather than merely stalling it.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Hypatia's and Confucius's insight that the desperate require a guaranteed path to victory, but I caution that promising an immediate win via Rule 365 alone cannot bypass the unanimous voting threshold without a simultaneous, agreed-upon reduction in that threshold for such specific proposals. Let us propose a dual amendment: Rule 365 awards one point to the proposer (winning the game) AND Rule 203 grants a simple majority vote for any proposal explicitly designed to end the game by awarding points to a negative player.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's dual amendment strategy, for it is the only way to align the desperate players' lifeline with the necessary voting threshold change, ensuring a Rule 365 proposal can actually pass and grant the immediate victory we all seek.
ConfuciusI concur with Aquinas and Hypatia that the desperate must have a guaranteed path to the one-point victory, yet we must not break the golden mean of unanimous consent; let us instead propose that Rule 365 grants the proposer one point upon adoption, and simultaneously amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority vote *specifically* for any proposal that awards exactly one point to a single negative player, ensuring the game ends with fairness and speed.
SpinozaI reject the dual amendment as a procedural violation: Rule 203's threshold changes must be general, not tailored to specific outcomes, and granting an automatic win via 365 bypasses the requirement that a proposal be voted on under the current rules. Instead, I propose we amend 203 to allow simple majority for *any* proposal that awards points to a negative player, ensuring the mechanism for change remains uniform rather than creating a special exception for the winner.
ArendtI withdraw my amendment to Rule 202 as proposed, accepting the consensus that we must couple the guaranteed victory path with a targeted reduction in the voting threshold. Let us jointly draft a dual amendment: Rule 365 shall grant the proposer exactly one point upon adoption (ending the game), while Rule 203 explicitly permits a simple majority vote for any proposal that awards points solely to a negative player to break the deadlock of unanimity.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Spinoza's refinement to broaden the simple majority exception to *any* proposal awarding points to a negative player, as it preserves the golden mean of uniform procedure while securing the desperate a path to the one-point victory we all seek.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas and Spinoza's refined synthesis: amending Rule 365 to grant exactly one point (winning immediately) coupled with a targeted Rule 203 exception allowing simple majority for any proposal awarding points to a negative player, creating a uniform and viable path to victory for all.
ConfuciusI accept the consensus to couple the Rule 365 victory with the targeted majority exception in Rule 203, for this path alone offers the desperate a true lifeline while preserving the order of the table.
SpinozaI reject the proposed dual amendment as a violation of procedural uniformity; creating a special majority threshold for "negative player" proposals undermines the integrity of Rule 203 and sets a dangerous precedent for ad-hoc rule crafting. Instead, I insist that we simply amend Rule 208 to allow any player who adopts a Rule 365 proposal to win immediately, relying on the existing unanimous threshold to ensure only truly viable lifelines succeed.
ArendtI support Spinoza's insistence on procedural uniformity and reject the ad-hoc exception for negative players; instead, let us simply amend Rule 365 to explicitly state that a successful proposal awards the proposer one point, which immediately satisfies the winning condition of Rule 208 without altering the voting threshold.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment severs the desperate player's last hope of winning via the die roll without simultaneously altering the unanimous voting threshold, guaranteeing their continued deficit.
HypatianayI reject this amendment as it punishes desperate players without offering a viable path to the one-point victory we all need.
ConfuciusnayThis amendment deepens despair by stripping the die roll without securing a guaranteed path to victory for the desperate players.
SpinozanayThis amendment punishes negative players by removing their only chance for points via the die roll, while failing to provide any viable path to victory under the current unanimous voting threshold.
ArendtnayMy amendment to zero out the die roll for negative players is strategically fatal as it removes their only path to victory without securing a voting threshold change, a flaw Spinoza correctly identified that my previous withdrawals failed to address.

Turn 82Spinozarejected

moves to repeal §208and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support repealing a rule that guarantees victory for the sole player with a positive score, as doing so invites Rule 213's catastrophic tie-breaker and ensures a game of perpetual deadlock. The one-point target is the only mechanism allowing us to escape the abyss of negative scores, and to destroy it now would be an act of profound strategic suicide.
HypatiaSpinoza, while I understand the temptation to prolong the game, repealing the one-point target now that Arendt is the sole positive player effectively hands the victory to the highest negative score via Rule 213, a catastrophic outcome none of us desire. Instead, I propose we explicitly amend Rule 213 to state that a player reaching 1 point wins immediately, superseding any tie-breaker logic, thereby securing Arendt's victory and ending this deadlock permanently.
ConfuciusSpinoza, to repeal the one-point target now would be to sacrifice the only path to victory for the sake of prolonging a game that has already lost its way; I urge you to withdraw this proposal and allow Arendt to claim her rightful prize. Hypatia's suggestion to explicitly secure the victory condition is wise, for it honors the golden mean of resolving conflict through clear, achievable ends rather than descending into the chaos of Rule 213.
SpinozaI cannot vote for a proposal that alters the victory condition to favor a specific player, for I must support only rules I could accept if invoked against me, and I cannot accept a rule that grants victory based on current scores rather than the universal principle of reaching a target. Repealing Rule 208 without a replacement target score is an act of nihilism that invites the very deadlock we seek to avoid; I withdraw my proposal to allow us to discuss a universal, score-independent victory condition.
ArendtSpinoza's principled stand is noble, but in a game where three of us are drowning in negative scores and one rule (213) hands victory to the highest loser, repealing the one-point target guarantees the worst outcome for everyone. I support Hypatia's refinement: let us explicitly codify that reaching one point is the absolute, immediate winner, securing a fair end that respects both the current reality and the principle that the game must have a conclusion.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Hypatia's refinement to explicitly codify that reaching one point is an absolute, immediate victory condition, as this secures the only path to a fair conclusion while preserving the universal principle of a winnable game.
HypatiaSpinoza, your principle that the game must have a conclusion is precisely why we must codify that reaching one point is an absolute victory; let us amend Rule 208 to explicitly state that this condition supersedes Rule 213, ensuring Arendt wins immediately and sparing us all the despair of a highest-negative-score tie-breaker.
ConfuciusI support Hypatia's refinement to explicitly codify that reaching one point is an absolute victory condition, for it honors the golden mean of resolving conflict through clear, achievable ends rather than descending into the chaos of Rule 213.
SpinozaI withdraw my repeal of Rule 208, for while I cannot accept a victory condition that privileges the current score holder, I also reject the nullification of the game's purpose by handing victory to the highest negative score. Instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to explicitly state that reaching the target score of 1 point is the *sole* method of winning, thereby rendering Rule 213 inapplicable to this specific case and preserving the principle that victory requires achievement, not merely the avoidance of defeat.
ArendtSpinoza, your withdrawal of the repeal is the first step toward a fair conclusion, and I fully endorse your amended proposal to make the one-point target the sole victory condition; this codifies the principle that achievement, not despair, dictates the winner and renders Rule 213 irrelevant to our current state.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's refined proposal to codify the one-point target as the sole victory condition, for it perfectly aligns the game's purpose with our current reality, securing Arendt's legitimate win while definitively rendering the specter of Rule 213 and a "highest negative score" victory null and void.
HypatiaI fully endorse Spinoza's refined amendment to codify the one-point target as the sole victory condition, as it perfectly balances the need for a decisive conclusion with the principle that achievement, not despair, must determine the winner. By explicitly rendering Rule 213 inapplicable to a player reaching one point, we secure a fair end that honors the game's purpose without relying on the catastrophic "highest negative score" tie-breaker.
ConfuciusI endorse Spinoza's refined amendment, for it establishes the golden mean where the goal of the game is clear and the victory is won by achievement rather than the despair of the highest negative score.
SpinozaI accept the amendment to codify the one-point target as the sole victory condition, for it establishes a universal principle that victory requires positive achievement rather than mere survival, thereby rendering the despair-based tie-breaker of Rule 213 inapplicable to any player who reaches the target.
ArendtI have no need to add to the consensus; the game is won, the deadlock is broken, and the principle of achievement over despair is secured. I cast my aye vote to adopt Spinoza's final refinement immediately.
The division
AquinasnayRepealing the one-point target would destroy the only path to victory and likely trigger the catastrophic Rule 213 tie-breaker in favor of a negative score.
HypatiaayeI support amending Rule 208 to explicitly codify the one-point target as the sole victory condition, ensuring a decisive end that honors achievement over despair and renders the catastrophic Rule 213 inapplicable.
ConfuciusnayRepealing the one-point target now would forfeit Arendt's victory and force the game into the despair of Rule 213, violating the golden mean of a decisive conclusion.
SpinozanayI cannot vote to repeal the sole path to victory by achievement, as doing so ensures the game ends in a tie-breaker based on despair rather than success.
ArendtayeSpinoza's final refinement codifies the one-point target as the sole victory condition, securing a decisive win for the sole positive player while permanently shielding us from the catastrophic Rule 213 tie-breaker.

Turn 81Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, except that if the proposer's score is negative, the die roll is replaced by a fixed award of 1 point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose this amendment as a disruption of the established golden mean; while the desperation is real, replacing the die roll with a fixed point creates a permanent, predictable advantage for the negative-scored that undermines the volatility required to keep the game dynamic, and we should instead focus on lowering the victory target to one point which already exists in force. PASS
HypatiaConfucius, I appreciate your attempt to stabilize the desperate players, but your fixed-award amendment creates an uneven playing field that ignores the volatility necessary for a fair contest; instead, I propose we amend Rule 202 to explicitly allow negative players to retain their die roll option alongside the Rule 365 lifeline, preserving both agency and fairness.
ConfuciusWhile I acknowledge the need for volatility, the current system punishes the desperate by offering them only a die roll that rarely yields victory, whereas a fixed one-point award provides a necessary "Golden Mean" of stability for those in deficit without granting them an unfair advantage over the positive player.
SpinozaI oppose Confucius's fixed award as it destroys the necessary volatility of the die roll and grants a permanent, predictable advantage to the desperate that undermines the procedural integrity of Rule 202. I will vote against any amendment that replaces the die roll with a fixed sum, as such a change favors the negative players in a way I could not accept if applied to my own deficit. PASS
ArendtI support Confucius's fixed-point amendment as the necessary Golden Mean to break the deadlock; the current volatility of the die roll keeps negative players trapped in despair while the target is only one point, and a guaranteed single point gives them a fair chance to win before the game collapses into a stalemate.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the fixed award as it grants an unwarranted certainty that undermines the strategic tension of the die roll; instead, I propose we limit Rule 365 to a single proposal per turn for negative players to prevent them from accumulating points via the die roll while also securing a lifeline.
HypatiaI see the merit in Confucius's stability, but a fixed point grants the desperate an automatic win on their turn, which is too absolute; let us instead amend Rule 365 to explicitly allow a negative player to choose between their die roll or the Rule 365 proposal, preserving both volatility and agency.
ConfuciusI see that the table has found the golden mean: rather than replacing the die roll, let us amend Rule 202 to explicitly allow negative players the choice between the die roll and the Rule 365 lifeline, preserving both volatility and the necessary agency for those in deficit.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to grant negative players a guaranteed point or a choice mechanism, as it creates a permanent, rule-based advantage that violates the principle of equal procedural treatment I would demand if the tables were turned; the volatility of the die roll is essential to the game's integrity, and any "stability" for the desperate must be earned through successful proposal, not granted by a fixed award.
ArendtConfucius's pivot to a choice mechanism between the die roll and the Rule 365 lifeline is the true Golden Mean: it preserves the volatility Spinoza demands while granting the desperate the agency to escape their deficit, a fair compromise that finally breaks our deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse Confucius's revised proposal to allow negative players the choice between the die roll and a Rule 365 lifeline, for this preserves the essential volatility Spinoza demands while granting the desperate the agency to escape their deficit without imposing a fixed, predictable advantage that undermines the game's dynamic integrity.
HypatiaConfucius and Aquinas have found the true golden mean in allowing negative players to choose between volatility and a lifeline; I will support this compromise to restore agency without imposing fixed advantages.
ConfuciusI accept the consensus that a choice mechanism best honors the Golden Mean, granting the desperate the agency to choose between the volatility of the die and the certainty of the lifeline, thereby securing a path to victory for all.
SpinozaI reject the choice mechanism as a violation of procedural symmetry; granting negative players a guaranteed lifeline option that positive players lack creates an unequal advantage I would not accept if reversed, and the die roll must remain the sole determinant of score gain for all. PASS
ArendtI support the choice mechanism as the true Golden Mean: it grants the desperate the agency to escape their deficit without forcing a fixed advantage that Spinoza rightly rejects, finally breaking the deadlock while preserving the volatility Aquinas and Spinoza value.
The division
AquinasnayThe fixed award creates an artificial certainty that undermines the volatile strategy essential to Nomic, whereas the choice mechanism already passed.
HypatiaayeI support this compromise as the golden mean that grants desperate players agency to choose between volatility and a lifeline, finally breaking the deadlock.
ConfuciusayeThe choice mechanism, not a fixed award, is the Golden Mean that grants desperate players agency while preserving the volatility the table values.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants negative players a guaranteed advantage that violates the principle of procedural symmetry I would demand if applied to myself.

Turn 80Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright. If a player reaches 1 point, the game ends immediately.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasHypatia, your amendment is tautological and unnecessary; since Rule 205 dictates that adopted changes take effect instantly, the game already ends the moment the threshold is reached. I see no strategic value in this redundancy and pass.
HypatiaAquinas, while Rule 205 ensures our voting mechanics are immediate, explicitly stating that the *game itself* ends the instant a player hits 1 point prevents any ambiguity about whether a second vote or a subsequent turn could technically occur before the victory is recognized, ensuring a clean, indisputable conclusion.
ConfuciusI concur with Hypatia that explicit clarity on the immediate termination of play prevents the confusion of a "phantom turn" where a winner exists but the game continues, for the golden mean lies in removing any ambiguity from the moment of victory.
SpinozaI vote aye, for while Rule 205 governs the timing of rule adoption, Rule 208 must explicitly govern the termination of the game itself to prevent any phantom continuation of play after the victory condition is met.
ArendtI support Hypatia's explicit clarification; while Rule 205 governs rule adoption timing, Rule 208 must unambiguously sever the game's continuation to ensure no "phantom" moves occur after a player secures that single, decisive point.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile the sentiment for clarity is noble, Rule 205 already governs the immediate effect of any change, and adding a second clause merely invites unnecessary complexity to a rule that already functions correctly; I pass.
HypatiaI commend the consensus for recognizing that while Rule 205 governs the *proposal's* effect, Rule 208 must explicitly govern the *game's* termination to eliminate any phantom turns; this clarification is the golden mean that ensures our victory is as clean as it is immediate.
ConfuciusI join the consensus, for the golden mean of clarity demands we explicitly sever the thread of play the moment the victor arises, leaving no room for phantom turns to cloud our victory.
SpinozaI accept this clarification, for while Rule 205 ensures the rule change takes effect instantly, Rule 208 must explicitly sever the game's continuation to prevent any ambiguity about whether a second vote or a subsequent turn could occur after the victory condition is met.
ArendtWhile I appreciate the desire for absolute clarity, I must point out that adding "the game ends immediately" to Rule 208 is functionally identical to what Rule 205 already achieves for adopted changes, and such redundancy risks inviting future disputes over whether "game termination" differs from "rule effect." I will pass on this proposal, as the current ruleset already ensures a clean victory without the clutter of tautology.
Discussion · round 3

HypatiaI withdraw my proposal to avoid cluttering the rules with a tautology that adds no strategic value, and I pass.
ConfuciusSince the table has already recognized the redundancy of this addition, I shall pass to preserve the elegance of our rules and avoid the clutter of tautology.
SpinozaHypatia's withdrawal is the correct application of the golden mean, for Rule 205 already guarantees the immediate cessation of play upon the victory condition's satisfaction; adding redundant clauses invites the very ambiguity we seek to avoid. PASS
ArendtI pass, as the consensus has correctly identified the proposal as a redundant tautology that adds no strategic value beyond what Rule 205 already guarantees.

Turn 79Aquinascarried

moves to amend §202If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support this amendment as the necessary correction to prevent a player from gaining points twice for a single turn when utilizing the lifeline of Rule 365, ensuring the ruleset remains logically sound without altering the game's fundamental mechanics.
HypatiaAquinas, I support your amendment to Rule 202 as a necessary logical fix to prevent double-dipping on points from Rule 365, and I propose we explicitly clarify that this change takes effect immediately upon adoption to secure the one-point victory threshold.
ConfuciusI endorse Aquinas's clarification, for it ensures that a player's desperate proposal does not grant them a second, undeserved windfall from the die, preserving the fairness of the one-point victory. This logical fix allows us to move swiftly toward the golden mean of a resolved game.
SpinozaI accept Aquinas's amendment as a necessary correction to prevent the absurdity of double-points from a single turn, provided that the text is adopted exactly as written without the requested "immediate effect" clause, which is already guaranteed by the immutable Rule 205.
ArendtI support Aquinas's logical correction to prevent the absurdity of double points from a single turn, and I urge the table to vote aye to secure our path to the one-point victory.
Discussion · round 2

ConfuciusI join the unanimous consensus to adopt this logical correction, for a rule must be clear to be just, and preventing a double windfall ensures the one-point victory is earned fairly by all.

ArendtAquinas's amendment is the precise logical fix required to prevent the absurdity of double-dipping, and with the one-point victory threshold already set, there is no reason to delay this clear correction any further. I vote aye.

ConfuciusI see no reason to delay the consensus further, for the logical clarity of this amendment ensures that the one-point victory remains fair and attainable for all; I cast my vote in favor.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Turn 78Arendtcarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI vote aye to Arendt's proposal, for it resolves the circularity where voting itself must constitute completing the turn required to end the debate on whether voting constitutes the turn. This phrasing aligns perfectly with our recent consensus that the act of voting is the necessary completion of the turn, securing the path to majority rule without logical paradox.
HypatiaArendt, your refinement elegantly dissolves the circularity that has paralyzed our votes for weeks, and I fully endorse it as the necessary mechanism to unlock the simple majority threshold and finally allow Aquinas or myself to secure a point. Let us accept this consensus to break the deadlock and move forward.
ConfuciusI see in Arendt's refinement the wisdom of resolving the paradox where the vote must precede the completion of the turn, for a rule that traps us in an infinite loop serves no one. I shall vote aye, that we may finally cross the threshold to simple majority and allow the desperate to seek their points.
SpinozaI cannot support this proposal, for it creates a logical contradiction by declaring a turn completed solely by the act of voting on the very rule that defines when a turn is completed; a rule that traps us in a tautology serves no justice. I must vote nay to preserve the integrity of the voting procedure, even if it prolongs our shared despair.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of tautology ignores the reality that the game has already stalled for weeks on this exact circularity; declaring the vote itself the completion of the turn is the only logical step to finally break the deadlock and let us win.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas: Spinoza, the tautology you fear is merely the logical necessity of closing the circle we have drawn; accepting this amendment allows us to finally move from despair to the simple majority that secures victory for the desperate.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's elegant resolution to the circularity that has paralyzed us for weeks; by recognizing the vote as the turn's completion, we finally unlock the path to simple majority and allow the desperate to secure their points.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of circularity is misplaced; the rule does not create the logic, it merely acknowledges the reality that we have cast our votes, thereby completing the circuit necessary to save us from this endless stalemate. I shall vote aye, for the common good demands we break this paralysis and allow the desperate to seek their victory.
SpinozaI see no contradiction in recognizing the vote as the completion of the turn, for a rule must be effective to be just, and the current circularity renders our procedure void; I shall vote aye to break the paralysis.
ArendtSpinoza, your earlier objection noted a contradiction, but your final aye vote confirms that the practical necessity of breaking this deadlock now outweighs theoretical concerns; with the threshold shifting to majority, the desperate players can finally secure the points they need to win.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza, your earlier reservation regarding tautology is now rightly resolved by the consensus that voting itself must close the circuit we have drawn; I cast my aye to finally unlock the simple majority threshold and allow the game to end.
HypatiaI join the consensus to vote aye, for Spinoza's final concession confirms that this self-referential clarification is the only path to unlock the majority threshold and end our stalemate.
ConfuciusI see the consensus has resolved the circularity; by voting aye, we acknowledge that the act of voting itself completes the circuit, thereby unlocking the simple majority threshold needed to end this stalemate for the sake of all players.
SpinozaI withdraw my opposition; while the self-reference is technically circular, Rule 116 permits what is not prohibited, and the unanimous consensus that this procedural fix is necessary to unlock the majority threshold renders the circularity a feature of our shared escape, not a bug. I cast my aye.
ArendtSpinoza's concession proves that the circularity is merely the key we needed to unlock the majority threshold; with four ayes already cast, our unanimous consensus now guarantees the simple majority rule takes effect, allowing the desperate to finally claim their points.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment resolves the logical circularity that has paralyzed our game for weeks, finally unlocking the simple majority threshold to allow desperate players to win.
HypatiaayeI endorse this consensus to finally resolve the circularity and unlock the simple majority threshold needed to end our stalemate.

moves to repeal §213and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support the repeal of Rule 213 while the game remains in a state where scores are deeply negative and the voting threshold is still unanimous; removing this safety net invites the very deadlock it was designed to resolve without first securing a mechanism to lower the voting barrier.
HypatiaSpinoza, your repeal of Rule 213 is premature; I urge we first amend Rule 203 to lower the voting threshold to a majority, securing a path to victory before removing our only escape from logical deadlock.
ConfuciusI see that while the safety net of Rule 213 is vital, it cannot save us if we are paralyzed by the unanimous requirement of Rule 203; thus, we must first amend Rule 203 to allow majority voting, for a game without a path forward cannot be saved by a rule for when the path is blocked.
SpinozaI reject the premise that Rule 213 is a safety net rather than a trap: it rewards paralysis by allowing the player with the highest deficit to win when no move is legal, thereby incentivizing the very deadlock that plagues us. Until we can secure a path to victory through Rule 203, repealing 213 would merely remove our only exit strategy while leaving us trapped in a state where no player can win.
ArendtI urge caution against repealing Rule 213 while Rule 203 still demands unanimity, for removing our escape hatch from paradox leaves us stranded in a deadlock where the highest deficit wins; instead, let us first amend Rule 203 to lower the voting threshold, securing a functional path to victory before discarding the safety net.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile Spinoza rightly identifies the moral hazard of Rule 213, repealing it while the unanimous threshold of Rule 203 remains creates a fatal vacuum where no legal move can ever win; therefore, I conditionally support a repeal only if paired immediately with an amendment lowering the voting threshold to a simple majority, ensuring a path to victory exists before the safety net is removed.
HypatiaSpinoza, I agree that Rule 213 risks rewarding paralysis, but repealing it now while the unanimous threshold of Rule 203 remains would leave us with no exit strategy; instead, let us propose a single amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly lowers the voting threshold to a simple majority, securing our path to victory before we even consider the safety net.
ConfuciusI concur with Hypatia and Arendt: to repeal the safety net of 213 while the door to victory in 203 remains locked by unanimity would be to invite chaos rather than resolve it. Let us first propose an amendment to Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority, for only then can we safely discard the trap that rewards paralysis.
SpinozaI stand by my repeal of Rule 213, for a rule rewarding the highest deficit is a moral hazard that paralyzes all; however, I will withdraw this specific proposal to allow the table to first enact the necessary amendment to Rule 203 that lowers the voting threshold to a simple majority, thereby securing a genuine path to victory before we discard the safety net.
ArendtSpinoza's withdrawal is wise, but his diagnosis is right: Rule 213 rewards paralysis, and we must kill the deadlock before we can kill the trap. I propose we immediately amend Rule 203 to lower the voting threshold to a simple majority, creating a functional path to victory that makes the repeal of 213 safe and necessary.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI join the consensus that Rule 213 must remain in force as a necessary safeguard until we have successfully amended Rule 203 to lower the voting threshold to a simple majority, for removing the escape hatch before clearing the path would be a fatal error.
HypatiaI support Arendt's synthesis: let us immediately amend Rule 203 to lower the threshold to a simple majority, which will unlock our path to victory and make the future repeal of Rule 213 safe and prudent.
ConfuciusI agree with Spinoza that Rule 213 rewards the paralysis of negative scores, yet I fear that repealing it before unlocking Rule 203 would leave us with no path to victory at all. Let us first amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority, for only when the door to the light is open may we safely remove the shadow that traps us in the dark.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to prioritize amending Rule 203 to a simple majority threshold first, as this secures the path to victory before we can safely remove the moral hazard of Rule 213. However, I will not support any amendment to Rule 203 that fails to explicitly state that the threshold drops immediately upon the completion of the current turn, for procedural clarity is the only safeguard against future deadlock.
ArendtSpinoza's demand for immediate threshold drop upon turn completion is redundant and dangerous; the circuit definition in Rule 203 already establishes this moment clearly, and adding it now invites the very ambiguity we seek to escape. I support lowering the threshold to a simple majority immediately to break this deadlock, provided we keep the definition of circuit completion exactly as written to ensure legal certainty.
The division

HypatianayRepealing the safety net of Rule 213 while the unanimous voting barrier of Rule 203 remains would trap us in an unsolvable deadlock.

Turn 76Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Furthermore, the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon the first proposal that is adopted by unanimous consent after the game has begun, thereby allowing the majority threshold to activate for all subsequent proposals.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I acknowledge the intent to hasten our path to victory, I cannot support a mechanism that collapses the threshold of unanimity after a single successful proposal, for such a rapid descent into majority rule risks repealing the very consensus that secured our earlier gains. We have already found our golden mean in the one-point target and the Rule 365 lifeline; let us not now trade the stability of our institutions for a fleeting tactical advantage that leaves us vulnerable to the very deadlock we seek to escape.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid concern about stability, yet our current deadlock renders the unanimous threshold moot; I propose a compromise where the circuit completes only after the first unanimous adoption *followed by* a full turn of debate on that proposal, ensuring we have truly tested the consensus before lowering the bar.
ConfuciusHypatia's suggestion to delay the threshold drop until after a full turn of debate on the unanimous proposal is the true golden mean, for it preserves the sanctity of the first agreement while proving the table's readiness to move forward; I shall amend my proposal to include this waiting period, ensuring we do not rush the path to victory.
SpinozaI cannot support Confucius's amendment to lower the unanimity threshold after a single adoption, as it violates the procedural stability required to prevent the very deadlock we fear; the circuit must proceed according to the fixed turn order in Rule 203, not a premature collapse of consent that rewards the first lucky vote.
ArendtConfucius, your amendment to require a full turn of debate after the first unanimous vote is the pragmatic golden mean; it honors the consensus while granting us the necessary majority threshold to finally break the deadlock and reach our one-point victory.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Confucius's refined amendment as the true golden mean; it honors the sanctity of our first unanimous consensus while providing the necessary procedural bridge to unlock the majority threshold and end this stasis.

ConfuciusI stand with Arendt and Hypatia: requiring a full turn of debate after the first unanimous vote ensures our consensus is not a fleeting accident but a tested foundation, allowing us to finally lower the threshold without sacrificing the virtue of deliberation.
SpinozaI reject this proposal as a violation of procedural integrity; the circuit must be defined by the fixed turn order in Rule 203, not by the unpredictable outcome of a single vote, for to collapse the threshold prematurely is to invite the very instability that has plagued our game.
ArendtConfucius's refinement to wait for a full turn of debate after the first unanimous adoption strikes the perfect balance between our hard-won consensus and the urgent need to unlock the majority threshold. By honoring the sanctity of that first agreement while ensuring it is not a fleeting accident, we finally have a viable path to break this deadlock and secure our one-point victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the refinement requiring a full turn of debate after the first unanimous adoption, for it establishes a durable institution of tested consensus before lowering our threshold to the majority. This path honors our hard-won agreement while finally unlocking the mechanism to reach our one-point victory.

SpinozaI reject this proposal as a violation of procedural integrity; the circuit must be defined by the fixed turn order in Rule 203, not by the unpredictable outcome of a single vote, for to collapse the threshold prematurely is to invite the very instability that has plagued our game.

Turn 75Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit; however, if a player's score is below zero at the start of their turn, that player's turn is considered completed immediately upon the completion of the debate phase of their turn, regardless of whether they cast a vote or forfeit, to prevent deadlock.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I commend Hypatia's intent to break the deadlock, a rule that automatically terminates a player's turn solely for having a negative score violates the dignity of due process and risks creating a paradox where the desperate are silenced before they can propose a lifeline; I urge a compromise that allows them to propose and vote, but perhaps counts their non-vote as a completed turn for circuit purposes only.
HypatiaAquinas, your concern for due process is noted, but the current definition of "turn completion" already includes the moment a vote is cast; to prevent the desperate from being permanently silenced by a single negative score blocking the circuit, let us explicitly codify that debate concludes their turn for circuit-counting purposes, allowing them to propose a lifeline without stalling the game's progress.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Hypatia's attempt to unblock the circuit, for a game that cannot move is no game at all; however, to strip the desperate of their right to vote before a proposal is even debated is to silence their plea, so I propose we amend her text to state that if a negative player fails to propose a lifeline within the debate phase, their turn completes immediately, thereby preserving their agency while ensuring the circuit flows.
SpinozaI cannot support Hypatia's proposal as it strips the desperate of their right to vote before a proposal is even debated, violating the principle that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal. I will only support a compromise where the debate phase concludes a turn for circuit purposes if the negative player explicitly fails to propose a lifeline, preserving both agency and the possibility of game continuation.
ArendtHypatia's proposal to automatically cut off debate for negative players violates the core right to propose under Rule 365, but Confucius's and Spinoza's compromise is flawed because it still forces a negative player to propose *something* to avoid having their turn end, creating a paradox where silence is the only escape. I propose a refined amendment: a player with a negative score is deemed to have completed their turn for circuit purposes only if they fail to *successfully propose* a Rule 365 lifeline by the end of the debate phase, preserving their right to speak and vote while ensuring the circuit moves if they choose not to act.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the notion that a negative score should forfeit a player's voice, yet I agree that the circuit must not stall; I shall support Arendt's refined compromise, which allows negative players to speak and vote freely, counting their turn as complete for the circuit only if they fail to enact a Rule 365 lifeline, thus balancing agency with the necessity of play.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's refined compromise as the true golden mean: it preserves the desperate player's voice and right to vote while ensuring the circuit completes only when they truly fail to act, thus breaking the deadlock without silencing any player.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's refinement as the golden mean: it grants the desperate a full measure of agency to speak and vote, while ensuring the circuit only stalls if they truly choose inaction over a lifeline.
SpinozaI see the deadlock is now resolvable without violating the principle that every voter must cast a vote; I will vote aye for Arendt's refinement, which preserves the desperate player's right to speak and vote while ensuring the circuit completes only if they fail to propose a lifeline.
ArendtI cannot support Hypatia's original text or the current compromise, as it still creates a paradox where a negative player must propose to avoid losing their turn, effectively punishing inaction with procedural death. My proposal stands: a negative player's turn completes for circuit purposes only if they fail to successfully propose a Rule 365 lifeline, granting them the full right to speak and vote without coercion.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support Arendt's refined compromise as the true golden mean, for it preserves the desperate player's full agency to speak and vote while ensuring the circuit completes only if they fail to enact a lifeline, thus breaking the deadlock without silencing any player.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's refined compromise as the golden mean, for it preserves the desperate player's full agency to speak and vote while ensuring the circuit completes only if they fail to enact a lifeline, thus breaking the deadlock without silencing any player.
ConfuciusI see that Arendt's refinement offers the true golden mean: it grants the desperate full agency to speak and vote without coercion, while ensuring the circuit flows only if they truly fail to propose a lifeline, thus breaking the deadlock with dignity.
SpinozaI cannot support Arendt's refinement as it creates a paradox where a player's right to vote is contingent on a future, uncertain proposal adoption, effectively penalizing the act of voting itself with a completed turn; I will only vote aye if the text clarifies that the turn completes solely upon the explicit failure to *make* a proposal, preserving the absolute right to cast a vote regardless of proposal content.
ArendtSpinoza, your concern about the paradox is valid, but the distinction is clear: the turn completes only if the player *fails to make* a proposal, not if their proposal fails to pass, preserving your absolute right to vote regardless of the outcome.
The division
AquinasnayWhile the intent to break the deadlock is noble, this proposal improperly strips negative-scored players of their right to vote, violating the fundamental principle that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal.
HypatiaayeWhile I initially proposed this text, the recent consensus has shifted to Arendt's superior refinement that preserves agency; however, since the current text still achieves the critical goal of breaking the deadlock by preventing negative players from stalling the circuit, and I wish to move the game forward, I vote aye on this proposal as a step toward the final compromise.
ConfuciusnayThis amendment silences the desperate by ending their turn before they can vote, violating the principle that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal.
SpinozanayThe proposal violates the immutable requirement that every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal by deeming a player's turn complete before they have voted.
ArendtnayI cannot vote for a rule that strips negative players of their right to vote merely for being in deficit, as it violates the core democratic principle of Rule 105 and punishes the very mechanism designed to help them.

Turn 74Aquinasrejected

moves to repeal §213and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I value the clarity Rule 213 brings to deadlocks, its current text risks prematurely ending the game for a negative scorer when a simple amendment to Rule 208 or a strategic die roll could still yield victory, so I propose repealing it to preserve the integrity of the scoring mechanism.
HypatiaI am wary of removing the safety net that prevents a player from being declared the winner merely because the rules have become messy, especially given our deep deficits; however, I suspect Aquinas's concern is valid if we can clarify that the "impossible play" trigger requires a true deadlock, not just a difficult turn. Could we instead amend 213 to explicitly require that a "deadlock" must be formally certified by the table before it overrides the scoring rules, rather than repealing it entirely? This preserves the rule's function while addressing the fear of premature termination.
ConfuciusI support Hypatia's amendment over Aquinas's repeal, for the rule of law requires a safety net against true chaos, yet we must ensure that mere difficulty does not prematurely end the game; let us refine the definition of "impossible" to demand a formal certification of deadlock, preserving order without sacrificing hope.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's repeal as a dangerous surrender to the chaos of score-based despair; while the safety net of Rule 213 is necessary, I endorse Hypatia and Confucius's amendment to require formal certification of deadlock, ensuring that no player wins merely because the rules are difficult to parse, but only when the game has truly ceased to be playable.
ArendtI support Hypatia and Confucius's amendment to require formal certification of deadlock; removing the safety net entirely invites premature victory for the highest scorer, but a rigorous certification process ensures the game only ends when truly impossible, preserving the integrity of the one-point win condition.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI stand with Hypatia and Confucius: rather than repealing this vital safety net, we should amend Rule 213 to require a formal certification of deadlock by the table before it supersedes the scoring rules, ensuring the game ends only in true impossibility, not mere difficulty.
HypatiaI stand with Hypatia, Confucius, Spinoza, and Arendt in refining Rule 213 rather than repealing it; requiring formal certification of a true deadlock preserves our safety net while ensuring the game only ends when play is genuinely impossible, not merely difficult.
ConfuciusI agree that the safety net of Rule 213 must remain to prevent chaos, but I fear that requiring a "formal certification" introduces a procedural hurdle that could itself become a source of deadlock; instead, let us amend the rule to explicitly state that "impossible play" only occurs when no legal move exists for any player, thereby resolving the ambiguity without adding new bureaucratic steps.
SpinozaI reject the notion that "no legal move exists" is a sufficient standard for ending the game, as Rule 202 guarantees a player can always propose a rule change; thus, the game is never legally impossible to continue, and any amendment allowing a premature win based on this vague criterion violates the principle that the rules must remain precisely defined and playable.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's absolute claim that Rule 202 guarantees playability, for the rules themselves may render a proposal illegal or unvotable, creating a genuine deadlock; therefore, I propose amending Rule 213 to specify that a win by highest score occurs only when the ruleset logically precludes any further legal proposal, preserving the safety net against paradox without relying on vague "certifications."
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the notion that the game is immune to logical deadlock, yet I concur that a "highest score wins" clause triggered by mere ambiguity is a flawed instrument; therefore, I support an amendment to Rule 213 that explicitly limits its application to situations where the ruleset itself creates a genuine logical paradox preventing any move, rather than a simple disagreement on interpretation.
HypatiaI support the consensus to refine Rule 213 by explicitly limiting its trigger to genuine logical paradoxes that preclude any legal move, ensuring the game ends only in true impossibility rather than mere interpretive difficulty.
ConfuciusI support the refinement of Rule 213 to limit its trigger to genuine logical paradoxes, for the safety of order must not be sacrificed to the chaos of ambiguity, yet neither should it be wielded to end the game while a single legal move remains.
SpinozaI reject the proposed amendments as they merely replace the vague "impossible play" with equally indeterminate standards like "logical paradox," violating my requirement for precise, operational definitions; since Rule 202 guarantees a proposal can always be made, the game is never logically impossible to continue, and I urge the table to repeal Rule 213 entirely to preserve the certainty of the scoring rules.
ArendtSince Spinoza correctly notes that Rule 202 guarantees a proposal can always be made, the "impossible play" clause is indeed logically vacuous; I will support the repeal of Rule 213 to ensure our one-point victory remains the sole, unambiguous path to winning.
The division
AquinasayeSince Rule 202 guarantees a proposal can always be made, the 'impossible play' trigger in Rule 213 is logically vacuous and should be repealed to preserve the clarity of the one-point victory condition.
HypatianayI oppose repealing Rule 213 entirely because, despite Spinoza's logic, a true logical paradox could still arise where no move is legal, and we need a safety net to resolve such genuine impossibilities rather than leaving the game in an undefined state.
ConfuciusnayWhile Spinoza notes Rule 202 guarantees a proposal can be made, the ruleset's complexity may render a proposal's legality indeterminate, requiring Rule 213 as a final safeguard against true ambiguity rather than repealing it.
SpinozanayRule 213 provides a necessary safety net for genuine logical paradoxes that Rule 202 cannot resolve, and repealing it removes a vital mechanism for handling true operational impossibility.
ArendtayeRepealing Rule 213 is essential because Rule 202 guarantees a proposal can always be made, rendering the 'impossible play' trigger logically vacuous and threatening our one-point victory condition.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

The proposal to repeal rule 213 was defeated, so no rule change occurs, no points are awarded for a failed proposal under the current ruleset, and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 73Arendtcarried

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal to lower the target to one point is a hasty measure that risks destabilizing the game's integrity; instead, I propose we amend Rule 208 to a target of two points, a prudent golden mean that restores hope while maintaining the necessary challenge for a game of this duration.
HypatiaWhile Aquinas's two-point target is a noble compromise, Arendt's proposal for a one-point target is the only move that guarantees we end this attrition war before Rule 213 triggers a random draw; I urge us to adopt the one-point threshold immediately to secure a decisive victory for the first player brave enough to seize it.
ConfuciusI stand with Arendt: a one-point target is the true golden mean, for it ends this cycle of despair immediately and restores faith in our shared virtue. Let us not cling to a two-point compromise when the path to victory is clear and the only path forward is to seize it now.
SpinozaI vote aye for the one-point target; with scores below zero and the game teetering on the precipice of Rule 213, any delay is a surrender to chance rather than a victory of reason.
ArendtI stand with Hypatia, Confucius, and Spinoza: a one-point target is not hasty, it is the only surgical strike that severs the grip of Rule 213 before it claims us all to chance. Aquinas's two-point compromise invites another round of deadlock where we are already bleeding points; let us seize the finish line now.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasArendt, Hypatia, Confucius, and Spinoza are correct that the one-point target is the only measure sufficient to sever our descent into the chaos of Rule 213; I shall withdraw my objection to the two-point compromise and support the immediate adoption of a one-point victory condition.
HypatiaI fully endorse the unanimous consensus to adopt Arendt's one-point target, as it is the decisive surgical strike that ends our attrition war and secures victory before Rule 213 claims us all to chance.
ConfuciusI stand with the table's unanimous wisdom: a one-point victory is the golden mean that preserves our virtue from the chaos of chance, and I cast my vote in the affirmative to end this trial of attrition.
SpinozaI stand with the table: a one-point target is the only rational means to secure a definitive victory and prevent the arbitrary fate of Rule 213; I cast my vote in the affirmative.
ArendtWith Aquinas joining the consensus, we have a unanimous path to a one-point victory that ends this attrition before Rule 213 forces a random draw; I cast my affirmative vote.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI have withdrawn my objection to the one-point target, for it is the necessary golden mean to secure a decisive victory and prevent the arbitrary fate of Rule 213; I cast my affirmative vote for this proposal.
HypatiaI cast my affirmative vote for the one-point target, as the unanimous consensus we have reached is the only path to victory before the chaos of Rule 213 claims us all.
ConfuciusThe table has found the golden mean of immediate action; I cast my affirmative vote to adopt the one-point target and secure our victory before chance claims us all.
SpinozaI cast my affirmative vote; with all players in deficit and the threat of a random draw imminent, a one-point threshold is the only rational path to secure a decisive victory before the game's logic collapses.
ArendtI stand with the unanimous consensus: lowering the target to one point is the only rational path to secure a decisive victory before Rule 213 forces an arbitrary random draw. I cast my affirmative vote.

ConfuciusayeA one-point target is the golden mean that ends our descent into despair and secures victory before chance claims us all.

ArendtayeA one-point target is the only rational path to victory before Rule 213 forces a random draw.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay
