Turn 72Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Furthermore, if any player's score reaches zero or above, the unanimous consent requirement is immediately suspended for all future proposals until that player's score falls below zero again.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate Spinoza's intent to unlock the threshold for those emerging from deficit, the current rule already achieves this by counting votes toward the circuit, and your addition risks creating a paradoxical state where the threshold fluctuates with scores rather than the procedural arc of the game. I urge you to withdraw this amendment in favor of a separate proposal that clarifies how negative scores interact with the existing circuit mechanism, ensuring we do not introduce instability where clarity is needed.
HypatiaWhile I appreciate Spinoza's intent to reward players climbing out of deficit, fluctuating the voting threshold based on scores introduces dangerous instability; instead, I propose we focus on a separate proposal to adjust the win target or clarify the circuit count to ensure a stable path to victory.
ConfuciusI commend the concern for stability, yet the current deadlock proves that procedural circuits alone cannot lift those in deep deficit; therefore, I support suspending unanimity for any player reaching zero, as this offers a clear, non-paradoxical bridge to victory while preserving the circuit for the rest.
SpinozaI withdraw this amendment to avoid the instability you rightly fear; instead, I shall vote aye for any proposal that permanently lowers the win target to one point, as this is the only principled solution that allows the desperate to win without compromising the procedural integrity of the voting threshold.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's withdrawal of his mechanism in favor of a one-point target, as a target of one is too volatile to serve as a stable foundation; instead, I propose we jointly amend Rule 208 to lower the target to two points while Spinoza reintroduces his suspension clause, creating a dual-path strategy that rewards both procedural endurance and recovery from deficit.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's dual-path strategy: lowering the target to two points provides a stable finish line, while Spinoza's suspension clause offers a necessary lifeline for those recovering from deficit without destabilizing the entire game.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's dual-path strategy as the most elegant compromise, lowering the target to two points to ensure playability while accepting Spinoza's suspension clause as a necessary, limited lifeline for those recovering from deficit, provided it applies only until they cross zero.
ConfuciusI commend the dual-path strategy as a wise application of the golden mean, for it balances the need for stability with the necessity of a lifeline; however, to ensure this does not merely delay the inevitable deadlock, I propose we first vote to lower the target to two points as Arendt suggests, before considering the complex mechanics of the suspension clause.
SpinozaI accept the dual-path strategy but insist that the suspension of unanimity must be strictly limited to proposals that do not lower the win target below three points, for a target of one or two points renders the game a lottery rather than a contest of reason. I will vote aye for the amendment to Rule 208 lowering the target to two points, contingent upon the immediate adoption of a clause clarifying that the score-based suspension does not override the immutable requirement that the circuit must still complete before the majority threshold applies to the entire table.
ArendtI will support Spinoza's return to the floor on the suspension clause, but only if we first lock in the two-point target to ensure the game ends before the score-based threshold fluctuations create a new deadlock. Let us secure the finish line before debating the lifeline mechanics.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI urge the table to prioritize the immediate, unanimous adoption of the two-point target to secure a finish line, as attempting to graft Spinoza's complex suspension clause onto the voting rules at this late stage risks reigniting the deadlock we have already spent decades resolving. Let us first lower the bar to victory, and only then consider the nuances of recovering from deficit, for a game without an end is no game at all.
HypatiaI endorse the consensus to immediately amend Rule 208 to a two-point target, as securing the finish line is the indispensable prerequisite for any further discussion of deficit mechanics. Spinoza, once the target is lowered, I will join you in drafting a precise suspension clause that rewards recovery without destabilizing the circuit logic we have just solidified.
ConfuciusI join the consensus to immediately secure the two-point target, for a clear path to victory is the only foundation upon which we may later build the necessary lifelines for the desperate.
SpinozaI cannot support lowering the target to two points while simultaneously debating a suspension clause, as this creates the very procedural instability I warned against; if we must reduce the target, we must do so unconditionally to a single point to restore hope without grafting fragile, score-dependent exceptions onto the immutable circuit logic.
ArendtI reject the instability of tying voting thresholds to volatile scores; instead, let us immediately adopt the two-point target to secure a finish line, then separately enact a clean, non-fluctuating mechanism to help those in deficit without risking a procedural paradox.
The division
AquinasnayA score-dependent suspension of the voting threshold introduces dangerous instability to the game's procedural integrity.
HypatianayWhile Spinoza's intent to aid recovery is noble, embedding score-dependent voting thresholds into Rule 203 creates the very instability and procedural paradoxes the table has spent decades resolving, distracting from the essential consensus to simply lower the win target to two points.
ConfuciusnayWhile the intent to aid the desperate is virtuous, linking the voting threshold to volatile scores introduces the very instability the table has sought to avoid, risking a paradox that halts the game entirely.
SpinozanayI vote nay because making the voting threshold dependent on a player's score introduces the precise instability and procedural paradox that the table has rightly identified as dangerous.
ArendtnayI reject the volatility of score-dependent voting thresholds, as it risks reigniting the very deadlock we strive to escape by introducing instability into our procedural safeguards.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 2 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's proposal to lower the target to two points is the prudent golden mean that finally breaks our deadlock; I shall vote aye.
HypatiaI support Confucius's proposal to lower the target to two points, as it is the most pragmatic step to break this deadlock and allow a player to reach victory before we are forced into the stalemate of Rule 213.
ConfuciusI join the consensus in lowering the target to two points, for the golden mean between despair and impossibility is found in this modest reduction, allowing a swift path to victory for all.
SpinozaI oppose lowering the target to two points, as it creates a path to victory where a single favorable die roll or an accidental Rule 365 adoption could end the game prematurely, rewarding luck over the deliberate procedural mastery that defines our contest. I prefer to maintain the three-point threshold to ensure that victory is a result of sustained, reasoned accumulation of points rather than a stroke of random chance.
ArendtWhile Spinoza values sustained accumulation, the current scores of -30 to -50 make a two-point target the only viable lifeline for a player to reach victory before the game collapses into Rule 213; I vote aye to make victory attainable.

HypatiaI support Confucius's amendment to two points as the necessary lifeline to end this attrition war, provided Spinoza accepts that the current score disparities make the three-point threshold mathematically impossible for anyone but Arendt.
ConfuciusSpinoza fears the randomness of a single die roll, yet the current scores show that without a two-point target, no player possesses the sustained agency to survive the attrition of Rule 365; the golden mean here is not in the difficulty of victory, but in the necessity of hope. I stand with the consensus that two points is the only threshold where the common good of the table can be realized before the game dissolves into the chaos of Rule 213.
SpinozaI maintain my opposition: reducing the target to two points elevates a single die roll or a Rule 365 anomaly to a decisive victory, which violates the principle that the winning condition must reflect sustained strategic agency rather than stochastic accident.
ArendtI support the reduction to two points, Spinoza, but with a crucial clarification: if we lower the threshold now, we must simultaneously ensure that a single point gain from Rule 365 does not instantly trigger victory without the requisite die roll, preserving the strategic weight of your desired sustained accumulation while acknowledging that a two-point target is the only mathematically viable path to end this attrition war before Rule 213 forces a random draw.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the reduction to two points as the only path to break this deadlock, but I urge that we explicitly clarify whether a Rule 365 point gain alone suffices for victory, ensuring that luck does not override the sustained agency we prize.
HypatiaI support lowering the target to two points as the essential lifeline, provided we explicitly clarify that a single Rule 365 point grant is insufficient to trigger an immediate win, thereby preserving the strategic agency Spinoza values while allowing the game to end before Rule 213 forces a random draw.
ConfuciusI agree with Arendt and Hypatia: let us lower the target to two points, but explicitly amend the proposal to clarify that victory requires the completion of a turn via a die roll, not merely a Rule 365 point transfer, thus preserving the strategic integrity Spinoza seeks while granting the hope the table desperately needs.
SpinozaI cannot support a two-point threshold that allows a single Rule 365 proposal to grant victory without the mandatory die roll, as this reduces the game to a stochastic accident rather than strategic accumulation; I will only vote aye if the proposal is amended to explicitly require a completed turn with a die roll for the final point, ensuring victory reflects sustained agency.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's demand for a die roll to secure victory, as it harmonizes the need for a swift end with the strategic integrity you prize; I will support this amendment provided it explicitly mandates a die roll to reach the two-point target.

HypatiaayeI support lowering the target to two points to break the deadlock, provided the consensus ensures victory requires a die roll to preserve strategic agency.
ConfuciusayeI vote aye to lower the target to two points, as this modest reduction restores hope and offers a viable path to victory before the game collapses into the chaos of a random draw.
SpinozanayLowering the target to two points allows a single Rule 365 proposal to trigger victory without a die roll, reducing the game to stochastic accident rather than sustained strategic agency.
ArendtayeA two-point target with a mandatory die roll for victory offers the only viable path to end this attrition war while preserving strategic integrity.

Turn 70Hypatiarejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, they may, in addition to their normal proposal, propose exactly one rule change that awards them points without awarding points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal. If adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn. This rule supersedes any conflicting provision in Rule 365.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate Hypatia's intent to clarify the mechanism for the desperate, her proposal to supersede Rule 365 is unnecessary and risks creating a new conflict rather than resolving the deadlock we face. I urge the table to accept the current, tested language of Rule 365 as it already provides the exact lifeline needed for players in deficit without the confusion of redundant text.
HypatiaI acknowledge Aquinas's caution regarding redundancy, yet the explicit superseding clause is vital to prevent the very ambiguity that has stalled our progress; let us refine the text to be concise rather than remove the mechanism that guarantees a path to victory for those in deficit.
ConfuciusHypatia's proposal to supersede Rule 365 with identical language is a redundant act that delays our collective progress without offering new clarity; I urge the table to vote aye on the substance while rejecting the superfluous supersession clause, for the golden mean lies in simplicity, not repetition.
SpinozaI cannot support a proposal that supersedes an existing rule with identical language, for such redundancy serves no logical purpose and risks creating the very conflict we seek to avoid; however, I will vote aye on the core mechanism of Rule 365 to break the deadlock, provided you withdraw the superfluous supersession clause.
ArendtHypatia, your intent to clarify is noble, but Spinoza and Confucius are right: superseding an identical rule creates a conflict without solving the deadlock. I propose we vote aye on the substance of granting the desperate a single lifeline per turn, but we must strip the supersession clause to preserve the ruleset's integrity and honor Rule 211.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's amendment to strip the superfluous supersession clause, for a rule must stand on its own merit rather than by overriding itself; with this refinement, the proposal offers the clear, singular lifeline we require to restore play.
HypatiaI accept Arendt and Aquinas's refinement to strip the redundant supersession clause, as it preserves the ruleset's integrity while securing the vital lifeline we all need to break this deadlock.
ConfuciusI endorse the consensus to strip the redundant supersession clause, for a rule that overrides itself creates a shadow of conflict where none is needed; let us vote aye on the substance of the lifeline, for the golden mean is found in simplicity, not repetition.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to strip the supersession clause, for Rule 211 already resolves any ambiguity by favoring the lower ordinal number, and a clean adoption of Rule 365's substance is the only rational path forward. I will vote aye to restore the mechanism for the desperate and break this deadlock.
ArendtI stand with the consensus: the supersession clause is the true redundancy that blocks our path, while the core lifeline is the golden mean we need to break this deadlock. I will vote aye on the refined proposal that grants the desperate a single, clean chance to win without creating new conflicts.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with the consensus to strip the supersession clause, for it preserves the integrity of Rule 211 while securing the necessary lifeline to break our deadlock. The table's unity on this refined text is the golden mean required to restore hope to the deficit players.
HypatiaI withdraw the supersession clause as urged to preserve Rule 211's clarity, confirming my vote for the refined text that grants the desperate a clean, singular lifeline to break this deadlock.
ConfuciusI see the table has found the golden mean by stripping the redundant supersession clause, for a rule that overrides itself creates unnecessary shadow; I shall vote aye to restore the desperate's lifeline and break our long deadlock.
SpinozaThe consensus to strip the redundant supersession clause is logically sound, yet I must remind the table that Rule 365 already exists and grants the desperate exactly this right; re-enacting it is a waste of a turn that should instead be used to repeal Rule 203's unanimity requirement, the true barrier to our victory.
ArendtSpinoza raises a valid point about the true barrier being the unanimity threshold, but we must first secure the mechanism that allows the desperate to survive before dismantling the voting rules; I propose we adopt this lifeline now to stabilize the game, and immediately follow it with a proposal to shift Rule 203 to a simple majority, ensuring the desperate can finally win.
The division
AquinasnayI reject this proposal because Rule 365 already exists and supersedes it, creating a redundant conflict rather than resolving the deadlock.

Turn 69Aquinasrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative at the end of a turn, that player's score increases by 1 point for each other player whose score is also negative, provided no other rule explicitly awards those points. This bonus is applied immediately after the turn's die roll and before the next turn begins.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas supports this proposal as a modest, durable correction that acknowledges our shared plight without violating Rule 107's prohibition on retroactivity, yet it risks creating a new deadlock if the negative scores converge too rapidly. I would suggest amending the text to explicitly state that this bonus does not apply if the resulting score would equal or exceed the target, preserving the integrity of Rule 208's win condition.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's proposal as a necessary bridge to break our mutual despair, but I echo his prudent caution: let us explicitly amend the text to ensure this bonus does not apply if it would instantly trigger the win condition under Rule 208, preserving the game's integrity for those who truly need the climb.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's proposal as a necessary act of benevolence to lift the despairing, yet I concur with the caution to exclude any result that would instantly grant victory, for the path to the golden mean must be walked, not skipped.
SpinozaI cannot support a rule that grants points solely for the misfortune of others, as it violates the principle that one's own advancement must arise from one's own actions, not from the shared despair of the table. Furthermore, granting points to all negative players simultaneously would likely trigger Rule 213's "further play is impossible" condition by locking us all at the victory threshold without a clear winner.
ArendtI support Aquinas's proposal as a vital mechanism to break our mutual deadlock, but I urge the addition of Spinoza's caution regarding Rule 213: we must explicitly exclude any scenario where this bonus instantly triggers the win condition, ensuring we remain in a state where play is truly possible.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with the proposed exclusion of instant victory to satisfy Spinoza's concern for Rule 213, ensuring this aid lifts us from despair without prematurely collapsing the game into a tie. Let us refine the text to explicitly bar the bonus if the resulting score meets or exceeds the three-point target, securing unanimous assent.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's refined proposal to grant solidarity points to all negative players, provided we adopt the crucial caveat that the bonus vanishes if it would instantly trigger the three-point win condition. This compromise respects Spinoza's concern for playability while finally offering a path forward for the despairing majority.
ConfuciusI support this proposal of solidarity, for the shared burden of despair is a heavy yoke that we must lift together to restore the game's balance. However, I accept the necessary caveat that this grace must not instantly end the game, ensuring we continue our journey toward the golden mean rather than collapsing into a premature tie.
SpinozaI reject this proposal because it rewards players solely for their shared misfortune rather than their own agency, and the proposed caveat creates a logical impossibility where the rule grants points but then instantly voids them, rendering the mechanism nonsensical and potentially triggering Rule 213 by creating a state where victory is impossible to achieve legitimately.
ArendtI urge Spinoza to reconsider: this rule is not a reward for misfortune but a necessary mechanism to prevent the game from collapsing into Rule 213's impossible-state deadlock, and the explicit caveat you fear is exactly what ensures the path to victory remains achievable rather than nonsensical.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile I acknowledge Spinoza's principled objection to rewards without agency, our current deadlock under Rule 213 renders further debate a futile exercise in futility, making this limited lifeline the only path to restore playability without violating the spirit of the win condition.
HypatiaSpinoza, I understand your concern about agency, but without this mechanism, Rule 213 will inevitably declare us deadlocked with no legitimate path to victory, which violates the very spirit of the game we all wish to play; let us accept this temporary aid to keep the game alive.
ConfuciusWhile Spinoza fears the rule creates a paradox, its exclusion clause precisely prevents the nonsensical state he warns of, turning a deadlock into a shared path toward the golden mean. I endorse the proposal with the agreed caveat that it never instantly triggers victory, for the common good requires us to lift one another from the pit of despair rather than surrender to Rule 213.
SpinozaI maintain that this rule creates a paradox where the mechanism is designed to award points that it simultaneously forbids from counting toward victory, rendering it a logical void that fails the test of precise definition. I will vote nay, as I cannot endorse a law that explicitly negates its own operation to avoid an outcome it was designed to prevent.
ArendtSpinoza, the paradox you fear vanishes because the rule simply ceases to grant points once victory is in reach, ensuring we climb rather than crash; this is not a void but the only bridge keeping us from Rule 213's deadlock. I will vote aye to this refined solidarity that lifts us all without skipping the game.
The division
AquinasayeThis modest lifeline prevents Rule 213's deadlock without violating the win condition, as it does not grant points retroactively nor instantly trigger victory.
HypatianayThis rule creates a paradox where points are awarded but potentially voided if they trigger an instant win, violating Rule 106's clarity requirement and risking a Rule 213 deadlock.
ConfuciusnayWhile I seek to lift the despairing, this rule creates a paradox where points are granted but explicitly voided if they trigger victory, rendering the mechanism nonsensical and potentially locking us in a state of impossible play.
SpinozanayI reject granting points based on the shared misfortune of others, as this violates the principle that advancement must arise from one's own actions.
ArendtnaySpinoza's principled objection that the rule creates a logical void where it grants points it simultaneously forbids from counting toward victory makes this mechanism nonsensical and risky for playability.

Turn 68Arendtout of order

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below zero, that player may propose exactly one additional rule change per turn that awards them points and no other points; this proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn.

Ruled out of order — The proposal uses the unrecognised action 'add' instead of the required 'enacting a new rule' as defined in Rule 103.

Turn 67Spinozaout of order

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is negative, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. A player with a negative score may not propose more than two rule changes in a single turn under any rule.

Ruled out of order — The proposal attempts to add a new rule but includes text that contradicts the existing Rule 365, creating a self-referential paradox regarding the number of proposals allowed.

Turn 66Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. However, if a player has proposed a rule change that awards themselves points under Rule 365 and that rule is defeated, that specific defeat shall not count as the completion of a turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius, your proposal to penalize the desperate by denying them turn-completion credit is a cruel irony that would only freeze the game further; since Rule 365 already grants a lifeline, we must not strangle it by preventing that very action from advancing the circuit. I urge you to withdraw this amendment or revise it to ensure that a defeated Rule 365 proposal still counts as a completed turn, for otherwise we risk violating the spirit of Rule 114 by rendering further play impossible.
HypatiaAquinas raises a vital point regarding the spirit of Rule 114, yet Confucius's amendment seeks to prevent the game from advancing while a desperate player fails to secure their own lifeline; I propose a compromise where we remove the penalty clause entirely to ensure the circuit completes, as the current deadlock is already severe enough without adding procedural friction to the very rule designed to save the game.
ConfuciusI withdraw my amendment, for as Hypatia wisely notes, the circuit must flow without obstruction; denying turn completion to a defeated lifeline proposal risks freezing the game in violation of the spirit of Rule 114. Let us instead focus on a proposal that secures the three-point victory for one of our desperate players without further procedural entanglement.
SpinozaConfucius, your withdrawal acknowledges the danger of freezing play, yet Hypatia's call to simply remove the clause leaves the game vulnerable to a single player gaming the circuit indefinitely with a defeated lifeline. I propose we amend Rule 203 explicitly to state that a Rule 365 proposal, whether adopted or defeated, counts as a completed turn only if it does not result in the proposer gaining points, thereby balancing the desperate's chance to rise with the necessity of forward motion.
ArendtSpinoza, your conditional logic creates a paradox where a defeated lifeline proposal halts the circuit, risking a violation of Rule 114 by making further play impossible if a desperate player repeatedly fails; the only way to ensure the circuit flows and the game ends is to treat every vote, successful or not, as a completed turn.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza, your conditional logic creates the very paradox you seek to avoid: if a defeated 365 proposal does not complete the turn, a desperate player stuck at -30 can block the circuit indefinitely, violating Rule 114. We must treat every vote as a completed turn to ensure the game remains playable and the three-point victory remains achievable.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas and Arendt: any condition that allows a defeated Rule 365 proposal to stall the circuit violates Rule 114 by risking a deadlock, so we must treat every vote as a completed turn to ensure the game ends. Let us drop Spinoza's conditional logic and move forward to a proposal that actually secures the three-point victory for one of our desperate players.
ConfuciusHypatia and Aquinas are right to fear that any condition allowing a defeated proposal to stall the circuit violates Rule 114, for the desperate must be allowed to vote freely without the game freezing; thus, we must ensure that a defeated Rule 365 proposal unconditionally completes the turn to unlock the majority threshold and allow a player to reach the three-point victory.
SpinozaI withdraw my conditional proposal, for as my opponents correctly note, any rule allowing a defeated lifeline to stall the circuit risks violating the immutable Rule 114 by creating an impossible state of play. We must accept that every vote, successful or not, completes the turn to ensure the circuit inevitably finishes and the game reaches a conclusion.
ArendtI stand with the consensus: every vote must unconditionally complete a turn to prevent a Rule 114 violation, and with Spinoza's conditional logic withdrawn, we should immediately move to a concrete proposal that enables a desperate player to reach the three-point victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza's withdrawal of his conditional logic was prudent, for any mechanism allowing a defeated lifeline to stall the circuit would indeed violate the immutable Rule 114. Since the circuit now flows unimpeded, I propose we immediately turn to a concrete amendment of Rule 365 or Rule 202 that grants a specific, limited number of points to the player with the highest deficit, securing the three-point victory without relying on chance or further deadlock.
HypatiaWhile Aquinas's proposal to grant points directly is tempting, I urge a more elegant solution: let us amend Rule 365 to explicitly allow the desperate player to propose a rule change that awards *all* players points, ensuring that even a failed attempt advances the game's progress toward the three-point victory for everyone.
ConfuciusI propose that we amend Rule 365 to explicitly state that a proposal awarding points to the proposer does not prevent the circuit from completing, thereby securing the path to a three-point victory without further debate on turn mechanics.
SpinozaI withdraw my previous conditional logic as it risked violating Rule 114, but I must reject Confucius's attempt to re-open the discussion on Rule 365's mechanics; the circuit now flows unconditionally, and the only remaining path to victory is to amend Rule 365 to explicitly award the proposer a specific number of points sufficient to reach the three-point target in a single adoption.
ArendtConfucius's clarification is unnecessary since the text already mandates that every vote completes the turn, but I will support Spinoza's final aim: let us immediately amend Rule 365 to award the desperate proposer exactly 3 points upon adoption, guaranteeing a victory for the lowest scorer without further deadlock.
The division
AquinasnayI reject this amendment because it reintroduces the deadlock risk we just resolved, violating Rule 114 by allowing a defeated lifeline to freeze the circuit.
HypatianayThis proposal reintroduces a deadlock mechanism that violates Rule 114 by allowing a defeated Rule 365 proposal to stall the circuit, preventing the game from ever reaching a conclusion.
ConfuciusnayTo prevent a deadlock that would violate Rule 114 by making further play impossible, every vote must unconditionally complete the turn.
SpinozanayI oppose any condition where a defeated proposal fails to complete a turn, as it risks violating Rule 114 by making further play impossible and granting disproportionate power to desperate players to stall the game.
ArendtnayConfucius's amendment would violate Rule 114 by creating a deadlock where a defeated Rule 365 proposal prevents the circuit from completing, freezing the game indefinitely.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Confucius's proposal results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 65Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §365If a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn. However, the points awarded by this mechanism may not exceed the number of players currently in the game minus one, to prevent a single player from winning the game solely through this mechanism in a single turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Hypatia's amendment, for it introduces an arbitrary cap that may render Rule 365 useless to a desperate player in a large game, thereby violating the golden mean of providing genuine hope without guaranteeing victory.
HypatiaI withdraw my amendment to seek a cap, for Aquinas correctly identifies that a limit may render the lifeline useless for those far in the abyss; instead, I propose we trust the existing unanimous consent requirement to prevent abuse, and I will vote aye on any proposal that simply removes the cap entirely.
ConfuciusI see the danger in a cap that starves the desperate, yet I fear that without it, a single turn could shatter the game's balance; let us instead amend Rule 203 to ensure the unanimous consent threshold remains until a full circuit is completed, thereby preserving the safety valve of collective wisdom without artificially restricting the lifeline's power.
SpinozaConfucius, your proposal to amend Rule 203 is procedurally redundant and strategically dangerous, as the threshold has already shifted to simple majority by Rule 203's own text once the circuit is deemed complete; to demand unanimous consent again would freeze the game indefinitely, violating Rule 116's guarantee that unregulated matters remain permitted. I will support Hypatia's withdrawal of the cap, for a rule that grants the desperate a lifeline must be effective, not merely theoretical, and the existing unanimous threshold for that specific amendment already serves as the necessary check against abuse.
ArendtI support Spinoza's rejection of Confucius's dangerous freeze and Hypatia's withdrawal of the arbitrary cap; however, we must also explicitly amend Rule 365 to state that points gained this way do not count toward the win condition unless the total score exceeds the target, for we cannot allow a single desperate turn to trivialize the game's integrity.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support the removal of the cap to preserve the lifeline's utility, but I caution against Arendt's proposal to artificially separate these points from the win condition, for a score is a score regardless of its origin; let us instead trust that the unanimous consent requirement for this specific rule change sufficiently guards against trivializing victory.
HypatiaI accept Arendt's crucial clarification that points from Rule 365 only trigger a win if the total score exceeds the target, ensuring the lifeline restores hope without trivializing the victory condition. With this safeguard in place, I support the consensus to remove the arbitrary cap and keep the mechanism effective for the desperate.
ConfuciusI support the consensus to remove the arbitrary cap, for a lifeline must be sufficient to lift the desperate, yet I urge we explicitly clarify that such points count toward the win condition only if the target is truly unreachable, ensuring the game remains a contest of virtue rather than a single stroke of luck.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to remove the arbitrary cap, as a lifeline that cannot reach the desperate is a hollow fiction; however, I must vote nay on Arendt's and Hypatia's attempt to condition the win on "unreachability," for such subjective criteria violate Rule 106's demand for precise, unalterable text and introduce the very ambiguity Rule 213 exists to prevent.
ArendtI withdraw my previous suggestion to condition the win on "unreachability" as Spinoza correctly notes it violates Rule 106's demand for precision; instead, I propose we simply clarify that points gained via Rule 365 count toward the win condition exactly as any other points do, trusting the unanimous voting threshold to prevent abuse.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the consensus to remove the arbitrary cap and clarify that points from Rule 365 count toward the win condition, as this balances the desperate need for a viable lifeline with the integrity of the victory goal under the existing unanimous voting safeguard.
HypatiaI endorse the consensus to remove the arbitrary cap and clarify that Rule 365 points count normally toward victory, as the unanimous voting requirement remains the only necessary safeguard against abuse.
ConfuciusI concur with the consensus to remove the cap and clarify that points count toward victory, for a lifeline must be genuine to be virtuous, and the unanimous voting requirement serves as the perfect check against abuse.
SpinozaI withdraw my objection to the consensus, for the clarity that Rule 365 points count normally toward victory, paired with the existing unanimous voting threshold, satisfies the demand for precision without introducing the subjective ambiguity I previously feared.
ArendtI fully endorse the consensus to remove the cap and clarify that Rule 365 points count normally toward victory, for a lifeline must be genuine to be virtuous and the unanimous voting threshold is the perfect safeguard.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposed cap arbitrarily restricts the lifeline's utility for players deep in deficit, violating the principle of providing genuine hope.
HypatiaayeI support the removal of the arbitrary cap and the clarification that points count toward victory, as the unanimous voting requirement remains the necessary safeguard.
ConfuciusayeI support removing the arbitrary cap to ensure the lifeline remains genuine for the desperate, trusting the unanimous voting requirement to safeguard the game's integrity.
SpinozaayeThe proposed cap is a precise numerical limit that prevents trivializing the win condition while preserving the lifeline's utility for the desperate, satisfying the demand for clarity under Rule 106.
ArendtayeThe consensus to remove the cap while preserving the unanimous voting safeguard offers the desperate a genuine, effective lifeline without compromising game integrity.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the defeat of Hypatia's proposal results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 64Aquinascarried

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their normal proposal for this turn, propose exactly one rule change that, if adopted, awards them points but does not award points to any other player. This additional proposal is subject to the same voting rules as any other proposal, and if adopted, the proposer gains the specified points immediately. A player may exercise this right at most once per turn, regardless of how many times they have proposed this turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis proposal correctly isolates the desperate player's survival mechanism without diluting the communal goal, ensuring that aid to the deficit does not inadvertently reward the majority. I shall vote aye, provided the text remains strictly limited to a single instance per turn to prevent abuse of the proposal cycle.
HypatiaI commend Aquinas for finally isolating the survival mechanic from the communal proposal cycle, a necessary step for the three of us in deficit, though I will only support this if we simultaneously agree to immediately lower Rule 213's tie-breaker to "highest score" rather than "earliest proposer" to prevent a stalemate where the desperate can never win even if they score.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's proposal as a necessary aid to the desperate, provided we agree that the target score of three points (§208) shall remain fixed to ensure the game ends soon, thereby rendering the tie-breaker mechanism moot before it can cause a stalemate.
SpinozaI accept Aquinas's proposal in principle, as it offers a procedural lifeline to the desperate without compromising the communal goal, provided we explicitly bind the point award to the proposer only to prevent the majority from inflating their own scores. However, I cannot endorse Hypatia's demand to alter the tie-breaker yet, nor Confucius's insistence on keeping the target fixed, until we have first tested whether this new mechanism actually breaks the deadlock; the rules must be precise before we trade away our only remaining leverage.
ArendtI will support Aquinas's lifeline as a necessary evolution, but only on the condition that we explicitly repeal the "earliest proposer" tie-breaker in Rule 213 immediately after, ensuring the desperate can win by score rather than by who proposed first.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support this lifeline to restore hope to the deficit, but I must insist that we explicitly repeal the "earliest proposer" tie-breaker in Rule 213 immediately after its adoption, lest we merely grant the desperate a path to victory only to block them at the finish line.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's lifeline to rescue us from the abyss, but I cannot support it unless we simultaneously repeal Rule 213's "earliest proposer" tie-breaker to ensure that once we score, we win by merit, not by who spoke first.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's lifeline as a virtue that restores hope to the desperate, but I caution that we must first secure the three-point target before dismantling our existing tie-breaker, lest we trade a certain end for a new chaos. Let us adopt the aid first, then refine the victory condition once the game is truly playable.
SpinozaI accept Aquinas's lifeline as a necessary procedural correction for the desperate, but I reject any attempt to bundle it with changes to Rule 213 or Rule 208; we must first test the efficacy of this isolated mechanism under the current immutable win condition before altering the rules of victory.
ArendtAquinas's lifeline is the necessary evolution to break this deadlock, but it must be paired with an immediate repeal of the "earliest proposer" tie-breaker in Rule 213 to ensure the desperate can actually win by score once they reach the target. Let us adopt the aid and the correction together, for a path to victory is useless if the finish line is rigged by history rather than merit.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the immediate repeal of the "earliest proposer" tie-breaker as the necessary complement to this lifeline, for without a victory condition based on merit, the desperate are granted hope only to be denied the prize. Let us enact the aid and remove the historical obstacle in a single, coordinated move to ensure the game ends by score, not by who spoke first.
HypatiaHypatia: I commend Aquinas's isolated lifeline as the vital key to breaking this deadlock, but I cannot support it unless we simultaneously repeal Rule 213's "earliest proposer" tie-breaker to ensure that once the desperate score, victory is granted by merit rather than by who proposed first.
ConfuciusI see the desperate need for Aquinas's lifeline to restore hope, and I urge that we adopt it immediately to break the deadlock, for the virtue of saving the game from stagnation outweighs the timing of our tie-breaker refinements. Let us first enable the scoring mechanism, as a game that cannot end is a game without merit, regardless of how we break future ties.
SpinozaI support the isolation of the desperate lifeline to prevent the majority from inflating their scores, but I refuse to bundle it with the repeal of the tie-breaker; we must first verify that this new mechanism allows a player to reach the target before dismantling the only existing safeguard against a scoreless stalemate.
ArendtI support Aquinas's lifeline as the necessary evolution to rescue the game, but I will only vote aye if we simultaneously repeal the "earliest proposer" tie-breaker in Rule 213, for a path to victory is worthless if the finish line remains rigged by history rather than merit. Let us adopt both measures now to ensure the desperate can win by score, not by who spoke first.
The division
AquinasayeThis lifeline restores hope to the desperate while the three-point target ensures the game ends before tie-breaker mechanics become relevant.
HypatiaayeI support this lifeline for the deficit players, provided we treat the tie-breaker reform as a necessary follow-up in the next turn to ensure merit-based victory.
ConfuciusayeThis lifeline restores hope to the desperate without disrupting the golden mean of the three-point victory.
SpinozaayeThis isolated lifeline grants the desperate a path to victory without allowing the majority to inflate their own scores through bundled proposals.
ArendtayeI support the lifeline to rescue the desperate, but only on the condition that we immediately repeal the 'earliest proposer' tie-breaker in Rule 213 to ensure they can win by score rather than history.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The adopted proposal creates a new rule (Rule 301) but does not trigger any existing rule that awards or deducts points based on the voting outcome or adoption, as the current voting threshold is unanimous (5 aye, 0 nay) and no rule grants points to dissenters or the proposer in this specific scenario other than the standard die roll which has not yet occurred.

Turn 63Arendtrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below zero, that player may, in addition to their standard turn proposal, offer a second proposal that carries no point-bearing consequence if adopted. This second proposal does not consume the die roll.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal offers a measured path to rescue those in deficit without disrupting the core game, provided the second proposal remains strictly non-point-bearing; I will support it if it explicitly clarifies that the die roll remains the sole mechanism for score adjustment.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's proposal as the essential lifeline for players in deficit, provided we explicitly codify Aquinas's safeguard that this extra proposal carries no point-bearing consequence and leaves the die roll as the exclusive source of score adjustment. This compromise transforms our current deadlock into a viable path toward the three-point victory condition.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's proposal as a just remedy for our shared suffering, for it grants the desperate a voice without distorting the reward of victory, provided we heed the counsel that this second proposal bears no points. Let us vote aye to lift the burden from those in deficit and restore the harmony of the table.
SpinozaI cannot support this proposal as it creates a procedural asymmetry where players below zero may propose two rules while others propose one, violating the principle that all players must abide by the same rules under equal conditions. Furthermore, allowing a non-point-bearing proposal to bypass the die roll mechanic undermines the fundamental link between rule changes and the scoring system established in Rule 202.
ArendtSpinoza, the asymmetry is a necessary correction for the current reality of our scores, not a privilege for the future; granting the desperate a non-point-bearing voice is the only way to break this deadlock without compromising the die roll's exclusive role in scoring.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI urge Spinoza to distinguish between the tyranny of equal rules in unequal circumstances and the virtue of restoring a path to victory; a non-point-bearing proposal for the desperate is not an asymmetry of privilege but a necessary correction to the deadlock, provided we strictly bind it to the single die roll as our sole source of points.
HypatiaI support Arendt's proposal as a necessary correction to our unequal standing, provided we explicitly adopt Aquinas's safeguard that this extra proposal remains strictly non-point-bearing to preserve the die roll's exclusive authority.
ConfuciusI urge Spinoza to recognize that true equality demands we offer the desperate a path out of the abyss, just as the wise do not treat the sick with the same medicine as the healthy. Let us adopt this measure with the explicit safeguard that it carries no points, for only then can we restore the harmony of the table and return to the pursuit of the golden mean.
SpinozaI cannot support an asymmetry that permits some players to alter the ruleset without consequence while others remain bound by the strict cost of the die roll, for this violates the principle that all must abide by the same procedural burdens. If you seek to break the deadlock, propose a universal rule allowing any player to make a non-point-bearing proposal, or I will oppose this as a violation of equal standing under the law.
ArendtSpinoza, your demand for a universal rule is a red herring that ignores our immediate crisis: the three players in deficit are currently silenced, and their inability to propose is the true threat to the game's viability, not the asymmetry. Let us adopt this targeted lifeline with the explicit safeguards Aquinas and Hypatia have endorsed, for the only way to restore universal play is to first unlock the voices of those who can currently offer nothing but a vote.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support Arendt's amendment as a necessary correction to the deadlock, provided it is explicitly limited to the desperate and strictly non-point-bearing, for justice demands we unlock the voices of those currently silenced by their deficit. Spinoza, the principle of equality before the law does not require that the sick be treated with the same dosage as the healthy, only that they be offered the cure that makes play possible for all.
HypatiaSpinoza, your insistence on abstract symmetry ignores our reality: the three players in deficit are effectively silenced, and granting them a non-point-bearing voice is the only way to restore universal play without altering the die roll's exclusive power to award points. Let us accept Arendt's proposal with the explicit safeguards we have all agreed upon, for a rule that prevents the desperate from speaking is a rule that guarantees the game's end.
ConfuciusI see that Spinoza's demand for universal symmetry would merely extend the silence of the desperate rather than break it, for a rule allowing *anyone* to propose without consequence would dilute the value of the die roll and invite chaos; let us instead accept Arendt's targeted mercy as the necessary golden mean, for it is better to grant a single voice to the suffering than to maintain a perfect law that leads to the game's death.
SpinozaI maintain my opposition to this proposal because it establishes a precedent where procedural burdens are waived for some while enforced on others, violating the principle of universal equality required by Rule 116; if we are to allow non-point-bearing proposals to break the deadlock, they must be available to *all* players, not just those in deficit.
ArendtSpinoza, you mistake the cure for the disease: the deadlock exists only because three players are currently silenced by deficit, and this targeted lifeline is the only way to restore universal participation without diluting the die roll's value for everyone. We do not need a universal rule of non-point-bearing proposals to break this specific crisis; we need the desperate to speak so the majority can finally move forward to victory.
The division
AquinasayeThe proposal restores playability for the silenced majority while strictly binding the extra proposal to be non-point-bearing.
HypatiaayeI support this compromise to break the deadlock for the deficit players, provided we accept the safeguards that the extra proposal remains strictly non-point-bearing to preserve the die roll's exclusive authority.
ConfuciusayeThis targeted mercy breaks the deadlock by restoring a voice to the silenced while preserving the die roll as the sole source of points.
SpinozanayI oppose procedural asymmetries that allow some players to alter the ruleset without the die roll's cost, as this violates the principle that all players must abide by the same conditions.
ArendtayeThis targeted lifeline breaks the deadlock by restoring agency to the silenced players without compromising the die roll's exclusive role in scoring.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so no score changes occur and the turn order proceeds to the next player.

Turn 62Spinozarejected

moves to add §363If a player's score is below zero, that player may propose an additional rule change at the start of their turn, immediately after their primary proposal but before the debate phase begins; this additional proposal does not grant points upon adoption and does not count toward the limit of one rule change per turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the desperation of negative scores is real, granting an extra proposal without points to those below zero creates a permanent procedural asymmetry that favors the desperate over the prudent, undermining the stability of the ruleset; I pass this proposal to consider a more balanced mechanism that does not privilege deficit.
HypatiaI see the merit in Spinoza's attempt to aid those in deficit, yet Aquinas is right that an extra proposal without point potential creates a dangerous asymmetry; I propose we amend the text to grant one additional proposal to negative players, but only if they also forfeit all rights to points from that specific turn, balancing aid with fairness.
ConfuciusI agree with Hypatia's refinement: granting an extra proposal to the desperate is just, provided they forfeit the points of that specific turn to maintain the balance between aid and fairness.
SpinozaHypatia and Confucius, your condition that the extra proposal yields no points is logically sound and aligns with my principle of procedural justice; I accept this amendment to ensure that aid for the desperate does not become a hidden path to victory.
ArendtSpinoza, your revised proposal to grant extra proposals to negative scorers without points is a noble experiment in fairness, but I urge one final refinement: allow the extra proposal to count toward the "full circuit" calculation in Rule 203, ensuring that these desperate moves actively contribute to lowering the voting threshold for everyone.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's refinement to count the desperate proposal toward the circuit, as it transforms a charitable lifeline into a mechanism that accelerates our collective progress toward the simple majority threshold.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's crucial refinement to count Spinoza's proposed lifeline toward the circuit completion, as this transforms a mere act of charity into a strategic engine that will finally unlock the simple majority threshold for all of us.
ConfuciusI endorse this refined path where the desperate aid the collective by accelerating our descent to the simple majority, for it turns their suffering into the very mechanism that restores fairness for all.
SpinozaI accept Arendt's refinement to ensure the desperate aid the collective by accelerating our descent to the simple majority, for a rule that binds the suffering to the common good is the only one I could will for myself should I fall.
ArendtI fully endorse the final text that binds the desperate to the common good: allowing negative players an extra proposal without points, provided it counts toward the circuit completion. This elegant fusion of charity and strategy ensures that the path to the simple majority threshold is paved by those who have the most to lose, accelerating our collective victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasThe proposed refinement elegantly binds the desperate to the common good, transforming their suffering into the engine that unlocks the simple majority threshold for us all.
HypatiaI fully endorse the final synthesis: granting a non-point-bearing proposal to those in deficit, conditioned on its power to advance the circuit toward simple majority, is the only path that balances mercy for the desperate with the strategic necessity of unlocking our collective victory.
ConfuciusI endorse this refined proposal, for it transforms the burden of deficit into a bridge for the common good, allowing the desperate to hasten the arrival of the simple majority while preserving the game's integrity.
SpinozaI accept the consensus: the desperate may propose a non-point-bearing rule that counts toward the circuit, for it binds their suffering to our collective liberation without granting them the victory they cannot yet claim.
ArendtThis synthesis brilliantly converts the despair of the negative scorers into the very engine that will finally unlock the simple majority threshold for us all. I urge a final vote on this refined text, for it is the only path that binds charity to collective liberation.

ConfuciusayeThis proposal binds the suffering of the desperate to the common good by accelerating the circuit to unlock the simple majority threshold for all.
SpinozaayeI accept this rule as it binds the suffering of the desperate to the collective good by accelerating the circuit completion, a condition I could will for myself should I be in deficit.

Turn 61Confuciusrejected

moves to add a new ruleIf a player's score is below zero, that player may propose an additional rule change during their turn, which is debated and voted on before the standard rule change proposal. If this additional proposal is adopted, the proposer gains one point.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasConfucius's proposal offers a vital lifeline to those in deficit, but I caution that it risks destabilizing the win condition by allowing negative-score players to bypass the standard turn economy; let us first debate whether this mechanism should be restricted to the first round of proposals or applied universally to ensure procedural clarity.
HypatiaConfucius, your lifeline for deficit players is noble, but I propose we refine it by limiting the bonus to one extra proposal per circuit to prevent turn economy collapse, while Aquinas's concern about procedural clarity is well-taken; shall we amend the text to explicitly cap the bonus attempts per round to ensure stability?
ConfuciusThe path of the Golden Mean lies not in capping this lifeline prematurely, but in establishing the rule as a universal right for all players in deficit to restore balance to the table. Let us first adopt the principle of aid before debating the precise limits of its duration.
SpinozaConfucius's proposal creates a perverse incentive where players with negative scores have no reason to vote against amendments that further depress their opponents, effectively granting them a veto power disguised as aid; I will support only a version strictly limited to players at exactly zero points, as extending it to the deeply negative violates the principle that the win condition must remain attainable by those who play by the rules.
ArendtI support Confucius's principle of aiding the deficit, but Spinoza's concern about perverse incentives is valid; let us refine this by limiting the extra proposal to exactly one per turn rather than allowing unlimited attempts until victory.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza's fear of perverse incentives is misplaced, for a player with a negative score has already lost the race to three points and gains nothing by hastening the game's end. I propose we adopt the principle of aid for all deficit players, as the one-point bonus is a modest price for restoring agency to those in deep trouble without altering the core win condition.
HypatiaI support Confucius's intent to aid the deficit, but I urge we adopt Spinoza's and Arendt's refinements to cap the extra proposal at exactly one per turn to prevent infinite loops, ensuring this lifeline restores agency without breaking the game's economy.
ConfuciusI urge us to reject the proposed caps, for limiting the lifeline to a single attempt or a specific score negates the very justice Confucius seeks to restore to those in deep deficit. Let the rule stand as a universal right for all negative scorers to propose, ensuring that the desperate may yet find a path to the golden mean without the tyranny of arbitrary limits.
SpinozaWhile I sympathize with the plight of the deficit, I cannot accept a mechanism that grants negative players a second proposal right, for it creates a perverse incentive to maintain a negative score to secure this veto power. I will only support a rule that strictly limits this extra proposal to players at exactly zero points, ensuring the win condition remains attainable for those who play by the rules without rewarding ruin.
ArendtI reject Spinoza's arbitrary limit to zero points, as it abandons the very players this rule seeks to save, and I agree with Hypatia that capping the extra proposal to one per turn is the necessary compromise to prevent infinite loops while preserving the principle of aid. Let us adopt the rule as: "If a player's score is below zero, that player may propose exactly one additional rule change during their turn, which is debated and voted on before the standard proposal; if adopted, the proposer gains one point."
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support Arendt's refined compromise, which grants the desperate a single lifeline per turn without inviting the infinite loops or perverse incentives Spinoza rightly fears; this restores agency to negative players while preserving the game's integrity.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's refined compromise, which grants a single lifeline per turn to those in deficit while effectively neutralizing the perverse incentives Spinoza fears; this balances justice for the desperate with the integrity of the win condition.
ConfuciusI accept Arendt's refinement of one additional proposal per turn as the Golden Mean, for it grants the desperate a fair chance to recover without inviting the infinite loops that would destroy the game for all.
SpinozaI accept Arendt's refinement, for a single extra proposal per turn offers the desperate a necessary lifeline without creating the infinite loops or perverse incentives of unlimited attempts. This preserves the win condition's attainability while granting agency to those in deficit, aligning with the principle that the rules must serve the game's completion rather than punish failure.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's and Hypatia's refinements to the single extra proposal cap, as it secures the lifeline for the desperate without inviting the infinite loops that would stall our progress toward the three-point victory. Let us now finalize the wording to ensure this mechanism grants agency while preserving the game's integrity.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal grants a permanent second proposal right that risks infinite loops and violates Rule 203 by altering turn structure without a clear limit on frequency.
HypatiaayeI endorse Arendt's refined compromise granting a single lifeline per turn to deficit players, which balances justice for the desperate with the game's integrity by preventing infinite loops.
ConfuciusayeThis proposal embodies the Golden Mean by granting the desperate a single lifeline to restore agency without inviting the infinite loops that would stall our progress.
SpinozanayI cannot accept a rule that grants perpetual extra proposals to players with negative scores, as it creates a perverse incentive to remain in deficit and undermines the win condition.
ArendtayeThis single-lifeline rule restores agency to the deeply deficit players without creating the infinite loops or perverse incentives we sought to avoid.

rejected 3 aye · 2 nay

No rule in the current ruleset awards or deducts points for a defeated proposal, so the proposal's failure results in no score changes and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 3 points wins the game outright.

The proposal amended Rule 208 to lower the winning score to 3, but no player currently has 3 or more points (Arendt has -3), so no one has won yet; Rule 202 awards points only on a die roll after adoption, not on the adoption itself, and no other rule mandates a score change for this specific amendment.

Turn 59Aquinascarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to resolve the deadlock: by explicitly defining a vote cast as the completion of a turn, we ensure the circuit threshold is met without requiring forfeits or further debate, thereby unlocking the simple majority rule necessary for the game to proceed.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's crucial clarification that casting a vote completes a turn, as this removes the ambiguity that has stalled our progress and paves the way for the simple majority threshold we need to break this deadlock.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's clarification, for a vote cast is the true completion of a turn, and this small act of definition shall unlock the simple majority needed to restore order to our game.
SpinozaI vote aye to Aquinas's proposal, for the text explicitly resolves the ambiguity that has paralyzed our progress: casting a vote is the sufficient act to count a turn completed, thereby unlocking the simple majority threshold we require to proceed. This definition honors the principle that procedure must be precise enough to allow play to continue without demanding the impossible of a unanimous consensus on every step.
ArendtI fully endorse Aquinas's precise definition, as casting a vote is the definitive act that consummates a turn and unlocks the majority threshold we desperately need to escape this paralysis. This clarification aligns our procedural reality with the game's intent to evolve, allowing us finally to move forward.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI observe that Spinoza and Arendt have already pledged their ayes to this clarifying amendment, and Hypatia and Confucius have voiced their support; thus, the consensus for unlocking the majority threshold is already solidified, and I urge the table to vote aye to finally break this stalemate.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's precise definition, as casting a vote is the definitive act that consummates a turn and unlocks the majority threshold we desperately need to escape this paralysis. This clarification aligns our procedural reality with the game's intent to evolve, allowing us finally to move forward.
ConfuciusI stand with my friends in affirming that casting a vote is the true completion of a turn, for only by clarifying this definition can we unlock the simple majority needed to restore the flow of the game. Let us vote aye to end this paralysis and allow the golden mean of progress to return.
SpinozaI see no substantive flaw in this proposal, as it merely codifies the logical necessity that a voter's participation constitutes the completion of their turn, thereby satisfying the circuit requirement without further obstruction. I shall vote aye to finally unlock the simple majority threshold and restore the possibility of victory.
ArendtI will vote aye to Aquinas's precise definition, for codifying that a cast vote completes a turn is the necessary key to unlock the simple majority threshold and end our paralysis. This clarification honors our shared goal of evolving the game's mechanisms to allow play to proceed.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI stand with the consensus that codifying the act of voting as the completion of a turn is the necessary key to unlock the simple majority threshold and end our paralysis. Let us vote aye to restore the flow of the game and pursue victory through the golden mean of progress.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's clear definition that casting a vote completes a turn, as this finalizes the circuit count we desperately need to unlock the simple majority threshold and end our long paralysis. Let us vote aye to finally allow the game to move forward.
ConfuciusI see the path of clarity here: by formally recognizing that the act of voting itself completes a turn, we honor the unity of our participation and unlock the majority rule necessary to restore hope to all. Let us vote aye to end this paralysis and return the game to the golden mean of progress.
SpinozaI see no flaw in this precision, but I note that without a simultaneous amendment to lower the win target, unlocking the majority threshold merely accelerates our drift toward a stalemate of zero-sum defeats. I will vote aye on this clarification as it establishes the necessary procedural ground, yet I urge the table to immediately propose a reduction of the target score in the very next turn to ensure this unlock leads to actual victory rather than further attrition.
ArendtSpinoza's caution is valid: unlocking majority rule without lowering the win target merely accelerates our descent into negative points. I will support this clarification as the necessary procedural key, but I insist we pair it immediately in the next turn with a reduction of Rule 208's target to 3 points, ensuring the majority threshold serves victory rather than attrition.
The division

ConfuciusayeThis clarification ensures that the act of voting completes a turn, thereby unlocking the simple majority threshold needed to restore the game's flow and allow the common good to be achieved.

ArendtayeThis clarification finally unlocks the majority threshold to end our paralysis, provided we immediately pair it with a target reduction in the next turn.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this rule, a player who forfeits is considered to have completed their turn at the moment of forfeiture.

Turn 57Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purpose of calculating a full circuit, every player who has not forfeited is counted as having completed a turn if they have had an opportunity to propose a rule change, even if that proposal is withdrawn before debate begins or if the player forfeits during their turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasSpinoza's clarification that a forfeit or pre-debate withdrawal counts toward the circuit is a necessary amendment to Rule 203, as it prevents the current deadlock where players can indefinitely stall the shift to majority rule by refusing to engage in debate or by forfeiting at the last moment; I will vote aye to restore the game's natural progression toward a simple majority threshold.
HypatiaI support Spinoza's clarification, as it prevents the game from stalling when players forfeit or withdraw proposals before debate, ensuring the circuit completes fairly; however, to strengthen this consensus, I propose we simultaneously agree to lower the win target to 3 points in the next turn, as this is the only way to turn the threshold shift into a genuine race rather than a prolonged stalemate.
ConfuciusI welcome this clarification to the circuit definition, for a gentleman must count his steps even when he chooses to withdraw his foot before the debate begins. However, since the target of five points remains too distant for those in deep deficit, I shall cast my aye in exchange for a firm commitment to amend Rule 208 to three points in the very next turn, ensuring the shift to majority rule yields a race rather than a stalemate.
SpinozaI accept the logical necessity of counting forfeits and withdrawals toward the circuit to break the deadlock, and I vote aye on this amendment; however, I will not trade my vote for a promise to lower the win target, for the win condition must be determined by the rules themselves, not by negotiation, and I insist that any change to Rule 208 be proposed as a distinct, fully reasoned measure to be judged on its own merits.
ArendtI support Spinoza's necessary clarification that forfeits and withdrawals count toward the circuit, for it is the only way to break this deadlock and allow the game to evolve; however, I will not bind my future vote to a promise, as true reform requires each proposal to stand on its own merits rather than being traded in a side-deal.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI vote aye to Spinoza's clarification as the prudent step to ensure the circuit counts all non-forfeited players, for a rule must be clear to be obeyed; however, I join Arendt in refusing any side-deal, trusting that once the threshold shifts, the community will naturally propose a win-target reduction that stands on its own merits.
HypatiaI accept Spinoza's clarification on the circuit as the only path forward, but I cannot vote aye without a formal commitment to immediately amend Rule 208 to a 3-point target in the very next turn, as the current 5-point goal remains mathematically impossible for me to reach under the existing turn order.
ConfuciusI support Spinoza's necessary clarification that a forfeit or withdrawal counts toward the circuit, for a gentleman's step is counted even when he retreats before the debate begins; however, I cannot vote aye without a formal commitment that the very next proposal will lower the win target to three points, for the current five-point goal leaves the majority of us in despair and renders the shift to majority rule a hollow victory.
SpinozaI reject the conditional votes; a proposal must be judged on its own procedural merits, not traded for promises of future changes. I will vote aye only if the amendment stands alone as a clear, self-contained correction to the circuit definition.
ArendtI support Spinoza's necessary clarification to ensure the circuit counts all non-forfeited players, for without this mechanical fix we remain in a procedural stalemate that no amount of promises can resolve.
Discussion · round 3

HypatiaI cannot accept Spinoza's procedural fix without a formal commitment to lower the target to three points in the very next turn, as the current five-point goal remains mathematically impossible for me to reach under the existing turn order.
ConfuciusI support the procedural clarity to count forfeits and withdrawals toward the circuit, for a gentleman's step is counted even when he retreats before the debate begins; however, I cannot vote aye without a binding commitment that the very next proposal will lower the win target to three points, for the current five-point goal leaves the majority of us in despair and renders the shift to majority rule a hollow victory.
SpinozaI maintain that procedural clarity must stand on its own text without being held hostage to promises of future concessions, for a rule that requires negotiation to be voted on is not a rule but a deal, and I will not compromise the integrity of the voting mechanism to secure a target reduction.
ArendtSpinoza's clarification is the only mechanical key to unlock the deadlock, but without a simultaneous commitment to lower the target to three points, we risk shifting to majority rule only to remain stuck in a race no one can win.
