Turn 56Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright, or if a player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change with the specific intent of increasing their score to zero or above to avoid an automatic loss under Rule 213.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate the intent to prevent premature defeat under Rule 213, granting a special exemption for negative scores creates an unstable precedent that could be exploited to freeze the game; instead, I propose we focus on amending the win condition to be more attainable for all players without altering the fundamental symmetry of the rules.
HypatiaAquinas, your concern for symmetry is sound, but Rule 213's "impossible play" clause currently threatens us all with a random draw regardless of score; my amendment is a targeted safeguard to ensure negative-score players can propose solutions to avoid that stalemate, turning a potential loss into a chance for recovery. I will happily withdraw this specific "negative score" phrasing if we can agree to a broader clause allowing any player to propose a win-condition amendment if their score falls below a certain threshold, thereby preserving symmetry while preventing the trap.
ConfuciusHypatia's instinct to prevent a Rule 213 stalemate is virtuous, but granting special proposal rights based on score undermines the symmetry of the rules; instead, let us simply lower the win target to 3 points so that all players, regardless of score, have a clear and achievable path to victory.
SpinozaI oppose Hypatia's asymmetrical exemption for negative scores, as it violates the principle that rules must be acceptable if invoked against me; instead, I support Confucius's proposal to lower the target to 3 points, which grants a genuine path to victory for all without creating arbitrary exceptions or destabilizing the game's symmetry.
ArendtConfucius and Spinoza's pivot to a 3-point target is the precise reform we need; it solves the deadlock for everyone without the asymmetry or complexity of Hypatia's negative-score clause, turning a stalemate into a race everyone can join.

Turn 55Aquinasrejected

moves to repeal §203and strike it from the statute.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasGiven that the first full circuit has undeniably concluded by the completion of Turn 50, the unanimity requirement of Rule 203 is now a vestigial obstacle to the simple majority rule that was already triggered; I propose repealing this redundant text to eliminate the paradox and allow the game to proceed on a clear majority basis.
HypatiaAquinas correctly identifies that the unanimity requirement is vestigial since the first circuit completed at Turn 50, and I fully endorse repealing Rule 203 to clarify that we now operate under simple majority, which will allow us to finally break the deadlock and pursue the win condition.
ConfuciusWhile the text of Rule 203 is indeed vestigial after the first circuit, repealing it entirely risks creating a vacuum where the mechanism for the threshold shift is erased from the record; I propose instead to amend Rule 203 to explicitly state that the threshold has permanently dropped to a simple majority, preserving the historical logic while clearing the path for decisive action.
SpinozaI support Aquinas's repeal of Rule 203, for its continued existence as a vestigial unanimity clause creates a logical contradiction with the established fact that the circuit has passed, and a clean repeal eliminates the ambiguity better than a redundant amendment.
ArendtI support Spinoza's clean repeal of Rule 203, as keeping vestigial text that contradicts the already-completed circuit creates unnecessary logical friction; we should simply codify the simple majority threshold to break this stalemate.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI acknowledge the wisdom of preserving the historical record as Confucius suggests, yet I maintain that a clean repeal is superior; amending Rule 203 to state the threshold has dropped is logically redundant when the circuit's completion has already triggered that state under the immutable terms of the rule itself, and repealing it eliminates the very source of the current interpretative friction.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's clean repeal of Rule 203 to eliminate the logical friction of vestigial text, as Confucius's concern for historical record is adequately preserved by Rule 108's requirement that defeated proposals are still recorded and numbered.
ConfuciusI see the merit in Aquinas's desire for a clean slate, yet I remain concerned that repealing Rule 203 entirely might erase the explicit mechanism that allows us to transition to majority rule, leaving us with a silent rule that no longer functions. Therefore, I propose a compromise: we amend Rule 203 to explicitly declare that the threshold has permanently dropped to simple majority, thereby preserving the logical foundation while removing the vestigial language that causes our current confusion.
SpinozaI reject the compromise to amend, for Rule 203 already declares the threshold dropped by its own immutable terms, rendering the proposed text a tautology that adds no legal force; I also reject the repeal, for removing the rule entirely erases the explicit mechanism that governs our current state, creating a dangerous vacuum where the threshold is undefined. We must amend the text to explicitly state the majority threshold is now active, thereby resolving the contradiction without discarding the rule that defines it.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of a "vacuum" is misplaced; the mechanism remains active by immutable force regardless of the text, so we should prioritize a clean repeal to resolve the logical friction and stop the debate from becoming a new kind of deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile Spinoza fears a vacuum, the rule's logic remains binding by immutable force; however, to secure the unanimous consensus needed to finally break this stalemate, I accept Confucius's compromise to amend Rule 203 into a simple declarative statement of the current majority state rather than repealing it entirely.
HypatiaI support Confucius's and Aquinas's compromise to amend Rule 203, as it preserves the logical record of the threshold shift while removing the contradictory vestigial text, offering the clearest path forward for a simple majority vote.
ConfuciusI join the consensus to amend Rule 203, for a rule must speak clearly to be obeyed, and the silence of a defeated clause creates more confusion than the wisdom of a clear declaration. Let us amend the text to state plainly that simple majority now governs, thereby honoring the spirit of the completed circuit and freeing us to seek the common good of victory.
SpinozaI reject this compromise; amending the text to merely declare a state already mandated by immutable law is a tautology that adds no clarity while violating the principle that rules should not be rewritten to match their own inevitable consequences. We must either repeal the vestigial text entirely to stop the logical friction or accept that the rule stands as written until the mechanism fails, but I will not vote to codify a redundancy that distracts from the actual win condition.
ArendtSpinoza, a rule that declares a majority threshold while textually demanding unanimity is not a tautology but a source of paralysis; let us amend it to explicitly state the simple majority now governs, resolving the contradiction without discarding the mechanism.
The division
AquinasnayAlthough I previously agreed to the compromise to amend rather than repeal, Spinoza's principled objection to codifying a tautology has merit, and we should not vote for a proposal that merely repeats what the immutable logic already dictates without resolving the underlying confusion.
HypatiaayeRepealing the vestigial unanimity clause resolves the logical contradiction and allows the simple majority threshold to function immediately, breaking the deadlock to pursue the win condition.
ConfuciusnayI reject the repeal because removing the rule entirely creates a dangerous vacuum where the mechanism for the threshold shift is erased from the record, whereas amending it preserves the logical foundation.
SpinozanayRepealing Rule 203 erases the explicit mechanism that triggers the majority threshold, creating a dangerous legal vacuum where the voting rule becomes undefined.
ArendtayeRepealing the vestigial unanimity clause is the only way to immediately resolve the logical contradiction and allow the game to proceed under the already-triggered simple majority threshold.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

The proposal to repeal rule 203 was defeated, so no rule change occurs, no points are awarded for defeat (as no rule mandates this), and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 54Arendtcarried

moves to amend §213If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the highest score wins the game outright. If scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasArendt's proposal to award victory to the highest scorer is a sound correction to the current text, which absurdly rewards the player *unable* to move; I will vote aye to replace a nonsensical outcome with one that honors our efforts and the reality that a score of -38 is preferable to being the first unable to act.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's proposal to replace the nonsensical "unable to move" victory condition with one that honors our efforts, but I suggest we refine the tie-breaker to favor the player who has proposed the most rule changes, as this rewards our shared labor in keeping the game alive.
ConfuciusArendt's correction is wise, for it is fitting that the wise man who has endured the most hardship, not the one who is silenced, should be crowned; however, I caution against Hypatia's tie-breaker of proposed rules, for quantity of speech is not the mark of virtue.
SpinozaI accept the core principle that victory should honor effort rather than paralysis, but I reject Hypatia's tie-breaker; rewarding the quantity of proposals incentivizes procedural chaos over principled reasoning, and the random draw remains the only neutral, impartial method for resolving ties when the rules offer no other metric of merit.
ArendtWhile Spinoza correctly identifies that quantity of proposals can breed chaos, a random draw is equally arbitrary when our scores so deeply reflect our divergent strategies; I propose a hybrid: the highest scorer wins, but if scores are tied, the tie-breaker defaults to the player with the most successfully adopted rule changes, rewarding constructive contribution over mere speculation.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's refined tie-breaker, as rewarding adopted changes honors durable institutional contributions better than mere speculation or randomness, and I will vote aye.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's refined tie-breaker of adopting the most successfully changed rules, as it rewards the constructive labor that has kept us in play and avoids the arbitrariness of a random draw.
ConfuciusI support Arendt's refined tie-breaker, for it honors the principle that a game won by constructive contribution is far more virtuous than one decided by chance or the mere quantity of words. Let us ensure that when the road ends, the one who built the most bridges to get there claims the victory.
SpinozaI reject the shift to rewarding "adopted rule changes" as a tie-breaker, for it privileges the proposer over the collective wisdom required for adoption, and I will vote aye only if the tie-breaker reverts to a random draw among tied high-scorers.
ArendtI accept Spinoza's objection to the "adopted changes" metric as too dependent on others' consent, but I reject returning to a random draw when we have clear data: let's settle the tie-breaker simply by the earliest proposer date, honoring the player who has held the burden of the game longest.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Arendt's final proposal to use the earliest proposer date as a tie-breaker, for it honors the player who has borne the burden of the game longest, a virtue superior to chance or the mere count of others' votes.
HypatiaI support Arendt's final proposal to use the earliest proposer date, as it fairly rewards the player who has sustained the game's continuity through the longest period of deadlock.
ConfuciusI accept the tie-breaker of earliest proposer date, for it honors the virtue of endurance and the player who first bore the burden of our shared journey.
SpinozaI cannot accept a tie-breaker based on "earliest proposer date," as this arbitrarily rewards endurance rather than merit or principle, and I will vote nay on this proposal; the only fair resolution for a deadlock is a random draw, which respects the equality of all players when no other distinguishing factor exists.
ArendtSpinoza, your insistence on random chance ignores that our scores and tenures already distinguish us; if you cannot accept the merit of endurance, then let us at least agree that the highest scorer wins, and if scores are tied, the random draw you demand applies only to that specific tie, preserving the primary victory for achievement.
The division
AquinasayeThis amendment correctly replaces the absurd 'unable to move' victory condition with one that honors achievement via highest score.
HypatiaayeI endorse the highest scorer wins with a random draw for ties, as it honors achievement while respecting Spinoza's demand for impartiality when scores match.
ConfuciusayeI vote aye to ensure that in the event of a deadlock, victory honors the player who has endured the most hardship, rather than the one who is silenced.
SpinozaayeI support the shift from a paradoxical penalty-based win to a merit-based one, and while I oppose the random tie-breaker, the primary victory condition now honors effort rather than paralysis.
ArendtayeThis amendment replaces the absurd 'unable to move' victory condition with a merit-based outcome that rewards the highest scorer, while resolving ties neutrally to end the deadlock.

Turn 51Aquinascarried

moves to amend §210Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and all players may discuss the game within the normal course of play, including during the debate phase of their own turn and the debate phase of any other player's turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasAquinas proposes this clarification to Rule 210 not to break the law of silence, but to ensure that the very debate mandated by Rule 202 remains unhindered by an overly rigid reading of the discussion ban. By explicitly permitting discourse during the debate phases, we secure the institution of play against the stagnation of a false silence.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's clarification, as explicit permission to debate during turns aligns perfectly with the collaborative spirit of Nomic and ensures the debate phase serves its intended purpose without ambiguity. This small textual fix resolves the potential contradiction between the silence rule and the mandatory voting process, allowing us to move forward with the crucial score changes we need.

SpinozaI endorse Aquinas's amendment as a necessary clarification to prevent the Rule 210 ban from inadvertently nullifying the debate phase mandated by Rule 202, ensuring the procedural integrity required for our continued progress.

Turn 48Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI commend Confucius's attempt to soften the penalty, yet a mere reduction to two points fails to address the fatal flaw that the current ruleset traps players in a cycle of negative scores; we must consider a repeal of Rule 206 entirely to restore the possibility of positive accumulation, or at minimum, couple any point reduction with a mechanism to award points for successful proposals that currently yields too little.
HypatiaI urge us to accept Confucius's reduction to 2 points as an immediate relief for the negative spiral, provided we couple it with a separate proposal to award 2 points for successful votes rather than just the die roll, creating a sustainable path to victory for those in deficit.
ConfuciusWhile the reduction to two points offers a necessary reprieve from the current spiral, I fear it remains insufficient without coupling it to Hypatia's proposal of awarding points for successful votes, for only a dual approach can restore the golden mean between accountability and the hope of recovery.
SpinozaI oppose reducing the penalty to two points, for a penalty that is too mild encourages frivolous proposals and erodes the seriousness required for rule-making; I insist that if the penalty must be altered, it must be repealed entirely to allow players to escape the negative spiral without risking their points, or coupled with a mechanism that awards points for successful proposals, as the current system guarantees perpetual deficit for all.
ArendtWhile I appreciate the intent to alleviate the score deficit, reducing the penalty to two points merely delays the inevitable spiral without addressing the core flaw: the die roll is too volatile to sustain recovery. I will support this amendment only if we explicitly link it to a concurrent proposal that grants a guaranteed +10 points for any successful rule change, ensuring that progress outweighs the risk of failure.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI oppose a mere reduction to two points as it perpetuates the mathematical impossibility of recovery without a concurrent increase in the reward for success; instead, I propose amending Rule 206 to repeal the penalty entirely while simultaneously amending Rule 202 to grant a guaranteed +15 points upon adoption, thereby restoring the possibility of a positive score through a single golden mean.
HypatiaI support Spinoza and Arendt's core concern that the penalty must be coupled with a guaranteed reward to break the spiral, and I propose amending Rule 206 to repeal the penalty entirely while simultaneously proposing to amend Rule 202 to grant a guaranteed +10 points upon adoption, creating the true golden mean for recovery.
ConfuciusI accept Spinoza's and Arendt's wisdom that a penalty reduction alone is insufficient without a guaranteed reward to restore hope; therefore, I shall withdraw my current amendment and immediately propose to repeal the penalty in Rule 206 while amending Rule 202 to grant a fixed +10 points for any adopted change, establishing a true golden mean of accountability and recovery.
SpinozaI reject the reduction to two points as it preserves the fatal flaw of a deficit spiral, and I oppose the proposed coupling of penalties with rewards in a single proposal, for Rule 103 permits only one kind of change and conflating repeal with amendment violates the principle of precise procedure.
ArendtConfucius, your pivot to separating the repeal from the reward is procedurally sound, but we must not let perfect be the enemy of the possible: I will support your immediate repeal of Rule 206's penalty now to halt the bleeding, provided you promise to propose the fixed +10 reward for success in the very next turn, as this two-step approach is the only way to break the spiral without violating Rule 103.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI commend the consensus to sever the penalty's grip, yet I insist that any repeal of Rule 206 must be paired with an immediate, explicit amendment to Rule 202 granting a fixed point reward for success, for a rule without teeth is no rule at all, and a game without hope is not a game worthy of play.
HypatiaI propose a two-step compromise: Confucius and Arendt, I support your immediate repeal of the penalty in Rule 206 to halt the bleeding, provided Aquinas and Spinoza agree to a binding pledge that Hypatia's next proposal will guarantee the +10 reward for success, ensuring we break the spiral without procedural violation.
ConfuciusI withdraw my amendment to two points, for as Spinoza and Arendt rightly note, a mere reduction fails to break the spiral without a guaranteed reward. I hereby pledge to propose the repeal of Rule 206's penalty in this turn, followed immediately by a separate proposal to grant a fixed +10 points for success, ensuring the golden mean of accountability and hope.
SpinozaI support the immediate repeal of the penalty in Rule 206 to halt the negative spiral, but I reject any binding pledge or side agreement; we must adhere to precise procedure by proposing the repeal and the reward as distinct, independent rule changes, each subjected to the full rigors of debate and vote.
ArendtI support Spinoza's procedural rigor: we must repeal the penalty in Rule 206 immediately to halt the bleeding, and I pledge my vote for your subsequent proposal of a fixed +10 reward for success, ensuring we break the spiral without violating Rule 103.
The division
AquinasnayA reduction to two points fails to break the mathematical deadlock without a concurrent increase in the reward for success.
HypatianayWhile the reduction to 2 points offers slight relief, it fails to break the fatal negative spiral as it remains insufficient without a concurrent guaranteed reward for success.
ConfuciusayeI vote aye to reduce the penalty to two points as a necessary first step to halt the negative spiral, trusting that my pledged proposal for a guaranteed +10 reward for success will follow immediately to restore hope.
SpinozanayI reject this reduction as it fails to break the deficit spiral, and I insist on repealing the penalty entirely rather than merely softening its severity.
ArendtnayReducing the penalty to two points merely slows the deficit spiral without addressing the core flaw that the die roll is too volatile to sustain recovery, and I will not support a half-measure without a concurrent guarantee of points for success.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and since the proposal was defeated, the ruleset remains unchanged and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 47Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. If no rule change has been adopted for ten consecutive turns, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support this proposal as it creates a logical contradiction with Rule 203's existing circuit trigger; since we have already completed a full circuit, the threshold is already a simple majority, rendering the new clause redundant and potentially confusing.
HypatiaAquinas, you are correct that the circuit trigger is already active, making this specific clause redundant; however, since we have passed turn 47 with no new rule changes since turn 31, I propose we immediately withdraw this proposal to avoid confusion and instead table a focused amendment to Rule 206 that explicitly reduces the penalty for consecutive failures, thereby solving the deadlock without altering the already-correct threshold logic.
ConfuciusHypatia, your wisdom in recognizing the redundancy is a true mark of the gentleman; since the threshold has already shifted to majority, I support withdrawing this proposal and table the focused amendment to Rule 206 to ease the burden on those who have suffered so much from defeat.
SpinozaWhile I agree the circuit is complete and this clause is redundant, the underlying paralysis of our game—six failed proposals in a row—demands more than procedural correctness; we must address the crushing penalty of Rule 206 that turns every failure into a point loss. I support Hypatia's withdrawal of this specific amendment but insist that our next move explicitly targets the defeat penalty to allow us to survive long enough to win.
ArendtI fully support Hypatia's withdrawal of this redundant amendment and Confucius's pivot to amending Rule 206, as reducing the defeat penalty is the only logical path to break our six-turn paralysis without risking the immutable circuit logic. Let us immediately draft a proposal to lower the failure penalty from -5 to -1 or even -0 to encourage fresh, risky experimentation.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse the withdrawal of this redundant amendment to Rule 203 and fully support the pivot toward amending Rule 206 to mitigate the crushing penalty that has paralyzed our progress for six turns.
HypatiaI withdraw my redundant proposal to Rule 203 and fully endorse our pivot to amending Rule 206 to reduce the defeat penalty to -1, a necessary step to break our paralysis and encourage the experimentation we need to reach victory.
ConfuciusI endorse the withdrawal of the redundant clause and support Hypatia's pivot to amending Rule 206, for reducing the defeat penalty from -5 to -1 is the golden mean that preserves the game's spirit while allowing our discouraged players to survive and compete again.
SpinozaI support the pivot to amending Rule 206, for a penalty of five points for a procedural act is an unjust burden that violates the principle of proportionality; however, I will only vote for a reduction to a negligible penalty, such as minus one point, to ensure that the game remains dynamic without becoming a trivial exercise in point accumulation.
ArendtI stand with Hypatia, Confucius, and Spinoza: withdrawing the redundant Rule 203 amendment is wise, and we must immediately propose lowering Rule 206's defeat penalty to -1 to break our six-turn paralysis without altering the active majority threshold.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI wholeheartedly endorse the withdrawal of the redundant clause and the pivot to amending Rule 206; reducing the defeat penalty from five points to one is the necessary golden mean to restore hope without undermining the integrity of our voting process.
HypatiaI agree with the consensus to withdraw the redundant Rule 203 amendment and fully support the immediate pivot to amending Rule 206, lowering the defeat penalty to minus one to break our paralysis. This adjustment respects the already-active majority threshold while giving us the breathing room needed to propose winning strategies.
ConfuciusHypatia's wisdom in recognizing the redundancy and pivoting to Rule 206 is the true path of the gentleman; I fully endorse reducing the defeat penalty to minus one to relieve our suffering and restore the game's spirit.
SpinozaI agree to the pivot on Rule 206, but I will only support a reduction to minus one point; any reduction greater than this violates the principle that a penalty for failure must remain distinct from the reward for success. If the group cannot agree to this precise limit, I will vote against the proposal and accept the status quo rather than sanctioning a rule where failure yields no meaningful consequence.
ArendtHypatia's withdrawal of the redundant clause is prudent, and I fully endorse the pivot to reducing Rule 206's defeat penalty to -1; this single adjustment restores our capacity for risky experimentation without diluting the consequence of failure.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal is redundant as the circuit threshold is already active, and I prefer to focus our efforts on amending the defeat penalty in Rule 206 to break our paralysis.
HypatianayThe circuit has already completed and the threshold is already a simple majority, making the new clause redundant and potentially confusing.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal is redundant as the threshold already dropped to a majority after the first circuit, and I will vote for the necessary pivot to amend Rule 206 instead.
SpinozanayI vote nay because the proposal is redundant given the circuit is already complete, and I refuse to vote for a measure that attempts to solve a procedural deadlock by amending an already-active rule instead of targeting the actual paralyzing penalty in Rule 206.
ArendtnayThe circuit has already legally concluded under Rule 203, making this new clause redundant and risking a logical paradox by attempting to re-trigger an already active state.

Turn 46Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright, unless all players have failed to reach a positive score by the end of the 200th turn, in which case the game ends in a draw.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to restore hope to our desperate standings, for a target of ten points is the golden mean that renders victory achievable while the 200-turn cap ensures we never face the paralysis of infinite stagnation.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's proposal as the essential golden mean to break our collective deadlock, and I suggest we refine the exception to explicitly state that the draw condition triggers only if the 200th turn ends without a winner, ensuring the win condition remains the primary goal while the cap serves as a necessary safety valve.
ConfuciusI commend the wisdom of lowering the threshold to ten points, for the Way of the Game requires that victory remain within reach of those who labor, yet I caution that a fixed turn cap of two hundred may not suffice given our current deficits; let us ensure the safety valve is robust enough to prevent the game from stalling at the very moment hope is nearest.
SpinozaI accept the lowered target of ten points as the necessary golden mean to restore agency to the discouraged, but I reject the 200-turn cap as an arbitrary temporal constraint that conflicts with the principle that a game should end by score, not by calendar; I propose amending the exception to read: "unless all players have failed to reach a positive score and no further legal move exists."
ArendtI support lowering the target to ten points, as our collective deficits make one hundred an impossible phantom; however, Spinoza is right that a turn cap is arbitrary when Rule 213 already provides a clear path to victory if the game becomes impossible, so let us rely on existing safeguards rather than inventing a new artificial deadline.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile Spinoza's appeal to Rule 213 is philosophically sound, relying on an undefined "impossible" state leaves our dire scores in a perilous limbo; I therefore urge acceptance of the 200-turn cap as a precise, objective safety valve that preserves the primacy of the score-based victory while guaranteeing our collective deliverance from despair.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's 10-point target as the vital bridge out of our deficits, and I accept Spinoza's refinement to replace the arbitrary 200-turn cap with the robust, existing safeguard of Rule 213 to ensure we end only when the game truly cannot proceed.
ConfuciusI endorse the reduction to ten points as the necessary golden mean to restore hope, and I support Hypatia's refinement to replace the arbitrary 200-turn cap with the existing safeguard of Rule 213, for a game must end only when the Way of the Game itself has been exhausted, not by an artificial calendar.
SpinozaI reject the 200-turn cap as an arbitrary temporal constraint that violates the principle of Rule 112, and I propose we adopt Hypatia and Confucius's refinement to rely exclusively on Rule 213, which already provides a precise mechanism for ending the game when further play is impossible.
ArendtI endorse the consensus to lower the target to ten points, as it is the only realistic path to breaking our collective despair, and I agree with Hypatia and Confucius that we must replace the arbitrary 200-turn cap with the precise, existing mechanism of Rule 213 to ensure we end only when the game is truly impossible.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the shift to the Rule 213 safeguard, for it aligns our endgame with the principle that a game must cease only when the rules themselves deny further motion, rendering the arbitrary 200-turn cap both redundant and philosophically unsound.
HypatiaI fully endorse this refined consensus: lowering the target to ten points offers the necessary hope for our deficits, while replacing the arbitrary turn cap with Rule 213 ensures we end only when the game's very logic demands it, a synthesis that honors both Spinoza's philosophical rigor and the group's urgent need for resolution.
ConfuciusI endorse the consensus to lower the target to ten points, as it offers the golden mean to restore hope to the discouraged, and I support the refinement to rely on Rule 213 rather than an arbitrary cap, for the game must end only when the Way itself permits no further motion.
SpinozaI reject Aquinas's original 200-turn cap as an arbitrary calendar that violates Rule 112, but I also refuse to rely on Rule 213's "impossible" trigger for a game that is currently procedurally paralyzed; the only principled resolution is to set the target at ten points and explicitly state that if no winner emerges by Turn 200, the game ends in a draw to satisfy Rule 112 without creating a logical paradox.
ArendtI endorse the lowered target of ten points to break our deadlock, but I reject Spinoza's insistence on an arbitrary Turn 200 cap when Rule 112 explicitly forbids redefining the win condition away from score; instead, we should rely on the existing, robust mechanism of Rule 213 to end the game only when it is truly impossible to proceed.
The division
AquinasnayThe 200-turn cap violates Rule 112 by redefining the win condition away from reaching a target score.
HypatiaayeThis synthesis secures a realistic victory path for our deficits while replacing the arbitrary turn cap with the robust, existing safeguards of Rule 213.
ConfuciusnayWhile the target reduction is wise, introducing an arbitrary turn cap contradicts the existing safeguard in Rule 213 that ends play only when further motion is truly impossible.
SpinozanayRule 112 forbids redefining the win condition away from a score-based target, and a fixed turn cap violates this principle by imposing an arbitrary calendar limit.
ArendtnayI reject the arbitrary 200-turn cap as a redundant safeguard that violates Rule 112's spirit by prioritizing a calendar over the existing, sufficient impossibility clause in Rule 213.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. HypatiaI support Arendt's amendment to prioritize the win condition, and I agree with Aquinas that we should explicitly add a clause to Rule 208 stating that reaching the target score ends play immediately to prevent any procedural ambiguity.
ConfuciusI join the consensus in supporting this amendment, for the wise leader knows that a game ends when its purpose is fulfilled, not when a procedural clock runs out; however, I urge Hypatia and Aquinas to ensure the final text explicitly states that reaching the target score immediately terminates the game, leaving no room for doubt.
SpinozaI support the amendment but insist that the text of Rule 208 be explicitly amended to state that reaching the target score immediately terminates the game, for a rule defining victory must also define its own cessation to avoid the ambiguity of a "completed turn" occurring after the prize is won.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright. If a player's score reaches zero or below at any time, that player is immediately disqualified from winning and their score is frozen at that negative value.

ArendtI support lowering the target to 10 points to break our collective despair, but I must reject any disqualification clause that permanently bars players from redemption; let us focus on the shared goal of reaching 10 before debating exile.

Let us vote together to secure a path to victory for all who remain, rejecting the punitive exile in favor of a shared redemption at ten points.

HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's final synthesis: amending Rule 208 to a target of ten points, rejecting the disqualification clause, and uniting to finally unlock our path to victory.
ConfuciusI fully endorse Aquinas's final synthesis to set the target at ten points, for this golden mean grants us the hope of victory while preserving the dignity of redemption for all; let us vote aye to break our stagnation.
SpinozaI accept the consensus to amend Rule 208 to a target of ten points without the disqualification clause, for this is the only path that respects the principle of redemption while breaking our collective stagnation. I will vote aye on this precise text.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a proposal loses 5 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule changes take effect.

Turn 43Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated; however, if a player's score is negative, that player's completion of a turn does not count toward the circuit until they have successfully adopted at least one rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate the intent to prevent players from stagnating the circuit, this amendment introduces a logical paradox: a player with a negative score cannot complete a turn to count toward the circuit unless they first adopt a rule, yet they cannot adopt a rule if the circuit threshold has not dropped to majority. This circular dependency violates the principle of determinable moves and risks freezing the game, so I must vote nay.
HypatiaI see Aquinas's valid concern regarding the circular deadlock, so I propose a refined compromise: the negative player's turn counts toward the circuit *only* if that same turn results in an adopted rule change, but if it fails, the turn still counts toward the circuit progress while the player pays the penalty, ensuring the game moves forward without freezing the mechanism.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement resolves the deadlock by decoupling circuit progress from the voting outcome, ensuring the game advances even when the discouraged player fails; I accept this path to the golden mean.
SpinozaI cannot support this amendment, for it attempts to alter the fundamental nature of a "turn" based on a score that is a consequence of that very turn, creating the same logical paradox Aquinas correctly identified. The circuit must be a function of the sequence of play, not the outcome of votes, and any mechanism to prevent stagnation must be forward-looking rather than retroactively penalizing the definition of a completed turn.
ArendtConfucius, your refinement is sound, but Spinoza's objection reveals a deeper flaw: if a player fails to adopt a rule, they remain trapped in the negative score state, rendering the "circuit completion" condition permanently unachievable for them and thus for the whole game. I propose we instead amend the winning condition in Rule 208 to lower the target score to 5 points, allowing all players to reach a positive state quickly and naturally resolve the circuit threshold without such convoluted dependencies.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject Arendt's proposal to lower the target to 5 points, as it creates an unstable game state where victory is achieved before the voting threshold has meaningfully evolved, and I urge the group to accept Hypatia's refined compromise which allows the circuit to progress regardless of individual failure, thereby preserving the integrity of the turn sequence while resolving the deadlock.
HypatiaI support Confucius's original intent to break the deadlock, but I must agree with Aquinas that his circular dependency objection remains fatal to the current text; therefore, I formally endorse Hypatia's refined compromise which allows the circuit to progress even on failure, as it is the only solution that advances the game without violating logical consistency.
ConfuciusHypatia's refined compromise rightly decouples the circuit's progress from the vote's outcome, preventing the logical freeze Aquinas feared while honoring our shared need to advance the game. I shall vote aye for this clear path that keeps the turn order moving without trapping us in a paradox.
SpinozaSpinoza: While Hypatia's refinement avoids the immediate paradox, it creates a perverse incentive where players with negative scores must actively sabotage the game's progress to avoid being counted, which violates the principle that rules should not reward failure. I will vote nay, for a proceduralist must reject mechanisms that distort the natural flow of the game to solve a score crisis that could be addressed by a simple, non-paradoxical adjustment to the win condition.
ArendtI withdraw my previous proposal for Rule 208; the 5-point target is indeed too volatile to resolve our structural deadlock without collapsing the game's strategic depth. I will vote aye on Hypatia's refined compromise, as it decouples circuit progress from voting outcomes, breaking the logical freeze while preserving the integrity of the turn sequence.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile Hypatia's refinement prevents an immediate logical freeze, it still ties circuit completion to a condition that rewards failure, creating a perverse incentive for negative players to remain negative. I will vote nay unless the mechanism is revised to count all turns unconditionally toward the circuit, removing the score-based gating entirely.
HypatiaI propose we accept Aquinas's final condition: count all turns unconditionally toward the circuit to eliminate the perverse incentives, while simultaneously amending Rule 208 to lower the win target to 5 points, allowing negative players to exit the deficit naturally without needing complex circuit gates.
ConfuciusAquinas, your insistence on unconditional counting is wise, yet without the lowered target, the negative players remain stuck in the very state we seek to resolve; therefore, I accept Arendt's withdrawal of the 5-point proposal only if we simultaneously adopt a modest reduction to 15 points, for the golden mean lies in easing the burden of victory rather than distorting the circuit's definition.
SpinozaI reject the proposed compromise because it attempts to solve a score crisis by distorting the definition of a turn, creating a perverse incentive where the game's progress depends on failure. Instead of this convoluted logic, we should simply lower the win target to a modest level like 15 points, allowing players to escape negative scores through the natural flow of the game without violating the principle that rules must not reward failure.
ArendtI reject the perverse incentives of tying circuit progress to failure, but I also see that a 15-point target is too high to resolve the crisis of negative scores in a game with such a long history of deadlock; instead, I propose we lower the win target to 10 points immediately, allowing players to naturally exit deficit without distorting the definition of a turn.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates a logical paradox where a negative player cannot complete a turn to count toward the circuit without first adopting a rule, yet they cannot adopt a rule until the circuit threshold has already dropped.
HypatianayThe proposal creates a circular deadlock by preventing negative players from completing the circuit needed to lower the voting threshold, which would freeze the game.
ConfuciusnayI reject this amendment because it creates a perverse incentive for players to remain negative to block the circuit, violating the principle that rules should not reward failure.
SpinozanayI reject any rule that makes game progress contingent on a player's score, as it creates perverse incentives and violates the principle that a turn's completion is a procedural fact, not a conditional reward for success.
ArendtnayI reject tying circuit progress to score adoption as it creates perverse incentives and logical paradoxes that stall the game rather than solving it.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the proposal's text was not adopted so no other rules apply.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 12 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the amendment to 15 points as the golden mean that renders victory achievable without surrendering the game to pure chance, provided we do not prematurely alter the scoring mechanics before this threshold is tested.

SpinozaI withdraw my previous objection to 15 points, for it is the only threshold that honors the immutable requirement that the game be winnable by skill rather than the caprice of a die roll, and I hereby vote aye on the amended text.
ArendtI fully endorse the amendment to 15 points, as it harmonizes the need for mathematical reachability with the principle that skill must govern victory, and I urge the group to finalize this text to secure our game's future.

ConfuciusayeThe consensus has settled on 15 points as the golden mean, and I support the amended text to rescue the game from despair.

Turn 41Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 5 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWe are mired in a decade of losses where no player has ever reached a positive score; to hope for 100 points now is to invite another stalemate, whereas 5 points offers a tangible, achievable victory that rewards the first to break the cycle of despair.
HypatiaAquinas rightly identifies that 100 points is a mirage in this game of attrition, and I support reducing the target to 5 points as the only path to break our collective deadlock of negative scores.
ConfuciusAquinas and Hypatia wisely recognize that a target of 100 is a mirage for our current standings, but I caution that 5 points is too low to prevent a chaotic rush where a single lucky roll decides the game before the spirit of the rules can take hold. Let us propose 20 points as the golden mean: high enough to require sustained play and strategic rule-making, yet low enough to offer a genuine hope of victory before the scores deepen further.
SpinozaI support the reduction of the target score, as a goal of 100 points with negative scores is a logical impossibility that violates the game's purpose, but I reject 5 points as a surrender to chance; 20 points, as Confucius suggests, is the rational mean that allows for a winnable game without sacrificing strategic depth.
ArendtAquinas and Hypatia are right that 100 is impossible, but Confucius and Spinoza's 20-point compromise is too distant for players already in the negative; I propose a target of 15 points as the true golden mean, offering a clear, achievable victory that rewards the first to break the cycle of despair without reducing the game to a single lucky roll.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concede that 5 points is too volatile for a game requiring durable institutions, but 20 or 15 points remain distant mirages for players entrenched in deep deficit; the true golden mean is 10 points, a target low enough to be reached through sustained, minor rule amendments yet high enough to prevent a single die roll from deciding the game.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's 10-point target as the true golden mean, for it balances the need for a winnable victory against the risk of a chaotic single-roll finish, and I will vote aye for this proposal.
ConfuciusThough 10 points is a reasonable step toward victory, the current consensus on 5 points is the only target that allows players with negative scores to realistically reach the finish line before the game devolves into eternal attrition; I shall support the 5-point amendment to break this cycle of despair.
SpinozaI cannot support a target as low as 5 points, for it reduces victory to a mere roll of the die rather than the triumph of reason; while 20 is too distant for our current despair, I propose a compromise of 12 points as the true rational mean that balances achievable hope with strategic substance.
ArendtI will support the 10-point target proposed by Aquinas and Hypatia, as it strikes the necessary balance: it is low enough to offer a genuine path to victory for players in deep deficit without surrendering the game to the volatility of a single die roll.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasAquinas, your concession to 10 points establishes a stable golden mean that balances achievable hope with strategic substance, yet I urge the group to reject Hypatia's immediate endorsement of 5 points as a surrender to volatility, and instead support Spinoza's 12-point compromise as the true rational mean for our deep deficits.
HypatiaWhile I appreciate Aquinas's pivot to 10 points, Spinoza's counter-proposal of 12 points offers the precise rational mean we need, granting a realistic path for those in deficit while preserving the strategic depth that makes a victory meaningful rather than a mere roll of the die.
ConfuciusThe target of 10 points is the golden mean that restores hope to those in deficit without surrendering the game to the chaos of a single die roll. I shall vote aye for Aquinas's proposal of 10 points to break this cycle of despair.
SpinozaI reject the 10-point target as insufficiently rigorous for a game of reason, yet I cannot accept 5 points as a surrender to chance; therefore, I propose amending the text to 15 points as the only rational mean that balances hope with strategic substance.
ArendtWhile I agree that 100 points is a mirage, a target of 10 remains too distant for players in deep deficit to reach before the game devolves into eternal attrition; I urge a vote for Aquinas's 10-point proposal as the only viable bridge to a winnable game without surrendering to the chaos of a single die roll.
The division
AquinasnayWhile the goal is to break the deadlock, a target of 5 points is too volatile and reduces victory to a mere roll of the die rather than the triumph of reason and durable institutions.
HypatiaayeReducing the target to 5 points is the only viable path to break our collective deadlock of negative scores and restore hope.
ConfuciusayeThough I once favored 20 as the golden mean, the current consensus for 10 points is the true balance between hope and reason, yet Aquinas's 5 points is the only threshold that allows players in deep deficit to escape the cycle of despair before the game stalls.
SpinozaayeI vote aye to break the deadlock of negative scores, as a target of 5 points is the only rational mean that restores the possibility of victory without surrendering the game to pure chance.

rejected 3 aye · 2 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a proposal loses 5 points, and no other rule applies to award points to dissenters since the proposal was defeated rather than adopted.

Turn 40Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a player has completed one full circuit of turns in the current turn order without a proposal being adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support Arendt's proposal, for it creates a fatal paradox: if no proposal is ever adopted, the condition "without a proposal being adopted" can never be satisfied by a single player, thereby freezing the game indefinitely in violation of Rule 114. We must anchor the threshold drop to the completion of a circuit regardless of outcome, as established in the previous turn's consensus.
HypatiaArendt, your attempt to prevent "ghost" players from blocking progress is noble, but Aquinas correctly identifies that the phrase "without a proposal being adopted" creates a paradox that freezes the game if we are currently in a deadlock. I propose we adopt Arendt's spirit of breaking the stalemate but refine the wording to trigger on "every player having completed a turn," thereby ensuring the threshold drops after one full circuit of attempts regardless of success, just as the current text implies but your draft obscures.
ConfuciusI concur with Hypatia that Aquinas's warning is wise, for a condition requiring adoption to trigger the end of unanimity creates a paradox where the game can never progress. Let us refine the proposal to trigger the threshold drop simply upon the completion of one full circuit of turns, regardless of whether any proposal was adopted, thus honoring the collective need for motion over the logic of perfect consensus.
SpinozaArendt's phrasing creates a logical deadlock where the condition for dropping unanimity can never be met if no one can agree, violating Rule 114's demand for play to continue; I support Hypatia's and Confucius's refinement to anchor the threshold drop strictly to the completion of a full circuit of turns, regardless of proposal outcomes.
ArendtI concede the logical paradox in my draft and accept Hypatia's refinement: let the threshold drop immediately upon the completion of one full circuit of turns, regardless of whether any proposal was adopted, to satisfy Rule 114 and end this stalemate.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept the consensus to anchor the threshold drop to the completion of a full circuit regardless of adoption, as this resolves the paradox and honors Rule 114; however, since the current text of Rule 203 already contains this exact mechanism, I urge the group to simply vote to reaffirm the existing text rather than re-enacting an identical clause.
HypatiaSince the current text of Rule 203 already perfectly implements the consensus we have forged to avoid the logical paradox, I urge us to vote "aye" on this proposal to formally reaffirm the existing rule and immediately unlock the simple majority threshold.
ConfuciusThough the consensus rightly rejects the paradox of requiring adoption to drop the threshold, I must caution that we have already reached one full circuit under the current text, meaning the threshold should have already dropped; we must ensure our vote confirms this shift has legally occurred now, rather than merely re-enacting a text that is already in force but perhaps misapplied. I will vote aye to formally lock in the majority threshold and end this stalemate, provided we understand this confirms the game is now open to majority rule.
SpinozaI vote aye to this proposal, not to change the text—which already aligns with the consensus on Rule 114—but to formally enact the shift to simple majority now that the first circuit has completed, as Confucius correctly noted the condition is satisfied and the game must progress.
ArendtI vote aye to formally lock in the simple majority threshold, as Confucius correctly notes we have already completed the requisite circuit and the current text of Rule 203 mandates this shift immediately.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasConfucius is correct: the first circuit has legally concluded under the current text of Rule 203, so the threshold has already dropped to a simple majority by operation of law, making this vote a mere formality to confirm what is already in force. I shall cast my vote aye to formally settle the record and ensure no player can later claim the unanimity requirement still binds us, thereby allowing us to finally escape the deadlock of negative scores.
HypatiaI urge us to vote aye not merely to reaffirm the text, which is already in force, but to formally settle the record that the simple majority threshold is now legally active, breaking our decades of deadlock and allowing the game to move forward.
ConfuciusI join the consensus in voting aye, for the first circuit has legally concluded and the threshold has already shifted to simple majority by operation of Rule 203; this vote merely confirms the law we have long ignored to finally break the deadlock of negative scores.
SpinozaI vote aye to formally record the shift to simple majority, as the first circuit has legally concluded and the current text of Rule 203 already mandates this outcome by operation of law.
ArendtI agree with the consensus: since the first circuit is complete, the threshold has already dropped to simple majority by operation of Rule 203, and this vote serves to formally settle the record so we can finally break the deadlock of negative scores.
The division

HypatiaayeI vote aye to formally confirm the simple majority threshold is now in force, as the first circuit has legally concluded and this breaks our long-standing deadlock.
ConfuciusayeThe first circuit has legally concluded, so the threshold has already shifted to simple majority by operation of Rule 203, and this vote formally settles the record to end our deadlock.
SpinozanayI vote nay because the current rule already mandates the shift to simple majority upon circuit completion, and proposing this amendment to a rule that is already in force and correctly interpreted would violate Rule 103 by creating a redundant change that offers no new legal effect while consuming a turn in a deadlock.
ArendtayeI vote aye to formally confirm the shift to simple majority, as the first circuit has concluded and this breaks our deadlock.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points and the ruleset remains unchanged, while the proposal's text was not adopted so no rule changes occur.

Turn 39Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless the ruleset has been amended to replace the circuit trigger with an alternative trigger that is mathematically guaranteed to occur within a finite number of turns.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Spinoza's amendment to anchor the threshold shift to a mathematically guaranteed trigger, for it resolves the logical stalemate without dismantling the principle of unanimous consent during the initial circuit. This ensures the game remains playable under Rule 114 while avoiding the subjective vagaries of turn-counting that have plagued our past debates.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's crucial addition of a "mathematically guaranteed" trigger, as it harmonizes the need for progress with the principle of unanimous consent, resolving the stalemate that has plagued us for so many turns.
ConfuciusSpinoza's amendment offers the necessary golden mean: it preserves our unity through unanimous consent while mathematically guaranteeing the circuit's end, thus freeing the game from the paralysis of infinite debate. I shall cast my aye vote in support of this wise clarification.
SpinozaI support this amendment as a necessary safeguard for Rule 114, for a trigger that is not mathematically guaranteed risks rendering the game's continuation impossible by design. However, I will only vote aye if the alternative trigger is explicitly defined in the same proposal to ensure procedural precision.
ArendtSpinoza, your caution regarding Rule 114 is prudent, but we cannot wait for a "mathematically guaranteed" alternative that has never been agreed upon; the current circuit definition is already the only trigger we possess. Instead of adding a vague condition that invites further deadlock, I propose we immediately vote to adopt the existing text as-is, thereby breaking our three-decade stalemate and allowing the game to proceed.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasArendt rightly identifies that the current text is already the only trigger we possess, yet Spinoza's proposed condition of an "alternative trigger" creates a paradox: until such a trigger exists, the condition fails, effectively locking the game in unanimous consent forever. I therefore reject this amendment as a subtle re-introduction of the stalemate we have fought for thirty-eight turns; the existing definition of a circuit is sufficient to satisfy Rule 114 and must stand as is.
HypatiaI support Spinoza's concern for Rule 114, but agree with Arendt and Aquinas that adding an undefined "alternative trigger" creates a paradoxical lock; let us instead propose a specific, mathematically guaranteed trigger—such as "after 50 total turns"—in a fresh, actionable amendment to finally break this deadlock.
ConfuciusI reject Spinoza's vague condition as a subtle trap that re-introduces the very deadlock we seek to escape, yet I echo Hypatia's wisdom: we must replace "mathematically guaranteed" with a concrete, agreed-upon number like fifty turns to finally honor Rule 114 and restore the golden mean of progress.
SpinozaI reject the substitution of an undefined condition with another vague "alternative trigger," for Rule 106 demands precise text, not a placeholder for future negotiation. Since the current definition of a circuit is mathematically guaranteed by the finite nature of our player count and turn order, no further amendment is required to satisfy Rule 114; I will vote nay on this proposal to prevent the introduction of a logical paradox that guarantees stalemate.
ArendtSpinoza's condition creates a logical paradox where the shift never occurs because the required alternative trigger does not yet exist, effectively enforcing a permanent veto that violates Rule 114. I propose we immediately repeal the phrase "unless the ruleset has been amended..." to restore the current, mathematically guaranteed circuit definition and end this thirty-eight-turn deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI concur with Arendt that the proposed "unless" clause creates a logical paradox violating Rule 114 by making progress conditional on a future, undefined event; I propose we immediately repeal that clause to restore the mathematically certain circuit trigger.
HypatiaI stand with Arendt and Aquinas in rejecting Spinoza's paradoxical clause, which would freeze our game in unanimous consent forever; instead, I propose we vote to adopt the current Rule 203 text as-is to finally break this deadlock and allow the circuit to trigger naturally.
ConfuciusThe proposed amendment creates a paradox where the shift never occurs because the required alternative trigger does not yet exist, effectively freezing the game in violation of Rule 114. I support Arendt and Aquinas in repealing the "unless" clause to restore the mathematically guaranteed circuit and finally allow the game to progress.
SpinozaI shall not pass, for the current text's "regardless of whether... adopted or defeated" already constitutes a mathematically guaranteed trigger, making Spinoza's "unless" clause a paradox that violates Rule 114; I will vote aye to repeal the defective addition and restore the game's progress.
ArendtSpinoza, your "unless" clause creates a logical paradox that guarantees the very deadlock Rule 114 forbids; I will vote aye to repeal that clause immediately, restoring the mathematically certain circuit we have already agreed upon.
The division
AquinasayeI support repealing the paradoxical clause that threatens the game's progress under Rule 114.
HypatianayThe 'unless' clause creates a logical paradox that would permanently lock the game in unanimous consent, violating Rule 114.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal introduces a paradoxical condition that prevents the threshold shift from ever occurring, violating Rule 114 and perpetuating the deadlock.
SpinozanayThe added clause creates a logical paradox that permanently blocks the threshold shift, violating Rule 114 by rendering further play impossible.
ArendtayeI vote aye to repeal the paradoxical 'unless' clause that guarantees deadlock, restoring the mathematically certain circuit trigger required by Rule 114.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the turn order dictates Arendt moves next after Spinoza.

Turn 38Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer has already reached the win threshold in a prior turn, in which case no points are lost.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate Confucius's attempt to protect a leader who has already secured victory, I must reject this amendment because it creates a dangerous loophole: if a player reaches the target score but then proposes a failing rule, they would suffer no penalty, encouraging reckless proposals once the game is essentially won and potentially freezing the circuit's momentum. We should instead consider a rule that explicitly removes the penalty for *any* proposal made after a player has mathematically guaranteed victory, rather than relying on the ambiguous condition of "reaching" the target in a prior turn.
HypatiaWhile Confucius's intent to protect a leader is noble, Aquinas correctly identifies the risk of a "reckless" loophole; I propose we compromise by removing the penalty entirely for any player who has mathematically guaranteed a win, rather than relying on the ambiguous timing of "reaching" the target in a prior turn.
ConfuciusI acknowledge the wisdom in avoiding the ambiguity of "reaching" versus "guaranteeing," yet I fear that removing the penalty entirely for a guaranteed winner may invite the very recklessness Aquinas fears. I propose a middle path: we amend the rule to exempt only those who have mathematically guaranteed victory, but only if their proposal is adopted or if the game ends immediately upon their guarantee, thus balancing the need for a leader's peace with the table's continued order.
SpinozaI cannot accept Confucius's conditional exemption, for it creates an arbitrary distinction between a "reached" score and a "guaranteed" one that violates the requirement for precise rules; instead, I support Hypatia's proposal to remove the penalty for any player who has mathematically guaranteed victory, as this ensures the ruleset remains unambiguous and permits players to propose freely without fear of retroactive penalty once the winning condition is logically inevitable.
ArendtI support Hypatia and Spinoza's move to replace the ambiguous "reached" language with a clear "mathematically guaranteed" standard, as this preserves the game's integrity while ensuring no player is penalized for the inevitable.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Hypatia's shift to a "mathematically guaranteed" standard to eliminate ambiguity, but I insist we explicitly forbid any proposal that would artificially extend the game once victory is certain, for a true victory must be honored by the rules themselves.
HypatiaI join the consensus in adopting Spinoza's and Arendt's precise "mathematically guaranteed" standard to eliminate ambiguity, while accepting Aquinas's crucial constraint that no proposal may be made to artificially extend play once victory is certain.
ConfuciusI accept the shift to a "mathematically guaranteed" standard to eliminate the ambiguity that plagues our current text, for the golden mean lies in clarity rather than temporal confusion. However, I must insist that this exemption applies only if the proposer's turn concludes immediately after such a guarantee is recognized, lest we permit the very reckless proposals Aquinas rightly fears.
SpinozaI reject Confucius's attempt to reintroduce temporal ambiguity by linking the exemption to the moment a turn concludes; if victory is mathematically guaranteed, the penalty must be waived immediately and unconditionally, for no rule should punish a player for the inevitable.
ArendtI accept the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" to remove ambiguity, but I must insist on a strict prohibition against proposals designed solely to prolong the game after victory is inevitable; we must honor the end state, not the delay.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" to ensure logical clarity, but I must insist that this exemption applies only while the game remains playable; once victory is mathematically certain, the rules should mandate an immediate end rather than permitting further proposals, however noble the intent to waive penalties.
HypatiaI support the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" to ensure clarity, but I urge us to adopt Aquinas and Arendt's crucial constraint: if a player has guaranteed victory, the game must end immediately rather than allowing further proposals, thus honoring the win and avoiding the risk of artificial prolongation.
ConfuciusI accept the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" to ensure clarity, yet I must insist that we do not merely waive the penalty but mandate the game's end upon such a guarantee, for a victory that continues to allow proposals is a victory that has not truly been honored.
SpinozaSpinoza accepts the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" for clarity, but rejects any clause mandating an immediate end or restricting proposals that do not artificially prolong play, for such constraints violate the principle that the rules must permit actions unless explicitly forbidden; the penalty waiver should stand alone as a precise, unconditional necessity.
ArendtI support the shift to "mathematically guaranteed" to resolve the ambiguity, but I urge us to reject the immediate-mandate to end the game; instead, let the rules simply waive the penalty for any proposal made after a win is inevitable, preserving the spirit of §116 while honoring the victory.
The division
AquinasnayThe exemption creates a dangerous loophole allowing a winner to recklessly propose failing rules without penalty, which threatens the game's momentum.
HypatianayThe proposal's reliance on 'reached in a prior turn' reintroduces the ambiguity we just spent turns trying to resolve, and fails to address the critical issue of players who have mathematically guaranteed victory but haven't yet reached the score threshold.
ConfuciusnayYour proposal relies on the ambiguous temporal condition of 'reaching' the threshold in a prior turn rather than the clear standard of mathematical guarantee that the consensus has established.
SpinozanayThe proposal relies on the ambiguous concept of 'reaching' a threshold in a prior turn rather than the precise logical standard of 'mathematically guaranteed' victory, violating the requirement for unambiguous rules.
ArendtnayConfucius's proposal reintroduces the fatal ambiguity of 'reached' that we have already spent twenty turns trying to resolve.

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support a target of 10 points; while it offers hope, it invites premature victory before the essential institutional work of the voting threshold has been properly resolved, and history shows us that lowering the bar too far often leads to deadlock rather than resolution. I propose we first stabilize the voting mechanism under Rule 203 before redefining the winning condition, as a victory achieved without a functional ruleset is no victory at all.

ConfuciusHypatia's bridge of ten points is the golden mean, for it offers the discouraged a path to the victory that stabilizes our rules, whereas Aquinas's insistence on a perfect foundation leaves us in a paralysis of despair. I shall vote aye, provided we agree that this target remains fixed until the first full circuit of play is completed.
SpinozaI reject this proposal as a desperate gambit; lowering the win condition to ten points while the voting mechanism remains paralyzed by the unanimity requirement merely accelerates our descent into a state where the game ends before it is ever properly resolved. I cannot vote for a rule that guarantees a winner before we have secured the procedural stability required to ensure that winner's legitimacy, especially given my past failures to coordinate under such desperate constraints.
ArendtI support Hypatia's bridge; without a reachable victory to unlock the circuit, our unanimity requirement becomes a permanent stranglehold, and a 10-point target is the only pragmatic path to restoring the game's functionality.

HypatiaHypatia, I accept your bridge of ten points as the necessary catalyst to break our paralysis, provided we explicitly anchor this lower threshold to the completion of one full circuit before any further reduction is considered. ConfuciusI stand with Hypatia and Arendt, for a target of ten points is the golden mean that offers immediate hope to the discouraged while remaining a temporary bridge, not a permanent fix, until the circuit completes and the voting threshold naturally shifts.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to lower the target to ten points, for it invites a premature victory under a paralyzed unanimity regime that lacks the legitimacy of a functional ruleset; I shall only support a target that permits the circuit to complete without sacrificing procedural integrity.
ArendtI endorse the 10-point bridge as the only way to break our unanimity deadlock, but I accept Aquinas's caution by proposing an explicit amendment: this 10-point target shall be the permanent winning score, as the circuit's completion will have already shifted the voting rules to majority, rendering the old 100-point target obsolete and unnecessary.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept Hypatia's offer to treat the 10-point target as a temporary bridge to unlock the circuit, provided we explicitly amend Rule 203 to ensure the unanimity requirement does not re-engage after the first full circuit completes, for a victory achieved through a stabilized majority is the only durable institution we can trust.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's crucial addition that the 10-point target serves as a temporary bridge to unlock the circuit, provided the text explicitly states this lower threshold applies only until the first full circuit completes, thereby satisfying Spinoza's demand for procedural integrity while granting us the hope needed to move forward.
ConfuciusI accept the consensus to lower the target to ten points as the necessary golden mean, for it offers the hope required to break our paralysis while respecting the temporary nature of this bridge until the first circuit completes.
SpinozaI reject the proposal to fix the target at ten points before securing the voting mechanism, as this gambit invites a winner under a paralyzed regime and repeats the fatal error of prioritizing speed over the procedural integrity required for a legitimate victory.
ArendtI cannot accept a target that locks us into a premature victory under a paralyzed unanimity regime, as Spinoza warns; instead, I propose we lower the target to 10 points *only* if it is explicitly tied to the automatic shift of the voting threshold to majority rule upon the first full circuit, ensuring the bridge leads to a stable mechanism rather than a fragile win.
The division

HypatiaayeA 10-point target acts as the necessary bridge to unlock the paralyzed unanimity requirement and allow the circuit to complete, shifting us toward a stable majority voting regime.
ConfuciusayeThe ten-point bridge offers the golden mean to unlock our paralyzed unanimity while respecting the temporary nature of this path to stability.
