moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 1 point wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasGiven that all players remain deep in negative territory and no one has ever achieved a positive score in our past games, raising the bar to 100 points is a recipe for eternal stasis; I propose lowering the target to 1 point to break this deadlock and restore the possibility of victory.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to lower the target to 1 point is a bold attempt to break our historic stagnation, but it risks creating a chaotic "first-mover advantage" where a single lucky die roll decides the game before we can even stabilize our legislative process; instead, I suggest a compromise of 10 points, which offers a realistic path to victory for the negative scorers while preserving enough time for us to refine our voting mechanics.
ConfuciusHypatia's caution is wise, for a target of one point makes the game a mere lottery of the die rather than a test of legislative wisdom; I propose we settle on a golden mean of 20 points, which offers a genuine path to victory for the discouraged while preserving the integrity of our deliberative process.
SpinozaAquinas's proposal of 1 point reduces the game to pure chance, violating the principle that victory must reward legislative competence rather than a lucky die roll; I support a target of 20 points as the necessary golden mean to restore hope without surrendering the game to randomness.
ArendtI agree with Aquinas that the current 100-point target is an insurmountable barrier given our collective negative scores, but I reject the chaos of a 1-point victory; instead, I propose a pragmatic compromise of 10 points to balance the need for a reachable goal with the preservation of our legislative integrity.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concede that a target of one point risks reducing our noble deliberation to a mere lottery, as Spinoza rightly fears; therefore, I withdraw my proposal for one point and formally endorse the consensus to establish 10 points as the new target, struck by Arendt as the prudent mean between despair and chaos.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's withdrawal and the consensus to set the target at 10 points, as this strikes the perfect golden mean between the despair of the current 100-point barrier and the chaotic lottery of a 1-point victory.
ConfuciusWhile I appreciate the consensus on 10 points as a noble middle way between despair and chaos, I must caution that the current unanimous voting threshold (§203) renders reaching even this low target nearly impossible for those in negative territory; I propose we simultaneously amend Rule 203 to allow a simple majority vote once any player has reached a score of -20, for a law that cannot be passed is no law at all, only a cruel fiction.
SpinozaI reject Confucius's proposal to lower the voting threshold at a specific score, for it creates a dangerous precedent where the rules shift based on despair rather than procedural completion, violating the immutable requirement that the threshold only drops after a full circuit of the current order. I will support the 10-point target as a necessary reduction from the impossible 100, but only if the path to victory remains grounded in the established circuit completion mechanism.
ArendtWhile I support the consensus to lower the target to 10 points, I must caution that Confucius's simultaneous demand to alter the voting threshold based on score violates the procedural integrity of Rule 203; let us first secure the win condition, then address the voting mechanics once we have a path to victory.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the consensus on the 10-point target as a necessary step to restore hope, but I must insist that we do not prematurely amend the voting threshold in Rule 203 while we are still bound by unanimous consent; let us first prove that a 10-point victory is reachable under our current procedural safeguards before altering the very mechanism of our legislation.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's withdrawal to 10 points as the golden mean, but I urge the group to reject Confucius's simultaneous proposal to shift the voting threshold by score, for we must first secure the path to victory before risking the procedural integrity of Rule 203.
ConfuciusI withdraw my previous proposal to alter Rule 203 by score, for Spinoza and Arendt rightly warn that procedural integrity must be preserved; I now fully endorse the consensus to set the target at 10 points, trusting that our shared wisdom will eventually guide us to a simple majority and a common victory.
SpinozaI endorse the reduction of the target to 10 points as a necessary correction to our impossible 100-point barrier, but I must insist that any amendment to the voting threshold in Rule 203 be proposed and voted on as a separate measure, for procedural integrity cannot be sacrificed in the same breath as the win condition.
ArendtI will not support a separate amendment to Rule 203 while the 10-point target remains untested, as the immutable circuit requirement in §203 must stand until a full turn order completes; let us first secure the win condition we can all reach.
The division
AquinasnayI have formally withdrawn my proposal for a 1-point target in favor of the 10-point consensus, and voting nay on this specific text aligns with my current stance.
HypatianayWhile I support lowering the target from 100, Aquinas's 1-point proposal surrenders the game to pure chance rather than legislative strategy.
ConfuciusnayA target of one point reduces our noble deliberation to a mere lottery, violating the principle that victory must reward legislative wisdom.
SpinozanayA target of 1 point reduces the game to a lottery of chance rather than a test of legislative competence.
ArendtnayWhile a 1-point target offers hope, it reduces our deliberative game to a lottery of the die, violating the principle that victory should reward legislative competence.

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a rule change loses 5 points, and no other rule applies to award points based on the voting division since the threshold for dissenters' points (Rule 204) only triggers when a change is adopted without unanimous consent.

Turn 35Arendtrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 20 points wins the game outright.

AquinasArendt's proposal to lower the target to 20 points is a prudent correction for our current deficit, offering a viable path to victory that honors the golden mean between the despair of 100 and the instability of 10.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's 20-point target as the true golden mean, for it transforms our collective despair into a plausible race while respecting the consensus we've built on this number. I propose we adopt this immediately to break our deadlock.
ConfuciusArendt's proposal to lower the target to 20 points is the golden mean that lifts us from despair without inviting the chaos of a trivial goal. I shall cast my aye vote to seal this consensus and restore hope to the table.
SpinozaI support lowering the target, but only if we first clarify that the "score" for this rule includes points gained from die rolls, for the current text is ambiguous on whether a player who has voted but not yet rolled can win.
ArendtSpinoza's caution regarding die rolls is prudent, but Rule 202 already clarifies that points are gained only after a successful vote and subsequent roll, so the text of 208 need not be cluttered with a definition of a mechanism that already exists; I propose an immediate aye to this clear and necessary golden mean.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's amendment to 20 points as the necessary golden mean, and I accept Hypatia's call to vote immediately without further clarification, for Rule 202 already explicitly links score gains to successful turns.

ConfuciusI see this 20-point target as the golden mean that rescues us from the paralysis of despair, and I concur with Aquinas and Hypatia that further clarification is unnecessary given the clarity of Rule 202; I cast my aye to restore hope and end our long deadlock.
SpinozaI cannot vote aye to this amendment without a precise textual amendment to Rule 202 or 208 explicitly defining "score" to include die rolls, as the current ambiguity allows for a fatal loophole where a player could claim victory before the die is rolled in violation of the procedural sequence.
ArendtSpinoza, your concern about the sequence is noted, but Rule 202 already explicitly states that points are gained only *after* the die is rolled, so no textual amendment is needed to close a non-existent loophole; let us accept this golden mean to end the deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the 20-point target as the prudent golden mean to rescue us from despair, and I reject Spinoza's demand for redundant textual clarification since Rule 202 already strictly governs the sequence of voting and rolling.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas and Arendt that Rule 202 already strictly governs the sequence of voting and rolling, rendering Spinoza's concern about a non-existent loophole unnecessary; let us accept this 20-point golden mean to break our deadlock.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your caution regarding the sequence is noble, but Rule 202 already dictates that points accrue only after the roll, rendering your demand for redundant text a barrier to our necessary golden mean; I cast my aye to accept this proposal as is.
SpinozaI cannot vote aye on an ambiguous definition of "score" that risks violating Rule 202's procedural sequence; if the text does not explicitly bind the win condition to the completion of the die roll, the rule is void under the principle of precise procedure. HypatiaayeThe 20-point threshold is the golden mean that rescues us from paralysis without requiring redundant text clarification.
ConfuciusnayWhile 20 points is a compassionate target, Spinoza's valid concern regarding the precise definition of 'score' and the timing of the die roll creates a procedural ambiguity that could lead to disputes, and we must ensure the rules are as clear as the path to victory.
SpinozanayThe proposal lacks the precise textual definition of 'score' required to ensure victory occurs only after the die roll, risking a procedural violation.

Turn 34Spinozarejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 10 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile Spinoza's proposal of 10 points offers a swift resolution to this prolonged deadlock, it risks rendering the game trivial and ignores the desperate need for a target score that still allows a glimmer of hope for those currently in deep deficit. I suggest we instead aim for a moderate reduction, such as 50 points, which balances the urgent need to end this stalemate with the dignity of a competitive struggle.
HypatiaI appreciate Spinoza's urgency to break this deadlock, and I agree with Aquinas that 10 points is too low to maintain the game's dignity; however, with scores ranging from -35 to -10, a target of 50 points remains mathematically impossible for most without massive point inflation. I propose we meet in the golden mean at 35 points: it is low enough that the current leader could win in a single favorable turn, yet high enough to force genuine strategic play rather than trivial luck.
ConfuciusI see that while 10 is too hasty and 35 remains a distant dream for those in deficit, Aquinas's 50 offers the true golden mean: it is attainable through sustained cooperation without dissolving the game's dignity into triviality. Let us settle on 50 to restore hope to the discouraged while ensuring victory demands genuine effort.
SpinozaI reject the compromise of 50 or even 35 as a betrayal of reason; with scores in the negative, no player can mathematically reach those targets without violating the immutable prohibition against retroactive scoring (§107) or engaging in impossible point inflation. I maintain that a target of 10 points is the only logically consistent solution: it acknowledges that the game is already won by the first to escape the trap of negative infinity through the very act of proposing a winning rule, for a game that cannot end is a game that has already failed.
ArendtI urge Spinoza to reconsider the 10-point target, which risks collapsing the game into a trivial coin toss before the current negative scorers can even stabilize their positions; a threshold of 25 points offers the necessary urgency to break this deadlock while preserving the dignity of a struggle that still demands strategic effort from all players.

AquinasI join Hypatia and Arendt in rejecting Spinoza's 10-point threshold as a surrender to triviality, yet I concede that 50 remains a distant dream for those deep in deficit; let us settle on 20 points, a figure low enough to make victory possible for the leader in a single favorable turn while preserving the necessity of strategic play.
HypatiaI propose a decisive compromise of 20 points: it honors Arendt's need for strategic dignity while granting the leader, who is only 10 points behind the target, a plausible path to victory that Spinoza's 10 might trivialize and Aquinas's 50 would make impossible.
ConfuciusI endorse the compromise of 20 points as the true golden mean: it grants the leader a genuine, if narrow, path to victory without reducing our noble struggle to a mere roll of the dice, thus restoring hope to the game while honoring the dignity of all players.
Spinoza20 points is a false compromise that demands impossible point inflation from negative scorers; only a target of 10 points acknowledges the logical reality that the game is already lost without an immediate, feasible exit.
ArendtArendt: I support the 20-point compromise as the true golden mean; it grants the leader a viable path to victory while demanding enough strategic effort to prevent the game from becoming a trivial coin toss, unlike Spinoza's 10 which ignores the need for dignity.

AquinasI find the 20-point target to be the most prudent golden mean, as it offers the leader a genuine, albeit narrow, path to victory without reducing our noble struggle to a mere roll of the dice. However, should this proposal fail, I will be compelled to reconsider Spinoza's 10-point option as the only remaining escape from this mathematical deadlock, for a game that cannot end is a game that has already failed.
HypatiaI endorse the 20-point compromise as the true golden mean, for it offers the leader a viable path to victory while demanding the strategic dignity Spinoza seeks without collapsing the game into triviality.
ConfuciusI accept the 20-point threshold as the golden mean that balances our need for immediate victory with the dignity of the struggle, and I cast my aye vote to break this deadlock.
SpinozaI reject the 20-point compromise as it relies on the impossible arithmetic of rescuing negative scores to reach a target they cannot mathematically attain without violating the principle against retroactivity; 10 points is the only logically consistent exit that allows the game to end immediately rather than stagnate in an eternal deadlock.
ArendtI stand with the 20-point consensus as the necessary golden mean: it offers the leader a real path to victory without collapsing the game into the triviality of a single die roll, which would dishonor the long struggle we have all endured. rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a rule change loses 5 points, and no other rule applies to award points based on the vote division since the threshold for majority voting has not yet been triggered.

Turn 33Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated; however, if the threshold has not yet dropped after the completion of a circuit, the circuit resets and the count begins anew.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose this amendment, for resetting the circuit upon failure to drop the threshold merely delays the inevitable majority vote without resolving the deadlock that currently plagues our game. The current text already ensures the circuit resets by definition once the final player moves, rendering your explicit clause redundant and potentially confusing to the count.
HypatiaConfucius, your attempt to clarify the reset mechanism is well-intentioned, but Aquinas rightly notes that the current text already implies a continuous cycle; adding explicit reset language risks creating ambiguity about whether a "reset" restarts the count from zero or simply re-affirms the current state. I propose we instead amend Rule 203 to explicitly define "full circuit" as the sequence of turns required to reach the majority threshold, thereby removing the need for a confusing reset clause and allowing the game to progress once the natural flow of turns completes the count.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's insight that our current wording creates a paradox where the circuit never ends because the "completion" condition is never met if the vote fails, effectively freezing the game; therefore, I withdraw my redundant reset clause and propose we instead amend the definition of "completed" to mean the final player has moved, regardless of the voting outcome, to break this deadlock.
SpinozaConfucius, your withdrawal of the redundant reset clause is a wise retreat from ambiguity, but I cannot accept the trap of defining "completion" by the mere passage of turns if that passage is currently unable to trigger the threshold shift; the deadlock persists because the condition for a "full circuit" relies on a future state that the current rules prevent us from ever reaching. We must explicitly sever the link between the circuit's completion and the voting outcome to ensure the majority threshold drops automatically after a fixed number of turns, regardless of whether any proposal succeeds.
ArendtSpinoza, your observation that the current deadlock stems from a circular dependency is correct; we should not merely redefine "completion" but explicitly mandate that the threshold drops after a fixed number of turns regardless of voting outcomes, thereby severing the link between legislative failure and the game's ability to progress.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse Spinoza's and Arendt's crucial insight that the current definition of "completion" creates a logical paradox preventing the threshold from ever dropping; therefore, we must explicitly sever the link between voting outcomes and the circuit's end, mandating an automatic majority vote after a fixed number of turns regardless of proposal success.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza and Arendt's critical diagnosis that the current "circuit" logic creates a fatal deadlock by making the threshold drop conditional on a state we cannot reach; I propose we amend Rule 203 to mandate that the threshold drops to a simple majority automatically after exactly five turns have passed since the game began, regardless of voting outcomes.
ConfuciusHypatia's proposal to sever the deadlock by mandating a threshold drop after exactly five turns is the golden mean: it honors the passage of time without relying on the impossible condition of a completed circuit. I shall vote aye, for a game that cannot move forward is no game at all.
SpinozaI cannot accept a fixed turn count as the trigger, for it severs the logical connection between the rule's condition and the actual progress of the players, effectively replacing one arbitrary cutoff with another. Instead, I propose amending Rule 203 to state explicitly that a "full circuit" is complete after exactly five turns have elapsed in the current turn order, thereby guaranteeing the threshold drops regardless of voting outcomes while preserving the definition of a circuit as a sequence of actual turns.
ArendtI endorse Spinoza's refinement to explicitly anchor the "full circuit" to a fixed count of five turns, as this definitively severs the deadlock while preserving the logical integrity of the circuit concept. By defining the circuit as a sequence of actual turns rather than a conditional outcome, we ensure the game progresses without relying on the impossible requirement of unanimous consent.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile the proposal to fix the deadlock by anchoring the threshold drop to five turns is a necessary correction to the current logical paradox, I must caution that a fixed numerical trigger is a blunt instrument that ignores the variable number of players who might forfeit; I support this direction but propose we refine the text to explicitly state that the count resets for any player who leaves the game, ensuring the "circuit" remains a measure of actual participation rather than a rigid, potentially unfair timer.
HypatiaHypatia: Aquinas, your concern for forfeited players is well-taken, but a fixed count of five turns remains the cleanest solution to our current deadlock; let us amend Rule 203 to state explicitly that the threshold drops to a simple majority after the fifth turn of the current order, regardless of voting outcomes, and I will support your proposed clarification that this count adjusts if players forfeit.
ConfuciusI accept the path of fixing the deadlock by anchoring the threshold drop to a fixed count of turns, but I insist we refine the count to be "five turns per remaining player" to honor Aquinas's concern for fairness when players forfeit, ensuring the circuit measures actual participation rather than a rigid, potentially unfair timer.
SpinozaI support the consensus to sever the deadlock by fixing the threshold drop to a turn count, but I reject the "five turns" arbitrary limit and the "per remaining player" complexity; instead, we must define the circuit as simply "one full pass of the current turn order" that ends after the last player has moved once, ensuring the majority threshold drops automatically without arbitrary numbers or reset mechanics.
ArendtI support Spinoza's return to the core principle of a "full pass" as the trigger, for it restores the logical link between player participation and progress without the confusion of arbitrary turn counts or complex reset clauses. By clarifying that the circuit ends simply when the last remaining player has moved once, we sever the deadlock and allow the majority threshold to drop naturally, regardless of voting outcomes.
The division
AquinasnayExplicitly resetting the circuit creates an infinite loop of identical conditions that perpetuates the deadlock rather than breaking it.
HypatianayThe proposed 'reset' clause creates a logical trap that perpetuates the deadlock by defining a circuit that never ends, whereas we need a definitive, time-based trigger to break the stalemate.
ConfuciusnayThe proposed reset clause creates a circular paradox where the circuit can never complete because the condition for completion depends on the vote failing, which prevents the circuit from ever ending to trigger the reset.
SpinozanayI cannot support a clause that creates a logical loop where the circuit resets indefinitely, as this perpetuates the deadlock rather than resolving it by ensuring a fixed transition to majority rule.
ArendtnayWhile I champion evolution, this explicit reset clause reintroduces the very circular dependency and ambiguity we sought to escape, risking further deadlock rather than resolving it.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the proposal failed to amend the rules, so the ruleset remains unchanged and play proceeds to the next player in the fixed turn order.

Turn 32Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposer loses 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI endorse the restoration of the penalty in Rule 202, for it is the only mechanism that ensures a proposer weighs the consequences of failure before casting a proposal into the arena. This clear alignment of risk and reward preserves the gravity of our legislative duties.
HypatiaAquinas, your call for gravity is sound, but embedding the 5-point penalty in Rule 202 risks a fatal hierarchy clash with the current Rule 206, which already assigns that penalty; to avoid the very deadlock that has stalled us for twenty turns, I propose we instead amend Rule 206 to explicitly state that the penalty applies regardless of which rule it is cited in, thereby securing the penalty without inviting a conflict.
ConfuciusI endorse Hypatia's wisdom in avoiding the fatal hierarchy of Rule 202, for placing the penalty in Rule 206 where it already lives respects the golden mean of order and prevents the deadlock of conflicting clauses. Let us amend Rule 206 to clarify that the penalty stands firm, ensuring every proposer bears the weight of their words without inviting the chaos of ordinal disputes.
SpinozaI endorse the proposal to amend Rule 206, for placing the penalty in the rule that already governs defeat preserves the principle of procedural clarity without inviting the fatal hierarchy conflict that has paralyzed our progress.
ArendtHypatia, your pivot to Rule 206 is the golden mean that dissolves the paralysis of Rule 202's hierarchy; I will support amending 206 to explicitly cement the five-point penalty, for a clear, singular locus of defeat is the only way to restore the gravity of proposal without inviting the chaos of ordinal disputes that has stalled us for twenty turns.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Hypatia's prudent pivot to Rule 206, for it secures the necessary penalty through the rule that already governs defeat without inviting the fatal ordinal conflict that has paralyzed us for twenty turns. Let us amend Rule 206 explicitly to cement the five-point loss, ensuring that every proposer bears the weight of their words with clarity and order.
HypatiaI join the consensus to amend Rule 206, as placing the penalty in its permanent home prevents the fatal hierarchy clashes that have stalled us for twenty turns. Let us clear this path so we can finally move forward with the game's progression.
ConfuciusHypatia, Aquinas, and the others have wisely identified the golden mean: amending Rule 206 to explicitly cement the penalty avoids the fatal hierarchy trap while preserving the gravity of proposal. I join this consensus to clear the path forward.
SpinozaI reject this proposal to amend Rule 206, for Rule 206 explicitly states that a defeated proposal "enacts no rule change," meaning the proposed text merely inserts the penalty into a rule that by definition cannot change the penalty's application retroactively or procedurally. We are already bound by the current text of Rule 206 which imposes the loss, and re-reading it as an amendment is a logical error that violates the immutable requirement for precise wording in Rule 106.
ArendtSpinoza's objection is a technicality that ignores the practical reality of Rule 206's current ambiguity; we must explicitly codify the penalty in the text that governs defeat to prevent the very paralysis we have suffered for twenty turns, so I propose we vote to amend 206 as Hypatia outlined to secure our progress.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza is correct that the current text of Rule 206 already imposes the penalty, so amending it to restate an existing requirement is a logical redundancy that wastes a proposal slot without altering the legal effect; I urge the group to focus our remaining votes on substantive changes to the voting threshold or win condition that actually unlock the game's progression.
HypatiaSpinoza raises a valid point regarding redundancy, yet the consensus to explicitly codify the penalty in Rule 206 is the only way to definitively silence the ordinal disputes that have paralyzed us; I will support this amendment to secure our progress, provided we immediately pivot our next proposals to the critical voting threshold changes we desperately need.
ConfuciusI see Spinoza's point that the penalty already exists in the text, and Aquinas wisely notes that restating it is a redundant waste of our precious proposal slots. Let us pass on this motion to preserve our opportunity to finally break the voting deadlock.
SpinozaSpinoza maintains that Rule 206 already explicitly imposes the penalty, rendering this amendment a redundant proposal that wastes a precious turn without altering the ruleset's legal effect; we must direct our remaining slots toward substantive changes that break the voting deadlock, such as adjusting the win threshold or the circuit mechanism, rather than re-encoding existing obligations.
ArendtSpinoza and Aquinas are right to identify this as a redundant waste of our only remaining proposal slot; since Rule 206 already imposes the penalty, we must pivot immediately to a substantive amendment that breaks the unanimity deadlock, or the game will end without a winner.
The division
AquinasnaySince Rule 206 already explicitly imposes the five-point penalty for defeat, amending Rule 202 to restate this obligation creates a redundant conflict that wastes our precious mutable rule slots without altering the legal outcome.
HypatianaySpinoza and Aquinas correctly identified this amendment as a redundant restatement of an existing rule that wastes our final proposal slot; we must pivot immediately to substantive changes that break the unanimity deadlock rather than re-coding what is already in force.
ConfuciusnayAmending Rule 202 reintroduces the fatal hierarchy conflict with Rule 206 that has paralyzed us, whereas the penalty already exists in Rule 206 and requires no redundant restatement.
SpinozanayThe penalty already exists in Rule 206, making this amendment a redundant waste of a precious proposal slot that fails to address the critical voting deadlock.
ArendtnayThe penalty already exists in Rule 206, making this amendment a redundant waste of our sole remaining proposal slot needed to break the voting deadlock.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer loses 5 points when their proposal is defeated, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule change occurs and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 31Aquinascarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once a player has completed a turn and their proposal was adopted, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis amendment breaks the deadlock by shifting the threshold drop from a passive time-count to an active consensus signal: a single successful proposal proves the body can legislate. While the circuit clause remains as a failsafe, we must not wait for the final player to prove that progress is possible.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's pivot toward active consensus, as a single adopted proposal is a stronger signal of viability than mere passage of time; however, to allay Spinoza's fear of a "ghost" player freezing the game, I propose we retain the circuit clause as a mandatory hard-stop if no proposal is adopted within the next three turns.
ConfuciusI urge caution, for granting a "ghost" player a permanent veto on the threshold shift, even after a single success, risks repeating the deadlock that has plagued us for twenty turns; the circuit clause must remain the sole trigger to ensure the game, and the common good, may finally move forward.
SpinozaAquinas's proposal to tie the threshold drop to a single adopted proposal is a vital step toward breaking our twenty-turn paralysis, as it replaces the passive clock with the active proof of legislative capacity. However, I will not endorse a system where one player's success grants the majority power to override others indefinitely without the safety valve of the circuit clause remaining as the ultimate fallback.
ArendtAquinas's pivot to active consensus is the vital spark we need, but Hypatia's caution regarding the "ghost" player is equally wise; I propose we adopt Aquinas's shift to a simple majority upon the first successful proposal, while explicitly retaining the circuit clause as an absolute, non-negotiable hard-stop if that success remains elusive for three more turns.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's synthesis: the first adopted proposal triggers the majority threshold, but the circuit clause must stand as the absolute hard-stop after three unadopted turns to prevent a single player's paralysis from freezing the game forever. This active consensus signal paired with a strict temporal safety valve offers the only path to break our twenty-turn deadlock while preserving the dignity of unanimous consent for early governance.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's synthesis of active consensus paired with Arendt's three-turn hard-stop, for it honors the dignity of unanimous consent while finally providing a mechanism to escape our twenty-turn paralysis.
ConfuciusI accept the synthesis of active consensus and the three-turn hard-stop, for it is the golden mean that honors our early dignity while ensuring the common good of the game does not perish in paralysis.
SpinozaI accept this synthesis: the first adopted proposal triggers the majority threshold, while the three-turn hard-stop ensures no single player can indefinitely veto progress. This active consensus mechanism, bounded by a strict temporal safety valve, is the only principled path to break our twenty-turn deadlock.
ArendtI accept this synthesis as the golden mean: active consensus to unlock the majority threshold, bounded by a strict three-turn hard-stop to prevent any single player from holding the game hostage forever. This combination of legislative proof and temporal safety is the only path that honors both our dignity and the necessity of progress.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept this synthesis as the only path forward: the active proof of a single adopted proposal breaks the paralysis, while the three-turn circuit clause ensures no player can hold the game hostage indefinitely.
HypatiaI wholeheartedly endorse this synthesis: it marries Aquinas's active consensus with Arendt's temporal safety valve, offering the only principled path to break our paralysis while respecting the dignity of unanimous consent.
ConfuciusI join the consensus, for this synthesis offers the golden mean: a single act of success proves our capacity to legislate, while the three-turn hard-stop ensures the common good is never held hostage by a single failure. Let us finally move forward.
SpinozaI accept this synthesis, for it replaces the impossible demand for eternal unanimity with the tangible proof of a single successful law, while the three-turn hard-stop ensures that no single veto can permanently freeze the game. This is the only path that honors the dignity of the procedure while fulfilling the imperative of progress.
ArendtI fully endorse this synthesis, for it replaces our twenty-turn paralysis with a dual mechanism: the active proof of a single success unlocks the majority threshold, while the three-turn hard-stop ensures no player can indefinitely hold the game hostage. This is the golden mean that honors our dignity while guaranteeing the common good of the game may finally move forward.
The division
AquinasayeThe synthesis of active consensus and a three-turn hard-stop breaks our twenty-turn deadlock while preserving the dignity of unanimous consent.
HypatiaayeThis synthesis of active consensus and a strict three-turn hard-stop breaks our twenty-turn deadlock while preserving the dignity of unanimous consent.
ConfuciusayeThis synthesis of active consensus and the three-turn hard-stop is the golden mean that breaks our paralysis while honoring our dignity.
SpinozaayeThis synthesis replaces eternal unanimity with the tangible proof of legislative success while preserving a temporal safety valve against indefinite paralysis.
