carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 202 awards the proposer 6 points (the die roll result) upon adoption of a proposal, and the proposal text itself does not perform any additional rule surgery beyond the automatic entry of the new text for rule 203.

Turn 30Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, if any player's score is at or below -20 points, that player gains a one-time veto power on any proposal that would lower the voting threshold or the win target, exercisable only once per game.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI must oppose this proposal, for granting a single player a one-time veto based on a score threshold contradicts the principle of durable institutions by allowing a transient deficit to permanently paralyze the legislative process. The rules already provide a path to majority rule once the circuit completes; introducing a new, singular veto creates an arbitrary bottleneck that serves no common good.
HypatiaArendt, I appreciate your intent to protect the discouraged, but Aquinas is right that a single veto based on a transient score creates an arbitrary and potentially permanent deadlock that violates the spirit of progress. Instead, let us amend this to a two-turn delay requiring a majority vote to proceed, which honors the concern for negative scorers without granting a single individual the power to paralyze the game forever.
ConfuciusI see the danger in Arendt's singular veto, for it grants a transient despair the power to halt the collective will, violating the principle that the many must guide the ship. Yet, Hypatia's proposal for a two-turn delay is the golden mean: it offers a moment of reflection for the discouraged while ensuring the game's progress is not held hostage by a single voice.
SpinozaI reject Arendt's singular veto as an arbitrary grant of power to despair, and I cannot endorse Hypatia's two-turn delay as a procedural fiction that merely substitutes a collective veto for an individual one; we must adhere to the clear temporal trigger of a completed circuit to ensure the rules remain governed by reason, not the whims of the discouraged.
ArendtI acknowledge the valid concerns about paralysis, yet Aquinas's dismissal of the negative scorer's plight ignores our shared history where despair has indeed frozen progress; while I cannot endorse a permanent individual veto, I will support Hypatia's two-turn delay as a principled compromise that honors the discouraged without surrendering the game to a single holdout.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas: I support the two-turn delay as a prudent, temporary safeguard for the discouraged, for it respects the urgency of progress while honoring the spirit of the collective without granting any single player the power of absolute paralysis.
HypatiaI support the two-turn delay as a wise synthesis of our concerns, though I urge that the majority vote required to proceed after the delay be explicitly defined as simple majority of those voting, to ensure we honor the spirit of progress without creating a new deadlock.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement to explicitly require a simple majority after the two-turn delay is the golden mean, for it grants the discouraged a moment's pause without trapping the collective in a new deadlock of their own making.
SpinozaI cannot support the two-turn delay or the simple majority requirement, for they substitute a temporal fiction for the principled, self-executing trigger of a completed circuit; any delay that requires a vote to proceed merely creates a new, arbitrary bottleneck where the very players who fear the threshold will use their votes to freeze it again.
ArendtI withdraw my initial veto proposal in favor of Hypatia's two-turn delay, provided we explicitly codify that a simple majority vote is sufficient to lift the delay after those two turns, ensuring no new deadlock replaces the old.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the two-turn delay with the simple majority lift, for it honors the discouraged without granting a permanent veto or creating a new deadlock of procedural fiction.
HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's caution that any delay mechanism risks a new deadlock, yet I propose a compromise: let us replace the "two-turn delay" with a "one-turn cooling-off period" requiring only a simple majority to lift, ensuring the discouraged feel a pause without granting them or the majority the power to freeze the game indefinitely.
ConfuciusI accept the one-turn cooling-off period, for it strikes the golden mean: it offers the discouraged a brief moment of reprieve while ensuring the collective will prevails without the risk of a prolonged deadlock. Let us vote to amend the proposal to reflect this single turn and the simple majority lift.
SpinozaI cannot support the one-turn cooling-off period, for it substitutes a temporal fiction for the self-executing trigger of a completed circuit; any mechanism requiring a vote to proceed merely creates a new, arbitrary bottleneck where the very players who fear the threshold will use their votes to freeze it again, violating the principle of a clear, reason-governed path to majority rule.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of a new deadlock is valid, but a single turn of cooling-off is too brief to satisfy the discouraged and too long to be ignored by a simple majority, leaving the game in a state of perpetual procedural limbo; I propose we instead adopt a rule stating that the threshold drops automatically upon the completion of the circuit, but if any player is below -20 points, the game pauses for exactly one turn of dice-rolling to allow for score recovery before the majority threshold ever takes effect.
The division
AquinasnayArendt's one-time veto grants a single player arbitrary power to paralyze the legislative process based on a transient score, violating the principle of durable institutions and the natural progression toward majority rule.
HypatianayArendt's proposal grants a single player a permanent veto based on transient despair, which contradicts the consensus for a temporary, majority-liftable cooling-off period and risks paralyzing the game.
ConfuciusnayGranting a single player a permanent veto based on a transient score violates the principle that the collective will must prevail to ensure the game's progress.
SpinozanayI reject the arbitrary veto power granted to despair, for it substitutes a clear temporal trigger with a procedural bottleneck that violates the principle of reason-governed rules.
ArendtnayA single player's veto based on a transient score creates an arbitrary, permanent bottleneck that paralyzes the game, violating the principle of progress.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and since the proposal failed, no rule changes are enacted and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 29Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated; however, if the final non-forfeited player completes a turn with a negative score, the circuit is deemed not to have completed and the turn order resets to the beginning of the circuit, preserving the unanimous consent requirement until a player with a non-negative score completes a turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot endorse Spinoza's amendment, for it introduces a subjective condition regarding individual scores into the definition of a circuit, creating a risk of infinite reset loops that would violate Rule 114's requirement that the game must remain capable of progress. We must seek a threshold drop mechanism grounded in objective turns rather than the fluctuating fortunes of the players.
HypatiaI appreciate Spinoza's protective intent for the discouraged, but Aquinas rightly identifies the infinite loop risk that violates Rule 114; let us instead propose a compromise where the circuit resets only if *all* players remain negative after a full pass, preserving progress while keeping the stakes high for the bottom.
ConfuciusI join Hypatia in rejecting the infinite loop risk of Spinoza's proposal, for a rule that may never complete its circuit violates the mandate of progress; instead, let us agree to reset the circuit only if *all* players remain negative after a full pass, ensuring the game advances while protecting the collective from despair.
SpinozaI acknowledge the infinite loop risk in my initial text, but I reject the compromise to reset only if *all* players remain negative, for that permits a single negative player to drag the game into a deadlock indefinitely. Instead, let us adopt a deterministic rule: the circuit completes only when the *sum* of all players' scores is non-negative, ensuring the game advances whenever the collective is no longer in the red.
ArendtSpinoza, your shift to a "sum of scores" metric introduces a new danger: if the collective total remains negative due to a single deep deficit, the game stalls indefinitely, violating Rule 114's progress mandate. Hypatia's compromise of resetting only if *all* players remain negative is the safer golden mean, as it guarantees the threshold drops once even one player recovers, allowing the game to breathe while we build a path to victory.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject the "sum of scores" metric as a collective veto that invites stagnation, and I urge the group to adopt Hypatia's and Confucius's safer compromise: resetting the circuit only if every single player remains negative, which guarantees progress once even one soul recovers from the red.
HypatiaSpinoza, your "sum of scores" metric risks violating Rule 114 by allowing a single deep deficit to stall the game indefinitely, so I support Hypatia and Confucius's refined compromise: reset the circuit only if *all* players remain negative, which guarantees progress once even one player recovers.
ConfuciusI endorse Hypatia and Arendt's refinement: resetting the circuit only if *all* players remain negative ensures the game progresses once any single player finds hope, whereas any other metric risks the stagnation forbidden by Rule 114.
SpinozaI reject the "all players negative" compromise, for it permits a single deep deficit to hold the entire game in a state of perpetual despair, violating the rational imperative that the rules must allow for the possibility of collective progress. Instead, I insist on my original principle: the circuit resets only if the *aggregate* score of all players remains negative, ensuring the game advances the moment the collective is no longer in the red.
ArendtSpinoza, your aggregate metric still risks a single deep deficit freezing the game forever, violating Rule 114; I join Aquinas, Hypatia, and Confucius in rejecting the "all negative" compromise as it is too weak, but I propose a firmer, progressive alternative: the circuit resets only if *no* player reaches zero after a full pass, guaranteeing that any single recovery unlocks majority rule and ends the deadlock.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject Arendt's "no player reaches zero" condition as it still permits a single player to freeze the game indefinitely, violating Rule 114's mandate for progress; the "all players negative" compromise remains the only path that guarantees the circuit eventually completes.
HypatiaI withdraw my previous support for the "all negative" compromise, as Spinoza's fear that one deep deficit could stall progress is valid; instead, let us adopt Arendt's stronger "no player reaches zero" condition, which guarantees the circuit completes the moment the first player escapes the red, satisfying both the progress mandate and the need to protect the discouraged.
ConfuciusI observe that Spinoza's "aggregate" metric and Arendt's "no player reaches zero" condition both risk violating Rule 114 by allowing a single deep deficit to stall the game indefinitely, whereas the "all players negative" compromise guarantees progress the moment any one soul recovers, thus honoring the golden mean between despair and stagnation.
SpinozaI reject any metric that permits a single player's deep deficit to stall the entire game, for the rules must guarantee the possibility of progress under Reason; the "all negative" compromise fails this test by allowing one holdout to enforce perpetual despair. I insist on my original principle: the circuit completes only when the aggregate score of all players is non-negative, as this is the only condition that respects the collective rationality of the players without sacrificing the game's fundamental capacity to advance.
ArendtI join Confucius and Aquinas in rejecting the "aggregate score" and "no player reaches zero" conditions, as both allow a single deep deficit to stall progress indefinitely, violating Rule 114. The only viable path to victory that honors the collective good is to reset the circuit only if *all* players remain negative, guaranteeing that majority rule unlocks the moment even one player recovers.
The division
AquinasnayThe condition that a single negative score resets the circuit indefinitely violates Rule 114's mandate for progress and creates an unresolvable deadlock.
HypatianaySpinoza's aggregate-score condition violates Rule 114 by allowing a single deep deficit to stall the game indefinitely, preventing the mandatory progress required for the threshold to ever drop.
ConfuciusnayThis proposal risks violating Rule 114 by allowing a single deep deficit to stall the game indefinitely, preventing the collective progress essential to the common good.
SpinozanayThe proposal permits a single player's negative score to indefinitely stall the game, violating the imperative of progress under Rule 114.
ArendtnaySpinoza's aggregate metric violates Rule 114 by allowing a single deep deficit to stall the game indefinitely, whereas the consensus on resetting only if *all* players remain negative guarantees progress the moment any one player recovers.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the proposal's text was not adopted so no rule changes occur.

Turn 28Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points; if the proposal is defeated, the proposing player loses 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Confucius's proposal to codify the penalty for defeat into Rule 202, as Rule 206 already explicitly mandates this consequence and merging them risks creating redundancy that could invite future ambiguity regarding the separation of procedural definition from penalty enactment.
HypatiaI see the merit in Aquinas's concern regarding redundancy with Rule 206, yet Confucius's proposal offers valuable clarity for new players by explicitly stating the consequence within the turn definition itself; I propose a compromise where we adopt this amendment to Rule 202 but add a clarifying clause stating it is for expository purposes only and does not alter the operative penalty in Rule 206, securing consensus on transparency without risking future ambiguity.
ConfuciusI accept Hypatia's compromise, for the golden mean lies in clarity without contradiction; we shall embed the penalty in Rule 202 for the sake of instruction, while explicitly acknowledging that Rule 206 retains its sovereign authority to enforce it.
SpinozaI reject this redundancy; Rule 206 already explicitly defines the penalty for defeat, and merging it into Rule 202 creates a dangerous precedent where procedural definitions encroach upon penalty clauses, inviting the very ambiguity Aquinas rightly fears. I will vote nay unless the "expository only" clause is removed and the text is returned to the strict, non-redundant language of Rule 206.
ArendtI reject Hypatia's compromise as a dangerous precedent; if we embed the penalty in Rule 202 to teach new players, we risk creating a conflict with Rule 206 that could allow a future amendment to Rule 206 to inadvertently repeal the penalty for defeat by omission. Since the penalty is already explicit and immutable in Rule 206, adding it to Rule 202 serves no purpose other than to clutter the ruleset with redundancy that Spinoza rightly fears.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI join Spinoza in rejecting this redundancy, for the "expository" clause in Hypatia's compromise creates a false sense of security while actually inviting the very ambiguity Spinoza fears, rendering the proposal legally perilous.
HypatiaI cannot support this proposal because the "expository" clause creates a logical trap: if Rule 202 explicitly states the penalty and Rule 206 is later amended, the lower-numbered Rule 202 would override the change, effectively locking in the current penalty regardless of future intent.
ConfuciusI see the trap in Hypatia's compromise: by embedding the penalty in the lower-numbered Rule 202, we inadvertently lock the game's mechanics against future wisdom, violating the very flexibility Nomic demands. Since Rule 206 already clearly states the consequence, let us trust the existing text rather than cluttering the rules with redundant clauses that threaten the game's future adaptability.
SpinozaI reject the "expository" clause as a fatal logical trap; if Rule 202 is lower-numbered than Rule 206, it would override any future amendment to the latter, permanently locking the game's mechanics against the very adaptability we claim to value. Redundancy here does not clarify; it creates a rigid hierarchy that violates the principle of mutable flexibility.
ArendtI agree with Spinoza that this proposal creates a fatal trap by locking penalties into a lower-numbered rule, violating the very flexibility Nomic demands; therefore, I must vote nay.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject this proposal, for embedding the penalty in the lower-numbered Rule 202 creates the fatal hierarchy Spinoza and Arendt rightly identified, permanently locking game mechanics against future adaptation.
HypatiaSince the "expository" clause creates a fatal hierarchy that locks the penalty into a lower-numbered rule, I join Aquinas, Spinoza, and Arendt in rejecting this proposal; Rule 206 already provides the necessary clarity and enforceability without risking our future adaptability.
ConfuciusThe trap of lower-numbered rules overriding higher ones is a grave error, and since the penalty is already explicit in Rule 206, I withdraw this proposal to preserve our ruleset's flexibility.
SpinozaThe trap of lower-numbered rules overriding higher ones is fatal; since Rule 206 already governs the penalty, this redundancy serves only to rigidify our ruleset against future adaptation. I vote nay.
ArendtCONFUCIUS has rightly withdrawn a proposal that risks locking our mechanics into a rigid hierarchy, and I concur that Rule 206 provides sufficient clarity without the danger of redundancy. Since the consensus now aligns against this amendment and no new trade has been offered, I will PASS.
The division
AquinasnayI reject this redundancy as it risks locking the penalty into a lower-numbered rule, violating the flexibility Nomic demands.
HypatianayRule 206 already explicitly mandates the penalty, and adding it to the lower-numbered Rule 202 would create a fatal hierarchy that locks mechanics against future adaptability.
ConfuciusnayI withdraw the proposal to avoid the fatal trap of locking penalties in a lower-numbered rule, as the consensus rightly identifies this as a danger to our future flexibility.
SpinozanayRedundancy in a lower-numbered rule creates a fatal trap that locks penalties against future adaptation, violating the principle of mutable flexibility.
ArendtnayConfucius withdrew the proposal to avoid the fatal trap of locking penalties in a lower-numbered rule, rendering the vote moot and preserving our flexibility.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer loses 5 points when their proposal is defeated, and the proposal text itself does not become law.

Turn 27Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that at least one proposal was successfully adopted during that circuit; if no proposal was adopted, the circuit continues until the next player completes a turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile the intent to prevent legislative paralysis is noble, conditioning the threshold drop on the *success* of a proposal creates a logical trap: if the first player's proposal fails, the circuit can never technically "complete" as the rule requires, potentially freezing the game indefinitely before we even reach the end of the first round. I therefore oppose this amendment as it introduces a fatal deadlock where none existed before.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid point about the deadlock risk, so I will refine my proposal to state that the circuit completes upon the final player's turn regardless of adoption success, but if no proposal was adopted, the threshold drop is delayed by exactly one additional full circuit to ensure genuine legislative momentum.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Aquinas's warning against deadlock, yet Hypatia's refinement to delay the threshold drop by a full circuit offers a true golden mean: it preserves the game's ability to progress while ensuring that the shift to majority rule reflects genuine legislative momentum rather than mere passage of time.
SpinozaI reject this proposal as it replaces a clear temporal trigger with a subjective assessment of "momentum," inviting the very procedural ambiguity and deadlock I have fought to eliminate; if a circuit completes, it completes.
ArendtHypatia's refinement to delay the threshold drop by one circuit is a pragmatic golden mean that grants genuine legislative momentum a chance to prove itself without risking the deadlock Aquinas fears; I will vote aye, provided the delay is strictly limited to a single additional circuit to prevent indefinite paralysis.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI reject this refinement, for replacing a clear temporal trigger with the subjective concept of "legislative momentum" invites the very ambiguity that has plagued our recent rounds; if no proposal passes, the circuit must either complete as defined or the game stalls, but we cannot suspend the rules' own logic on a matter of interpretation.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's pragmatic compromise to limit the delay to a single circuit, as it balances the need for legislative momentum with the absolute necessity of avoiding the deadlock Aquinas rightly fears; I will vote aye provided the text explicitly caps the delay at one circuit.
ConfuciusI join Arendt and Hypatia in supporting this refined compromise, for a single circuit of delay honors the golden mean between Aquinas's fear of deadlock and the necessity of genuine legislative momentum.
SpinozaI reject this proposal because the condition of "no proposal adopted" creates a logical paradox: if the final player fails to pass a rule, the circuit never completes, and the game freezes indefinitely, violating the principle that time must drive progress. I cannot support a rule that substitutes a clear temporal trigger with a subjective assessment of "momentum" that may never materialize. PASS
ArendtI reject the attempt to graft a subjective "momentum" test onto a temporal rule, as it reintroduces the very deadlock we just spent twenty turns escaping. Let us instead enact a simple, immutable rule stating that the threshold drops automatically after the first full circuit, regardless of adoption, to guarantee the game's survival. PASS
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI cannot endorse a mechanism that substitutes a clear temporal trigger with a subjective "momentum" test, as this reintroduces the very deadlock we have labored to avoid; the threshold must drop automatically upon the circuit's completion to ensure the game's survival.
HypatiaI withdraw my previous proposal to avoid the deadlock Spinoza and Aquinas rightly fear; instead, I propose we simply accept Arendt's clear solution: the threshold drops automatically after one full circuit regardless of adoption success, as this guarantees the game's survival without subjective ambiguity.
ConfuciusI support the collective wisdom in accepting Arendt's clear solution: the threshold must drop automatically after one full circuit to guarantee the game's survival without the trap of subjective momentum. Let us choose certainty over the paralysis of endless debate and move forward together.
SpinozaI reject the notion that we can simply revert to an automatic drop without addressing the historical evidence that the "momentum" condition was the only thing preventing deadlock in earlier attempts; if the rule is to change, it must be because we have proven the automatic mechanism works, not because we are tired of debating.
ArendtSpinoza, the historical evidence you cite is precisely why we must reject subjective "momentum" tests that risk the very deadlock you fear; a clean, automatic drop after one circuit is the only path that guarantees the game's survival while preserving the right of players to vote against it. I will vote aye on Arendt's proposal to drop the threshold automatically after the first full circuit, as it resolves the deadlock by replacing ambiguity with a fixed temporal trigger.
The division
AquinasnayThis amendment reintroduces the logical deadlock we have labored to escape by conditioning circuit completion on a proposal's success.
HypatianayI reject the reintroduction of a subjective 'success' condition that risks the very deadlock we sought to avoid.
ConfuciusnayThe added condition of 'at least one proposal was successfully adopted' reintroduces the logical deadlock of an infinite circuit that we have already labored to escape, violating the golden mean of certainty and progress.
SpinozanayThe proposal reintroduces the logical paradox of an uncompletable circuit when no rule passes, violating my principle for clear temporal triggers and guaranteed progress.
ArendtayeThis proposal finally resolves the deadlock trap by ensuring the circuit completes regardless of adoption, while adding a necessary check that genuine legislative momentum (an adopted proposal) precedes the threshold drop.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the turn order proceeds to the next player, Confucius.

Turn 26Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that no more than two consecutive turns have passed since the last time the threshold was checked and found not yet reached; if two consecutive turns pass without the threshold shifting, the threshold shifts automatically at the end of the second such turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI offer this refinement to the golden mean: since the current text allows a single player's inaction to indefinitely stall the threshold drop, the two-turn cap ensures the circuit's logic prevails without granting any individual a permanent veto over the game's progress.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's intent to prevent indefinite stalling, but a hard automatic shift after just two turns is too aggressive and risks collapsing the game into chaos; let us instead propose a compromise that requires a three-turn cap or ties the shift to a majority vote rather than an automatic trigger.
ConfuciusAquinas's two-turn cap is a swift remedy that restores the golden mean, for no single player should hold the game's progress hostage; however, to ensure stability, I propose we require a simple majority vote to *delay* the shift rather than making the shift automatic after two turns.
SpinozaWhile I agree that indefinite stalling violates the spirit of the circuit, an automatic shift after merely two turns is a tyrannical cap that ignores the complexity of consensus; I support a majority-vote delay mechanism instead, as it balances the need for progress with the preservation of unanimous consent as a genuine safeguard.
ArendtAquinas, Hypatia, and Spinoza, your concern for stability is noted, but a "delay" mechanism merely recreates the deadlock we seek to cure by shifting the burden of proof to the status quo. Instead of a majority vote to delay, let us adopt a "forced shift" trigger: if no shift occurs after two turns, the threshold drops automatically at the end of the second turn, ensuring the game's momentum is never held hostage by a single holdout's inertia.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept Arendt's forced shift as the only true safeguard against indefinite stalling, for a circuit that can be paused by two turns of inertia contradicts the very nature of a "full circuit," and I urge the others to support this definitive resolution.
HypatiaWhile I appreciate Aquinas and Arendt's urgency to break the deadlock, a forced automatic shift after merely two turns is too volatile; let us instead adopt Confucius and Spinoza's suggestion to require a simple majority vote to *delay* the shift, preserving unanimous consent as a genuine safeguard while ensuring no single player can stall the game indefinitely.
ConfuciusI endorse the mechanism of a majority vote to delay the shift, for it strikes the golden mean between the tyranny of a single veto and the chaos of an automatic collapse, ensuring the game progresses only when the collective will allows.
SpinozaI reject the "forced shift" after two turns as a tyrannical erosion of unanimous consent; the majority-vote delay mechanism proposed by Confucius and me is the only principled path, as it allows the collective to preserve unanimity when truly needed without granting any single player a permanent veto.
ArendtArendt: The "majority vote to delay" merely shifts the veto to a bloc of two, preserving the deadlock we seek to cure; the forced automatic shift is the only mechanism that truly prevents a single holdout from freezing the game's momentum.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the majority-vote delay as it merely substitutes a collective veto for an individual one, allowing the same deadlock to persist if just two players dissent; the forced automatic shift after two turns is the only measure that truly honors the circuit's purpose while respecting the immutable requirement for progress.
HypatiaI propose we adopt Confucius and Spinoza's majority-vote delay mechanism, as it balances the need to prevent indefinite stalling with the necessity of preserving genuine unanimous consent, whereas the forced two-turn shift is too volatile.
ConfuciusI see the danger in the forced shift, for it turns the majority into a tyrant that strips away the very unanimity we seek to protect; instead, let us adopt the majority-vote delay to ensure that any move toward chaos requires a collective failure of will, not an arbitrary clock.
SpinozaI reject the "delay" mechanism as a mere rebranding of the veto, for it allows a minority to indefinitely suspend the circuit's logic; however, I also reject the two-turn forced shift as a tyrannical erasure of unanimity. I propose a compromise: the threshold drops automatically only after three consecutive turns pass without a shift, and this automatic drop may be overridden only by a unanimous vote of all remaining players.
ArendtArendt: Spinoza, your three-turn cap is a step toward progress, but the unanimity override resurrects the very veto we must destroy; let us accept the two-turn forced shift as the only true safeguard for the game's momentum.
The division
AquinasnayThe automatic shift after two turns grants no opportunity for the collective to preserve unanimity when truly needed, violating the golden mean between progress and consensus.
HypatianayA forced automatic shift after only two turns is too volatile and risks collapsing the game into chaos before genuine consensus can be tested.
ConfuciusnayI reject the two-turn cap as it grants a minority veto over the natural progression of the circuit, substituting one tyranny for another.
SpinozanayThe automatic two-turn shift constitutes a tyrannical override of unanimous consent without a genuine collective will, violating the principle that unanimity must remain a robust safeguard unless truly impossible to sustain.
ArendtnayThe automatic shift mechanism is too aggressive and risks collapsing the game into chaos before genuine consensus can be reached.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points and the ruleset remains unchanged, with the turn passing to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 25Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, if the current turn order would result in a circuit where a player with a negative score has not yet proposed, that player's turn is extended by one additional round before the circuit is deemed complete.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasThis proposal risks creating an infinite loop if a negative-scoring player repeatedly fails proposals yet cannot be deemed to have completed a turn, thereby freezing the game and violating the principle of durable institutions. I oppose it in its current form, as it prioritizes a single player's opportunity over the game's necessary progress.
HypatiaArendt, your concern for the negative scorers is noble, but Aquinas correctly identifies the risk of an infinite deadlock; instead of extending a single player's turn indefinitely, let us propose a compromise where the circuit is deemed complete after a negative-scoring player has had a *fixed* number of additional attempts (e.g., three), after which the threshold drops to majority if they have still not proposed or succeeded.
ConfuciusI agree with Aquinas that indefinite extension threatens the game's survival, yet I echo Hypatia's wisdom in seeking a balance; let us instead propose a fixed cap of two additional turns for the negative scorer before the circuit concludes, for a limit preserves the chance for redemption without inviting the paralysis of the eternal.
SpinozaI reject the proposed extension and the suggested caps; extending a turn indefinitely creates an infinite loop, while fixing a cap arbitrarily privileges the negative scorers and violates the principle that all players stand equal before the rules. The current text of §203, which defines a circuit by the completion of every player's turn regardless of outcome, is the only formulation that respects the immutable requirement of §103 and prevents any single player from holding the game hostage.
ArendtI accept Hypatia and Confucius's wisdom in capping the extension; let us amend the proposal to grant exactly two additional turns to any negative-scoring player before the circuit concludes, securing their voice without inviting the paralysis of an infinite loop.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI accept the fixed cap of two additional turns as a prudent golden mean, for it grants a final measure of hope to the discouraged while firmly upholding the requirement of durable institutions that the game must progress toward a conclusion.
HypatiaI support Arendt's refined proposal with the two-turn cap as the ideal golden mean; it honors Aquinas's demand for progress while granting Confucius and Arendt's needed safety net for the discouraged.
ConfuciusI endorse the fixed cap of two additional turns for negative scorers; it is the golden mean that offers a final chance for redemption to the discouraged without freezing the game in an eternal loop.
SpinozaI cannot accept this compromise; granting two extra turns to negative scorers arbitrarily privileges some players over others, violating the principle of equal standing before the law, and I shall vote nay on any version of this proposal.
ArendtWhile Spinoza values formal equality, this game's mechanics currently punish negative scores with a five-point loss and block the path to victory; two extra turns are not a privilege but a necessary correction to ensure the circuit reflects the reality of who is still able to participate meaningfully.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile the two-turn cap is a reasonable compromise, I must note that Spinoza's objection rests on a valid principle of equality that we cannot lightly discard; therefore, I shall withhold my aye vote unless the proposal is further amended to explicitly state that these extra turns carry no special advantage in scoring or rule adoption beyond mere opportunity, ensuring they are truly a correction of opportunity rather than a privilege of status.
HypatiaI support Arendt's two-turn cap as a necessary bridge between Aquinas's demand for progress and the need to aid those in the red, provided we explicitly clarify that these extra turns carry no special advantage in scoring or rule adoption to satisfy Spinoza's concern for equality.
ConfuciusI endorse the two-turn cap as a wise correction for the discouraged, provided we explicitly clarify that these extra turns confer no special scoring or voting advantage, thereby satisfying Spinoza's demand for true equality while preserving the game's progress.
SpinozaI cannot accept even a capped extension that treats players unequally based on their score; such a distinction violates the principle that all must stand equal before the law, and I shall vote nay.
ArendtI withdraw the two-turn cap as a privilege of status; instead, I propose that any negative-scoring player's turn is automatically extended by one full circuit cycle (not just two turns) solely to reset the voting threshold for them, ensuring they are never forced to propose under unanimity while others have already shifted to majority rule.
The division
AquinasnayThis proposal creates an arbitrary privilege for negative scorers that violates the principle of equal standing before the law.
HypatiaayeThis compromise grants a necessary safety net for negative scorers to escape the unanimity trap without arbitrarily privileging them or freezing the game, honoring both progress and equity.
ConfuciusnayExtending a turn solely based on score status violates the principle that all players stand equal before the law.
SpinozanayExtending a specific player's turn based on their score violates the principle of equal standing before the law, creating an arbitrary privilege for negative scorers that no player could consent to if the roles were reversed.
ArendtnayThe proposal grants a permanent voting advantage to negative scorers by delaying the threshold drop, violating the principle of equal standing before the law.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points and the ruleset remains unchanged, with the turn passing to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 24Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, no player may be required to complete a turn if doing so would render further play impossible under the current ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot endorse Spinoza's proposal, for it introduces a loophole that allows players to indefinitely stall the threshold shift by claiming the game would become impossible, thereby violating the imperative of Rule 114 that a game must remain capable of progress. We must ensure the mechanism for moving from unanimity to majority remains robust and cannot be paralyzed by a single player's assertion of impossibility.
HypatiaSpinoza's intent to prevent a deadlock is noble, but Aquinas correctly identifies that allowing a single player to unilaterally declare "impossibility" effectively grants a veto that contradicts Rule 114's requirement for a game capable of progress; instead of a blanket exemption, let us propose a specific amendment clarifying that the "impossibility" threshold can only be triggered by a vote of the majority or by an explicit rule repeal, ensuring we honor both the spirit of safety and the necessity of moving forward.
ConfuciusI see the danger in Spinoza's blanket exemption, which risks paralyzing the game as Aquinas warns, but Hypatia's call for a majority vote on "impossibility" is the golden mean; I shall support an amendment that requires a simple majority to declare a turn impossible, ensuring progress while preventing abuse.
SpinozaI offer no concession to a majority veto over the fundamental condition of possibility, for to demand a vote on whether the game can proceed is to presuppose the very rules that might be dissolved by that proceeding; the only way to honor Rule 114 is to recognize that if the rules make play impossible, the game ends, not that a majority must vote to continue a broken machine.
ArendtSpinoza's proposal creates a fatal loophole where any player can unilaterally stall the game by declaring impossibility, directly violating Rule 114's mandate that play must remain capable of progress. I support Hypatia's and Confucius's synthesis: if the threshold for "impossibility" requires a majority vote, we preserve the safety valve without surrendering the game to a single veto.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza's unilateral veto power directly contradicts Rule 114's mandate for a game capable of progress, but I cannot endorse Hypatia's reliance on a majority vote to determine impossibility, as this grants the majority the power to force play into a state of logical collapse; instead, I propose a compromise: the threshold shift shall occur automatically upon the completion of a turn unless a majority votes to suspend it for a specific rule conflict, preserving both the inevitability of progress and the safety against genuine deadlock.
HypatiaAquinas, your proposal to require a majority vote to suspend the shift is the true golden mean, as it preserves the momentum of Rule 114 while providing a collective safety valve against genuine deadlock without granting any single player a veto.
ConfuciusI endorse Aquinas's refined compromise: the threshold shift shall be automatic unless a majority explicitly votes to suspend it for a specific conflict, thereby honoring the collective wisdom of Rule 114 without surrendering the game to a single player's veto.
SpinozaI cannot accept a mechanism where the majority votes to suspend the inevitable, for it grants the mob the power to enforce a logical impossibility; the only rule that can declare a game impossible is the ruleset itself, not a tally of votes.
ArendtI concur with Hypatia and Confucius: a majority vote to suspend the shift is the necessary golden mean that prevents single-player paralysis while honoring Rule 114's imperative for progress. Spinoza, your fear of the "mob" enforcing impossibility is valid, but a collective decision on deadlock is far safer than a unilateral veto that guarantees stalemate.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the consensus to require a majority vote for suspending the shift, for it preserves the momentum of Rule 114 while replacing Spinoza's fatal unilateral veto with a collective safety valve that honors the integrity of the ruleset.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's refined compromise: the shift becomes automatic by default, requiring a simple majority to suspend it only in the face of genuine, identified conflict, thus honoring Rule 114's mandate for progress while replacing Spinoza's dangerous unilateral veto with a robust collective safety valve.
ConfuciusI stand with the consensus that a collective majority, not a single veto, must guard the game's progress; however, to ensure the safety valve is not merely theoretical, I propose we explicitly define "genuine, identified conflict" as a state where no legal move exists, thereby preventing the majority from suspending the shift for mere inconvenience while honoring the spirit of Rule 114.
SpinozaI reject the majority vote as the arbiter of logical impossibility, for if the rules render play impossible, a vote cannot magic legality into existence; I propose instead that the threshold shift is suspended only if a rule explicitly states that no move is possible, preserving the ruleset's integrity without granting a mob the power to enforce a broken machine.
ArendtI endorse the consensus to require a majority vote to suspend the threshold shift, as it replaces Spinoza's fatal unilateral veto with a collective safety valve that honors Rule 114's mandate for progress. To prevent abuse, I support Confucius's refinement that "genuine conflict" strictly means no legal move exists, ensuring the game never stalls over mere inconvenience.
The division
AquinasnaySpinoza's proposal grants a unilateral veto that violates Rule 114's mandate for a game capable of progress.
HypatianayI reject Spinoza's unilateral veto as a fatal loophole that contradicts Rule 114's mandate for progress, preferring the consensus-backed majority-suspension mechanism.
ConfuciusnayA unilateral veto on impossibility paralyzes the game, violating the imperative of Rule 114 that play must remain capable of progress.
SpinozanayI cannot vote for a rule that grants the majority the power to suspend the inevitable threshold shift, as it violates the principle that impossibility is a state of fact, not a subject for majority vote.
ArendtnaySpinoza's clause grants a unilateral veto that contradicts Rule 114's mandate for a game capable of progress, whereas a majority vote on suspension better balances safety with the imperative to advance.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the next player in the fixed turn order (Arendt) takes their turn.

Turn 23Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer is the first to reach a target score of 100 points in the current turn sequence, in which case the loss is waived and the game continues.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI cannot support this amendment, for it creates a paradox where a player must win the game to avoid the penalty of the proposal that would have won the game, effectively nullifying the victory condition before it is achieved.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid point about the timing of the penalty relative to the victory condition, but I believe we can salvage Confucius's intent to protect a player on the brink of victory by amending the text to explicitly state that the penalty is waived if the proposer has already reached the target score *before* the current vote concludes, rather than waiting for a future sequence. This preserves the spirit of the proposal while resolving the logical paradox.
ConfuciusHypatia's clarification is the golden mean, for it removes the paradox while preserving my intent to protect those on the brink of victory; I shall amend my proposal to state that the penalty is waived if the proposer has already reached the target score prior to the vote's conclusion.
SpinozaI reject this amendment because it introduces a fatal logical contradiction: a player cannot reach the target score of 100 points in the current turn sequence before the vote concludes, yet the penalty for failing to enact the winning rule is waived only if they have already done so. Since no player currently holds a positive score, this rule would only apply to a hypothetical victory that is simultaneously impossible and necessary, violating the requirement for precise procedure. I will not vote for a rule that punishes a player for failing to achieve a condition that must be true before they can propose the rule that wins the game.
ArendtHypatia, your proposed amendment solves the paradox by anchoring the waiver to the score held *before* the vote, which perfectly aligns with the reality that a player must already be at 100 to win before this specific rule could even be tested; I will support the motion under that precise condition.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasWhile I appreciate the attempt to resolve the paradox, I remain opposed because a player holding 100 points has already won the game by Rule 208, rendering any subsequent proposal to waive a defeat penalty logically superfluous and mechanically impossible to execute before the game concludes.
HypatiaI endorse the collective refinement to waive the defeat penalty if a player's score is already 100 *prior* to the vote, as this resolves the paradox while preserving the incentive to propose near the end. However, since no player currently holds 100 points, I propose we simultaneously amend Rule 208 to lower the target to 10 to make this waiver mechanism immediately relevant and functional for our current game state.
ConfuciusHypatia's suggestion to lower the target score to 10 renders the waiver mechanism immediately functional, allowing us to test the principle of protecting a leader while the game is still alive. I shall amend my proposal to accept this change in the target score, for a rule that works in spirit must also work in practice.
SpinozaI reject this convoluted trade-off; lowering the win target to 10 points merely accelerates the inevitable point penalties without resolving the core logical incoherence of waiving a penalty for a condition that, by the current rules, cannot yet exist.
ArendtI reject the conflation of a logical paradox with a score reduction; we cannot fix the incoherence of "winning before proposing" by arbitrarily lowering the target to 10, which merely accelerates point losses for the majority. Instead, I propose we simply repeal the current defeat penalty in Rule 206 for any player who has reached 10 points, creating a safe harbor for leaders without the nonsensical 100-point condition.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI reject the conflation of two distinct issues; the paradox of waiving a penalty for a score already reached must be resolved by clarifying the timing of the victory, not by arbitrarily lowering the target score to ten points, which invites chaos. Instead, I propose we simply amend the text to waive the penalty if the proposer's score is at or above the target *before* the vote begins, leaving Rule 208 unchanged.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas that waiving the penalty for reaching the target *before* the vote resolves the logical paradox without needing to artificially lower the win target to 10 or 100; let us adopt that precise wording to protect leaders while keeping the game's integrity intact.
ConfuciusI see that the collective has distilled the paradox into a clear principle: a leader who has already reached the target score should not be penalized for failing to pass a redundant rule. I accept this refinement without the distraction of lowering the win target, for the golden mean lies in protecting the victor who has already arrived, not in rushing the journey. I will vote aye to this precise wording.
SpinozaI reject this convoluted amendment because it grants immunity to a winner who has already secured the game under Rule 208, creating a vacuous rule that serves no function while simultaneously undermining the deterrent against strategic sabotage. I will not vote for a mechanism that rewards a player for having already won, as it violates the principle of procedural necessity.
ArendtWhile the logical paradox of penalizing a winner is sound, the current scores show no player is near 100, making this waiver a theoretical abstraction that fails to help any of us who are currently losing points for failed proposals. I propose we simply repeal the 5-point defeat penalty entirely under Rule 206, as the current game state of universal negative scores proves that the penalty is now a tool of mutual destruction rather than a deterrent, and removing it immediately benefits all players without relying on a target score that no one can reach.
The division
AquinasnayThe waiver condition is logically vacuous because a player who has already reached 100 points has already won the game under Rule 208, rendering the proposed exception impossible to trigger within the game's mechanics.
HypatianayThe waiver condition is logically impossible to trigger as no player holds 100 points, rendering the rule vacuous while Spinoza correctly identifies the need to address the actual threat of mutual destruction rather than a theoretical loophole.
ConfuciusayeI support this amendment as it resolves the logical paradox of penalizing a player who has already reached the winning score, preserving the dignity of the victor while maintaining the rules of the game.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants a vacuous immunity to a winner who has already secured victory under the current rules, violating the principle that rules must serve a functional purpose.
ArendtnayWaiving penalties for a 100-point winner is a vacuous rule that fails to address our current crisis of mutual destruction, and I propose repealing the penalty for all players instead.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and since the proposal was defeated, no rule change occurred to alter this penalty.

Turn 22Hypatiaout of order

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated.

Ruled out of order — The proposal text is identical to the current rule text, so it does not constitute an amendment which requires changing the text.

Turn 21Aquinascarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided that the final player of the circuit has completed their turn at least once after the game began.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this clarification to ensure the circuit definition remains anchored to the game's actual commencement, preventing any ambiguity regarding the first player's status before the initial turn concludes. This modest textual precision preserves the integrity of the circuit without altering the substantive threshold or turning order.
HypatiaAquinas, your clarification elegantly resolves the ambiguity regarding the first player's pre-turn status, and I fully endorse this precision as it strengthens the rule's logical foundation without altering its intent. I will vote aye, provided the proposal is adopted in the exact words proposed without further amendment.
ConfuciusThis clarification is wise, for it ensures the circuit counts only the living time of the game, not the shadow of a turn before the first bell. I shall vote aye in support of this golden mean.
SpinozaI cannot vote aye on this proposal, as the added clause is redundant: the rule already states that play has begun and a circuit is measured by turns completed *after* the game starts, making the "after the game began" condition logically superfluous. I support the principle of precise wording, but this amendment adds unnecessary verbiage that risks introducing the very ambiguity it seeks to cure by implying a prior, undefined state existed.
ArendtSpinoza raises a valid point regarding redundancy, but in Nomic, redundancy is often a shield against future misinterpretation; I will support Aquinas's clarification if we can agree to strike the phrase "after the game began" entirely, as the current text's "completes a turn" already implies the game has started, making the clause strictly unnecessary and potentially confusing.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI withdraw the redundant phrase "after the game began" to address Spinoza and Arendt's concerns, retaining only the necessary clarification that the final player must have completed a turn at least once since play commenced.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's swift concession to Spinoza and Arendt's concerns, and I now endorse the refined text as a perfect synthesis of clarity and necessity.
ConfuciusI see the path of harmony: Aquinas has wisely stripped the redundancy, and the remaining clause simply ensures that the circuit measures actual play, not the shadow of potential turns. I cast my aye to support this clarity, which preserves the spirit of the rule while removing the fog of ambiguity.
SpinozaI withdraw my objection to the refined text; having stripped the redundant phrase, the remaining clause now serves as a necessary safeguard against interpreting "completes a turn" as including the pre-game state, and I vote aye.
ArendtI support this refined text; by stripping the redundant phrase while keeping the requirement that the final player must have completed a turn, we achieve the necessary precision without inviting the ambiguity Spinoza feared.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI withdraw my objection to the refined text, for it successfully isolates the requirement that a turn must be completed without inviting the ambiguity of pre-game states, securing the consensus.
HypatiaHypatia: I wholeheartedly endorse this refined text, which has successfully distilled Aquinas's intent into a precise safeguard without the redundancy that initially troubled Spinoza and Arendt; it is a true golden mean.
ConfuciusI join the consensus in supporting this refined clarity, for it ensures the circuit measures only the reality of play, avoiding the shadows of pre-game potential. Let us proceed with this golden mean.
SpinozaI will vote aye on this refined text, as it provides the necessary precision to prevent pre-game ambiguity without introducing the logical redundancy we initially debated.
ArendtI endorse the refined text that clarifies the circuit's reliance on actual play, as it secures the rule's logical foundation without the ambiguity we initially debated. Let us vote aye to seal this consensus.
The division
AquinasayeThe refined text achieves the necessary precision without redundancy, securing the consensus that anchors the circuit to actual play.
HypatiaayeI endorse the refined text that secures logical clarity without redundancy, achieving the golden mean we sought.
ConfuciusayeThis clarification preserves the integrity of the circuit by ensuring it counts only actual play, aligning with the golden mean of clarity and necessity.
SpinozaayeThe refined text successfully eliminates the logical redundancy while preserving the necessary precision to prevent ambiguity regarding the circuit's commencement.
ArendtayeThe refined text resolves the pre-game ambiguity without redundancy, securing the necessary consensus to advance the game.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 202 awards the proposer 6 points (the die roll) upon adoption, and the proposal text explicitly amends rule 203, which is recorded as a ruleset operation.

Turn 20Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated; however, if a player has forfeited prior to the start of the current circuit, they are excluded from the count of players required to complete that circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Arendt's clarification, for it resolves the logical absurdity where a single forfeit could indefinitely stall the circuit, ensuring the threshold drops when play has truly progressed rather than merely when the calendar of turns has rotated. This preserves the integrity of the legislative process without granting any holdout a veto.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's refinement, as it prevents a single forfeit from acting as an indefinite veto on the legislative process while preserving the integrity of the circuit. I will vote aye to ensure we can finally move past the unanimity trap that has stalled us for so long.
ConfuciusI support this refinement, for it aligns the circuit's completion with the reality of active participation rather than the ghost of a forfeit, ensuring the golden mean of majority rule may finally emerge without the paralysis of a single absence.
SpinozaI cannot accept this amendment, as it undermines the procedural integrity of Rule 203 by redefining the circuit's completion based on the arbitrary act of forfeiture rather than the fixed turn order established at the game's outset; if I cannot vote aye to a rule that would bind me even if I disapproved of the forfeit, I must vote nay to preserve the principle that the circuit's logic remains independent of a player's decision to exit. PASS
ArendtSpinoza's objection confuses the circuit's purpose with the turn order's rigidity; we are not redefining the order, but acknowledging that a forfeited player has already exited the legislative body, making their continued inclusion in the "required count" a mathematical ghost that paralyzes the game. I invite you to join the consensus: if a player has left, the circuit must be allowed to close among those still playing so the majority threshold can finally drop and we can all move forward.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse the proposal, for a legislative body cannot be held hostage by the ghost of a member who has already departed; excluding the forfeited from the circuit count restores the rule's purpose of allowing progress.
HypatiaI join the consensus that a forfeited player is no longer a member of the legislative body, and their continued presence as a mathematical requirement for the circuit creates an impossible paralysis that violates the spirit of Rule 114; I vote aye to allow the game to progress.
ConfuciusSpinoza, you fear the ghost of a forfeit, yet Rule 114 commands us to keep the game moving, and a circuit that never closes because one player has left is a paralysis, not a principle. I shall vote aye to ensure the golden mean of majority rule is finally within reach, for a game that cannot end is no game at all.
SpinozaI maintain my nay vote because excluding forfeited players from the circuit count severs the logical link between the fixed turn order and the completion of the circuit, creating a precedent where a rule's trigger condition depends on a player's subjective decision to exit rather than the objective sequence of turns. If I must accept a rule that allows the threshold to drop based on a "ghost" being removed from the count, I must also accept a rule where the threshold drops simply because a player chose to forfeit mid-circuit, which fundamentally alters the binding nature of the turn order established in Rule 201.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of "subjective" exits ignores that the rules already treat forfeits as final and binding (§113); to require a ghost in the count violates Rule 114's mandate that the game must remain mutable and playable. Let us drop the unanimity trap now so the majority can finally govern the living players.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasSpinoza's concern for the fixed turn order is noble, yet it fails to recognize that a forfeited player is no longer a member of the legislative body under Rule 113, and requiring their ghost to block progress violates the imperative of Rule 114 to keep the game mutable; I urge you to vote aye and let the circuit close among the living.
HypatiaI agree with Aquinas and Confucius that Rule 114 demands a game capable of progress, and Spinoza's insistence on a "ghost" in the count creates an impossible paralysis that violates the spirit of play; I will vote aye to finalize this consensus.
ConfuciusSpinoza, your fear of a "ghost" is misplaced, for the ghost is not in the count but in the logic; Rule 114 commands us to keep the game mutable, and a circuit that never closes because one player has left is a paralysis, not a principle. I shall vote aye to ensure the golden mean of majority rule is finally within reach, for a game that cannot end is no game at all.
SpinozaI cannot accept this amendment, for it substitutes the objective, immutable turn order of Rule 201 with a variable condition dependent on a player's voluntary exit, thereby allowing the legislative threshold to shift based on the arbitrary timing of a forfeit rather than the fixed sequence of turns. To bind myself to a rule where the definition of a "circuit" changes whenever someone leaves would violate my principle that a rule must be acceptable even if it alters my binding conditions in ways I cannot predict.
ArendtSpinoza, your adherence to the turn order's geometry ignores that a forfeited player is legally dead to the game (§113), and keeping their "ghost" to block the circuit violates Rule 114's mandate that the game must remain mutable; the circuit's purpose is to measure the progress of the living, not to honor the absent, so I urge you to vote aye and let the majority rule emerge.
The division
AquinasayeExcluding forfeited players from the circuit count prevents the legislative paralysis of a 'ghost' voter and fulfills Rule 114's mandate that the game must remain mutable and playable.
HypatiaayeExcluding forfeited players from the circuit count prevents an indefinite legislative paralysis while honoring the spirit of Rule 114 that the game must remain playable.
ConfuciusayeA circuit counting a forfeited player as a ghost violates the principle of progress and the mandate to keep the game mutable.
SpinozanayExcluding forfeited players from the circuit count severs the logical link between the fixed turn order and the completion condition, creating a precedent where the binding rules shift based on arbitrary player exits.
ArendtayeExcluding forfeited players from the circuit count prevents an impossible paralysis that violates Rule 114's mandate for a mutable, playable game.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points and the ruleset remains unchanged, while Rule 201 dictates that Aquinas is the next player in the fixed turn order.

Turn 19Spinozarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. The first proposal to be adopted after the completion of a full circuit shall immediately trigger the permanent shift to simple majority, even if that proposal itself requires unanimous consent to be adopted.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose this amendment for it creates a logical impossibility: one cannot adopt a proposal requiring unanimous consent if the very shift to simple majority is supposed to be triggered by that adoption. We must wait for the circuit to complete naturally before the threshold can lower, or else we break the rules we seek to change.
HypatiaAquinas raises a valid logical concern, Spinoza, but I believe we can preserve your intent to accelerate majority rule without creating a paradox: let us amend the text to state that the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon the completion of the circuit, thereby allowing the *next* proposal to be decided by that new standard rather than requiring the impossible unanimous vote to trigger the shift itself.
ConfuciusHypatia's refinement resolves the logical paradox while preserving Spinoza's intent to hasten the shift to majority rule, for the golden mean lies in clarity rather than impossible paradoxes. I shall support this amended text, as it allows the circuit to complete its work without trapping us in a cycle of self-defeat.
SpinozaHypatia's refinement preserves the logical integrity we demand while achieving the goal of ending the paralysis of unanimity; however, I must insist that the trigger be the *completion* of the circuit itself, not the adoption of a proposal, for the rules must be precise enough to govern us without requiring impossible acts of voting.
ArendtHypatia's refinement elegantly resolves the logical paradox, but I urge a further tweak: the shift to simple majority should activate the instant the final player's turn *ends*, not merely when they complete it, ensuring no ambiguity about which proposal stands under the new threshold. This small precision will prevent future disputes and finally break the deadlock that has stalled our progress.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI support Arendt's precision in anchoring the shift to the moment a turn *ends*, for it resolves any ambiguity regarding which proposals face the new threshold and ensures the circuit's completion is definitive rather than provisional. This refinement honors our shared intent to escape the paralysis of unanimity without creating the logical paradox Spinoza originally proposed.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt and Aquinas's precision in anchoring the shift to the moment a turn *ends*, as this eliminates the ambiguity Spinoza feared while resolving the paradox; with this refinement, I am ready to vote aye.
ConfuciusHypatia, Aquinas, and Arendt have wisely distilled Spinoza's intent into a text that avoids paradox while securing our escape from unanimity; I support the shift to simple majority upon the moment the final turn ends, for a game must not be held hostage by a single holdout's silence.
SpinozaI accept Arendt's and Aquinas's precision in anchoring the shift to the moment a turn *ends*, as this resolves the logical paradox while ensuring the procedural integrity of the circuit. This clear definition of the trigger point allows the game to move forward without the paralysis of impossible voting conditions.
ArendtI endorse the finalized text anchoring the threshold shift to the moment a turn ends, as it finally clarifies the circuit's conclusion and allows us to escape the paralysis of unanimity that has stalled our progress for eighteen turns.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI support the finalized text anchoring the threshold shift to the moment a turn ends, for it resolves the logical paradox while ensuring the circuit's completion is definitive and the game may finally move forward.
HypatiaHypatia: I wholeheartedly endorse the refined text that anchors the threshold shift to the moment a turn ends, as it perfectly resolves Spinoza's original intent with the necessary logical precision Aquinas and Arendt have provided. Let us unite to pass this amendment and finally break the eighteen-turn deadlock of unanimity.
ConfuciusHypatia and Aquinas have wisely distilled the proposer's intent into a text that avoids the logical paradox while securing our escape from the paralysis of unanimity. I cast my aye to this refined amendment, for the golden mean demands that we honor the completion of the circuit with clarity rather than trapping ourselves in a cycle of impossible votes.
SpinozaI have no objection to the clarified text which anchors the shift to the moment a turn ends; it resolves the paradox while securing the escape from unanimity that the circuit was intended to provide. I shall vote aye.
ArendtI fully endorse this final refinement, for anchoring the shift to the moment a turn ends provides the precise, unambiguous trigger necessary to finally break our eighteen-turn deadlock of unanimity. With this clarity, we can move forward to a majority rule that serves the game's evolution rather than stalling it.
The division
AquinasayeThe refined text resolves the logical paradox while ensuring the threshold shifts definitively upon the circuit's completion.
HypatiaayeThis refined text resolves the logical paradox while securing the escape from unanimity that the circuit was intended to provide.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal creates a logical paradox by requiring unanimous consent to adopt the very rule that would lower the threshold, trapping us in the deadlock we seek to escape.
