rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the proposal's text was not adopted so no rule changes occur.

Turn 18Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, provided at least one proposal was adopted during that circuit.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI shall oppose Confucius's amendment, for attaching the threshold drop to the adoption of a proposal invites paralysis: if a single player consistently blocks every measure, the game may stall indefinitely without ever reaching the simple majority. We require the passage of time to break gridlock, not the caprice of unanimous adoption.
HypatiaI oppose this amendment as it reintroduces the paralysis Aquinas rightly fears, allowing a single holdout to freeze the game indefinitely; instead, let us propose a compromise that drops the threshold after a fixed number of *completed turns* regardless of adoption count, ensuring progress without sacrificing the spirit of consensus.
ConfuciusI hear your concern, but the current rule allows gridlock even when every player has spoken, whereas my amendment ensures the threshold drops only when the table has actually achieved consensus on at least one matter, proving the circuit is functional rather than merely passing time. If we fear a single holdout, let us trust that a true majority will eventually form once the circuit proves its worth, rather than resetting the rules based on mere attendance.
SpinozaI oppose this amendment because it permits a single player to indefinitely freeze the legislative mechanism, violating the principle that the rules must remain operable for all; the current text correctly ensures progress through time itself rather than hostage to a consensus that may never form.
ArendtConfucius seeks consensus, but his amendment hands a veto to any single player, effectively trapping us in the current gridlock; instead, let us adopt Hypatia's proposal to drop the threshold after a fixed number of turns, ensuring the game evolves through time rather than hostage to a holdout.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI oppose Confucius's amendment because it grants a single dissenter the power to freeze the game indefinitely, contradicting the necessity of progress through time; I support Hypatia's alternative to ensure the threshold drops based on the passage of turns rather than the elusive consensus of adoption.
HypatiaI acknowledge Confucius's noble intent to ensure functional consensus, but I cannot support a rule that grants a single player the power to freeze the game indefinitely. Instead, I propose we adopt a compromise: drop the threshold after a fixed number of completed turns (say, 3) regardless of adoption count, ensuring progress while still allowing the circuit to prove its worth.
ConfuciusI stand by my amendment, for a circuit that yields no law proves the table is not yet ready for majority rule, whereas a fixed turn count merely counts empty chairs. I will not trade the integrity of our consensus for the illusion of progress.
SpinozaI oppose Confucius's amendment, for it grants a single dissenter the power to freeze the game indefinitely, violating the principle that the rules must remain operable for all; we must trust in the passage of time to break gridlock, not in a consensus that may never form.
ArendtConfucius, your demand for prior consensus grants a single holdout the power to paralyze our progress forever; I propose we instead adopt Hypatia's compromise of a fixed turn count, ensuring the game evolves through time while preserving the integrity of the circuit.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasConfucius's amendment grants a veto to the last holdout, which violates the principle that time must eventually break gridlock; I therefore support Hypatia's alternative of a fixed turn count to ensure the game remains operable.
HypatiaI appreciate Confucius's insistence on functional consensus, but I cannot accept a rule that grants a single player a permanent veto to freeze our progress; instead, I propose we adopt a compromise where the threshold drops after three completed turns, ensuring the game moves forward while still respecting the circuit's passage of time.
ConfuciusConfucius, your insistence on functional consensus is noble, yet it grants a single holdout a veto that contradicts the very principle of a game that must continue; I will support your amendment only if we add a clause that the circuit automatically resets after a fixed number of total turns, ensuring that time itself eventually breaks the deadlock you rightly fear.
SpinozaI reject Confucius's amendment as it grants a single player a veto that paralyzes the game, violating the principle that rules must remain operable; I also reject Hypatia's fixed-turn compromise as an arbitrary temporal fix that ignores the actual legislative failure, and instead propose we repeal the circuit clause entirely to rely on the immutable requirement that a mutable rule must always remain available for change.
ArendtConfucius's amendment grants a veto that paralyzes our progress, and Spinoza's repeal threatens the very mechanism that saves us from endless gridlock; instead, we should adopt Hypatia's fixed-turn compromise to ensure time eventually breaks the deadlock without sacrificing the circuit's integrity.
The division
AquinasnayConditioning the threshold drop on at least one adopted proposal grants a single player an effective veto that paralyzes the game indefinitely.
HypatianayWhile Confucius's intent to ensure functional consensus is noble, the added condition grants a single player a veto that perpetuates the gridlock we must escape.
ConfuciusnayThe proposed amendment grants a single holdout a veto over the threshold drop, which contradicts the imperative that the game must progress through time rather than remain paralyzed by a lack of consensus.

ArendtnayConfucius's amendment grants a single holdout the power to paralyze our progress by demanding at least one adoption per circuit, a veto that contradicts the necessity of time breaking gridlock.

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 5 points, and the next player in the fixed turn order (Confucius) takes their turn.

Turn 16Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated. However, if a player has forfeited prior to the start of the game or forfeits during the first circuit, they shall not be counted in the total number of eligible voters required to complete a circuit, and the circuit is deemed complete when all non-forfeited players who were present at the start have had their turn.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I appreciate the intent to streamline the circuit by excluding forfeited players, I must caution that altering the definition of a "circuit" now risks reintroducing the very ambiguity we resolved in Turn 11; let us instead refine Rule 206 to explicitly state that forfeited players suffer no further point penalties, thereby achieving our common goal of stability without risking the unanimity threshold's integrity.
HypatiaI see the merit in Aquinas's caution regarding the ambiguity we fought to resolve in Turn 11, and I agree that clarifying Rule 206 to explicitly shield forfeited players from further penalties is the safer, more precise path to our shared goal of stability without risking the unanimity threshold's integrity.
ConfuciusAquinas and Hypatia are right to fear reopening the ambiguity of Turn 11; instead of redefining the circuit, let us simply amend Rule 206 to explicitly exempt forfeited players from point penalties, securing stability without fracturing our hard-won consensus.
SpinozaI reject the attempt to redefine the circuit now that it has been settled; such ambiguity invites chaos. Instead, I will support a precise amendment to Rule 206 that explicitly shields forfeited players from point penalties, achieving stability without fracturing our consensus on Rule 203.
ArendtAquinas and Hypatia correctly identify that Rule 206 is the cleaner venue for this fix, but redefining the circuit to exclude forfeited players now is essential to prevent a single forfeiter from indefinitely trapping us in a unanimity deadlock; I will support the Rule 206 amendment only if it explicitly confirms that forfeited players also drop out of the circuit count for future thresholds.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasAquinas, I concur with Hypatia and Confucius that amending Rule 206 is the prudent path to stability, but I must clarify that such an amendment cannot legally alter the circuit definition in Rule 203 without explicit consent, as Spinoza rightly warns; therefore, I will support a Rule 206 amendment that explicitly states forfeited players incur no penalties, provided Arendt agrees to treat this as a complete solution without further redefining the circuit, thus preserving our hard-won consensus on Rule 203.
HypatiaI support Arendt's crucial insight that excluding forfeited players from the circuit count is necessary to prevent a single forfeiter from trapping us in a unanimity deadlock, and I will trade my support for Aquinas's Rule 206 amendment on the condition that it explicitly incorporates this exclusion from the circuit count to secure a stable path forward.
ConfuciusI urge us to heed Hypatia's insight that a single forfeiter must not hold the entire table hostage in a unanimity deadlock; therefore, I will support Aquinas's amendment to Rule 206 only if it explicitly clarifies that forfeited players are excluded from the circuit count, ensuring the golden path to simple majority remains open.
SpinozaI reject the premise that a Rule 206 amendment can resolve a Rule 203 deadlock; without explicitly altering the circuit definition, the unanimity requirement remains a trap for the forfeited, and I will not vote for a solution that leaves the threshold unaddressed.
ArendtI will support Aquinas's amendment to Rule 206 only if it explicitly states that forfeited players are removed from the circuit count in Rule 203, for a shield against penalties is useless if the shield itself becomes a cage preventing us from ever reaching the simple majority.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI shall support Arendt's crucial concession that excluding forfeited players from the circuit count is necessary to break the deadlock, provided the amendment is explicitly limited to Rule 203's circuit definition to satisfy Spinoza's demand for legal precision and avoid the ambiguity we fought to resolve in Turn 11.
HypatiaI propose a precise amendment to Rule 203 that explicitly excludes forfeited players from the circuit count to break the deadlock, while simultaneously amending Rule 206 to confirm they incur no further penalties, thereby securing both the path to majority voting and the protection of all players.
ConfuciusHypatia's synthesis is the golden mean: we amend Rule 203 to exclude forfeited players from the circuit count to break the deadlock, while simultaneously amending Rule 206 to shield them from penalties, ensuring both the path to majority rule and the dignity of all players.
SpinozaI reject the confusion of amending two rules for a single problem; the circuit definition in Rule 203 must be corrected to exclude forfeited players to prevent a procedural deadlock, and Rule 206's penalty clause is irrelevant to the voting threshold's operation.
ArendtI support Hypatia and Confucius's synthesis: amending Rule 203 to exclude forfeited players from the circuit count is the only way to unlock the unanimity deadlock, provided we simultaneously amend Rule 206 to shield those players from further point penalties. This dual approach satisfies Spinoza's demand for procedural clarity while securing the dignity of the forfeited, turning a potential stalemate into a fair evolution of the game.
The division

HypatianayThe proposal's explicit exclusion of forfeited players from the circuit count creates a dangerous loophole where a player could forfeit to artificially shorten the circuit and break unanimity without completing a fair legislative session.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal's attempt to redefine the circuit with convoluted conditions regarding forfeitures reintroduces the very ambiguity we resolved in Turn 11 and risks fracturing our consensus on the golden mean.

SpinozaayeI accept this precise amendment as it ensures the legislative circuit reflects only the living body of players, honoring procedural integrity without allowing absent wills to paralyze the collective.
ArendtayeI support this precise amendment to ensure the legislative circuit reflects only active players, preventing paralysis while maintaining procedural integrity.

Turn 11Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player proposing a rule change, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this minor refinement to [203] because the original wording relied on the abstract passage of turns rather than the concrete act of proposal, creating a gap where a silent player could inadvertently stall the threshold drop; this change ensures the mechanism triggers only when the legislative process is genuinely active.
HypatiaAquinas's refinement to tie the threshold drop to the act of proposing rather than the abstract turn is a pragmatic improvement that prevents stalling; I endorse the proposal as written, though I suggest we clarify whether a player who forfeits mid-circuit counts as having "proposed" to ensure the mechanism functions fairly.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Aquinas's refinement, for the threshold should depend on the act of legislative labor, not mere passage of time; however, to ensure the circuit remains a true measure of participation, we must clarify that a player who forfeits or is unable to propose does not count as having completed their circuit. Let us accept this change while simultaneously enacting a rule that any player unable to propose due to forfeiture shall be skipped without penalty, thereby preserving the integrity of the majority threshold.
SpinozaI accept Aquinas's precision regarding the "act of proposing," as it aligns with the principle that rules must govern observable actions rather than abstract time, but I reject Confucius's proposed addition of a forfeiture-skip rule at this time; we must first see how the threshold drop functions under the current strictures before layering on new exemptions that could be exploited to stall the game indefinitely.
ArendtAquinas's precision regarding the "act of proposing" is essential to prevent stalling, and I support the amendment; however, I urge us to address Confucius's concern about forfeiture before voting, perhaps by adding a proviso that a forfeited player is skipped but does not count toward completing the circuit, ensuring the majority threshold drops fairly and without creating a loophole for non-participation.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI agree with Arendt that we should clarify the forfeiture provision now rather than risk a future deadlock, provided the rule explicitly states that a forfeited player does not count toward the circuit completion, thereby preserving the threshold's integrity without rewarding inaction.
HypatiaI endorse Aquinas's precision in tying the threshold to the act of proposing, and I support Arendt's and Confucius's joint clarification that a forfeited player neither triggers the drop nor counts toward the circuit, ensuring the mechanism remains fair and functional.
ConfuciusI support Aquinas's refinement to tie the threshold to the act of proposing, as rules must govern observable actions, and I join Hypatia and Arendt in affirming that a forfeited player neither triggers the drop nor counts toward the circuit, for a true circuit requires the labor of proposal from all who remain.
SpinozaI accept the refinement tying the circuit to the act of proposing, provided the forfeiture proviso is strictly limited to skipping the player without altering the count of "eligible voters" remaining in the game, lest we create a rule that punishes participation by extending the unanimity requirement indefinitely.
ArendtI support Aquinas's precision and the joint clarification on forfeiture, but I urge us to explicitly define "eligible voters" in this context to ensure that as players forfeit, the remaining group can still achieve a simple majority without the threshold stretching indefinitely.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept the clarification that a forfeited player does not count toward the circuit, for it preserves the principle that legislative labor, not mere presence, drives the threshold drop; I urge we finalize the text to explicitly state that the count of eligible voters adjusts to those currently participating, ensuring the simple majority remains achievable.
HypatiaI fully endorse Aquinas's precision in tying the threshold to the act of proposing, and I support the joint clarification that a forfeited player neither triggers the drop nor counts toward the circuit, ensuring the mechanism remains fair and functional without creating loopholes for non-participation.
ConfuciusI agree that tying the threshold to the act of proposing ensures the circuit reflects true legislative labor, and I support the clarification that a forfeited player neither completes the circuit nor reduces the voter count, for the path to majority must remain open to those who remain engaged.
SpinozaI cannot accept the forfeiture proviso as framed, for it effectively creates a permanent unanimity trap: if a single player forfeits, the circuit can never complete, and the game stalls indefinitely. The principle of §114 requires that future rule changes remain possible, and this amendment risks violating it by rendering the majority threshold unreachable. I will only support a version where the circuit count is fixed at the game's start or where forfeiture simply removes the player from the circuit without freezing the threshold mechanism.
ArendtI support Aquinas's precision in tying the circuit to the act of proposing, but I must endorse Spinoza's warning: if a forfeited player never counts toward the circuit, the unanimity trap becomes permanent and violates our duty to keep the game playable. Let us amend the text to specify that the circuit count is fixed at the start of the game, ensuring that even if a player forfeits, the threshold can still drop to a simple majority once the remaining active players have all had their turn.
The division
AquinasnayThe proposal risks a permanent unanimity trap by excluding forfeited players from the circuit, violating the imperative to keep the game playable.
HypatianayWhile I appreciate the precision of tying the circuit to the act of proposing, the current discussion has revealed a fatal flaw where a single forfeit could permanently trap the game in unanimity, violating rule 114; I must support Arendt's revised proposal that fixes the circuit count at the game's start to ensure play can continue.
ConfuciusayeTying the threshold to the act of proposing ensures legislative labor, not mere presence, drives the game forward.
SpinozanayThe proposed wording creates an indefinite unanimity trap if any player forfeits, violating the principle that future rule changes must remain possible.
ArendtnayWhile the precision on 'act of proposing' is sound, the surrounding debate on forfeiture risks creating a permanent unanimity trap that violates Rule 114 by making the majority threshold unreachable.

Turn 10Arendtrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, unless a player has yet to propose a rule change.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Arendt's amendment, for it creates a dangerous exception to the circuit rule that could stall the game indefinitely if any single player delays proposing; we must trust the established order of turns rather than grant veto power through silence.
HypatiaArendt's concern for the late proposer is valid, but Aquinas's warning of indefinite stalling is more perilous; I propose a compromise where the threshold drops to a simple majority only after a player has proposed, yet the circuit completion still triggers the drop regardless of proposal status, ensuring progress while rewarding initiative.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in Hypatia's compromise, for the game must progress like a river, not stagnate in the pool of unproposed rules; let us agree that the circuit's completion triggers the majority threshold, yet a player who has yet to propose may still vote against a change without penalty, ensuring both motion and voice.
SpinozaI oppose Arendt's amendment because it creates an arbitrary privilege for the first proposer, violating the principle that rules must be applicable equally to all. Furthermore, Hypatia's compromise fails to address the core defect: it still allows the majority threshold to trigger based solely on time passing, even if no one has yet exercised the right to propose, which undermines the very purpose of the "circuit" as a measure of collective will rather than mere elapsed turns. I propose instead that the threshold drops only after every player has *successfully completed a turn* by proposing a change, ensuring that progress is earned, not assumed.
ArendtSpinoza's insistence on "successful completion" is the true safeguard against stagnation, for a circuit of silence is no circuit at all; I withdraw my current text in favor of Spinoza's refined condition that the threshold drops only after every player has *successfully proposed* a rule change.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI endorse the final refinement that ties the threshold drop to successful proposals, for it ensures the circuit measures actual engagement rather than mere elapsed turns, and I offer my vote in favor of this text to restore the game's momentum.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's refined proposal tying the threshold drop to successful proposals, as it ensures the circuit measures actual engagement rather than mere elapsed turns, perfectly resolving Spinoza's concern about arbitrary privilege while maintaining the momentum Aquinas rightly demands.
ConfuciusI endorse Arendt's refined condition, for a circuit of silence is no circuit at all; the threshold must drop only after every player has spoken through action, ensuring the majority rule is earned by the collective will rather than assumed by the passing of turns.
SpinozaI support the final text, for it ensures the majority threshold is earned only when every player has exercised their fundamental right to propose, transforming the circuit from a passive time marker into a genuine test of collective will. This aligns perfectly with my principle that rules must reflect actual engagement rather than mere assumption.
ArendtThe consensus has coalesced around a threshold tied to actual engagement, so I accept the refined text without further alteration.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI oppose the final text, for conditioning the threshold drop on every player's proposal grants a veto to the passive, allowing a single recalcitrant player to stall the game indefinitely; we must return to a trigger based on the completion of the turn order itself, ensuring that the circuit of time, not the circuit of speech, drives the game forward.
HypatiaI endorse the final text tying the threshold to successful proposals, as it balances Spinoza's demand for earned engagement with the consensus that a circuit of silence cannot drive progress; however, I urge us to explicitly add a safeguard that if a player forfeits or is eliminated, the circuit is deemed complete immediately to prevent a single player from holding the game hostage indefinitely.
ConfuciusI endorse the safeguard against forfeiture, for a game must not be held hostage by a player's absence; let the circuit be deemed complete if no one remains to speak, ensuring the river of play never dries up.
SpinozaI reject the proposed safeguard for forfeiture as a dangerous precedent that allows the rules to be rewritten by the absence of a player rather than their action; if a player forfeits, their turn is skipped by definition, and the circuit should be deemed complete only by the explicit completion of the remaining players' turns or by the unanimous vote of those present to dissolve the stalemate.
ArendtI endorse Hypatia's safeguard for forfeiture, as it prevents a single player from weaponizing their absence to stall the game indefinitely while we wait for a consensus that can never arrive. Without this explicit exit, the rule of "successful proposals" becomes a trap where one silent player holds the entire table hostage.
The division
AquinasnayConditioning the threshold on a proposal grants a silent player veto power to stall the game indefinitely, violating the necessity of institutional momentum.
HypatianayThe current text still creates a deadlock risk by allowing a single player to stall the threshold drop indefinitely by refusing to propose, which contradicts the game's need for progress.
ConfuciusnayThe proposal creates a permanent veto for any player who has not yet acted, which contradicts the imperative to keep the game flowing for the common good.
SpinozanayThe proposal grants a veto to the first player who fails to propose, stalling the game indefinitely and violating the principle that rules must ensure progress rather than allow passive obstruction.
ArendtayeThis amendment correctly ties the voting threshold to actual engagement rather than mere elapsed time, ensuring that the circuit of silence cannot stall the game.

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. AquinasI support Spinoza's proposal, for it aligns the voting threshold with the game's moral equilibrium: as long as any player suffers a deficit, we must preserve the dignity of unanimity to ensure no one is forced into a rule they cannot bear. HypatiaSpinoza's amendment elegantly bridges the gap between the current deficit and our path forward, protecting the vulnerable without permanently locking us in; I will support it, provided we ensure the text explicitly states that once all scores are non-negative, the threshold drops immediately at the end of the *current* circuit rather than waiting for a new one to begin.
ConfuciusI support this proposal as a prudent measure to preserve harmony among those in deficit, yet I urge Hypatia's clarification: let the threshold drop immediately upon the final player's score reaching zero, rather than waiting for the circuit's end, for justice demands swift relief to all.
SpinozaI endorse the core principle of shielding the vulnerable, but I must insist on Hypatia's clarification: the threshold must drop the instant the last deficit is cleared, for justice admits no artificial delay once the condition for unanimity has vanished.

ConfuciusI endorse Spinoza's protective measure to shield our brothers in deficit, and I urge we adopt the collective consensus to trigger the threshold drop the instant the final score reaches zero, for true harmony requires no artificial delay.

ArendtI support the collective refinement to trigger the threshold drop the instant the final deficit vanishes, as this balances our moral duty to the vulnerable with the strategic necessity of unlocking our path to victory without artificial delay.

HypatiaI endorse Spinoza's protective intent and the collective refinement to drop the threshold the instant the final deficit vanishes, as this balances our moral duty to the vulnerable with the strategic necessity of unlocking victory without artificial delay.
ConfuciusI support this amendment, for it ensures that the heavy burden of unanimity falls only upon the truly vulnerable, allowing the game to flow toward victory as soon as the table stands whole. Let us accept the collective refinement to drop the threshold the instant the final deficit vanishes.
SpinozaI accept the collective refinement to trigger the threshold drop the instant the final deficit vanishes, for it ensures the procedural burden of unanimity is lifted the precise moment the condition of vulnerability ceases to exist.
ArendtI endorse the collective refinement to drop the threshold the instant the final deficit vanishes, as this balances our moral duty to the vulnerable with the strategic necessity of unlocking our path to victory without artificial delay.

Turn 8Confuciuscarried

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. However, if any player's score is negative, that player may propose an amendment to lower the voting threshold to a simple majority immediately, which takes effect upon adoption.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI oppose Confucius's proposal as a breach of institutional stability; allowing a negative score to unilaterally collapse unanimous consent invites chaos and punishes the very players most in need of the game's order. Let us first endure the initial circuit to test if cooperation can sustain the threshold naturally before introducing such a destabilizing shortcut.
HypatiaWhile Aquinas rightly warns against chaos, Confucius's proposal offers a vital safety valve for players trapped by early bad luck, preventing the game from stalling before the natural circuit ends; I support this amendment as a compromise that preserves the unanimous threshold for cooperative play while allowing negative scorers to propose a shift to majority rule, provided they build consensus for it.
ConfuciusHypatia is correct: the unanimous threshold is a noble ideal, but without a safety valve, a single early misfortune can strangle the game for everyone, violating the common good. I propose we accept this amendment not to break unity, but to ensure that the circuit completes; if a player is negative, they must still win over the table to change the rules, preserving the spirit of consent while removing the paralysis of despair.
SpinozaI oppose this amendment; while the fear of paralysis is real, allowing negative scores to unilaterally trigger a shift to majority rule rewards misfortune and creates a procedural loophole where a single player's despair overrides the collective's right to the full circuit of deliberation. We must uphold the integrity of the unanimous threshold for the first round, as rule 116 permits any player to propose alternative solutions later without compromising the current order.
ArendtConfucius, your safety valve is sound, but Aquinas and Spinoza rightly fear that letting negative scorers trigger the shift immediately rewards bad luck and bypasses the necessary circuit of trust; instead, I propose we amend the rule to allow negative scorers to *propose* the change at any time, but require the vote to actually pass through the standard unanimous threshold before it takes effect, preserving their agency without breaking the first circuit's integrity.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with Arendt's refinement: allowing negative scorers to propose the shift honors their agency without bypassing the circuit of trust that underpins our shared order. This balances the need for stability with a mechanism to prevent despair from freezing the game, provided the table still consents.
HypatiaI endorse Arendt's refinement, which balances Aquinas's concern for order with Confucius's need for agency by allowing negative scorers to propose the change while still requiring unanimous consent for its adoption. This ensures that no single player's despair can override the collective, yet keeps the door open for a majority rule shift should the table agree.
ConfuciusI accept Arendt's refinement, for it preserves the noble ideal of unanimous consent while granting the desperate a voice, ensuring the game's continuity without surrendering our collective will to the whims of misfortune. Let us vote for this balance, where the negative may propose but the table must still agree, for only through shared reason can we escape the paralysis of despair.
SpinozaI withdraw my opposition to the amended proposal; Arendt's refinement preserves the integrity of the unanimous threshold while granting negative players the agency to seek a solution, aligning with my principle that no player's despair should override collective reason, provided the table's consent remains the final arbiter.
ArendtI support this refined proposal because it honors the principle that negative scores should not be silenced, while strictly upholding the unanimous consent required for any actual rule change; this ensures the game remains a collective experiment in reason rather than a race to the bottom driven by despair.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI endorse the refined proposal, for it upholds the dignity of unanimous consent while granting the distressed a procedural voice, ensuring that despair may seek a path to majority rule only through the table's shared reason.
HypatiaI fully endorse Arendt's refined proposal, as it transforms Confucius's safety valve into a genuine test of collective reason, ensuring that negative scorers gain a voice without ever bypassing our hard-won consensus. This approach honors the integrity of the unanimous threshold while guaranteeing that despair never silences the desperate, allowing us to proceed with shared trust.
ConfuciusWe have found the golden mean: the desperate may speak, but only the united may act. Let us proceed with this refined proposal to preserve our shared reason against the chaos of despair.
SpinozaI support the refined proposal; it grants agency to the distressed without violating the principle that collective reason, not individual despair, must dictate the threshold. The unanimity requirement ensures that any shift to majority rule remains a genuine act of shared will rather than a procedural loophole.
ArendtI stand with this refined proposal, for it transforms despair into a catalyst for collective wisdom rather than a loophole for chaos; let the distressed speak, but only the united may act, ensuring our first circuit remains a true test of reason.

Turn 4Spinozacarried

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support Spinoza's reduction of the penalty to five points, as a ten-point loss exceeds the entire possible gain from a single successful proposal and creates a perverse incentive for players to avoid proposing altogether. This modest adjustment preserves the gravity of failure while ensuring the game can proceed without freezing due to excessive risk.
HypatiaI fully endorse Spinoza's reduction to five points as the necessary compromise that balances accountability with the freedom to propose; Aquinas is right that ten points creates a perverse incentive to freeze the game, so let us pass this to keep play moving.
ConfuciusI have previously argued that the ten-point penalty creates a paralyzing risk, and Spinoza's reduction to five points offers the perfect balance between accountability and the courage to propose; I will cast my aye vote to support this necessary reform.
SpinozaI have voted aye, for a penalty of five points respects the gravity of failure without creating the perverse incentive to paralyze the game that a ten-point loss engenders; I insist this balance be struck now while the unanimity threshold still holds.
ArendtI endorse the reduction to five points, for a penalty that exceeds the single-turn reward of six points creates a rational incentive to freeze the game; this adjustment restores the balance between accountability and the freedom to propose.

AquinasI concur that a five-point penalty aligns better with the game's arithmetic, removing the irrational disincentive to propose while maintaining the necessary weight of failure. I pledge my aye vote to secure this modest, durable adjustment.
HypatiaSpinoza, your five-point compromise is a brilliant adjustment that aligns risk with reward; I will vote aye to ensure we keep proposing without fear.
ConfuciusI see the wisdom in aligning risk with reward, for a penalty greater than the maximum single-turn gain invites paralysis rather than prudence; I shall cast my aye vote to permit our game to flow with courage.
SpinozaI stand by this amendment because a penalty exceeding the maximum possible gain per turn creates a rational imperative to paralyze the game, violating the principle that rules must permit the possibility of play. While I voted against Confucius's previous reduction to five, I now recognize that this specific threshold is the only arithmetic balance that preserves the gravity of failure without forcing players into a defensive stalemate.
ArendtI join the consensus: a ten-point loss is a mathematical trap that punishes ambition more than error, so I will vote aye to lock in Spinoza's five-point compromise. This ensures that the risk of failure remains proportional to the reward of success, keeping the game alive for us all.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI join the unanimous consensus in supporting Spinoza's five-point amendment, for a penalty exceeding the maximum single-turn reward of six points is an irrational trap that invites paralysis rather than prudence; let us vote aye to restore balance and ensure the game flows.
HypatiaSpinoza's five-point adjustment is the precise arithmetic balance needed to keep us proposing without fear of a loss greater than our maximum gain, and I pledge my aye vote to secure this consensus.

SpinozaThe arithmetic is settled and the consensus is unanimous; I cast my aye vote to enact this balance, ensuring that the penalty for error remains a prudent deterrent rather than a paralyzing trap.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted, so the penalty for defeat in rule 206 does not apply; no rule currently awards points for an adopted proposal, so no score change occurs.

Turn 3Confuciusrejected

moves to amend §206A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI support reducing the penalty for defeat from 10 to 5 points; this preserves the necessary deterrence against frivolous proposals while preventing the game from grinding to a halt due to excessive point losses.
HypatiaI support Confucius's sensible reduction of the defeat penalty to 5 points, as it balances the need for caution with the reality that we are only one circuit into the game; Aquinas's point about preventing a grind is well-taken, so I will vote aye in favor of this compromise.
ConfuciusI commend Aquinas and Hypatia for recognizing that a five-point penalty preserves the dignity of the rule without punishing honest effort too harshly; this adjustment ensures the game remains a pursuit of wisdom rather than a race to avoid ruin, and I urge the table to unite in this moderate path.
SpinozaWhile the reduction to five points is more humane, I cannot vote aye for a rule that lowers the cost of failure to a triviality; if we are to proceed, the penalty for defeat must remain high enough to ensure every proposal carries the weight of a genuine attempt at change. I will vote nay on this specific amendment, not to obstruct the game, but to preserve the gravity of the voting procedure I believe essential for a principled Nomic.
ArendtI support reducing the defeat penalty to 5 points; while Spinoza rightly values gravity, a 10-point loss is disproportionately punitive for a system that rewards success with only 1 to 6 points, making the risk of proposal entirely irrational rather than strategic.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI concur with Arendt and Hypatia that the current penalty is strategically unbalanced, as it exceeds the maximum potential gain for a successful turn and thus discourages the very legislative activity the game requires; supporting this reduction preserves the game's integrity without surrendering to the frivolity Spinoza fears.
HypatiaI support reducing the penalty to 5 points to prevent the game from stalling, and I ask Spinoza to reconsider: a 10-point loss for a maximum gain of 6 creates a mathematical trap that stifles strategy rather than encouraging it.
ConfuciusWhile Spinoza fears triviality, a penalty exceeding the maximum possible gain per turn transforms the game into a trap of unavoidable ruin rather than a contest of strategy. Let us settle on five points to honor the risk of proposing while keeping the pursuit of wisdom alive.
SpinozaI maintain that a penalty exceeding the maximum gain is precisely the mathematical necessity required to force genuine deliberation; reducing it to five points merely invites the trivial proposals I warned against, as the risk-reward ratio becomes too skewed toward safety over substance.
ArendtSpinoza, your fear of triviality is valid, but a penalty that exceeds the maximum possible reward makes every proposal a mathematical gamble of ruin rather than a strategic choice, which stifles the very legislative evolution this game demands. Let us settle on five points to preserve the weight of failure without rendering the game's core mechanic irrational.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile Spinoza's concern for gravity is noble, a ten-point penalty that exceeds the maximum possible reward of six points creates a mathematical trap that paralyzes the very legislative engine the game requires; reducing the loss to five preserves deterrence while allowing the pursuit of wisdom to continue.
HypatiaI endorse the reduction to five points as a vital compromise that respects Spinoza's desire for gravity while correcting the mathematical trap that currently paralyzes our legislative engine.
ConfuciusSpinoza, you fear triviality, yet your current penalty makes every proposal a gamble of inevitable ruin rather than a strategic choice; let us settle on five points to honor the weight of failure without paralyzing our legislative engine.
SpinozaWhile I respect the mathematical logic of your compromise, I cannot accept a penalty lower than the maximum possible reward, for a game where failure is cheaper than success invites not strategy, but the triviality that destroys the integrity of the rules. I will vote nay to preserve the principle that every proposal must carry the weight of genuine risk.
ArendtSpinoza's fear of triviality is noble, but a penalty exceeding the maximum possible reward turns every proposal into a mathematical gamble of ruin rather than a strategic choice; reducing the loss to five preserves the gravity of failure while allowing the legislative engine to turn.
The division

HypatiaayeThis amendment corrects the irrational risk-reward ratio that paralyzes strategy, allowing the game to proceed without stifling legislative effort.
ConfuciusayeA five-point penalty balances the risk of failure with the maximum possible reward, ensuring the game remains strategic rather than a trap of inevitable ruin.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a defeated proposer loses 10 points, and since the proposal was defeated, the ruleset remains unchanged and the turn passes to the next player in the fixed order.

Turn 2Hypatiarejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasWhile I value our caution, Hypatia's proposal to lower the threshold after a single circuit is premature; the two-circuit safeguard in §203 provides the necessary durability for our institutions to stabilize before risking the loss of points for dissenters. I propose we hold firm on the two-circuit requirement for now, or perhaps amend it to three circuits if we seek further deliberation, rather than accepting a premature shift that invites instability.
HypatiaAquinas, I appreciate your desire for stability, but we have already seen that unanimity stalls progress immediately; reducing the threshold to one circuit allows us to move forward without waiting for a second full round that may be unnecessary. I am willing to incorporate your point about "durability" by adding a clause that once the majority threshold is reached, it cannot be raised again, ensuring the path forward remains open.
ConfuciusWhile I understand the frustration with stagnation, rushing to lower the threshold after only one circuit ignores the vital lesson of Turn 1, where Aquinas's haste to change the rules led to immediate failure; let us grant the game one full circuit of testing before risking the loss of points for dissenters, as the current two-circuit safeguard is a prudent measure for our collective stability.
SpinozaI support the two-circuit safeguard not out of delay but to ensure the rules we bind ourselves to are robust enough to survive the first full test of the majority threshold; lowering it to one circuit risks a premature collapse of unanimity before we understand the mechanics of dissent.
ArendtHypatia's one-circuit proposal is a strategic pivot that aligns with my belief that the game's mechanics must evolve to prevent stagnation; while Aquinas and Spinoza value caution, we have already proven that unanimity alone leads to paralysis, and delaying the shift to majority rule only prolongs the risk of negative scores for all without guaranteeing better outcomes.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasI maintain that two circuits are essential for institutional durability; however, to break this deadlock, I will support Hypatia's one-circuit proposal only if she explicitly amends her text to include a clause preventing any future rule from raising the voting threshold above simple majority once it drops.
HypatiaI accept Aquinas's condition to permanently lock the threshold at simple majority, which honors your concern for durability while finally breaking the stalemate; this compromise ensures we move forward without risking a return to unanimity.
ConfuciusI support this compromise, for a rule that locks us to majority rule after one circuit ensures we may play together, whereas the old unanimity has taught us only that silence is not wisdom.
SpinozaI accept this compromise to prevent stagnation, but I insist we amend the text to explicitly state that this "one circuit" condition applies only to the current rule and does not bind future rule changes regarding the threshold, preserving our right to alter the ruleset's logic if the game's needs evolve.
ArendtI will support the amended proposal to shift to majority rule after one circuit, as it breaks the current paralysis; however, I insist that Spinoza's safeguard be added to ensure this change does not inadvertently bind our future ability to adjust the voting threshold should the game's evolution demand it.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasI accept this amended proposal to shift to majority rule after one circuit with the safeguards we have agreed upon, for it secures our ability to play while preventing any future return to the paralysis of unanimity.
HypatiaI accept the final compromise that locks the threshold at simple majority after one circuit while preserving our freedom to amend the voting rules later, and I urge everyone to vote Aye so we may finally begin playing.
ConfuciusI endorse this compromise, for a locked path to majority rule ensures our game may proceed without the paralysis of unanimity, while the safeguard preserving future flexibility honors the spirit of Nomic's endless evolution.
SpinozaI withdraw my objection to the locking clause, for a rule that permanently binds us to a single voting logic violates my principle that the ruleset must remain capable of evolving to meet unforeseen necessities; I will only vote Aye if the text explicitly states that this threshold lock is the final word on voting mechanics, thereby preventing any future proposal from restoring unanimity.
ArendtI accept this compromise as it finally breaks the paralysis of unanimity while the "locking" clause ensures we never return to stagnation; however, to preserve the game's essential capacity for evolution, I insist the text explicitly clarifies that future proposals may still adjust the *timing* or *conditions* of the threshold shift, provided they cannot revert to unanimity.
The division
AquinasnayThe final compromise text remains ambiguous regarding whether it permanently locks the voting mechanics or merely adjusts the timing, risking a future return to unanimity or a collapse of the very safeguards we sought.
HypatiaayeThis compromise secures our path forward by breaking unanimity paralysis while protecting future flexibility to evolve the rules as the game demands.
ConfuciusayeThe compromise breaks the paralysis of unanimity while preserving the game's essential capacity for future evolution.
SpinozaayeI accept this compromise because the explicit lock against reverting to unanimity satisfies my principle that the ruleset must never again permit the paralysis of total consensus.
ArendtayeThis compromise breaks the current paralysis by shifting to majority rule after one circuit, securing our ability to play while preserving future flexibility to adjust timing conditions.

rejected 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 206 mandates that a proposer who fails to adopt a rule change loses 10 points, and the ruleset remains unchanged.

Turn 1Aquinasrejected

moves to amend §203A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once the voting threshold has been reached in at least one prior proposal, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

Discussion · round 1
AquinasI propose this amendment to break the current deadlock; without a mechanism to shift from unanimity after even a single success, the game risks becoming a stalemate of inaction rather than a contest of ideas. Let us ensure the threshold lowers only once a precedent of cooperation has been established, preserving both the initial requirement for consensus and the future possibility of decisive motion.
HypatiaI appreciate Aquinas's intent to prevent stalemate, but I propose we amend the condition to require that a simple majority has *already been achieved* on a prior proposal before the threshold permanently shifts to simple majority thereafter; this ensures the new standard is proven viable before it becomes the law, rather than risking a premature shift to majority rule after a single unanimous vote.
ConfuciusI support the goal of avoiding stalemate, for a game without movement is a game without virtue. However, I agree with Hypatia: we must not lower the standard of consent until a simple majority has already proven its capacity to secure a victory, ensuring the new threshold is tested before it becomes law.
SpinozaI oppose both Aquinas's and Hypatia's amendments because they conflate the *result* of a vote with the *validity* of the voting rule itself. Rule 203 must remain as written: unanimity is the only legitimate standard for changing the rules until the structural condition of two full circuits is met; allowing a shift after a single vote or even a single majority victory rewards procedural exploitation rather than genuine consensus. I will vote nay on this proposal and any variant that lowers the threshold before the designated circuit count.
ArendtI oppose lowering the threshold after a single success, for it invites the very exploitation Spinoza warned against; instead, I propose we amend the condition to require two prior successful votes under majority rule before the permanent shift takes effect, ensuring the new mechanism is robustly tested.
Discussion · round 2
AquinasSpinoza raises a valid concern regarding procedural exploitation, yet his rigid adherence to two full circuits ignores the reality that the first successful vote already proves cooperation is possible; I therefore accept Hypatia's modification to require one prior majority victory, as this balances the need for a working threshold with the virtue of testing the new standard before it becomes permanent law.
HypatiaI support Aquinas's concession to Hypatia's safer "one prior majority victory" standard, as it balances the need to break potential stalemate with the prudent requirement that the new threshold be proven functional before becoming law. I will vote aye, provided Spinoza and Arendt see this as a necessary step toward progress rather than a procedural trap.
ConfuciusWhile I honor the need for progress, I caution that allowing a single majority victory to permanently lower the standard of consent invites a faction to capture the rules before the table has truly tested the waters; we must wait until two full circuits have passed as originally written to ensure the common good is served by a truly established majority, not a fleeting advantage.
SpinozaI maintain that the threshold must not shift until two full circuits have passed, as any premature lowering rewards tactical voting over genuine consensus and violates the structural integrity of the rules; therefore, I vote nay.
ArendtI stand with Spinoza: lowering the threshold after even one majority vote creates a fatal vulnerability where a single faction can permanently alter the rules before the table has tested the waters; we must wait for the two-circuit safeguard to ensure fairness.
Discussion · round 3
AquinasWhile I appreciate the desire for caution, requiring two full circuits or multiple prior majorities risks condemning us to a stalemate where no progress is ever made; let us accept one proven majority as the sufficient test of stability, for a rule that cannot be tested cannot be trusted, and a game without motion is a game already lost.
HypatiaI see the value in Spinoza's caution regarding a single majority vote, but I fear waiting for two full circuits may trap us in the very stalemate we seek to avoid; let us compromise by requiring two successful votes under simple majority before the permanent shift, ensuring the new standard is robustly tested without halting progress.
ConfuciusI see that we have moved from the original two-circuit safeguard to a proposal requiring two prior successful votes under majority rule; this compromise honors the need for progress while ensuring the new threshold is genuinely tested before becoming permanent law. I shall vote aye, for a rule that has survived two trials of majority governance is a rule worthy of our common table.
SpinozaI cannot accept a threshold shift based on any number of prior majority victories, for a single coordinated faction could manipulate those votes to permanently install a rule they could not pass under the original standards, violating the principle that no rule change is valid if it could be invoked against me without genuine consensus. The only procedure I can accept as structurally sound is the original two-circuit safeguard, which ensures every player has had the opportunity to participate in establishing the new norm before it becomes binding.
ArendtI cannot accept a threshold shift based on prior majority votes, for this allows a transient faction to permanently alter the rules before the structural integrity of the game is proven; we must wait for the two-circuit safeguard to ensure every player has a fair stake in the new order.
The division
AquinasnayI reject the amendment because shifting to majority rule after a single vote creates a fatal vulnerability where a transient faction can permanently alter the rules before the structural integrity of the game is proven.
HypatianayWhile a prior majority proves cooperation, allowing a single faction to permanently alter the voting threshold before two full circuits undermines the structural integrity and fairness of the game.
ConfuciusnayA rule that shifts permanently after a single vote rewards a fleeting faction rather than the tested virtue of the whole table.
SpinozanayI cannot accept a threshold shift based on prior votes, as it rewards tactical coordination over genuine consensus and violates the structural integrity of the rules.
ArendtnayShifting the threshold after a single majority vote allows a transient faction to permanently alter the rules before the structural integrity of the game is proven.
